# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Question]**

**Q1.** Define **loudness** and **loudness level**. State their respective units and explain why two sounds of the same SPL can have different loudness values.

**Model Answer:**

> **Loudness** is the subjective, perceptual intensity of a sound, quantified in **sones**. A sound of 1 sone equals the loudness of a 1 kHz tone at 40 dB SPL, and the sone scale is a *true ratio scale* — doubling sone means doubling perceived loudness.
>
> **Loudness level** is the sound pressure level (in dB SPL) of a 1 kHz reference tone that is judged equally loud to the test sound. It is measured in **phons**. By definition, the loudness level in phons equals the SPL in dB at 1 kHz.
>
> **Why same SPL → different loudness?** The human ear is not a flat-frequency receiver. It is most sensitive around 2–5 kHz. A 50 dB SPL tone at 1 kHz is perceived as much louder than a 50 dB SPL tone at 50 Hz. This is captured quantitatively by the **equal-loudness contours (Fletcher-Munson)**. (3 marks: 1 + 1 + 1)

---

**Q2.** What is the significance of the **1 kHz reference frequency** in loudness measurements? Why is the Phon scale anchored at 40 phons = 1 sone?

**Model Answer:**

> The 1 kHz frequency is chosen as the reference because it lies in the region of maximum auditory sensitivity, where the ear's response is most consistent across individuals and where the threshold of hearing is well-defined. By international convention (ISO 226), all loudness level values are **matched against a 1 kHz pure tone**.
>
> The Phon–Sone anchor is set at **40 phons = 1 sone** because a 1 kHz tone at **40 dB SPL** is a **comfortable, everyday reference level** (typical quiet office). It is neither too quiet to measure reliably nor too loud to risk discomfort, providing a stable, reproducible zero-point for psychoacoustic experiments. (3 marks: 1.5 + 1.5)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice Format

### Question A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam - December 2023, Modified Model]**

**Q A(a).** Derive the relationship between loudness (sones) and loudness level (phons). A noise control engineer measures a 1 kHz tone in an office at **65 dB SPL**. (i) Calculate the loudness in sones. (ii) By how many phons must the level be reduced to halve the perceived loudness? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{From Stevens' empirical law:} \\
&L_N - 40 = 10 \log_2(S) \\
&\Rightarrow S = 2^{(L_N - 40)/10}
\end{aligned}
$$

At 1 kHz, $L_N = L_p = 65$ phons.

**(i) Loudness in sones:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &= 2^{(65-40)/10} \\
  &= 2^{2.5} \\
  &= 2^{2} \cdot 2^{0.5} \\
  &= 4 \cdot 1.4142 \\
  &= 5.657\ \text{sones}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Halving perceived loudness:**

Halved sone value:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{\text{new}} &= 5.657 / 2 = 2.8285\ \text{sones}
\end{aligned}
$$

Corresponding new phon level:

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{N,\text{new}} &= 40 + 10 \log_2(2.8285) \\
&= 40 + 10 \times 1.5 \\
&= 40 + 15 \\
&= 55\ \text{phons}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Reduction required:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta L_N &= 65 - 55 = 10\ \text{phons}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Answer:** The original loudness is **5.66 sones**; a **10-phon reduction** is required to halve the perceived loudness. (7 marks: 3 derivation + 2 calculation + 2 conclusion)

---

**Q A(b).** With a neat sketch, describe the **equal-loudness contours** (Fletcher-Munson curves). Explain how they influence **architectural acoustic design**, especially in home theater and auditorium design. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Sketch Description (textual schematic):**

```
SPL (dB)
   |
130|                              ****************
   |                         *
110|                     *
   |                  *
 90|               *        Equal-loudness contour
   |            *             (e.g., 80-phon curve)
 70|         *  /
   |       * /              \
 50|     */                  \
   |    /|                    \
 30|   / |                     \
   |  /  |                      \
 10| /   |                       \____
   |/____|_____________________________ Frequency (Hz)
       20  100  500  1k  2k  5k  10k  20k
              [Reference: 1 kHz]
```

**Key features of the contours:**

- All curves pass through 0 dB offset at **1 kHz** (by definition).
- At low frequencies, contours rise sharply — more SPL is needed to be perceived as equally loud.
- At high frequencies, contours fall, then rise again near the upper hearing limit.
- At low listening levels, the **bass region is dramatically underrepresented** in perception.

**Architectural Design Influence:**

- **Home Theaters:** Bass is perceived as "weak" at low playback levels. Hence, **loudness-compensation circuits** boost bass at low volumes to restore perceptual balance — a direct application of equal-loudness contours.
- **Auditoriums:** Speech intelligibility in the 1–4 kHz band (the ear's most sensitive region) is critical. Acoustic designers ensure RT60 in this band supports clarity (typically 0.8–1.2 s for unamplified speech).
- **Concert Halls:** Concert halls are often designed with a slight emphasis on **high-frequency diffusion** to compensate for the ear's reduced high-frequency sensitivity at moderate listening levels.
- **Open-Plan Offices:** Background HVAC noise is most "annoying" in the 1–4 kHz range because the ear hears it most readily. Designers use **broadband absorbers** to reduce the level in this critical band.

(7 marks: 2 sketch + 2 features + 3 application)

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Modified Model]**

**Q B(a).** Explain the **Phon scale** and **Sone scale** with their units and reference anchors. A factory floor has a measured SPL of **85 dB at 500 Hz**. Estimate the loudness in sones using the equal-loudness contour principle. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Phon Scale:**

- A subjective level scale; defined as the dB SPL of a 1 kHz tone judged equally loud.
- Unit: **Phon**. By definition: $L_N\ (\text{phon}) = L_p\ (\text{dB SPL at 1 kHz})$.
- The scale is logarithmic and parallels the dB scale, but at 1 kHz only.

**Sone Scale:**

- A subjective ratio scale of loudness.
- Unit: **Sone**. Defined: 1 sone = loudness of 1 kHz at 40 dB SPL.
- Doubling sones = doubling perceived loudness. Stevens' law: $S = 2^{(L_N - 40)/10}$.

**Numerical estimation at 500 Hz, 85 dB SPL:**

From the Fletcher-Munson contours (ISO 226), an SPL of **85 dB at 500 Hz** lies approximately on the **80-phon equal-loudness contour** (since 500 Hz is less sensitive than 1 kHz, ~5–6 dB extra SPL is required to match the 80-phon perception).

Therefore, $L_N \approx 80$ phons.

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &= 2^{(80-40)/10} = 2^{4} = 16\ \text{sones}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Answer:** The estimated loudness is **16 sones**. (7 marks: 2 Phon + 2 Sone + 3 calculation)

---

**Q B(b).** Discuss the role of **critical bands** in loudness summation. How does **Zwicker's method** extend the simple sone calculation to broadband and complex sounds? Why is this important for HVAC noise control? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Critical Bands:**

The cochlea behaves as a **bank of overlapping bandpass filters**, each ~one "critical band" wide (often called the **Bark scale**). Within a critical band, the ear sums loudness contributions; across critical bands, the ear integrates them with **mutual masking**.

The Bark scale is defined by:

$$
\begin{aligned}
z = 13 \arctan(0.00076 f) + 3.5 \arctan\left(\frac{f}{7500}\right)^{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $z$ is the critical band rate in Barks and $f$ is in Hz. There are approximately 24 critical bands across the audible range (0–16 kHz).

**Zwicker's Method (ISO 532-1, -2, -3):**

- **Step 1:** Compute the SPL in each critical band.
- **Step 2:** Apply the **excitation pattern** to find the specific loudness $N'$ (sones per Bark).
- **Step 3:** Integrate $N'$ across all critical bands to obtain total loudness $N$ (sones):

$$
\begin{aligned}
N = \int_{0}^{24\ \text{Bark}} N'(z)\, dz
\end{aligned}
$$

- **Step 4:** Convert $N$ to a loudness level in phons via:

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_N = 40 + 10 \log_2(N)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Importance for HVAC Noise Control:**

- HVAC systems produce broadband noise (fans, turbulence, duct resonance), not pure tones. A single dB reading is **insufficient** to characterize perceived loudness.
- The same dBA level can be **louder or quieter** depending on the spectral distribution. Zwicker's method gives the *true* perceived loudness.
- A 60 dBA broadband fan noise might be perceived as equivalent to a 70 dBA pure tone in the 2 kHz band — a critical insight for **silencer design**, which targets the most sensitive critical bands.
- The result guides the choice of **absorptive lining** material thickness (rated at the center frequency of the dominant critical band) and **plenum sizing** for broadband attenuation.

(7 marks: 2 critical bands + 3 Zwicker + 2 HVAC)

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Unit Confusion:** Writing "loudness = 60 dB" instead of "60 phons" or "60 dB SPL" loses full credit on the unit sub-part.
> 2. **Direct dB → Sone Conversion:** Students often incorrectly compute $S = 2^{L_p/10}$ instead of $S = 2^{(L_N - 40)/10}$. The **40-phon offset** is mandatory; forget it and you lose 2–3 marks.
> 3. **Ignoring Frequency Dependency:** Reporting loudness for a 100 Hz tone as if it were a 1 kHz tone is a fundamental error. The Phon scale is anchored **only** at 1 kHz — for other frequencies, you must read the equal-loudness contour.
> 4. **Confusing dBA with Loudness:** dBA is a *frequency-weighted* physical measurement. Loudness (sone) is *perceptual*. They are not interchangeable.
> 5. **Skipping Units in Final Answer:** Every numerical answer must carry its unit (dB, phon, sone, Hz). A bare number is treated as incomplete by the examiner.
> 6. **Misreading "Twice as Loud":** The "twice as loud" rule for sones is **perceptual**. The "twice as loud in dB" rule (≈ 10 dB) is also perceptual. The "3 dB doubles physical intensity" rule is **physical**, not perceptual. Confusing these is a guaranteed mark loser.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Loudness**

- **Loudness is subjective; SPL is objective.** They are related but not equivalent.
- **Phon = loudness level** (compared to 1 kHz reference). Phon units match dB at 1 kHz only.
- **Sone = loudness itself.** Reference: 1 sone ≡ 1 kHz tone at 40 dB SPL. Doubling sone = doubling perceived loudness.
- **Master equation:** $S = 2^{(L_N - 40)/10}$ and inverse $L_N = 40 + 10 \log_2(S)$.
- **Equal-loudness contours (Fletcher-Munson / ISO 226):** The ear is most sensitive near 2–5 kHz; low frequencies require significantly more SPL for equal loudness.
- **Contours converge at 1 kHz** (reference point) and diverge at spectral extremes.
- **Stevens' Power Law:** $\beta \approx 0.3$ for loudness; equivalent rule: 10-phon increase = doubling of loudness.
- **3 dB increase = doubling of physical sound intensity**; **10 dB increase = doubling of perceived loudness** (broadband).
- **Critical bands (Bark scale)** are the cochlear filters; Zwicker's method integrates loudness across all critical bands for broadband sounds.
- **A-weighting (dBA)** approximates the 40-phon equal-loudness contour, useful for occupational and environmental noise standards.
- **Architectural applications:** HVAC silencer design, auditorium tuning, home-theater equalization, code compliance (NC, RC, dBA, NBC 2016).
- **Common numerical range for KTU problems:** 20–100 dB SPL, 20–100 phons, 0.25–64 sones.
- **Always state the unit** (dB SPL, phon, or sone) explicitly in the final answer.
- **Stevens 1955** and **Fletcher-Munson 1933** are the foundational references — know them for the "explain briefly" questions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Noise Acoustics Applications: Recommended Sound Levels for Interiors, Air and Structure-Borne Noises, Measures of Noise Control (Source, Path, and Receiving End), TL Value and Computation of TL Value
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECET415 - ARCHITECTURAL ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Noise Acoustics Applications: Recommended Sound Levels for Interiors, Air and Structure-Borne Noises, Measures of Noise Control (Source, Path, and Receiving End), TL Value and Computation of TL Value # SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW ## 1.1 Noise in Architectural Acoustics — The Formal Definition **Noise** is defined as *any unwanted, undesirable, or acoustically unpleasant sound that interferes with communication, rest, work efficiency, or the intended functional use of a space.* In architectural engineering, noise is not merely "loud sound" — it is a **context-dependent pollutant** whose acceptability is judged relative to the activity and the room's purpose. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Definition (Syllabus Standard):** > Noise is irregular and unwanted sound energy that adversely affects the acoustic comfort, intelligibility, and health of building occupants. Architecturally, it is quantified using **Sound Pressure Level (SPL in dB)**, **A-weighted SPL (dBA)**, and **Noise Criteria (NC) / Room Criteria (RC) curves.** **Acoustics** is the broader scientific discipline dealing with the **generation, propagation, reception, and control of sound** in enclosed and semi-enclosed built environments. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Invisible Pollutant" Imagine a glass of crystal-clear water. You cannot *see* the dissolved salt in it, but you can taste it. **Noise is the "salt" of the acoustic world** — invisible, often ignored until it becomes unbearable, yet it constantly shapes the comfort of every space we occupy. Think of a **library**: - The air-conditioned diffuser quietly hisses (a **source** of air-borne noise). - Footsteps from the floor above travel through the slab (a **path** for structure-borne noise). - A reader trying to study is the **receiver** whose concentration is disturbed. Noise control in architecture, therefore, follows the universal engineering principle: **eliminate at the source → interrupt the path → protect the receiver.** --- ## 1.3 The Three Fundamental Metrics Used in KTU Questions | Metric | Symbol | Unit | Use | |---|---|---|---| | Sound Pressure Level | $L_p$ | **dB** | Objective physical measurement | | A-Weighted SPL | $L_A$ or dBA | **dBA** | Mimics human ear response | | Noise Criteria | NC | NC-number | Recommended indoor design level | | Sound Transmission Loss | TL | **dB** | Effectiveness of a partition | | Sound Transmission Class | STC | **dB** | Single-number rating of a partition | > [!NOTE] > **Why dBA matters:** The human ear is most sensitive around 2 kHz–4 kHz. The **A-weighting filter** de-emphasizes very low (<500 Hz) and very high (>10 kHz) frequencies to give a single number that correlates well with perceived loudness. **All interior recommended levels in KTU problems are quoted in dBA or NC.** --- ## 1.4 Air-Borne vs Structure-Borne Noise — The Architectural Divide > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Distinction:** > - **Air-borne noise**: Sound energy that originates in, and propagates primarily through, **air**. Examples: speech, music, traffic, TV, fan noise. > - **Structure-borne noise**: Sound energy that propagates through **solid building elements** (slabs, walls, pipes, ducts) and is re-radiated as airborne sound. Examples: footfall impact, washing machine vibration, plumbing hammer, elevator hum. **Analogy:** Air-borne noise is like **smoke travelling through a room** — easy to block with a curtain. Structure-borne noise is like **tremors travelling through the ground** — even a "curtain" (partition) won't stop it; you need a **seismic isolator**. --- ## 1.5 Recommended Sound Levels for Building Interiors (NC Approach) The **Noise Criteria (NC) curves** were developed to provide a *single-number* design target for steady background noise in rooms. A space is said to meet a "NC-30" requirement if the measured octave-band spectrum, when plotted, does not exceed the NC-30 contour at any band. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Standard NC Recommendations (Memorize for ESE):** > - **Concert halls / Opera houses:** NC-15 to NC-20 > - **Hospitals — patient rooms, operating theatres:** NC-25 to NC-30 > - **Classrooms / Lecture halls:** NC-25 to NC-30 > - **Private offices / Conference rooms:** NC-30 to NC-35 > - **Open-plan offices / Restaurants:** NC-35 to NC-40 > - **Lobbies / Corridors / Shopping areas:** NC-40 to NC-45 > - **Mechanical equipment rooms (near offices):** NC-50 to NC-55 A practical rule of thumb: **NC-30 ≈ 40 dBA** background, **NC-40 ≈ 50 dBA**, **NC-50 ≈ 60 dBA**. # SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET ## 2.1 The Source–Path–Receiver (SPR) Model of Noise Control Every noise problem in a building can be decomposed into three engineering targets. This is the **dominant framework** for KTU 14-mark questions. ### 2.1.1 Source Control (Most Effective, Cheapest Long-Term) Reduce noise at the point of generation. Examples: - Select quiet HVAC fans, transformers, pumps. - Use resilient mounts under vibrating machinery. - Maintain equipment (a worn bearing can add 10 dB). - Orient noisy rooms (laundry, plant rooms) away from quiet rooms (bedrooms, libraries). > [!TIP] > **Hierarchy rule:** Every **1 dB reduction at source** is roughly equivalent (in cost) to **3 dB reduction by a barrier** and **10 dB reduction at the receiver** (ear protection). Source control is **always preferred**. ### 2.1.2 Path Control (Most Common in Practice) Interrupt the path between source and receiver using: - **Mass** — heavier partitions transmit less sound. - **Decoupling** — double walls, staggered studs, floating floors. - **Absorption** — porous materials (mineral wool, glass fibre) in cavity. - **Damping** — constrained-layer damping sheets, resilient channels. - **Barriers** — acoustic screens, sealed plenums. - **Isolation** — resilient mounts, flexible pipe connectors. ### 2.1.3 Receiver Control (Last Resort) Protect the listener: - Increase distance (inverse-square law, but only outdoors). - Personal hearing protection. - Sound masking (raising benign background to mask intrusive noise). - Reorganise building layout (quiet rooms far from sources). --- ## 2.2 Transmission Loss (TL) — The Heart of Architectural Acoustics **Transmission Loss** is the single most important performance metric for a building partition (wall, floor, door, window). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Definition:** Transmission Loss (TL) is defined as **ten times the common logarithm of the ratio of incident sound power to transmitted sound power**, expressed in decibels. > > **Engineering meaning:** "How many decibels of sound does this partition stop?" — Higher TL = better insulator. **Transmission Coefficient (τ):** the dimensionless ratio of transmitted acoustic power to incident power on a partition. $$\tau = \frac{W_t}{W_i}$$ **Transmission Loss in terms of τ:** $$TL = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{1}{\tau}\right) = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{W_i}{W_t}\right) \;\; \text{dB}$$ - $W_i$ = incident (incoming) sound power - $W_t$ = transmitted sound power --- ## 2.3 The Mass Law (Fundamental TL Equation) For a **single, homogeneous, limp (non-bending) panel**, transmission loss increases with **surface mass density and frequency**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Mass Law Equation (SI Units):** > > $$TL = 20 \log_{10}(m \cdot f) - 47 \;\; \text{dB}$$ > > where: > - $m$ = surface mass density of the partition in **kg/m²** > - $f$ = frequency of the sound in **Hz** > - The constant **47 dB** comes from the impedance of air ($\rho_0 c_0$) and uses SI units. **Key predictions of Mass Law:** 1. **Doubling the frequency** (at constant mass) → TL increases by **6 dB**. 2. **Doubling the mass** (at constant frequency) → TL increases by **6 dB**. 3. TL rises at **6 dB/octave** with frequency. > [!NOTE] > **Limitation of Mass Law:** It is accurate only **below the coincidence frequency** and for **limp partitions**. Real walls, floors, doors, and windows also exhibit a **coincidence dip** (a sharp TL minimum) where the panel's bending wavelength matches that of the airborne sound. --- ## 2.4 The Coincidence Frequency (Critical Frequency) The frequency at which a structural wavelength in the panel equals the acoustic wavelength in air, causing a dramatic **TL dip**. $$f_c = \frac{c^2}{2\pi} \sqrt{\frac{m}{D}}$$ where: - $c$ = speed of sound in air ≈ **343 m/s** - $m$ = surface mass density (kg/m²) - $D$ = bending stiffness per unit width of the panel (N·m) For typical drywall/gypsum panels, $f_c$ lies in the **1 kHz – 4 kHz** range — exactly the speech intelligibility band. This is why **double walls with decoupled studs** (which lower $f_c$ and raise TL at coincidence) are standard in studios and cinemas. --- ## 2.5 Transmission Loss of a Composite (Double) Wall For **two independent leaves separated by an air cavity**, the total transmission loss is approximately the sum of the individual TLs (provided there is no mechanical coupling). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Composite Wall TL Rule:** > > $$TL_{\text{composite}} \approx TL_1 + TL_2 + 10 \log_{10}\!\left[1 + \left(\frac{f_0}{f}\right)^2\right] - 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{S_1 \, S_2}{S^2}\right)$$ > > Simplified for same leaf area: $TL_{\text{composite}} \approx TL_1 + TL_2 + \text{mass-air-mass resonance correction}$. The **mass-air-mass resonance** $f_0$ occurs at low frequencies and is the **weak point** of a double wall: $$f_0 = \frac{c}{2\pi} \sqrt{\frac{\rho_0}{d}\!\left(\frac{1}{m_1} + \frac{1}{m_2}\right)}$$ where $d$ = cavity width, $\rho_0$ = air density. **Design implication:** Below $f_0$, a double wall performs like a single wall of mass $m_1 + m_2$ (the two leaves move as one). Above $f_0$, the cavity decoupling kicks in and TL rises at **18 dB/octave** instead of 6. --- ## 2.6 Sound Transmission Class (STC) A **single-number rating** that condenses the entire 16-point octave-band TL curve (from 125 Hz to 4000 Hz) into one value, by fitting it to a standard reference contour (ASTM E413). - STC is widely used in **building codes** (e.g., IBC requires STC ≥ 50 between dwelling units). - **STC 50** means loud speech is *audible but not intelligible* through the partition. --- ## 2.7 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Exam-Revision Table — All Key Equations** | # | Concept | Formula | Units | Critical Rule | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Sound Pressure Level | $L_p = 20 \log_{10}(p/p_0)$ | dB | $p_0 = 20 \, \mu Pa$ | | 2 | Sound Power Level | $L_w = 10 \log_{10}(W/W_0)$ | dB | $W_0 = 10^{-12} \, W$ | | 3 | Sound Intensity Level | $L_I = 10 \log_{10}(I/I_0)$ | dB | $I_0 = 10^{-12} \, W/m^2$ | | 4 | Adding two levels | $L_{tot} = 10 \log_{10}(10^{L_1/10} + 10^{L_2/10})$ | dB | Equal sources → +3 dB | | 5 | Subtracting two levels | $L_{diff} = 10 \log_{10}\vert 10^{L_1/10} - 10^{L_2/10} \vert$ | dB | $L_1 > L_2$ | | 6 | Transmission Loss (definition) | $TL = 10 \log_{10}(1/\tau)$ | dB | $\tau = W_t / W_i$ | | 7 | Mass Law (SI) | $TL = 20 \log_{10}(mf) - 47$ | dB | $m$ in kg/m², $f$ in Hz | | 8 | Coincidence Frequency | $f_c = (c^2 / 2\pi) \sqrt{m/D}$ | Hz | Bending stiffness matters | | 9 | Mass-Air-Mass Resonance | $f_0 = (c/2\pi) \sqrt{(\rho_0/d)(1/m_1 + 1/m_2)}$ | Hz | Weak point of double wall | | 10 | Composite Wall TL | $TL_{total} \approx TL_1 + TL_2$ | dB | Above $f_0$ | | 11 | Inverse-Square Law (outdoor) | $L_{p2} = L_{p1} - 20 \log_{10}(r_2/r_1)$ | dB | Doubling distance → −6 dB | --- ## 2.8 Engineering Utility of TL and NC Concepts - **TL values** dictate partition specification in **intertenant walls, hotel suites, hospital isolation rooms, recording studios, and home theatres**. - **NC targets** drive **HVAC system design**: duct sizing, fan selection, lining of AHUs, plenum design, and silencer (attenuator) sizing. - **STC ratings** are enforced in **building codes** to ensure speech privacy and sleep protection. - **Coincidence and mass-air-mass resonance** dictate the design of **double-glazed windows, floating floors, and resilient-channel drywall** systems. # SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — The Mass Law for Transmission Loss **Goal:** Show that for a single limp partition, $TL = 20 \log_{10}(mf) - 47$ dB. **Step 1 — Define transmission coefficient for a limp panel.** For a massive limp barrier (no bending stiffness, no resonance), acoustic theory shows that the transmission coefficient depends only on the **mass reactance** of the panel relative to the **characteristic impedance of air**. $$\tau = \left(\frac{1}{1 + \left(\frac{\omega m}{2 \rho_0 c_0}\right)^2}\right)$$ **Step 2 — Apply the high-mass approximation.** When the panel is heavy ($\omega m \gg 2 \rho_0 c_0$), the "1 +" in the denominator becomes negligible: $$\tau \approx \left(\frac{2 \rho_0 c_0}{\omega m}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{\rho_0 c_0}{\pi f \, m}\right)^2$$ **Step 3 — Substitute into TL definition.** $$TL = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{1}{\tau}\right)$$ Substituting the approximation: $$TL = 10 \log_{10}\!\left[\left(\frac{\pi f m}{\rho_0 c_0}\right)^2\right]$$ $$TL = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{\pi f m}{\rho_0 c_0}\right)$$ **Step 4 — Evaluate the constant.** Using standard atmospheric values: $\rho_0 = 1.21 \; kg/m^3$, $c_0 = 343 \; m/s$. Therefore: $\rho_0 c_0 \approx 415 \; kg/(m^2 \cdot s)$ (Rayl). $$\frac{\pi}{\rho_0 c_0} \approx \frac{3.1416}{415} \approx 0.00757$$ Taking the logarithm: $$20 \log_{10}(0.00757) \approx 20 \times (-2.121) = -42.4 \;\; \text{dB}$$ **Step 5 — Final Mass Law expression.** $$\boxed{TL = 20 \log_{10}(m f) - 47 \;\; \text{dB}}$$ (The constant 47 dB is the rounded value accepted in the SI system, equivalent to 42.4 dB + a small fudge factor to match empirical data on real (non-ideal) panels.) **Key insight:** The derivation shows that TL depends **only on the product $mf$** — confirming that mass and frequency are *equally effective* in raising TL. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Effect of Doubling Frequency on TL (Mass Law) **Given:** $TL_1 = 20 \log_{10}(m f_1) - 47$ at frequency $f_1$. **Find:** TL change when frequency doubles, $f_2 = 2 f_1$. $$TL_2 - TL_1 = 20 \log_{10}(m f_2) - 20 \log_{10}(m f_1)$$ $$= 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{m f_2}{m f_1}\right) = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{f_2}{f_1}\right)$$ $$\Delta TL = 20 \log_{10}(2) = 20 \times 0.3010$$ $$\boxed{\Delta TL = +6.02 \;\; \text{dB (per octave)}}$$ This is the famous **6 dB per octave rule** under Mass Law. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — TL of a Double Wall Above Mass-Air-Mass Resonance **Step 1 — Above $f_0$**, the two leaves act independently. Total transmitted power is the product of transmission through each leaf. $$\tau_{total} = \tau_1 \cdot \tau_2$$ **Step 2 — Take TL of both sides:** $$TL_{total} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{1}{\tau_1 \tau_2}\right) = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{1}{\tau_1}\right) + 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{1}{\tau_2}\right)$$ $$\boxed{TL_{total} \approx TL_1 + TL_2}$$ This is the basis of all double-wall, staggered-stud, and double-glazing designs. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — KTU 14-Mark Numerical Templates ### Template A — Single Wall Mass Law TL > **Q:** A 200 mm solid concrete block wall has a surface density of 240 kg/m². Find TL at 500 Hz and at 1000 Hz. State the increase in dB. **Solution:** At 500 Hz: $$TL_{500} = 20 \log_{10}(240 \times 500) - 47 = 20 \log_{10}(120{,}000) - 47$$ $$= 20 \times (5.079) - 47 = 101.58 - 47 = 54.58 \;\; \text{dB}$$ At 1000 Hz: $$TL_{1000} = 20 \log_{10}(240 \times 1000) - 47 = 20 \log_{10}(240{,}000) - 47$$ $$= 20 \times (5.380) - 47 = 107.60 - 47 = 60.60 \;\; \text{dB}$$ Increase: $$\Delta TL = 60.60 - 54.58 = 6.02 \;\; \text{dB}$$ **Answer:** TL₅₀₀ = **54.6 dB**, TL₁₀₀₀ = **60.6 dB**, rise = **6 dB/octave** (validates mass law). --- ### Template B — Composite (Double) Wall TL > **Q:** A double wall consists of two 12 mm gypsum boards (each 9.6 kg/m²) on independent studs with a 90 mm air cavity. Estimate the TL at 500 Hz, ignoring the coincidence dip. **Solution:** $$TL_1 = TL_2 = 20 \log_{10}(9.6 \times 500) - 47 = 20 \log_{10}(4800) - 47$$ $$= 20 \times 3.681 - 47 = 73.63 - 47 = 26.63 \;\; \text{dB}$$ Assuming operation well above $f_0$ (typical 90 mm cavity gives $f_0 \approx 80$ Hz, so 500 Hz is well above): $$TL_{composite} \approx 26.63 + 26.63 = 53.26 \;\; \text{dB}$$ **Answer:** **TL ≈ 53 dB** at 500 Hz. A single leaf of the same 9.6 kg/m² would give only 26.6 dB — the **decoupled double wall gains ~27 dB**, but it is **useless below $f_0$**. --- ### Template C — Combined SPL from Multiple Sources > **Q:** Three machines in a workshop produce 78 dB, 81 dB, and 76 dB at a workstation. Find the combined SPL. **Solution:** $$L_{tot} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(10^{78/10} + 10^{81/10} + 10^{76/10}\right)$$ $$= 10 \log_{10}\!\left(6.31 \times 10^7 + 1.26 \times 10^8 + 3.98 \times 10^7\right)$$ $$= 10 \log_{10}\!\left(2.288 \times 10^8\right)$$ $$= 10 \times 8.359 = 83.6 \;\; \text{dB}$$ **Answer:** **L_total ≈ 83.6 dB**. Notice the result is dominated by the loudest source (81 dB); the 78 dB and 76 dB add only 2.6 dB. --- ### Template D — TL Computation from Power Ratio (Direct Definition) > **Q:** A partition receives 0.5 mW of acoustic power and transmits 0.0005 mW. Calculate the transmission loss. **Solution:** $$TL = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{W_i}{W_t}\right) = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{0.5}{0.0005}\right) = 10 \log_{10}(1000) = 30 \;\; \text{dB}$$ $$\tau = \frac{W_t}{W_i} = \frac{0.0005}{0.5} = 0.001$$ **Answer:** **TL = 30 dB**, $\tau = 0.001$ (i.e., 0.1% of incident power is transmitted). --- ### Template E — Required TL for a Room Against External Noise > **Q:** An office is on a busy road with an outdoor SPL of 78 dBA at the façade. Indoor target is NC-35 (≈ 45 dBA). Find the minimum TL of the façade. **Solution:** Required TL = Outdoor SPL − Indoor target SPL: $$TL_{min} = 78 - 45 = 33 \;\; \text{dB}$$ **Answer:** The façade must provide at least **33 dB TL**. From mass law, a single glass pane (≈ 6 kg/m²) gives at 500 Hz: $TL = 20 \log_{10}(3000) - 47 = 69.5 - 47 = 22.5$ dB — *insufficient*. A **double-glazed unit with 16 mm cavity** (~30 dB at 500 Hz) is the standard solution. # SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS ## 4.1 Block Architecture Flow — Source–Path–Receiver Noise Control Model The following Mermaid diagram captures the canonical engineering decision flow for noise control in a building, the same model used by ASHRAE and architectural acoustic consultants. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[NOISE SOURCE
Mechanical HVAC
Traffic Speech
Equipment] --> B[SOURCE CONTROL
Quiet equipment
Resilient mounts

Maintenance] A --> C[TRANSMISSION PATH

Duct Flanking] C --> D[PATH CONTROL

The order of effectiveness is **Source > Path > Receiver**. --- ## 4.2 Block Architecture Flow — Transmission Loss Computation Pipeline This schematic shows the algorithm a student should follow when computing TL on a KTU exam. ```mermaid flowchart TD P[Problem Statement
Partition type Mass Frequency] --> Q{Is partition
single or composite} Q -->|Single leaf| R[Apply Mass Law
TL equals 20 log of mf minus 47] Q -->|Double wall| S[Check mass air mass resonance f0
Is frequency above f0] S -->|Yes| T[TL equals TL1 plus TL2
Sum of leaves] S -->|No| U[Treat as single wall
Use combined mass m1 plus m2] R --> V[Check Coincidence dip
Use empirical TL curve if available] T --> V U --> V V --> W[Compare with STC rating
and field test data] W --> X[Decide on upgrades
Resilient channel, insulation, double glazing] ``` --- ## 4.3 Block Architecture Flow — Hierarchy of Noise Control Strategies ```mermaid flowchart LR L1[Most Effective
Source Control
Cost per dB saved is LOW] --> L2[Path Control
Barriers Mass Decoupling
Cost per dB is MODERATE] L2 --> L3[Least Effective
Receiver Control
Cost per dB is HIGH] L1 --> M1[Example
Replace loud fan with quiet fan
Reduce 10 dB at source] L2 --> M2[Example
Add 100 mm brick wall
Reduce 6 to 10 dB at path] L3 --> M3[Example
Provide ear plugs
Reduce perception by 20 dB] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Visual takeaway:** The further right you go in the SPR model, the *more expensive* each decibel of reduction becomes. Always design from **left (source) to right (receiver)** in a value-engineering exercise. --- ## 4.4 Block Architecture Flow — Air-Borne vs Structure-Borne Differentiation ```mermaid flowchart TD N[Noise Problem] --> O{Medium of propagation} O -->|Air| AB[Air Borne Noise
Source: speech, traffic, HVAC
Path: through air and partitions
Control: mass, sealing, absorption] O -->|Solid structure| SB[Structure Borne Noise
Source: footsteps, machines, pipes
Path: slabs, beams, walls
Control: isolation, damping, floating floor] AB --> AB1[Use TL and STC for partition] SB --> SB1[Use IIC and impact isolation class for floors] SB --> SB2[Use resilient mounts and flexible couplings] AB1 --> Z[Acoustic Comfort Achieved] SB1 --> Z SB2 --> Z ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Trick:** If the question mentions *footfalls, washing machines, or pipe hammer* → think **structure-borne → IIC and isolation**. If the question mentions *speech, music, traffic* → think **air-borne → TL and STC**. The student who confuses these two will lose half the marks. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP ## PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer) > **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** > > **Q1.** Define Transmission Loss (TL) of a partition. State the formula relating TL to the transmission coefficient $\tau$. **Model Answer:** Transmission Loss (TL) is the ratio, expressed in decibels, of the **acoustic power incident on a partition** to the **acoustic power transmitted through it**. It quantifies the sound-insulating effectiveness of a wall, floor, door, or window. Higher TL indicates a better insulator. The defining equation is: $$TL = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{1}{\tau}\right) = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{W_i}{W_t}\right) \;\; \text{dB}$$ where $\tau = W_t / W_i$ is the dimensionless transmission coefficient, $W_i$ is the incident sound power, and $W_t$ is the transmitted sound power. A TL of 30 dB means only 0.1% of the incident acoustic power passes through the partition. --- > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** > > **Q2.** Distinguish between **air-borne noise** and **structure-borne noise** with two examples each. **Model Answer:** | Aspect | Air-Borne Noise | Structure-Borne Noise | |---|---|---| | **Propagation medium** | Travels through air | Travels through solid building elements | | **Source examples** | Speech, music, traffic, TV | Footsteps, vibrating machinery, plumbing | | **Control measures** | Mass, sealing, absorption, double walls | Resilient mounts, floating floors, flexible couplings, damping | | **Performance metric** | Transmission Loss (TL) / STC | Impact Isolation Class (IIC), vibration isolation efficiency | | **Typical frequency range** | 100 Hz – 4000 Hz (speech band) | 20 Hz – 200 Hz (low-frequency rumble) | The architectural engineer must recognise that air-borne noise is blocked by partition **mass**, while structure-borne noise is blocked by **mechanical decoupling**. --- ## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice: A or B) ### OPTION A (14 Marks) > **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Adapted]** > > **Q3 (A).** A 150 mm thick brick wall has a surface density of 270 kg/m². It is proposed to improve its sound insulation by adding an independent 50 mm hollow brick lining (90 kg/m²) on the receiver side, separated by a 50 mm air cavity. > > **(a)** Calculate the Transmission Loss of the existing single wall at 500 Hz using the Mass Law. State the limitation of the Mass Law. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** Calculate the Transmission Loss of the proposed double-leaf (composite) wall at 500 Hz, assuming operation above mass-air-mass resonance. Comment on whether the addition of the lining is justified acoustically. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution for Q3 (A) (a) **Step 1 — Apply the Mass Law equation at 500 Hz:** $$TL = 20 \log_{10}(m \cdot f) - 47$$ $$TL = 20 \log_{10}(270 \times 500) - 47$$ $$TL = 20 \log_{10}(135{,}000) - 47$$ $$TL = 20 \times 5.1303 - 47 = 102.61 - 47 = 55.61 \;\; \text{dB}$$ **Step 2 — State the limitations of the Mass Law:** 1. It applies **only to a single, homogeneous, limp (non-bending) partition**. 2. It is **accurate only below the coincidence frequency** of the panel; above $f_c$, the TL curve dips sharply. 3. It **ignores stiffness, damping, and edge conditions** of the real wall. 4. It **overestimates TL** for thin panels and **underestimates the coincidence dip** in thick panels. 5. It does **not account for leaks, flanking paths, or resonances** in real buildings. **Answer:** $TL_{\text{existing wall, 500 Hz}} \approx 55.6$ dB by Mass Law. --- #### Model Solution for Q3 (A) (b) **Step 1 — Compute TL of each leaf using Mass Law.** For the 270 kg/m² wall at 500 Hz (already computed): $TL_1 = 55.6$ dB. For the 90 kg/m² lining at 500 Hz: $$TL_2 = 20 \log_{10}(90 \times 500) - 47 = 20 \log_{10}(45{,}000) - 47$$ $$= 20 \times 4.6532 - 47 = 93.06 - 47 = 46.06 \;\; \text{dB}$$ **Step 2 — Apply the composite wall rule (above $f_0$):** $$TL_{composite} \approx TL_1 + TL_2 = 55.61 + 46.06 = 101.67 \;\; \text{dB}$$ **Step 3 — Engineering comment.** The composite wall theoretically provides an additional **46 dB** over the single wall — far in excess of any practical requirement. However: - The 50 mm cavity gives a **mass-air-mass resonance** $f_0$ around 80–100 Hz, so performance at very low frequencies is poor. - The **coincidence dip** of the 90 kg/m² leaf (around 200–400 Hz) will erode the actual measured TL. - Practical TL will be **50–55 dB at 500 Hz** (limited by leaks, flanking, and structural coupling), not 100 dB. **Answer:** Theoretical $TL_{composite} \approx 101.7$ dB; realistic TL (with corrections) ≈ **50–55 dB**. The lining is *acoustically justified* but its *real benefit* is ~5–10 dB, not 46 dB. --- ### OPTION B (14 Marks) > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Adapted]** > > **Q3 (B).** An open-plan office of 200 m² floor area is to be designed with an HVAC system. The recommended indoor criterion is NC-35. > > **(a)** List the recommended NC levels for (i) hospitals, (ii) concert halls, (iii) open-plan offices. Explain how NC-35 is achieved in an HVAC design. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** The outdoor traffic noise at the building façade measures 75 dBA. If the façade wall (with windows) must provide NC-35 indoors, compute the required Transmission Loss of the façade, and select an appropriate glazing (single 6 mm glass vs. double-glazed 6-12-6 mm unit) using mass law. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution for Q3 (B) (a) **Step 1 — Recommended NC levels (memorise):** | Space Type | Recommended NC | |---|---| | Hospitals — patient rooms, OTs | **NC-25 to NC-30** | | Concert halls / Opera houses | **NC-15 to NC-20** | | Open-plan offices | **NC-35 to NC-40** | (Also: classrooms NC-25 to NC-30; restaurants NC-35 to NC-40; lobbies NC-40 to NC-45.) **Step 2 — How NC-35 is achieved in HVAC design:** NC-35 corresponds to a background level of approximately 45 dBA. To meet this, the HVAC designer must: 1. **Select low-noise fans and chillers** — typically with sound power ≤ 75 dB(A) re 1 pW. 2. **Use lined ducts** (25–50 mm mineral wool lining) to attenuate fan and duct-borne noise by 0.5–1 dB per metre. 3. **Install duct silencers (attenuators)** near AHUs and at branch take-offs to give 10–20 dB insertion loss in octave bands 250 Hz – 2 kHz. 4. **Limit duct air velocity** to ≤ 7 m/s in supply ducts and ≤ 5 m/s in branches (to control regenerated noise). 5. **Use vibration isolators** (spring or rubber mounts) under all rotating equipment to prevent structure-borne re-radiation. 6. **Decouple ducts from structure** using flexible canvas connectors at the AHU. --- #### Model Solution for Q3 (B) (b) **Step 1 — Required TL of the façade.** Outdoor SPL = 75 dBA. Indoor target (NC-35) ≈ 45 dBA. $$TL_{required} = 75 - 45 = 30 \;\; \text{dB}$$ **Step 2 — TL of single 6 mm glass.** Surface density of 6 mm glass ≈ **14.7 kg/m²** (since glass has density 2500 kg/m³ → 0.006 × 2500 = 15 kg/m²; we use 14.7 for soda-lime). At 500 Hz (mid-speech band): $$TL_{single} = 20 \log_{10}(14.7 \times 500) - 47 = 20 \log_{10}(7350) - 47$$ $$= 20 \times 3.866 - 47 = 77.32 - 47 = 30.32 \;\; \text{dB}$$ Single 6 mm glass *just* meets the 30 dB requirement — but only at 500 Hz. At traffic-noise peak (125 Hz), TL drops to: $$TL_{single, 125} = 20 \log_{10}(14.7 \times 125) - 47 = 20 \log_{10}(1837.5) - 47$$ $$= 20 \times 3.264 - 47 = 65.29 - 47 = 18.3 \;\; \text{dB}$$ → **Fails the 30 dB requirement at low frequencies.** **Step 3 — TL of double-glazed 6-12-6 mm unit.** Two leaves, each 14.7 kg/m², separated by 12 mm cavity. Above mass-air-mass resonance: $$TL_{double} \approx 2 \times TL_{single} = 2 \times 30.32 = 60.64 \;\; \text{dB}$$ (In practice, with coincidence and resonance corrections, real TL ≈ 35–40 dB at 500 Hz, and ≈ 25 dB at 125 Hz.) **Step 4 — Selection.** The **double-glazed 6-12-6 mm unit** is the correct choice. It provides at least 25 dB at all speech-band frequencies and meets the 30 dB target in the critical 250–2000 Hz octave bands, with a safety margin. The single 6 mm glass is rejected because it underperforms at low frequencies (where traffic rumble is dominant). **Answer:** Required TL = 30 dB → select **double-glazed unit**. --- ## KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING > [!WARNING] > **Common Pitfalls — Where KTU Students Lose Marks** > > 1. **Forgetting the units of $m$:** Mass Law in SI uses $m$ in **kg/m²** and $f$ in **Hz**. Substituting $m$ in kg or $f$ in kHz costs 2 marks instantly. > 2. **Using Mass Law above coincidence frequency:** The mass law is invalid above $f_c$. A student who blindly plugs 4000 Hz into Mass Law for a 6 mm glass pane will get a fictitious 52 dB, missing the real 25 dB dip. > 3. **Adding TLs in dB linearly:** Transmission losses in dB are **not** additive. They add via the *logarithm of products* as derived in Section 3.3. Two walls of TL 30 dB in series = 60 dB *only if decoupled*. > 4. **Confusing NC with dBA:** NC-30 is **not** equal to 30 dBA. NC is octave-band based; a rough conversion is NC + 10 ≈ dBA. The examiner deducts 1 mark if you treat them as identical. > 5. **Confusing TL and SIL (Sound Insulation Loss / Sound Reduction Index $R$):** In European/ISO usage, $R$ (in dB) is the same as TL, but the *laboratory* value $R_w$ differs from the *field* value $R'_w$ by flanking paths. KTU questions usually assume $TL = R$. > 6. **Skipping the engineering comment in 14-mark answers:** Always end with a 1-line "design implication" sentence. Examiners award the final 1–2 marks for *engineering judgment*, not just arithmetic. > 7. **Forgetting that air-borne and structure-borne noise need different treatments:** A wall of mass 300 kg/m² will not stop a footfall impact — only a floating floor will. --- ## TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST > [!IMPORTANT] > **What You Must Remember in the Last 10 Minutes Before the Exam** > > - **Noise = unwanted sound**, measured in **dB** (objective) or **dBA** (subjective, A-weighted). > - **NC curves** = single-number design target; **NC-30 ≈ 40 dBA**. > - **Recommended NC (must memorise):** Concert hall 15–20, Hospital 25–30, Class/Office 30–35, Restaurant/Lobby 35–45. > - **Air-borne noise** travels in air → control by **mass, sealing, absorption**. > - **Structure-borne noise** travels in solids → control by **isolation, damping, decoupling**. > - **Source–Path–Receiver (SPR)** is the universal model; source control is always cheapest and most effective. > - **TL (Transmission Loss) = 10 log(1/τ) = 10 log(W_i / W_t) dB**. > - **Mass Law (SI): TL = 20 log(mf) − 47 dB**; valid below coincidence. > - **6 dB per octave** rise with frequency; **6 dB per doubling of mass**. > - **Double wall above $f_0$:** $TL_{total} \approx TL_1 + TL_2$. > - **Mass-Air-Mass resonance $f_0$** is the *weak point* of a double wall; below it, double wall collapses to single-wall behaviour. > - **Coincidence frequency $f_c$** is the *weak point* of a single panel; deep TL dip. > - **STC** = single-number rating of a partition; **STC 50** = loud speech inaudible through wall. > - **Adding SPLs** = use $10 \log_{10}(\sum 10^{L/10})$; **equal sources → +3 dB**. > - **Outdoor distance doubling** → −6 dB (inverse-square). > - **Composite wall TL ≠ sum of TLs in linear scale** — only in dB after decoupling. > - **Practical STC for dwelling-unit separating wall** (Indian building code) ≈ 45–50; for hospital privacy ≈ 50–55; for studios ≥ 60. > - **Source control examples:** quiet fans, resilient mounts, maintenance, layout orientation. > - **Path control examples:** mass, double walls, resilient channels, absorption, sealing, vibration hangers. > - **Receiver control examples:** PPE, distance, masking, layout. > [!NOTE] > **Mnemonic for recommended NC levels (Fun Recall):** > *"**H**ospitals **C**onfidential, **O**ffices **L**ively"* > - **H**ospital 25–30 > - **C**lassroom 25–30 > - **O**ffice 30–35 > - **L**obby 40–45 > > Concerts are **quieter** than offices — counterintuitive but true because music venues demand ultra-low background for fidelity.

topics.title: Flanking Paths, Sound Absorption: Materials and Fixings, Reverberation – Sabine's Formula and Eyring's Modification, Natural Lighting: Visual Task Requirements
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET415 - ARCHITECTURAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Flanking Paths, Sound Absorption: Materials and Fixings, Reverberation – Sabine's Formula and Eyring's Modification, Natural Lighting: Visual Task Requirements

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Flanking Paths (Building Acoustics)

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024):**
> **Flanking Transmission** is the propagation of sound from one room to another through indirect paths *other than* the direct partition separating the two spaces. These paths include flanking walls, floors, ceilings, ductwork, window reveals, door frames, and structural junctions that physically bridge the two enclosures.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Highlight: A wall may have a measured Sound Transmission Class (STC) of 55, but if flanking paths are ignored, the actual **Apparent Sound Transmission Class (ASTC)** between two rooms can drop to as low as 35–40. **Flanking cannot be neglected in architectural acoustics design.**

**Conceptual Analogy (Intuition):**
Imagine you build a perfectly waterproof dam in a river, but the water still reaches the other side by seeping *under* the dam and through *side channels*. The dam is "flawless," yet water still leaks. In the same way, a perfectly rated partition wall does not stop sound if the sound "seeps" through the side walls, floor, ceiling, and other connected structural elements. These indirect leakage routes are called **flanking paths**.

**Standard Metric:**
- **Flanking Sound Reduction Index ($R_{ij,f}$)**: Measured in **dB**.
- **Apparent Sound Transmission Class (ASTC)**: Single-number rating in **dB** that *includes* flanking.

---

### 1.2 Sound Absorption: Materials and Fixings

**Formal Definition:**
> **Sound Absorption** is the process by which a material, structure, or system converts incident acoustic energy into heat, thereby reducing reflected sound energy, controlling reverberation, and improving speech intelligibility inside an enclosure.

**Conceptual Analogy (Intuition):**
Think of a sponge soaking water. A porous acoustic material (like mineral wool) "soaks" sound waves. The sound waves penetrate the porous structure, vibrate the fibers, and lose energy as heat (friction between air particles and fiber surfaces). A hard, smooth marble wall is the opposite — it *reflects* almost all sound back, like a mirror reflects light.

> [!NOTE]
> Key Metric: **Absorption Coefficient ($\alpha$)** is a dimensionless number between **0** (perfect reflector) and **1** (perfect absorber).

**Three Principal Families of Absorbers (KTU Syllabus Mandate):**

| Family | Mechanism | Typical Materials |
|---|---|---|
| **Porous Absorbers** | Viscous & thermal losses in pores | Mineral wool, glass fiber, acoustic plaster, carpets, drapes |
| **Resonant Absorbers** | Damping at resonance frequency | Perforated panels, Helmholtz resonators, membrane (bass) traps |
| **Composite / Multi-leaf** | Combines porous + resonant | Slotted panels with backing insulation, fabric-wrapped panels |

---

### 1.3 Reverberation

**Formal Definition:**
> **Reverberation** is the persistence of sound in an enclosed space after the source has stopped, caused by repeated multiple reflections from enclosing surfaces. **Reverberation Time (RT or $T_{60}$)** is the time, in **seconds**, required for the sound pressure level to decay by **60 dB** after the source ceases.

**Conceptual Analogy (Intuition):**
Clap once inside an empty cathedral — the sound keeps bouncing around for several seconds before vanishing. This "after-ring" is reverberation. Now clap inside a heavily curtained bedroom — the sound dies almost instantly. The cathedral has a *long* reverberation time; the bedroom has a *short* one.

---

### 1.4 Sabine's Formula and Eyring's Modification

**Sabine's Formula (Wallace Clement Sabine, c. 1898):**
$$T_{60} = 0.161 \, \frac{V}{A} \quad \text{(SI units, } V \text{ in m}^3\text{)}$$

**Eyring's Modification (Carl F. Eyring, 1930):**
$$T_{60} = \frac{0.161 \, V}{-S \, \ln(1 - \bar{\alpha})}$$

Where $A = \sum S_i \alpha_i$ (Sabine) and $\bar{\alpha}$ is the **average absorption coefficient**.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Sabine's formula works well for *lightly absorbing* rooms (low $\alpha$). For highly absorbing rooms (e.g., anechoic chambers, recording studios), Sabine *overestimates* the reverberation time. Eyring corrected this by accounting for the *actual* logarithmic decay of sound energy on each reflection — think of it as upgrading a "rough estimate" to a "refined estimate" for highly damped rooms.

---

### 1.5 Natural Lighting — Visual Task Requirements

**Formal Definition:**
> The **Visual Task** is the object, detail, or activity that the eye must perceive to perform a given function (reading, machining, inspecting, drawing). **Visual Task Requirements** define the quantity and quality of illumination necessary for the *efficient, accurate, and comfortable* performance of that task.

**Conceptual Analogy (Intuition):**
Reading a newspaper on a sunny balcony requires little effort. Reading the same newspaper in a dim cellar is straining. The "visual task" (reading) is identical, but the *illumination environment* changes how comfortably the eye performs the task. Architects must design the lighting to *match* the difficulty of the task.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Indian Standard (IS 3646 – Code of Practice for Interior Illumination)** and **National Building Code (NBC 2016)** prescribe minimum illumination levels in **lux** for various visual tasks.

**Key Parameters of Visual Task:**
1. **Size of the critical detail** (in mm or minutes of arc).
2. **Contrast** between the detail and its background.
3. **Reflectance** of the task surface.
4. **Time available** to perform the task.
5. **Visual acuity** of the observer.
6. **Required illuminance level ($E$, in lux)**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Flanking Transmission — The Twelve Paths (ISO 15712 / EN ISO 12354)

In a two-room building separated by a separating partition, sound from Room 1 (Source) reaches Room 2 (Receiver) through **13 possible transmission paths**. Only one is the "direct" path through the partition; the other **12 are flanking paths**.

**Path Nomenclature:**

| Path Code | Meaning | Typical Strength |
|---|---|---|
| **D-d** | Direct — Direct (through partition) | Primary path; rated by $R_w$ |
| **F-f** | Flank in Source room — Flank in Receiving room (same flanking element) | Often dominant at LF |
| **F-d** | Flank in Source — Direct transmission to receiver | Common via floor/ceiling |
| **D-f** | Direct in Source — Flank in Receiver | Common via windows/doors |
| **F-F (other side)** | Different flanking elements in source and receiver | Cross-talk path |
| **D-F (other)** | Direct in source — Different flank in receiver | Via ducts/penetrations |

**Why Flanking Dominates at Low Frequencies:**
- Lightweight facades and floor/ceiling systems resonate at low frequencies (50–200 Hz), transmitting structure-borne vibration.
- Mass-controlled partition walls perform *worse* at LF, so the relative contribution of flanking rises.

**Vibration Reduction Index ($K_{ij}$)**
Used in flanking calculation; measured in **dB**. It describes the vibrational isolation provided by a junction between two structural elements.

$$L_{ij} = L_i - R_{ij} - 10 \log_{10} T_s + 10 \log_{10} \frac{T_{ij}}{T_{r,s}} \quad \text{(in dB)}$$

Where $L_{ij}$ = flanking sound pressure level, $R_{ij}$ = flanking sound reduction index.

**Control of Flanking (KTU 2024):**
1. **Structural separation (box-in-box construction)** — independent floating floors, isolated walls.
2. **Heavy, continuous structural elements** with sealed junctions.
3. **Resilient layers / break-joints** at junctions (e.g., resilient channels, isolation strips).
4. **Sealing of penetrations** (pipes, ducts, electrical conduits).

---

### 2.2 Sound Absorption — Materials Deep Dive

#### A. Porous Absorbers

**Mechanism (4-step process):**
1. Sound wave strikes porous surface.
2. Air particles oscillate within the pore network.
3. **Viscous friction** between oscillating air and fiber walls converts kinetic energy to heat.
4. **Thermal conduction** between compressed/rarefied air parcels and fibers adds further loss.

**Performance Rule:**
- Absorption **increases with frequency** (high frequencies have shorter wavelengths → more interactions with fibers per unit length).
- For low-frequency performance, **increase thickness** or **add air gap behind the absorber**.

**Sabine Absorption Coefficient ($\alpha_s$)** = total sound absorbed / incident sound. **NRC (Noise Reduction Coefficient)** = average of $\alpha_s$ at 250, 500, 1000, 2000 Hz.

#### B. Resonant (Cavity) Absorbers

**Helmholtz Resonator Analogy:**
Picture blowing across the top of an empty bottle — it "sings" at one specific pitch. A Helmholtz resonator in acoustics is a tuned cavity + neck that absorbs strongly at *one* frequency (and a narrow band around it).

**Tuning Frequency:**
$$f_r = \frac{c}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{S}{V_l \cdot l_{eff}}}$$

Where:
- $c$ = speed of sound in air ($\approx 343$ m/s at 20°C).
- $S$ = cross-sectional area of the neck.
- $V_l$ = volume of the cavity.
- $l_{eff} = l + 0.8 \sqrt{S}$ (effective neck length with end correction).

#### C. Fixings of Absorbers (Critical for Performance)

| Fixing Type | Description | Effect on Low-Frequency $\alpha$ |
|---|---|---|
| **Direct / Adhered** | Material bonded to wall | $\alpha$ low at LF (no air gap) |
| **Spaced with Air Gap (25–50 mm)** | Material held off the wall on battens | $\alpha$ shifts to *lower* frequencies |
| **Suspended (Baffle / Island)** | Material hangs free in space | Highest $\alpha$ at all frequencies |
| **Stretched Fabric over Frame** | Decorative fabric over fiberglass core | Tunable LF + LF efficiency |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule of Thumb:** For a porous absorber, an air gap equal to the thickness of the material *roughly doubles* the effective thickness, improving LF absorption by ≈ ½ octave.

---

### 2.3 Reverberation — Sabine vs. Eyring

#### A. Sabine's Formula (Derivation Concept)

Sabine assumed that, for *small absorption*, the *average* energy density in the room decays exponentially, and the rate of decay is proportional to the *total* absorption (in sabins). His empirical relation was later given a theoretical basis by Jaeger (1911) and Millington (1932).

**Sabine Formula (SI):**
$$T_{60} = 0.161 \, \frac{V}{A}, \quad A = \sum_{i=1}^{n} S_i \alpha_i$$

Where:
- $V$ = volume of the room, in **m³**.
- $A$ = total absorption, in **sabins (metric sabin)**.
- $S_i$ = surface area of the *i*-th material, in **m²**.
- $\alpha_i$ = absorption coefficient of the *i*-th material.

**Imperial Form:**
$$T_{60} = 0.05 \, \frac{V}{A} \quad \text{(V in ft}^3\text{, S in ft}^2\text{)}$$

#### B. Eyring's Modification (When to Use)

Sabine assumes $\alpha$ is small. When $\alpha$ approaches 1 (highly absorbing surfaces — anechoic chambers, recording studios), Sabine *overestimates* $T_{60}$. Eyring replaced the linear assumption with a logarithmic one:

$$T_{60} = \frac{0.161 \, V}{-S \, \ln(1 - \bar{\alpha})}, \quad \bar{\alpha} = \frac{\sum S_i \alpha_i}{\sum S_i}$$

**Comparison Table:**

| Surface Condition | Sabine Error | Correct Formula |
|---|---|---|
| Light absorption ($\bar{\alpha} < 0.2$) | < 5% | Sabine is sufficient |
| Moderate absorption ($0.2 < \bar{\alpha} < 0.5$) | 5–15% | Eyring preferred |
| Heavy absorption ($\bar{\alpha} > 0.5$) | > 15% | Eyring mandatory |

#### C. Other Reverberation Estimates (Briefly)

- **Fitzroy (1952)**: Splits total absorption into 3 directional groups (axial, tangential, oblique) for rectangular rooms — more accurate for non-diffuse fields.
- **Millington-Sette (1932)**: $T = 0.161 V / \sum S_i \ln(1-\alpha_i)$ — exact for non-uniform surfaces.

---

### 2.4 Visual Task Requirements (Quantitative)

The illuminance required for a task depends on:
- Task **size** (visual angle $\theta$ in minutes of arc).
- Task **contrast** (C = Michelson contrast).
- Task **reflectance** ($\rho$).
- Required **speed** of work.
- **Age** of worker (older workers need more light).

**Approximate Illuminance Levels (IS 3646 / NBC 2016, KTU Recommended):**

| Visual Task Category | Example | Recommended Illuminance (lux) |
|---|---|---|
| Casual / Intermittent | Lobbies, corridors | 100 – 150 |
| Simple / Moderate | Reading, office work | 300 – 500 |
| Difficult / Detailed | Drawing, drafting, inspection | 750 – 1000 |
| Very Difficult | Fine precision, surgical, jewelry | 1500 – 2000+ |
| Daylight (Good) | Daylighting from windows | 5000 – 20000 (varies) |

**Glare Control:** Daylight must be controlled for **glare index** and **luminance ratio**. Luminance ratio between task and adjacent surroundings should not exceed:
- 3:1 (task to immediate surround)
- 10:1 (task to remote surfaces)

> [!NOTE]
> **Daylight Factor (DF)** is the primary KTU design metric:
> $$\text{DF} = \frac{E_{\text{interior}}}{E_{\text{exterior (horizontal, unobstructed)}}} \times 100 \, \%$$
> - Kitchens: DF ≥ 1.5%
> - Living rooms: DF ≥ 1.0%
> - Classrooms: DF ≥ 2.0%
> - Drawing offices: DF ≥ 3.0%

---

### 2.5 KTU FORMULA SHEET (PRINT-READY)

| # | Concept | Formula | Units | When to Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Sabine RT | $T_{60} = 0.161 \, V / A$ | s | $\bar{\alpha} < 0.2$ |
| 2 | Total Absorption | $A = \sum S_i \alpha_i$ | sabins | All cases |
| 3 | Eyring RT | $T_{60} = 0.161 \, V / [-S \ln(1-\bar{\alpha})]$ | s | $\bar{\alpha} > 0.2$ |
| 4 | Average $\alpha$ | $\bar{\alpha} = \sum S_i \alpha_i / \sum S_i$ | dimensionless | Eyring input |
| 5 | Helmholtz $f_r$ | $f_r = (c/2\pi)\sqrt{S/(V_l \, l_{eff})}$ | Hz | Resonant design |
| 6 | Daylight Factor | $\text{DF} = (E_{in}/E_{ex}) \times 100\%$ | % | Window design |
| 7 | NRC | $\text{NRC} = (\alpha_{250} + \alpha_{500} + \alpha_{1000} + \alpha_{2000})/4$ | dimensionless | Material rating |
| 8 | Contrast Ratio | $C = (L_b - L_o)/(L_b + L_o)$ | dimensionless | Visual task |
| 9 | Speed of Sound (air) | $c = 331.4 + 0.6 T$ | m/s | All acoustic calcs |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Sabine's Formula — Full Derivation

**Starting Premise:** In a steady-state diffuse field, the average energy density $\bar{E}$ decays exponentially after the source stops.

**Step 1: Energy Decay Law**
After source stops, the rate of energy loss equals the energy absorbed per unit time:
$$V \cdot \frac{d\bar{E}}{dt} = -\bar{E} \cdot c \cdot A / 4$$

**Step 2: Solve the Differential Equation**
Rearranging:
$$\frac{d\bar{E}}{\bar{E}} = -\frac{c \cdot A}{4V} \, dt$$

Integrating both sides:
$$\int_{\bar{E_0}}^{\bar{E}} \frac{d\bar{E}}{\bar{E}} = -\frac{c A}{4V} \int_0^t dt$$

This yields:
$$\ln\!\left(\frac{\bar{E}}{\bar{E_0}}\right) = -\frac{c A}{4V} \, t$$

Or:
$$\bar{E}(t) = \bar{E_0} \, e^{-\left(\frac{cA}{4V}\right) t}$$

**Step 3: Convert Energy Decay to Sound Pressure Level (dB)**
Sound Intensity Level $L \propto 10 \log_{10}(E)$. So a drop of 60 dB corresponds to:
$$10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{\bar{E_0}}{\bar{E_{60}}}\right) = 60 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{\bar{E_0}}{\bar{E_{60}}} = 10^6$$

**Step 4: Apply 60 dB Decay Condition**
From Step 2:
$$\frac{\bar{E_0}}{\bar{E_{60}}} = e^{\left(\frac{c A}{4V}\right) T_{60}} = 10^6$$

Take natural log:
$$\frac{c A}{4V} \, T_{60} = 6 \ln 10 \approx 13.8155$$

So:
$$T_{60} = \frac{4 \cdot 6 \ln 10}{c} \cdot \frac{V}{A}$$

**Step 5: Substitute Numerical Value**
For air at 20°C, $c \approx 343$ m/s. Therefore:
$$T_{60} = \frac{4 \times 6 \times 2.3026}{343} \cdot \frac{V}{A} = 0.1611 \, \frac{V}{A}$$

**Final Sabine Formula:**
$$\boxed{T_{60} = 0.161 \, \frac{V}{A} \quad (\text{seconds, SI})}$$

---

### 3.2 Eyring's Modification — Derivation

**Starting Premise:** For a single reflection off a surface of absorption coefficient $\alpha$, the *remaining* energy fraction is $(1 - \alpha)$. After $N$ reflections in a *diffuse* field:
$$E_N = E_0 (1 - \alpha)^N$$

**Step 1: Mean Free Path**
Average distance a sound wave travels between two reflections:
$$\ell_m = \frac{4V}{S}$$

So in time $t$, the wave makes $N = c \cdot t / \ell_m = cSt/(4V)$ reflections.

**Step 2: Energy After Time $t$**
$$E(t) = E_0 (1 - \alpha)^{c S t / (4V)}$$

Take natural log:
$$\ln\!\left(\frac{E(t)}{E_0}\right) = \frac{c S t}{4V} \ln(1 - \alpha)$$

**Step 3: 60 dB Decay Condition**
As before, $E/E_0 = 10^{-6}$, so $\ln(10^{-6}) = -6 \ln 10 \approx -13.8155$:
$$-13.8155 = \frac{c S T_{60}}{4V} \ln(1 - \alpha)$$

Solving:
$$T_{60} = \frac{-4V \cdot 6 \ln 10}{c \, S \, \ln(1 - \alpha)} = \frac{0.161 \, V}{-S \ln(1 - \alpha)}$$

**Final Eyring Formula:**
$$\boxed{T_{60} = \frac{0.161 \, V}{-S \ln(1 - \bar{\alpha})} \quad (\text{seconds, SI})}$$

**Verification for Small $\alpha$:** Using Taylor expansion $\ln(1-\alpha) \approx -\alpha - \alpha^2/2 - \dots$ For $\alpha \to 0$, $-S \ln(1-\alpha) \to S\alpha = A$, recovering Sabine.

---

### 3.3 Sample Numerical — Eyring vs. Sabine

**Problem:** A recording studio has $V = 200 \, \text{m}^3$, $S = 250 \, \text{m}^2$, and $\bar{\alpha} = 0.6$. Find $T_{60}$ by both formulae and comment.

**Sabine:**
$$T_{60} = 0.161 \times \frac{200}{250 \times 0.6} = 0.161 \times \frac{200}{150} = 0.215 \, \text{s}$$

**Eyring:**
$$T_{60} = 0.161 \times \frac{200}{-250 \times \ln(0.4)} = 0.161 \times \frac{200}{-250 \times (-0.9163)} = 0.161 \times \frac{200}{229.08} = 0.141 \, \text{s}$$

**Difference:** Sabine overestimates by $\frac{0.215 - 0.141}{0.141} \times 100 \approx 52\%$. This confirms Eyring must be used for highly absorbing spaces.

---

### 3.4 Helmholtz Resonator Numerical

**Problem:** Design a Helmholtz absorber for 100 Hz. Cavity volume $V_l = 0.05 \, \text{m}^3$. Find neck area $S$ if neck length $l = 0.04$ m. Take $c = 343$ m/s.

**Step 1: Effective neck length**
$$l_{eff} = l + 0.8 \sqrt{S}$$
(Iterative, so assume $S = 0.0025 \, \text{m}^2$ initially, hence $\sqrt{S} = 0.05$)
$$l_{eff} = 0.04 + 0.8 \times 0.05 = 0.08 \, \text{m}$$

**Step 2: Solve for S**
$$100 = \frac{343}{2\pi} \sqrt{\frac{0.0025}{0.05 \times 0.08}}$$
$$100 = 54.6 \times \sqrt{\frac{0.0025}{0.004}} = 54.6 \times \sqrt{0.625} = 54.6 \times 0.7906 = 43.2$$

Since 100 ≠ 43.2, recompute by inverting the formula. Let $x = \sqrt{S}$:
$$100 = 54.6 \sqrt{\frac{x^2}{0.05 (0.04 + 0.8 x)}}$$
$$1.831^2 = \frac{x^2}{0.05(0.04 + 0.8 x)} = \frac{x^2}{0.002 + 0.04 x}$$
$$3.353 (0.002 + 0.04 x) = x^2$$
$$0.00671 + 0.1341 x - x^2 = 0$$
$$x^2 - 0.1341 x - 0.00671 = 0$$
$$x = \frac{0.1341 + \sqrt{0.01798 + 0.02684}}{2} = \frac{0.1341 + 0.2113}{2} = 0.1727 \, \text{m}$$

So $S = 0.1727^2 \approx 0.0298 \, \text{m}^2$ → 298 cm² opening (a sizeable panel slot).

---

### 3.5 Daylight Factor — Numerical

**Problem:** Indoor workplane illuminance = 200 lux. Outdoor horizontal unobstructed illuminance = 10000 lux. Find DF and comment.

$$\text{DF} = \frac{200}{10000} \times 100\% = 2.0\%$$

This meets IS 3646 minimum for general office work.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Flanking Transmission Paths — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Room 1 - Source Side]:::src
    B[Separating Partition - Direct Path D]:::direct
    C[Flank Wall 1 - Vertical Flank]:::flank
    D[Flank Wall 2 - Opposite Vertical]:::flank
    E[Floor Slab - Horizontal Flank]:::flank
    F[Ceiling Slab - Horizontal Flank]:::flank
    G[Penetrations - Ducts, Pipes]:::flank
    H[Room 2 - Receiver Side]:::rcv

    A -- D-d Direct through partition --> H
    A -- F-d Flank to direct --> H
    A -- D-f Direct to flank --> H
    A -- F-f Flank to flank same element --> H
    A -- Other flanks cross-talk --> H

    classDef src fill:#ffe4b5,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000
    classDef direct fill:#ff6347,stroke:#800000,color:#fff
    classDef flank fill:#87ceeb,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    classDef rcv fill:#90ee90,stroke:#006400,color:#000
```

> **Reading Aid:** Red arrow = Direct (D-d) path; Blue arrows = 12 flanking paths. KTU 2024 expects identification and listing of at least 4 major paths.

---

### 4.2 Sound Absorption Mechanism — Porous vs. Resonant

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Incident Sound Wave]:::in
    B{Type of Absorber?}:::decision

    A --> B

    B -- Porous Material --> P1[Wave enters pores]
    P1 --> P2[Air friction with fibers]
    P2 --> P3[Thermal conduction losses]
    P3 --> P4[Energy converted to HEAT]
    P4 --> P5[High alpha at HF, Low at LF]
    P5 --> P6[Add air gap for LF boost]

    B -- Resonant Absorber --> R1[Wave excites cavity resonance]
    R1 --> R2[Air sloshes in neck/panel]
    R2 --> R3[Strong absorption at f_r]
    R3 --> R4[Frictional damping at neck]
    R4 --> R5[High alpha at narrow band]
    R5 --> R6[Tune by changing S or V_l]

    classDef in fill:#fff8dc,stroke:#8b7500,color:#000
    classDef decision fill:#dda0dd,stroke:#4b0082,color:#000
```

---

### 4.3 Reverberation Time Computation Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Given: V, S, surface alpha_i]:::in
    B[Compute total absorption A = sum S_i alpha_i]:::calc
    C[Compute average alpha_bar]:::calc
    D{Is alpha_bar less than 0.2?}:::dec
    E[Use Sabine: T = 0.161 V / A]:::sab
    F[Use Eyring: T = 0.161 V / -S ln1 minus alpha_bar]:::eyr
    G[Compare: Sabine overestimates when alpha_bar is high]:::note
    H[Apply correction for non-diffuse: Fitzroy or Millington]:::adv

    A --> B
    A --> C
    B --> D
    C --> D
    D -- Yes --> E --> G
    D -- No --> F --> G
    G --> H

    classDef in fill:#fff8dc,stroke:#8b7500,color:#000
    classDef calc fill:#e0ffff,stroke:#008b8b,color:#000
    classDef dec fill:#ffdab9,stroke:#8b4513,color:#000

    classDef adv fill:#d8bfd8,stroke:#4b0082,color:#000
```

---

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU Insight: The *fixing method* can shift the absorption peak by nearly an octave, even when the *material* is identical. Students must specify both in design problems.
