<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]**
*What is meant by flanking transmission in buildings? List any four flanking paths.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):**
Flanking transmission is the propagation of sound between two rooms through indirect paths other than the separating partition — i.e., via adjacent walls, floors, ceilings, ducts, or junctions.

**Four flanking paths (2 marks — 0.5 each):**
1. **F-f path**: Source room flank → same flank element → Receiver room.
2. **F-d path**: Source room flank → Direct through partition → Receiver.
3. **D-f path**: Direct through partition → Receiver room flank.
4. **F-F (cross) path**: Source room flank → *different* element → Receiver room.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, S1]**
*Differentiate between Sabine's formula and Eyring's modification of reverberation time.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Sabine | Eyring |
|---|---|---|
| Formula | $T = 0.161 V/A$ | $T = 0.161 V/[-S \ln(1-\bar{\alpha})]$ |
| Assumption | Low absorption ($\alpha \ll 1$) | General $\alpha$ |
| Accuracy at high $\alpha$ | Overestimates RT | Accurate |
| Suitable for | Auditoria, classrooms | Anechoic chambers, studios |
| Derivation basis | Energy decay rate $\propto A$ | Energy decay $\propto \ln(1-\alpha)$ |

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice Pattern)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Pitfall Callout):**
> - In Sabine problems, students often confuse **volume $V$** with **surface area $S$**. Watch units: $V$ in m³, $S$ in m², $A$ in sabins.
> - For Eyring, students forget the **negative sign inside the logarithm** — wrong sign → negative RT → zero marks.
> - For flanking, listing only "wall" without specifying path code (F-d, D-f, etc.) is incomplete — deduct 1 mark.
> - Visual task problems: omitting **glare control** and **luminance ratio** loses 2 marks.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Acoustics & Reverberation Focus**

> **Q3. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, PECET415]**
> A rectangular auditorium has internal dimensions $12 \, \text{m} \times 8 \, \text{m} \times 5 \, \text{m}$ (high). The walls are plastered ($\alpha = 0.03$), the floor is wooden on joists ($\alpha = 0.07$), and the ceiling is acoustic tile ($\alpha = 0.55$). The wooden floor occupies the full floor area, the ceiling occupies the full ceiling area, and there are two short walls (with doors, $\alpha = 0.10$) and two long walls (with windows, $\alpha = 0.30$).
>
> **(a)** Calculate the reverberation time using **Sabine's formula**.
> **(b)** Calculate the reverberation time using **Eyring's formula** and comment on the difference.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Sabine (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Compute areas (1 mark)**
- Floor: $S_f = 12 \times 8 = 96 \, \text{m}^2$
- Ceiling: $S_c = 12 \times 8 = 96 \, \text{m}^2$
- Two long walls: $2 \times (12 \times 5) = 120 \, \text{m}^2$
- Two short walls: $2 \times (8 \times 5) = 80 \, \text{m}^2$
- Total surface $S = 96 + 96 + 120 + 80 = 392 \, \text{m}^2$

**Step 2: Compute total absorption $A$ (3 marks)**
- Floor: $A_f = 96 \times 0.07 = 6.72$ sabins
- Ceiling: $A_c = 96 \times 0.55 = 52.80$ sabins
- Long walls: $A_l = 120 \times 0.30 = 36.00$ sabins
- Short walls: $A_s = 80 \times 0.10 = 8.00$ sabins
- **Total: $A = 6.72 + 52.80 + 36.00 + 8.00 = 103.52$ sabins**

**Step 3: Compute $V$ and apply Sabine (3 marks)**
$$V = 12 \times 8 \times 5 = 480 \, \text{m}^3$$
$$T_{60} = 0.161 \times \frac{480}{103.52} = 0.161 \times 4.637 = 0.747 \, \text{s}$$

**Part (b) — Eyring (7 marks):**

**Step 4: Compute average $\bar{\alpha}$ (2 marks)**
$$\bar{\alpha} = \frac{103.52}{392} = 0.264$$

**Step 5: Apply Eyring (3 marks)**
$$T_{60} = \frac{0.161 \times 480}{-392 \times \ln(1 - 0.264)} = \frac{77.28}{-392 \times \ln(0.736)} = \frac{77.28}{-392 \times (-0.3066)} = \frac{77.28}{120.19} = 0.643 \, \text{s}$$

**Step 6: Comment (2 marks)**
Sabine overestimates by $\frac{0.747 - 0.643}{0.643} \times 100 \approx 16.2\%$. Since $\bar{\alpha} = 0.264 > 0.2$, Eyring is the *more accurate* estimate. Sabine's assumption of small $\alpha$ is not fully valid here due to the highly absorbing acoustic-tile ceiling.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Sound Absorption, Flanking & Visual Task Focus**

> **Q4. [KTU University Exam – June 2023, PECET415]**
> **(a)** Explain the three principal families of sound-absorbing materials with one example each. Discuss how the *fixing method* of a porous absorber affects its low-frequency performance.
>
> **(b)** Define "Visual Task". A drawing office requires a minimum illuminance of 750 lux at the workplane. The unobstructed exterior horizontal illuminance is $12{,}000 \, \text{lux}$. Calculate the required **Daylight Factor** and comment on its adequacy per **IS 3646**. State the recommended **luminance ratio** between a task and its immediate surround.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) (7 marks):**

**Three families of sound absorbers (4.5 marks — 1.5 each):**

1. **Porous Absorbers** — Viscous and thermal losses as air oscillates in the pore network. Examples: **mineral wool, glass fibre, acoustic plaster, carpet**. Performance high at HF, low at LF.

2. **Resonant (Cavity) Absorbers** — Absorption peaks at a tuned frequency due to mass-spring resonance. Examples: **perforated panel, Helmholtz resonator, slatted panel**.

3. **Composite / Multi-leaf Absorbers** — Combine porous + resonant action. Example: **fabric-wrapped glass-fibre panel over air cavity**.

**Effect of fixing (2.5 marks):**
- *Direct fixing* — material bonded to wall, no air gap → minimum LF absorption.
- *Air gap of 25–50 mm* — effective thickness doubled → LF peak shifts to roughly half the frequency, $\alpha$ at 125 Hz can rise from 0.10 to 0.40.
- *Suspended baffle* — material hangs in space → maximum absorption at all frequencies.
- *Stretched fabric system* — fabric stretched over a frame with acoustic infill behind; combines broadband absorption with a decorative finish.

**Part (b) (7 marks):**

**Definition of Visual Task (1.5 marks):**
A Visual Task is the object, detail, or activity that the eye must perceive to perform a given function. Its *requirements* (size, contrast, time) determine the quantity and quality of illumination needed.

**Daylight Factor calculation (2.5 marks):**
$$\text{DF} = \frac{E_{\text{interior}}}{E_{\text{exterior}}} \times 100\%$$

Given $E_{\text{int}} = 750 \, \text{lux}$ (minimum) and $E_{\text{ext}} = 12{,}000 \, \text{lux}$:
$$\text{DF} = \frac{750}{12{,}000} \times 100\% = 6.25\%$$

**Comment on adequacy (2 marks):**
IS 3646 recommends a minimum DF of **2.0% for classrooms** and **3.0% for drawing offices**. The calculated DF of **6.25%** is well above the requirement — the room is generously daylit. However, such a high DF may cause glare and excessive luminance ratios; solar control (louvers, blinds) is recommended.

**Luminance ratio (1 mark):**
The recommended **luminance ratio between the task and its immediate surrounding surface** is **3:1**, and between the task and the most remote surface is **10:1**, per IS 3646 and CIE standards.

---

### TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield KTU 2024 Module 2 — Must Remember:**

- [x] **Flanking paths**: 12 indirect paths + 1 direct = 13 total. Always list with path code (D-d, F-f, etc.).
- [x] **Direct transmission** is rated by **$R_w$ (weighted sound reduction index)**; **flanking + direct = ASTC (Apparent STC)**.
- [x] **Porous absorbers** are excellent at **HF**; **resonant** are tuned to a specific **$f_r$**; **fixing with air gap** boosts LF absorption.
- [x] **NRC** = average of $\alpha$ at 250, 500, 1000, 2000 Hz. **$\alpha_{sabin}$** for one sabin = $1 \, \text{m}^2$ of perfect absorber.
- [x] **Sabine formula**: $T_{60} = 0.161 \, V/A$ — accurate only when $\bar{\alpha} < 0.2$.
- [x] **Eyring formula**: $T_{60} = 0.161 \, V/[-S \ln(1 - \bar{\alpha})]$ — use when $\bar{\alpha} > 0.2$.
- [x] **Speed of sound at 20°C**: $c \approx 343$ m/s. **Constant in Sabine derivation**: $0.161$ comes from $4 \cdot 6 \ln 10 / 343 \approx 0.1611$.
- [x] **Helmholtz tuning**: $f_r = (c/2\pi) \sqrt{S/(V_l \, l_{eff})}$. Smaller cavity volume $\rightarrow$ *higher* $f_r$ and vice-versa.
- [x] **Visual task parameters**: size, contrast, reflectance, time, observer age.
- [x] **DF minima (IS 3646)**: kitchens 1.5%, living rooms 1.0%, classrooms 2.0%, drawing offices 3.0%.
- [x] **Luminance ratios**: 3:1 (task to immediate), 10:1 (task to remote).
- [x] **Glare index** must be controlled with diffusers, louvres, and blinds in daylit spaces.
- [x] Sabine → Imperial: $T = 0.05 V/A$ (ft units); Sabine → SI: $T = 0.161 V/A$ (m units).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Units of Light
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET415 - ARCHITECTURAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Units of Light

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In architectural lighting engineering, light is evaluated not by the raw energy it carries (radiometric quantities) but by how the human eye perceives it (photometric quantities). The **Units of Light** constitute the standardized system of photometric measurements defined by the **International System of Units (SI)** that quantify the visible radiation emitted, transmitted, or received by a surface, weighted by the standard luminosity function $V(\lambda)$ which models the spectral sensitivity of the human eye under photopic (daylight) vision conditions.

The four fundamental photometric quantities used in KTU architectural engineering are:

- **Luminous Flux (Φ)** — measured in **lumen (lm)**
- **Luminous Intensity (I)** — measured in **candela (cd)**
- **Illuminance (E)** — measured in **lux (lx)**
- **Luminance (L)** — measured in **candela per square meter (cd/m²)** or **nit**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The candela (cd) is one of the **7 base SI units** and forms the foundation for deriving all other photometric units. This makes it the single most important unit to master for the board examination.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are watering a garden with a hose. The total amount of water flowing out per second is like **luminous flux** (the total "light energy" coming out). If you put your thumb over the hose to make a narrow, powerful jet, the water is now *concentrated* in one direction — this concentration per unit solid angle is like **luminous intensity**. When the water jet hits the soil, the wetness on a small patch of ground is like **illuminance** (light falling on a surface). Finally, if that wet patch is bright and visible from far away because of its reflective material, that visible brightness is like **luminance** (light leaving a surface toward your eye).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Intuition for Students:** Flux is the *amount*, intensity is the *concentration in a direction*, illuminance is the *amount arriving on a surface*, and luminance is the *amount leaving a surface toward the observer*. They are all related but represent different physical "stages" of light interaction.

## 1.3 Base Physical Constants

- **Standard luminosity function peak:** $V(\lambda) = 1$ at $\lambda = 555$ nm (green-yellow)
- **Maximum spectral luminous efficacy:** $K_m = 683$ **lumens per watt (lm/W)**
- **Luminous efficacy of 555 nm monochromatic radiation:** 1 W radiant power = 683 lm

These constants are **bolded** because they appear as direct 2-mark questions in KTU Part A papers.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Photometric Quantities — Operational Logic

### 2.1.1 Luminous Flux (Φ) — Unit: Lumen (lm)

- **Definition:** The total quantity of visible light emitted by a source per unit time, weighted by the eye's sensitivity function.
- **Logic:** A 100 W incandescent bulb emits roughly **1500 lm**, while a 100 W LED can emit **15,000 lm**. The same electrical power produces vastly different *visible* light because of differing spectral efficiency.
- **SI Base:** Derived unit (lm = cd · sr)

### 2.1.2 Luminous Intensity (I) — Unit: Candela (cd)

- **Definition:** The luminous flux emitted per unit solid angle in a given direction.
- **Base SI Unit:** The candela is defined as the luminous intensity, in a given direction, of a source that emits monochromatic radiation of frequency $540 \times 10^{12}$ Hz and has a radiant intensity in that direction of **1/683 watt per steradian**.
- **Why it matters:** Used in architectural specifications of luminaires (e.g., "intensity distribution curve").

### 2.1.3 Illuminance (E) — Unit: Lux (lx)

- **Definition:** The luminous flux incident per unit area of a surface.
- **Logic:** A textbook needs about **300–500 lx**, an office needs **400–500 lx**, while bright sunlight provides **100,000 lx**.
- **SI Unit:** 1 lx = 1 lm/m²

### 2.1.4 Luminance (L) — Unit: cd/m² (nit)

- **Definition:** The luminous intensity per unit projected area of a surface in a given direction.
- **Why it matters:** This is the quantity the human eye actually perceives as "brightness." A white wall reflecting light has luminance; an LED screen displaying pixels has luminance.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Symbol | Formula | SI Unit | Boundary / Use Case |
|---|----------|--------|---------|---------|---------------------|
| 1 | Luminous Flux | $\Phi$ | $\Phi = \dfrac{Q}{t}$ | lumen (lm) | Total light emitted |
| 2 | Solid Angle | $\omega$ | $\omega = \dfrac{A}{r^2}$ | steradian (sr) | 1 sr = sphere area/r² |
| 3 | Luminous Intensity | $I$ | $I = \dfrac{\Phi}{\omega}$ | candela (cd) | Directional flux |
| 4 | Illuminance | $E$ | $E = \dfrac{\Phi}{A}$ | lux (lx) | Flux on surface |
| 5 | Illuminance (point source) | $E$ | $E = \dfrac{I \cos \theta}{d^2}$ | lux (lx) | Inverse square + cosine law |
| 6 | Luminance | $L$ | $L = \dfrac{I}{A \cos \theta}$ | cd/m² (nit) | Apparent brightness |
| 7 | Luminous Efficacy | $\eta$ | $\eta = \dfrac{\Phi}{P}$ | lm/W | $K_m = 683$ lm/W at 555 nm |
| 8 | Conversion Flux ↔ Energy | $\Phi$ | $\Phi_v = K_m \int V(\lambda) \Phi_{e,\lambda} d\lambda$ | lm | Spectral weighting |
| 9 | Hemispherical flux | $\Phi$ | $\Phi = 2\pi I$ (uniform hemisphere) | lm | Downward directed lamp |
| 10 | Spherical flux (isotropic) | $\Phi$ | $\Phi = 4\pi I$ | lm | Point source in all directions |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Memorize the conversions — **1 lx = 1 lm/m²** and **1 cd = 1 lm/sr**. These are tested as 2-mark conversions almost every year.

## 2.3 Real-World Architectural Utility

In professional lighting design software (DIALux, Relux, AGi32), the four units serve specific roles:

- **Lumen (lm)** — used to specify the *output* of a luminaire (e.g., "a 2000 lm downlight").
- **Candela (cd)** — used in **photometric data files (IES files)** to describe intensity distribution per solid angle.
- **Lux (lx)** — used to verify code compliance (e.g., NBC 2016 requires 300 lx for office desks).
- **Luminance (cd/m²)** — used in glare analysis and visual comfort calculations (UGR, DGP).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Inverse Square Law of Illuminance

**Statement:** The illuminance $E$ on a surface varies inversely as the square of the distance $d$ from a point source.

**Given:**
- Point source with luminous intensity $I$ (cd)
- Distance from source to surface = $d$ (m)
- Surface perpendicular to the direction of light

**Step 1 — Express luminous flux passing through a sphere of radius $d$:**
A point source emitting uniformly in all directions distributes its total flux $\Phi$ over a sphere of area $4\pi d^2$. The flux passing through unit area at distance $d$ is the illuminance.

**Step 2 — Write the relationship between intensity and flux:**

$$I = \dfrac{\Phi}{\omega}$$

where $\omega$ is the solid angle subtended.

**Step 3 — Substitute solid angle for a full sphere:**

$$I = \dfrac{\Phi}{4\pi}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for total flux:**

$$\Phi = 4\pi I$$

**Step 5 — Flux density (illuminance) on sphere of radius $d$:**

The flux $\Phi$ spreads over surface area $A = 4\pi d^2$, so:

$$E = \dfrac{\Phi}{A} = \dfrac{4\pi I}{4\pi d^2}$$

**Step 6 — Cancel and simplify:**

$$E = \dfrac{I}{d^2}$$

This is the **Inverse Square Law**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Assumption:** This derivation assumes a *point source* and a *surface normal to the direction of light*. If the surface is tilted, the cosine correction must be added (see Derivation 2).

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Cosine Law of Illumination (Lambert's Law for Incidence)

**Statement:** When light strikes a surface at an angle $\theta$ from the normal, the illuminance is reduced by a factor of $\cos \theta$.

**Step 1 — Consider a surface element $dA$ tilted at angle $\theta$ from the beam direction.**

**Step 2 — The projected area perpendicular to the light direction is:**

$$A_{\perp} = A \cos \theta$$

**Step 3 — Apply inverse square law to the projected area:**

$$E = \dfrac{I}{d^2}$$

This gives illuminance on the *projected* area. The actual illuminance on the tilted area is flux divided by the tilted area.

**Step 4 — Compute illuminance on tilted surface:**

$$E = \dfrac{\Phi}{A} = \dfrac{\Phi \cos \theta}{A}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute $\Phi = I \cdot d\omega$ and $d\omega = A \cos \theta / d^2$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
E &= \dfrac{I \cdot d\omega}{A} \\
&= \dfrac{I \cdot (A \cos \theta / d^2)}{A} \\
&= \dfrac{I \cos \theta}{d^2}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6 — Final combined form:**

$$\boxed{E = \dfrac{I \cos \theta}{d^2}}$$

This is the **fundamental point-source illuminance equation** used in every KTU numerical problem.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Relationship between Luminance and Illuminance for a Diffuse (Lambertian) Surface

**Given:** A perfectly diffusing surface with reflectance $\rho$ and illuminance $E$ (lx).

**Step 1 — For a Lambertian surface, luminance $L$ is uniform in all directions and given by:**

$$L = \dfrac{\rho \cdot E}{\pi}$$

**Step 2 — Derivation sketch:** A diffuse surface scatters incident flux $\Phi = E \cdot A$ uniformly into the upper hemisphere (solid angle $= 2\pi$ sr). The intensity in any direction is $I = \Phi / (2\pi) = E \cdot A / (2\pi)$. The luminance (intensity per projected area $A \cos \theta$) integrates to give the factor of $\pi$ in the denominator.

$$\begin{aligned}
L &= \dfrac{I}{A \cos \theta} \\
&= \dfrac{E \cdot A / (2\pi)}{A \cos \theta}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Apply Lambert's cosine law for emission and integrate over the hemisphere. The net result after integration is:**

$$L = \dfrac{\rho \cdot E}{\pi}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Result:** A surface with $\rho = 0.7$ (70% reflectance) under 500 lx illuminance has luminance $L = 0.7 \times 500 / \pi \approx 111.4$ cd/m². This is the standard office wall brightness.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Numerical Problem Patterns

| Pattern | Given | Find | Key Formula |
|---------|-------|------|-------------|
| **Type 1** | $I$, $d$ | $E$ (perpendicular) | $E = I/d^2$ |
| **Type 2** | $I$, $d$, $\theta$ | $E$ (inclined) | $E = I \cos \theta / d^2$ |
| **Type 3** | $E$, $A$ | $\Phi$ | $\Phi = E \cdot A$ |
| **Type 4** | $\Phi$, $\omega$ | $I$ | $I = \Phi / \omega$ |
| **Type 5** | $\Phi$, $P$ | $\eta$ | $\eta = \Phi / P$ |
| **Type 6** | $E$, $\rho$ | $L$ (Lambertian) | $L = \rho E / \pi$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Hierarchy of Photometric Quantities

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Light Source] --> B[Luminous Flux Phi in lumens]
    B --> C[Luminous Intensity I in candelas]
    C --> D[Illuminance E in lux]
    D --> E[Luminance L in cd per m2]
    B --> F[Solid Angle omega in steradians]
    C --> F
    D --> G[Surface Area A in m2]
    B --> G
    E --> H[Reflectance rho]
    D --> H
    A --> I[Electrical Power P in watts]
    I --> J[Luminous Efficacy eta in lm per W]
    B --> J
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Inverse Square Law & Cosine Law Interaction

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Point Source] --> B[Light Travels Distance d]
    B --> C[Spreads Over Sphere Area 4 pi d squared]
    C --> D[Illuminance E equals I over d squared]
    E[Surface Tilted at angle theta] --> F[Projected Area A cos theta]
    F --> D
    D --> G[Final E equals I cos theta over d squared]
    H[Lambertian Surface rho] --> I[Reflects Light Uniformly]
    I --> J[Luminance L equals rho E over pi]
    D --> J
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Conversion Flow Between Units

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[1 Watt Radiant at 555 nm] --> B[683 lumens]
    B --> C[Divided by solid angle omega in sr]
    C --> D[Result in candelas cd]
    B --> E[Divided by area A in m squared]
    E --> F[Result in lux lx]
    D --> G[Divided by projected area A cos theta]
    G --> H[Result in cd per m squared nit]
    F --> I[Multiplied by reflectance rho divided by pi]
    I --> H
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Block-Level Functional Architecture Mapping:** The diagrams above trace the *causal* path of how radiant energy is converted by the eye's response into perceived brightness — from a source's raw output (lm) to the visual sensation on the retina (cd/m²). This is the mental model KTU examiners expect students to internalize.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Luminous Flux and Luminous Intensity. State their SI units.  `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

**Luminous Flux ($\Phi$):** It is the total quantity of visible light energy emitted per unit time from a source, evaluated according to the standard luminosity function $V(\lambda)$.

$$SI \text{ unit: lumen (lm)}$$

**Luminous Intensity ($I$):** It is the luminous flux emitted per unit solid angle in a specified direction.

$$I = \dfrac{\Phi}{\omega}$$

$$SI \text{ unit: candela (cd)}$$

The candela is one of the 7 base SI units. Numerically, 1 cd = 1 lm/sr.

---

### Question 2: Differentiate between Illuminance and Luminance. Give one example of each in architectural context.  `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Illuminance ($E$) | Luminance ($L$) |
|--------|-------------------|-----------------|
| Definition | Luminous flux incident per unit area of a surface | Luminous intensity per unit projected area emitted in a given direction |
| Unit | lux (lx) = lm/m² | candela/m² (nit) |
| Quantity type | Incident (incoming) light | Emitted/reflected (outgoing) light |
| Measured with | Lux meter at the surface | Luminance meter or calculated from intensity |
| Example | 500 lx on a study desk | 250 cd/m² of a glowing white wall under the same desk lamp |
| CIE/IS code | Used in NBC 2016 lighting schedules | Used in UGR glare index calculations |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common 1-mark loss:** Students frequently state illuminance and luminance as the *same* quantity. They are not — illuminance measures light *arriving*; luminance measures light *leaving* a surface toward the observer. KTU examiners specifically deduct for this confusion.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A: Inverse Square Law & Cosine Law (14 Marks)  `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a) State and derive the Inverse Square Law of illumination. A lamp with luminous intensity 200 cd in all directions is suspended 4 m above a working plane. Find the illuminance directly below the lamp.** (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Statement:** The illuminance on a surface due to a point source varies inversely as the square of the distance between the source and the surface.

**Derivation:**

Let a point source $S$ have luminous intensity $I$ candelas uniformly distributed in all directions.

Step 1: The total flux emitted by the source is $\Phi = 4\pi I$ lumens.

Step 2: At distance $d$, this flux falls on a sphere of surface area $A = 4\pi d^2$.

Step 3: The flux per unit area (illuminance) is:

$$E = \dfrac{\Phi}{A} = \dfrac{4\pi I}{4\pi d^2}$$

Step 4: Simplifying:

$$\boxed{E = \dfrac{I}{d^2}}$$

**Numerical:**
- $I = 200$ cd
- $d = 4$ m
- $\theta = 0°$ (directly below, normal incidence)

$$E = \dfrac{200}{(4)^2} = \dfrac{200}{16} = 12.5 \text{ lux}$$

**Answer:** 12.5 lx

---

**(b) Two lamps of luminous intensity 300 cd and 200 cd are suspended 6 m and 4 m above a horizontal surface respectively. The lamps are 8 m apart. Calculate the total illuminance on the surface directly below the midpoint of the line joining the two lamps. Use the cosine law of illumination.** (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- Lamp 1: $I_1 = 300$ cd at height $h_1 = 6$ m
- Lamp 2: $I_2 = 200$ cd at height $h_2 = 4$ m
- Horizontal separation: $L = 8$ m
- Point of interest: midpoint, so horizontal offset = $L/2 = 4$ m from each lamp

**Step 1 — Compute distance from each lamp to the midpoint:**

For Lamp 1:

$$d_1 = \sqrt{h_1^2 + 4^2} = \sqrt{36 + 16} = \sqrt{52} = 7.211 \text{ m}$$

For Lamp 2:

$$d_2 = \sqrt{h_2^2 + 4^2} = \sqrt{16 + 16} = \sqrt{32} = 5.657 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2 — Compute angle of incidence $\theta$ for each lamp:**

For Lamp 1:

$$\cos \theta_1 = \dfrac{h_1}{d_1} = \dfrac{6}{7.211} = 0.832$$

For Lamp 2:

$$\cos \theta_2 = \dfrac{h_2}{d_2} = \dfrac{4}{5.657} = 0.707$$

**Step 3 — Apply cosine law $E = I \cos \theta / d^2$ for each lamp:**

For Lamp 1:

$$E_1 = \dfrac{300 \times 0.832}{(7.211)^2} = \dfrac{249.6}{51.99} = 4.80 \text{ lx}$$

For Lamp 2:

$$E_2 = \dfrac{200 \times 0.707}{(5.657)^2} = \dfrac{141.4}{32.0} = 4.42 \text{ lx}$$

**Step 4 — Total illuminance (superposition):**

$$E_{total} = E_1 + E_2 = 4.80 + 4.42 = 9.22 \text{ lx}$$

**Answer:** $\approx 9.22$ lux

---

### Question B: Luminous Efficacy & Lambertian Surfaces (14 Marks)  `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a) Define luminous efficacy. A 40 W fluorescent lamp emits 2400 lumens. Calculate its luminous efficacy. If 4 such lamps are used to illuminate a room of area 30 m², find the average illuminance on the working plane, assuming 70% of the emitted flux reaches the working plane.** (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Definition:** Luminous efficacy ($\eta$) of a source is the ratio of luminous flux emitted to the electrical power consumed.

$$\eta = \dfrac{\Phi}{P} \quad \text{unit: lm/W}$$

**Step 1 — Efficacy of one lamp:**

$$\eta = \dfrac{2400 \text{ lm}}{40 \text{ W}} = 60 \text{ lm/W}$$

**Step 2 — Total flux from 4 lamps:**

$$\Phi_{total} = 4 \times 2400 = 9600 \text{ lm}$$

**Step 3 — Flux reaching the working plane (70% of total):**

$$\Phi_{util} = 0.70 \times 9600 = 6720 \text{ lm}$$

**Step 4 — Average illuminance:**

$$E = \dfrac{\Phi_{util}}{A} = \dfrac{6720}{30} = 224 \text{ lx}$$

**Answer:** Efficacy = 60 lm/W; Average illuminance = 224 lx

---

**(b) A Lambertian surface of reflectance 0.6 is illuminated to an illuminance of 800 lx. Calculate its luminance. If this surface is replaced by a specular mirror of reflectance 0.85, what is the luminance at the angle of reflection (assume perfect specular reflection, intensity 50 cd)?** (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Luminance of Lambertian surface:**

For a perfectly diffusing (Lambertian) surface:

$$L = \dfrac{\rho \cdot E}{\pi}$$

$$L = \dfrac{0.6 \times 800}{\pi} = \dfrac{480}{\pi} = 152.79 \text{ cd/m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Luminance of specular mirror surface:**

For a specular (mirror-like) surface, the luminance is concentrated in the mirror-reflection direction. The luminance in that specific direction is:

$$L_{spec} = \dfrac{I}{A \cos \theta}$$

Given $I = 50$ cd, assuming surface area $A = 1$ m² and $\theta = 0°$ (normal viewing):

$$L_{spec} = \dfrac{50}{1 \times 1} = 50 \text{ cd/m}^2$$

**Step 3 — Comparison:**

The Lambertian surface (152.79 cd/m²) appears *brighter* uniformly across viewing angles, while the specular surface (50 cd/m²) appears bright only in the mirror direction. Note that the specular surface has higher reflectance (0.85) but the light is concentrated in one direction.

**Answer:** $L_{Lambertian} \approx 152.79$ cd/m²; $L_{specular} = 50$ cd/m² in mirror direction

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students often forget the factor of $\pi$ in the Lambertian formula. The correct formula is $L = \rho E / \pi$, NOT $L = \rho E$. Losing the $\pi$ costs 2 marks in numerical problems. Also, when using the cosine law $E = I \cos \theta / d^2$, the angle $\theta$ is measured from the *normal* to the surface, not from the surface plane — another common 1-mark deduction.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Luminous Flux (Φ)** is the *amount* of visible light; unit is **lumen (lm)**. It is analogous to total water flow.
- **Luminous Intensity (I)** is the *directional concentration* of flux; unit is **candela (cd)**, a base SI unit. 1 cd = 1 lm/sr.
- **Illuminance (E)** is flux *received* per unit area; unit is **lux (lx)**. 1 lx = 1 lm/m². Used for code compliance.
- **Luminance (L)** is the *perceived brightness*; unit is **cd/m² (nit)**. Used in glare and visual comfort analysis.
- **Solid angle** $\omega$ is measured in **steradian (sr)**, with full sphere = $4\pi$ sr.
- **Inverse Square Law:** $E = I / d^2$ — valid for point sources and normal incidence.
- **Cosine Law:** $E = I \cos \theta / d^2$ — combined with inverse square law, this is the most-used equation in numericals.
- **Luminous Efficacy** $\eta = \Phi / P$, unit lm/W. Theoretical max is **683 lm/W** at 555 nm.
- **Lambertian surface:** $L = \rho E / \pi$ — most architectural surfaces (painted walls) approximate this.
- **SI base constant:** $K_m = 683$ lm/W at $\lambda = 555$ nm — appears frequently in 2-mark questions.
- **Conversion ladder:** 1 lm → divide by $\omega$ (sr) → 1 cd; divide by $A$ (m²) → 1 lx; divide by $A \cos \theta$ → 1 cd/m².
- **Typical illuminance values to memorize:** Sunlight = 100,000 lx; Office = 500 lx; Living room = 150 lx; Moonlight = 0.3 lx.
- **Don't confuse incident vs. emitted light:** Illuminance is *incoming*; Luminance is *outgoing* (or reflected).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Light
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET415 - ARCHITECTURAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Light

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition of Light

In the context of **Architectural Engineering**, **Light** is defined as the narrow band of **electromagnetic radiation** that is perceptible to the human visual system, having wavelengths ranging approximately from **380 nm to 780 nm** (1 nm = $10^{-9}$ m). This visible band of the electromagnetic spectrum is sandwiched between ultraviolet radiation (shorter wavelengths) and infrared radiation (longer wavelengths).

From a purely architectural standpoint, light is far more than a mere physical quantity. It is the **primary medium** through which the built environment is perceived, experienced, and interpreted. Light governs:
- **Visual perception** of form, color, texture, and spatial depth.
- **Physiological well-being** of occupants (circadian rhythm, alertness, hormonal balance).
- **Psychological states** (mood, productivity, comfort).
- **Aesthetic expression** (highlighting materials, creating drama, defining ambiance).
- **Energy performance** of the building (lighting load, cooling load, daylight harvesting).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** In Architectural Engineering (PECET415), the study of light is divided into two broad domains: **Natural Light (Daylighting)** and **Artificial Light (Electric Lighting)**. A complete architectural lighting design must integrate both for energy efficiency, occupant comfort, and visual performance.

## 1.2 The Dual Nature of Light

Light exhibits a **wave-particle duality**, which is critical to understand for KTU conceptual questions:

| Nature | Description | Architectural Relevance |
|---|---|---|
| **Wave Nature** | Light propagates as transverse electromagnetic waves characterized by wavelength ($\lambda$), frequency ($f$), and amplitude. | Determines color (wavelength), penetration, reflection, refraction, and diffraction. |
| **Particle Nature** | Light consists of discrete packets of energy called **photons**, where energy $E = h \cdot f$ (Planck's relation). | Explains photoelectric effect, photosynthesis, solar cell operation, vision chemistry. |

**Where:**
- $h$ = **Planck's constant** = $6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s
- $f$ = frequency of light in Hertz (Hz)
- $c$ = speed of light in vacuum = $3 \times 10^8$ m/s
- $c = f \cdot \lambda$

## 1.3 The Visible Spectrum — Color and Wavelength

The visible spectrum is divided into the following color bands (as defined by the **International Commission on Illumination — CIE**):

| Color | Wavelength Range (nm) |
|---|---|
| Violet | 380 – 450 |
| Blue | 450 – 495 |
| Green | 495 – 570 |
| Yellow | 570 – 590 |
| Orange | 590 – 620 |
| Red | 620 – 780 |

> [!NOTE]
> **White light** is not a single wavelength; it is the **superposition of all visible wavelengths** in approximately equal proportions, as demonstrated by Newton's prism experiment. Daylight (specifically **D65 standard illuminant** in photometric standards) is the architectural reference for "white light" used in design calculations.

## 1.4 Photopic, Scotopic, and Mesopic Vision

The human eye responds to light differently based on ambient luminance levels:

- **Photopic Vision (Day Vision):** Operates at luminance levels above approximately **3 cd/m²**. Mediated by **cone cells** concentrated in the **fovea centralis** of the retina. Provides high acuity and full color discrimination.
- **Scotopic Vision (Night Vision):** Operates below approximately **0.001 cd/m²**. Mediated by **rod cells** distributed in the peripheral retina. No color perception; only shades of grey.
- **Mesopic Vision:** Intermediate range (0.001 – 3 cd/m²). Both rods and cones are active. Critical for nighttime exterior architectural lighting, road lighting, and security lighting design.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All standard **photometric quantities** in architectural practice (illuminance, luminance, luminous flux) are based on the **photopic luminous efficiency function $V(\lambda)$**, which represents the spectral sensitivity of the human eye under photopic conditions. This is why two light sources of identical radiometric power can appear drastically different in brightness depending on their spectral distribution.

## 1.5 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Currency Exchange" Analogy for Light:**
> Think of light as a **universal currency** in a building. Different nations (physical quantities) accept different currencies, but the building "spends" everything in one form: **visible light energy**.
>
> - **Radiant energy (watts)** is like *raw gold* — universally valuable but impractical for daily transactions.
> - **Luminous flux (lumens)** is like *spendable cash* — adjusted by the human eye's sensitivity to each color, making it the architect's "working currency."
> - **Illuminance (lux)** is the *cash on a surface* — how much money (light) has been deposited on the desk (workplane).
> - **Luminance (cd/m²)** is the *brightness of the money itself* — what you actually *see* when light reflects from a surface.
>
> Just as a building designer must understand the "exchange rates" between flux, intensity, illuminance, and luminance, the student of architectural engineering must master the **photometric chain** to design spaces that look right, feel right, and perform efficiently.

## 1.6 Light Sources in Architectural Context

Architectural light sources are classified into two master categories:

### A. Natural Light (Daylight)
- **Direct sunlight** — Direct beam from the sun.
- **Diffuse skylight** — Light scattered by clouds, sky dome, and atmosphere.
- **Reflected light** — Light bounced from ground, water, adjacent buildings, terrain.
- **CIE Standard Skies** — Mathematical models (CIE Overcast Sky, CIE Clear Sky, CIE Intermediate Sky) used in daylighting simulation.

### B. Artificial Light
- **Incandescent lamps** — Tungsten filament, low efficacy (~12–15 lm/W), warm color.
- **Fluorescent lamps (CFL, T5, T8)** — Mercury vapor + phosphor coating, moderate efficacy (~50–100 lm/W).
- **LED (Light Emitting Diode)** — Semiconductor junction, high efficacy (~100–200+ lm/W), long life, dominant modern source.
- **HID lamps (Metal Halide, High Pressure Sodium)** — Used in industrial, commercial, and outdoor lighting.
- **OLED, Induction lamps, Laser-excited phosphor** — Emerging technologies.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 emphasis:** Modern KTU questions frequently test the comparison between **luminous efficacy** (lm/W), **Color Rendering Index (CRI or Ra)**, and **Correlated Color Temperature (CCT in Kelvin)** of different lamp types. Memorize the qualitative ranking.

## 1.7 Key Photometric Symbols and SI Units (Foundational Reference)

| Symbol | Quantity | SI Unit | Unit Symbol |
|---|---|---|---|
| $Q_v$ | Luminous energy | lumen-second | lm·s |
| $\Phi_v$ | Luminous flux | lumen | lm |
| $I_v$ | Luminous intensity | candela | cd |
| $E_v$ | Illuminance | lux | lx (= lm/m²) |
| $L_v$ | Luminance | candela per square meter | cd/m² |
| $K$ | Luminous efficacy | lumen per watt | lm/W |
| $V(\lambda)$ | Spectral luminous efficiency | dimensionless | — |
| $K_m$ | Maximum spectral luminous efficacy | 683 lm/W | lm/W |

> [!NOTE]
> The **candela (cd)** is the SI base unit from which all other photometric units are derived. One candela is defined as the luminous intensity, in a given direction, of a source that emits monochromatic radiation of frequency $540 \times 10^{12}$ Hz (corresponding to $\lambda = 555$ nm) and that has a radiant intensity in that direction of $\frac{1}{683}$ W per steradian.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Photometric Chain — From Source to Surface to Eye

The architectural lighting design problem is best understood as a three-stage chain:

**Stage 1 — Source → Flux ($\Phi$):** A lamp converts electrical power into luminous flux. The total light emitted in all directions is measured in **lumens (lm)**.

**Stage 2 — Flux → Illuminance ($E$):** The flux falls onto a workplane (desk, floor, wall). The **density of flux on the surface** is illuminance, measured in **lux (lm/m²)**.

**Stage 3 — Illuminance → Luminance ($L$):** The surface reflects the flux according to its reflectance. What the eye actually perceives is the **brightness (luminance)** of the surface, measured in **cd/m²**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Visual perception depends on luminance, not illuminance.** A highly reflective white wall under 100 lx may look brighter than a black wall under 500 lx. This is a frequent KTU conceptual trap.

## 2.2 Solid Angle — The Geometric Foundation

A **solid angle** ($\omega$) is the three-dimensional analog of a planar angle. It measures the "spread" of a cone of light in space.

$$\omega = \frac{A}{r^{2}}$$

Where $A$ is the area intercepted on a sphere of radius $r$. The SI unit is the **steradian (sr)**. The total solid angle surrounding a point in space is $4\pi$ steradians.

For a point source, the relationship between luminous flux and intensity is:

$$\Phi = I \cdot \omega$$

Or equivalently:

$$I = \frac{d\Phi}{d\omega}$$

The candela is therefore exactly $1 \; \text{lm/sr}$.

## 2.3 Luminous Intensity ($I$)

**Definition:** The luminous flux emitted per unit solid angle in a specified direction.

$$I_v = \frac{d\Phi_v}{d\omega}$$

**Unit:** candela (cd) = lm/sr

**Architectural interpretation:** A spotlight has high intensity in its beam direction but low intensity elsewhere. A bare bulb has roughly uniform intensity in all directions ($I = \Phi / 4\pi$).

## 2.4 Illuminance ($E$)

**Definition:** The luminous flux incident per unit area on a surface.

$$E_v = \frac{d\Phi_v}{dA}$$

**Unit:** lux (lx) = lm/m²

**Typical architectural illuminance values (as per IS 3646 / EN 12464):**

| Space Type | Recommended Illuminance (lux) |
|---|---|
| Corridors, lobbies | 100 – 150 |
| Offices (general work) | 300 – 500 |
| Detailed drafting, design studios | 750 – 1000 |
| Conference rooms | 300 – 500 |
| Classrooms | 300 – 500 |
| Hospital operating theatres | 1000 – 2000 |
| Residential living rooms | 100 – 200 |
| Industrial precision work | 1000+ |

## 2.5 Luminance ($L$)

**Definition:** The luminous intensity per unit projected area of a surface (in a given direction).

$$L_v = \frac{dI_v}{dA \cdot \cos\theta} = \frac{d^{2}\Phi_v}{d\omega \cdot dA \cdot \cos\theta}$$

**Unit:** cd/m²

**Architectural relevance:** Luminance governs **glare**, **visual comfort**, and the **brightness contrast** that defines what we see. The luminance of surfaces is what the eye and brain process to form the visual image.

For a **Lambertian (perfectly diffuse) surface**, luminance is independent of viewing direction:

$$L = \frac{\rho \cdot E}{\pi}$$

Where $\rho$ is the reflectance of the surface (0 to 1).

## 2.6 Luminous Efficacy ($K$)

$$K = \frac{\Phi_v}{P}$$

Where $P$ is the electrical power input (watts). Efficacy is the "exchange rate" from electrical power to visible light.

| Lamp Type | Typical Efficacy (lm/W) | CRI (Ra) | CCT (K) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Incandescent | 12 – 15 | 100 | 2700 |
| Halogen | 15 – 25 | 100 | 2800 – 3000 |
| CFL | 50 – 70 | 80 – 85 | 2700 – 6500 |
| Fluorescent T5 | 80 – 100 | 80 – 95 | 3000 – 6500 |
| Metal Halide | 70 – 100 | 80 – 95 | 3000 – 5000 |
| High Pressure Sodium | 80 – 140 | 20 – 30 | 2000 – 2200 |
| **LED (modern)** | **100 – 200+** | **80 – 98** | **2700 – 6500** |
| Low Pressure Sodium | 100 – 200 | -40 (monochromatic yellow) | 1800 |

> [!NOTE]
> The **theoretical maximum luminous efficacy** is **683 lm/W**, achieved only at $\lambda = 555$ nm under photopic conditions. Real lamps are far below this because of thermal, spectral, and electrical losses.

## 2.7 Luminance Contrast and Visual Performance

The **contrast** between a task and its background determines visibility:

$$C = \frac{\vert L_{task} - L_{background} \vert}{L_{background}}$$

Or in reflectance form for diffusing surfaces:

$$C = \frac{\vert \rho_{task} - \rho_{background} \vert}{\rho_{background}}$$

**Visibility is improved** by:
- Increasing contrast (preferred for tasks).
- Increasing background luminance (up to the discomfort glare limit).
- Increasing task size (when other factors fixed).

## 2.8 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **high-yield reference** for solving any numerical problem on "Light" in KTU examinations. Every formula below is examinable.

| # | Formula | Description | Units | Applicable When |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\Phi = I \cdot \omega$ | Flux from intensity and solid angle | lm = cd·sr | Point source, uniform $\omega$ |
| 2 | $E = \dfrac{\Phi}{A}$ | Average illuminance on a surface | lx = lm/m² | Uniform distribution |
| 3 | $E = \dfrac{I}{d^{2}}$ | Inverse Square Law | lx | Point source, normal incidence |
| 4 | $E = \dfrac{I}{d^{2}} \cos\theta$ | Combined Law (point source, oblique surface) | lx | Point source, inclined surface |
| 5 | $E_{n} = E \cos\theta$ | Lambert's Cosine Law | lx | Diffuse surface, oblique ray |
| 6 | $I = I_{0} \cos^{n}\theta$ | Candlepower distribution (n-th power cosine) | cd | Symmetric reflector/lamp |
| 7 | $L = \dfrac{\rho E}{\pi}$ | Luminance of Lambertian surface | cd/m² | Diffuse (matte) surface |
| 8 | $L = \dfrac{I}{A \cos\theta}$ | Luminance definition (general) | cd/m² | Any surface |
| 9 | $\omega = \dfrac{A}{r^{2}}$ | Solid angle from intercepted area | sr | Any geometry |
| 10 | $DF = \dfrac{E_{indoor}}{E_{outdoor}} \times 100\%$ | Daylight Factor | % | Horizontal interior plane |
| 11 | $DF = \dfrac{A_{w} \cdot \tau \cdot \theta}{A \cdot (1 - R^{2})}$ | Daylight Factor (BRE formula) | % | Simplified room |
| 12 | $U = \dfrac{E_{avg}}{E_{nominal}}$ | Utilization Factor (UF) | dimensionless | Interior design method |
| 13 | $N = \dfrac{E \cdot A}{\Phi \cdot U \cdot MF}$ | Number of luminaires (Lumen method) | count | Interior lighting design |
| 14 | $K = \dfrac{\Phi}{P}$ | Luminous efficacy | lm/W | Lamp specification |
| 15 | $C = \dfrac{\vert L_{t} - L_{b} \vert}{L_{b}}$ | Luminance contrast | dimensionless | Visibility analysis |
| 16 | $V(\lambda)_{max} = 1.0$ at 555 nm | Photopic luminous efficiency | — | Photometric calculations |

**Where in the BRE Daylight Factor formula:**
- $A_w$ = net area of window glass (m²)
- $\tau$ = transmittance of glass
- $\theta$ = visible angle of sky from center of window (steradians or angle correction factor)
- $A$ = total area of working plane (m²)
- $R$ = average reflectance of internal surfaces
- $E_{nominal}$ = average illuminance on the workplane assuming perfectly maintained installation
- $MF$ = Maintenance Factor (depends on lamp type, cleaning interval, environment)
- $U$ = Utilization Factor (depends on room index, luminaire type, reflectance scheme)

## 2.9 Room Index (RI / K)

Used in the lumen method to find the **utilization factor** from manufacturer photometric data:

$$K = \frac{L \times W}{H_{m} \times (L + W)}$$

Where:
- $L$ = length of room (m)
- $W$ = width of room (m)
- $H_m$ = mounting height of luminaire above the working plane (m)

Room index typically ranges from 0.6 (small rooms) to 5.0 (large open halls).

## 2.10 Maintenance Factor (MF)

$$MF = LLMF \times LSF \times RSMF \times RSMF_{room}$$

Where:
- **LLMF** = Lamp Lumen Maintenance Factor (lumen depreciation over time)
- **LSF** = Lamp Survival Factor (fraction of lamps still operating)
- **RSMF** = Room Surface Maintenance Factor (dirt on room surfaces)
- **RSMF (luminaire)** = Luminaire Maintenance Factor (dirt on luminaire optics)

Typical MF values: clean environment = 0.8; industrial = 0.5 – 0.6.

## 2.11 Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world application:** Every modern building project from a residence to a stadium is governed by these equations. The **Lumen Method** (Equation 13 in the formula sheet) is the cornerstone of interior lighting design used by electrical consultants worldwide. The **Inverse Square Law** governs façade lighting, accent lighting, and sports field design. The **Daylight Factor** is the metric used in **ECBC (Energy Conservation Building Code, India)**, **BREEAM**, and **LEED** rating systems to qualify buildings for energy efficiency credits.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Inverse Square Law of Illuminance

**Statement:** The illuminance at a point on a surface receiving light from a point source varies inversely as the square of the distance between the source and the point.

### Step 1 — Consider a point source
Consider an isotropic (uniform in all directions) point source $S$ with luminous intensity $I$ candelas. The source emits a total luminous flux $\Phi$ uniformly into a solid angle of $4\pi$ steradians.

$$\Phi = I \cdot 4\pi$$

### Step 2 — Flux passing through an imaginary sphere
Imagine a transparent sphere of radius $r_1$ centered on the source. The total flux $\Phi$ passes through the entire surface area $A_1 = 4\pi r_1^{2}$ of this sphere.

### Step 3 — Illuminance on the sphere
By definition, illuminance is flux per unit area:

$$E_1 = \frac{\Phi}{A_1} = \frac{\Phi}{4\pi r_1^{2}}$$

### Step 4 — Substitute flux in terms of intensity
Since $\Phi = 4\pi I$, we obtain:

$$E_1 = \frac{4\pi I}{4\pi r_1^{2}} = \frac{I}{r_1^{2}}$$

### Step 5 — Repeat for a different radius
For a sphere of radius $r_2$:

$$E_2 = \frac{I}{r_2^{2}}$$

### Step 6 — Form the ratio
$$\frac{E_1}{E_2} = \frac{r_2^{2}}{r_1^{2}}$$

### Step 7 — Generalize
Therefore, for any distance $d$ from a point source, the illuminance on a surface **normal to the direction of the light** is:

$$\boxed{E = \frac{I}{d^{2}}}$$

**Units check:** $I$ in cd, $d$ in m, $E$ in lm/m² = lx. Since $1 \; \text{cd} = 1 \; \text{lm/sr}$ and $1 \; \text{sr} = 1 \; \text{m}^{2}/\text{m}^{2}$ when $d$ cancels, the units are consistent. ✓

## 3.2 Derivation of Lambert's Cosine Law of Illumination

**Statement:** The illuminance on a surface varies as the cosine of the angle between the direction of the incident light and the normal to the surface.

### Step 1 — Setup
Consider a point source $S$ emitting a narrow beam of light that strikes an inclined surface at point $P$. Let the angle between the light ray and the surface normal be $\theta$. Let $d$ be the distance from $S$ to $P$.

### Step 2 — Illuminance on a surface element normal to the ray
The illuminance $E_n$ on a small surface element oriented **perpendicular** to the ray at point $P$ would be (by Inverse Square Law):

$$E_n = \frac{I}{d^{2}}$$

### Step 3 — Tilt the surface
Now consider the actual surface element $dA$ which is inclined at angle $\theta$ to the perpendicular. The area of the projection of $dA$ onto the plane perpendicular to the ray is:

$$dA_{\perp} = dA \cdot \cos\theta$$

### Step 4 — Same flux, larger area
The same luminous flux $d\Phi$ that would have illuminated $dA_{\perp}$ now illuminates the larger area $dA$. Therefore, the illuminance on $dA$ is:

$$E = \frac{d\Phi}{dA} = \frac{d\Phi}{dA_{\perp}/\cos\theta} = E_n \cdot \cos\theta$$

### Step 5 — Combine with Inverse Square Law
Substituting $E_n = I / d^{2}$:

$$\boxed{E = \frac{I}{d^{2}} \cos\theta}$$

This is the **Combined Law of Illumination** — the single most important equation in architectural point-source lighting design.

**Limiting cases:**
- $\theta = 0°$ (ray perpendicular to surface): $E = I/d^{2}$ (maximum illuminance).
- $\theta = 90°$ (ray grazing the surface): $E = 0$ (no illuminance).
- $\theta = 60°$: $E = 0.5 \cdot I/d^{2}$ (half the maximum).

## 3.3 Derivation of the Daylight Factor (BRE Simplified Method)

**Statement:** The Daylight Factor (DF) is the ratio of interior illuminance at a point to the exterior unobstructed horizontal illuminance under standard CIE overcast sky conditions, expressed as a percentage.

### Step 1 — Identify the three flux components
The total interior illuminance at a point has three contributions:

$$E_{in} = E_{sky} + E_{reflected\,ext} + E_{reflected\,int}$$

Where:
- $E_{sky}$ = illuminance due to direct light from the visible sky patch through the window.
- $E_{reflected\,ext}$ = illuminance due to light from external reflecting surfaces (ground, opposite buildings).
- $E_{reflected\,int}$ = illuminance due to light reflected from internal room surfaces.

### Step 2 — Sky Component (SC)
For a window of net glass area $A_w$ and visible transmittance $\tau$:

$$SC = \frac{\theta \cdot A_w \cdot \tau}{A \cdot (1 - R^{2})}$$

Where $\theta$ is the visible angle of sky subtended at the working point.

### Step 3 — Externally Reflected Component (ERC)
For a ground reflectance $\rho_g$ and a ground-obstruction factor $\Psi$:

$$ERC = \frac{\rho_g \cdot \Psi \cdot A_w \cdot \tau}{A \cdot (1 - R^{2})}$$

### Step 4 — Internally Reflected Component (IRC)
This is the most complex term, derived from the theory of **inter-reflections** between diffuse room surfaces. Using the standard formula (assumes uniform diffuse room):

$$IRC = \frac{R \cdot W}{(1 - R) \cdot A}$$

Where $W$ is the weighted area of glazed openings (incorporating the room geometry) and $R$ is the average internal surface reflectance.

### Step 5 — Combine
$$\boxed{DF = SC + ERC + IRC}$$

This is the most general form. A typical target in KTU/GRIHA-compliant design: $DF \geq 1.5\%$ to $2\%$ at the working plane for general office/residential spaces.

## 3.4 Derivation of the Number of Luminaires (Lumen Method)

### Step 1 — Required total flux on workplane
The design illuminance $E$ (lux) on a workplane of area $A$ (m²) requires a total flux:

$$\Phi_{required} = E \cdot A \quad \text{(lm)}$$

### Step 2 — Account for utilization factor
Only a fraction $U$ of the lamp flux reaches the workplane (the rest is absorbed by walls, ceiling, floor, or trapped in luminaires). So the **total installed flux** must be:

$$\Phi_{installed} = \frac{E \cdot A}{U}$$

### Step 3 — Account for maintenance factor
As lamps age and luminaires collect dust, the flux reaching the workplane drops. To compensate over the maintenance cycle, we must install MORE flux initially:

$$\Phi_{lamp\,total} = \frac{E \cdot A}{U \cdot MF}$$

### Step 4 — Convert to number of luminaires
If each luminaire contains $n$ lamps and each lamp produces $\Phi_L$ lumens:

$$N = \frac{\Phi_{lamp\,total}}{n \cdot \Phi_L} = \boxed{\frac{E \cdot A}{U \cdot MF \cdot n \cdot \Phi_L}}$$

This is the **Lumen Method equation** (Equation 13 in the formula sheet), and it is **the most frequently asked 14-mark derivation in KTU Module 2**.

## 3.5 Derivation of Luminance of a Perfectly Diffusing Surface

### Step 1 — Definition
A perfectly diffusing (Lambertian) surface emits (or reflects) light such that its luminance is the same in all directions.

### Step 2 — Setup
The surface has area $A$ and reflectance $\rho$. It is illuminated uniformly with illuminance $E$ (lux). The total flux reflected is:

$$\Phi_{reflected} = \rho \cdot E \cdot A \quad \text{(lm)}$$

### Step 3 — Assume Lambertian emission
A Lambertian source has luminous intensity in direction $\theta$ from the normal:

$$I(\theta) = I_{0} \cos\theta$$

The total flux emitted by such a surface is found by integrating over the hemisphere:

$$\Phi = \int_{hemisphere} I(\theta) \, d\omega = I_{0} \int_{0}^{2\pi} \int_{0}^{\pi/2} \cos\theta \cdot \sin\theta \, d\theta \, d\phi$$

### Step 4 — Evaluate the integral
$$\int_{0}^{2\pi} d\phi = 2\pi$$
$$\int_{0}^{\pi/2} \cos\theta \sin\theta \, d\theta = \frac{1}{2}$$

Therefore:

$$\Phi = I_{0} \cdot 2\pi \cdot \frac{1}{2} = \pi \cdot I_{0}$$

### Step 5 — Solve for $I_0$
$$I_{0} = \frac{\Phi}{\pi} = \frac{\rho \cdot E \cdot A}{\pi}$$

### Step 6 — Luminance
By definition, luminance of the Lambertian surface is the intensity per unit projected area:

$$L = \frac{I_{0}}{A} = \frac{\rho \cdot E}{\pi}$$

$$\boxed{L = \frac{\rho \cdot E}{\pi} \;\; \text{cd/m}^{2}}$$

This is the **fundamental link between photometric illuminance and perceived luminance** for matte architectural surfaces like painted walls, concrete, plaster, and acoustic ceilings.

## 3.6 Worked Example (KTU-style)

**Problem:** A 100 W incandescent lamp emits 1500 lm uniformly in all directions. It is suspended 2.5 m above a horizontal desk. Calculate:
(a) The illuminance on the desk directly below the lamp.
(b) The illuminance on a surface inclined at 30° to the horizontal at the same horizontal distance.

**Solution:**

**(a) Direct illuminance on horizontal desk:**

By the Inverse Square Law:

$$E = \frac{I}{d^{2}}$$

First, find the intensity. Since the lamp is isotropic:

$$I = \frac{\Phi}{4\pi} = \frac{1500}{4\pi} = 119.37 \; \text{cd}$$

Therefore:

$$E = \frac{119.37}{(2.5)^{2}} = \frac{119.37}{6.25} = 19.10 \; \text{lx}$$

**(b) Illuminance on the inclined surface:**

The angle of incidence $\theta$ (between the light ray and the normal to the surface) is given as 30°.

By the Combined Law:

$$E_{inclined} = \frac{I}{d^{2}} \cos\theta = 19.10 \times \cos(30°) = 19.10 \times 0.8660$$

$$E_{inclined} = 16.54 \; \text{lx}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This problem exemplifies the **two laws of illumination** that KTU Module 2 universally tests. The horizontal illuminance is 19.10 lx; tilting the surface by 30° reduces illuminance to 16.54 lx — a 13.4% reduction, exactly equal to $(1 - \cos 30°) \times 100\%$.

## 4.1 Block Diagram — The Photometric Quantities Chain

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the relationship between all fundamental photometric quantities and the architectural elements they describe.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Electrical Power P in W] --> B[Lamp converts to Radiant Energy]
    B --> C[Eye response weighting V lambda]
    C --> D["Luminous Flux Phi in lm"]
    D --> E1[Per unit solid angle]
    D --> E2[Per unit area on surface]
    D --> E3[Per unit projected area of source]
    E1 --> F1["Luminous Intensity I in cd"]
    E2 --> F2["Illuminance E in lx"]
    E3 --> F3["Luminance L in cd per m2"]
    F1 --> G1[Directional property of source]
    F2 --> G2[Light arriving at desk floor wall]
    F3 --> G3[Brightness perceived by eye]
    F2 --> H[Surface reflectance rho]
    H --> F3
    F3 --> I[Visual perception brightness glare]
    I --> J[Architectural lighting outcome]
    G1 --> J
    G2 --> J
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Components of Daylight Factor

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    EXT[Exterior Horizontal Illuminance Eout] --> DIV[/Division by/]
    INT[Interior Illuminance at Point Ein] --> DIV
    DIV --> DF[Daylight Factor DF percent]
    EXT -.-> SKY[Sky Component SC]
    EXT -.-> ERC[Externally Reflected Component ERC]
    INT -.-> IRC[Internally Reflected Component IRC]
    SKY --> SUM[Sum of three components]
    ERC --> SUM
    IRC --> SUM
    SUM --> DF
    SKY --> SCDET[Net window area times glass transmittance times sky angle]
    ERC --> ERCDET[Ground reflectance times obstruction factor]
    IRC --> IRCDET[Room surface reflectance and inter-reflection]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Lumen Method Design Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step 1 Determine design illuminance E from IS 3646 EN 12464] --> B[Step 2 Calculate room area A = L x W]
    B --> C[Step 3 Select luminaire type and lamp flux PhiL]
    C --> D[Step 4 Calculate Room Index K = L W divided by Hm times L + W]
    D --> E[Step 5 Look up Utilization Factor U from manufacturer data using K and reflectances]
    E --> F[Step 6 Determine Maintenance Factor MF from lamp type and environment]
    F --> G[Step 7 Calculate N = E x A divided by U x MF x n x PhiL]
    G --> H[Step 8 Round up N to practical layout and verify uniformity]

```

## 4.5 Schematic — Light Ray Geometry for Laws of Illumination

> [!NOTE]
> The Mermaid engine cannot faithfully render the inclined-plane geometry of a light ray striking a tilted surface. The following block diagram conveys the conceptual relationship between the perpendicular distance, oblique distance, and angle of incidence — these are the three variables of the **Combined Law of Illumination**.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SRC[Point Source S] --> RAY[Light Ray]
    RAY --> ANG[Angle theta between ray and surface normal]
    RAY --> DIST1[Perpendicular distance d]
    RAY --> DIST2[Oblique distance d divided by cos theta]
    SURF[Inclined Surface dA] --> PROJ[Projected Area dA x cos theta]
    NORM[Surface Normal Vector] --> ANG
    PROJ --> EQ[Equation: E = I cos theta divided by d squared]
    DIST1 --> EQ
    ANG --> EQ
    EQ --> OUT1[Illuminance on horizontal plane]
    EQ --> OUT2[Illuminance on inclined plane]
    EQ --> OUT3[Illuminance on vertical plane]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model Paper)]**

**Define the following photometric quantities and state their SI units:**
(a) Luminous flux
(b) Luminous intensity
(c) Illuminance

**Model Answer:**

(a) **Luminous flux ($\Phi_v$):** The total quantity of visible light energy emitted per unit time by a source, weighted by the standard photopic luminous efficiency function $V(\lambda)$. It represents the **time rate of flow of light**. **SI unit: lumen (lm).**

(b) **Luminous intensity ($I_v$):** The luminous flux emitted by a source per unit solid angle in a specified direction. It is the **directional light-emitting strength** of a source. **SI unit: candela (cd) = lm/sr.**

(c) **Illuminance ($E_v$):** The luminous flux incident per unit area on a surface. It describes the **density of light falling on a workplane**. **SI unit: lux (lx) = lm/m².**

**Marks split (typical KTU):** 1 mark each for correct definition + 0.5 mark each for the unit + 0.5 mark each for the symbol. Total = 6 × 0.5 = 3 marks.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 (Model Paper)]**

**State the Inverse Square Law and Lambert's Cosine Law of illumination. Why are they not strictly applicable in real architectural interiors?**

**Model Answer:**

**Inverse Square Law:** *The illuminance at a point on a surface receiving light from a point source varies inversely as the square of the distance from the source.*

$$E = \frac{I}{d^{2}}$$

**Lambert's Cosine Law:** *The illuminance on a surface is proportional to the cosine of the angle between the direction of the incident light and the normal to the surface.*

$$E = E_{n} \cos\theta$$

**Why not strictly applicable in real interiors (1 mark):**
1. Real lamps are not mathematical point sources — they have finite physical extent.
2. In interiors, light undergoes multiple **inter-reflections** from walls, ceiling, and floor, which the basic laws do not account for. The "inter-reflected component" is significant and is handled by the **Utilization Factor (UF)** and **Room Index** approach.
3. Real luminaires have **non-uniform angular distribution** of intensity; they are not perfectly isotropic.
4. Atmospheric and particulate absorption in large spaces introduces additional losses.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Scheme mandates **internal choice** in Part B. Both Question A and Question B below are fully solved alternatives. Attempt any ONE.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, PECET415 Module 2]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the **Combined Law of Illumination** for a point source illuminating an inclined surface. State clearly the assumptions and define all variables.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A 200 W incandescent lamp emits 3000 lumens uniformly in all directions. It is suspended 4 m above the floor of a hall. Calculate the illuminance on:
(i) the floor directly below the lamp.
(ii) a vertical wall 3 m away from the point directly below the lamp.
(iii) an inclined surface (tilted at 45° to the horizontal) located at the same horizontal distance as (ii) from the lamp's vertical axis.

Take the height of the wall point as 1.5 m above the floor.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Derivation:** (Refer to Section 3.2 of these notes for the full derivation. Key steps to write in the exam:)

**Assumptions (1 mark):**
1. The source is a mathematical **point source** (negligible physical size).
2. The source is **isotropic** (emits uniformly in all directions) — valid for a bare bulb; for a luminaire, replace $I$ with the directional intensity.
3. The light propagates in **straight lines** without scattering (no atmospheric absorption).
4. The receiving surface is a **perfectly diffusing** (Lambertian) surface — relevant for $E$ calculations, not for $I$.

**Derivation (5 marks):**

Consider a point source $S$ of intensity $I$ cd. Let the ray from $S$ strike a surface element $dA$ at point $P$. Let:
- $d$ = distance from $S$ to $P$
- $\theta$ = angle between the ray $SP$ and the normal to the surface at $P$

Step 1: The illuminance on a hypothetical surface element $dA_{\perp}$ placed **perpendicular** to the ray at $P$ is, by the Inverse Square Law:

$$E_{n} = \frac{I}{d^{2}}$$

Step 2: The actual surface element $dA$ is tilted at angle $\theta$. Its projection onto the plane perpendicular to the ray has area $dA_{\perp} = dA \cos\theta$.

Step 3: The same flux $d\Phi$ that would have illuminated $dA_{\perp}$ is now spread over the larger area $dA$. Hence the actual illuminance is:

$$E = \frac{d\Phi}{dA} = \frac{d\Phi}{dA_{\perp}/\cos\theta} = E_{n} \cos\theta$$

Step 4: Substituting $E_n$:

$$\boxed{E = \frac{I \cos\theta}{d^{2}}}$$

This is the **Combined Law of Illumination**.

**Definition of variables (1 mark):**
- $E$ = illuminance on the inclined surface (lux)
- $I$ = luminous intensity of the source in the direction of the point (candela)
- $d$ = distance from source to the point (meters)
- $\theta$ = angle of incidence (between the ray and the surface normal)

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Problem:**

**Given:**
- Power = 200 W
- Total flux $\Phi$ = 3000 lm (uniform emission)
- Suspension height $H = 4$ m above floor
- Vertical wall point: horizontal distance 3 m, height 1.5 m above floor

**Step 1: Find luminous intensity of the lamp**

Since the lamp is isotropic:

$$I = \frac{\Phi}{4\pi} = \frac{3000}{4 \times 3.14159} = \frac{3000}{12.566} = 238.73 \; \text{cd}$$

**(i) Illuminance on floor directly below the lamp:**

The ray is perpendicular to the floor ($\theta = 0°$), and the distance equals the suspension height.

$$E_1 = \frac{I}{H^{2}} = \frac{238.73}{(4)^{2}} = \frac{238.73}{16}$$

$$E_1 = 14.92 \; \text{lx}$$

**(ii) Illuminance on the vertical wall at the given point:**

Coordinates: lamp is at (0, 0, 4); point on wall is at (3, 0, 1.5) — with z measured from floor.

Distance from lamp to point:

$$d = \sqrt{3^{2} + (4 - 1.5)^{2}} = \sqrt{9 + 6.25} = \sqrt{15.25} = 3.905 \; \text{m}$$

The normal to a **vertical wall** is horizontal. The angle of incidence $\theta$ is the angle between the ray and the horizontal normal.

The horizontal projection of the ray = 3 m. The ray makes an angle $\alpha$ with the horizontal:

$$\cos\alpha = \frac{3}{d} = \frac{3}{3.905} = 0.7682$$

So $\theta = \alpha$ (the angle between the ray and the wall normal) and:

$$\cos\theta = 0.7682$$

Therefore:

$$E_2 = \frac{I \cos\theta}{d^{2}} = \frac{238.73 \times 0.7682}{(3.905)^{2}} = \frac{183.41}{15.25}$$

$$E_2 = 12.03 \; \text{lx}$$

**(iii) Illuminance on the inclined surface (45° to horizontal):**

Same point, same distance. The normal to the 45° surface makes 45° with the vertical (and 45° with the horizontal). We need the angle $\theta$ between the ray and this normal.

The ray from the lamp (0,0,4) to the point (3,0,1.5) has direction vector:
$$\vec{r} = (3, 0, -2.5)$$
Magnitude: $|\vec{r}| = \sqrt{9 + 6.25} = \sqrt{15.25} = 3.905$ m

Unit ray direction:
$$\hat{r} = \left(\frac{3}{3.905},\; 0,\; \frac{-2.5}{3.905}\right) = (0.7682,\; 0,\; -0.6402)$$

The normal to the 45° surface (assumed tilted away from the lamp in the +x direction at 45° from horizontal):
$$\hat{n} = \left(\cos(-45°),\; 0,\; -\sin(-45°)\right) = (0.7071,\; 0,\; 0.7071)$$

(Normal points "upward and outward" away from the floor side.)

Angle between ray and normal:

$$\cos\theta = \hat{r} \cdot \hat{n} = (0.7682)(0.7071) + (0)(-0.6402) \cdot \;... $$

Wait — let me redo this carefully. The normal pointing "upward" toward the lamp side (so that light hits the front face):

$$\hat{n} = (0.7071,\; 0,\; 0.7071)$$

$$\cos\theta = (0.7682)(0.7071) + (0)(0) + (-0.6402)(0.7071) = 0.5432 - 0.4527 = 0.0905$$

Therefore:

$$E_3 = \frac{I \cos\theta}{d^{2}} = \frac{238.73 \times 0.0905}{15.25} = \frac{21.60}{15.25}$$

$$E_3 = 1.42 \; \text{lx}$$

**Final Answers:**
- (i) $E_1 = 14.92$ lx
- (ii) $E_2 = 12.03$ lx
- (iii) $E_3 = 1.42$ lx

**Marks split (typical KTU 14-mark question):**
- Part (a): 1 mark assumptions + 1 mark for Inverse Square statement + 1 mark for cosine derivation step + 1 mark for combined formula + 1 mark for units + 2 marks for variable definitions and interpretation = 7 marks.
- Part (b): 1 mark for $I$ calculation + 2 marks for (i) + 2 marks for (ii) + 2 marks for (iii) = 7 marks.

---

### Question B (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, PECET415 Module 2, Alternative Choice]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Define **Daylight Factor**. Explain its three components (Sky Component, Externally Reflected Component, Internally Reflected Component) with neat sketches. State the typical minimum recommended Daylight Factor for an architectural workspace as per **IS 3646 / ECBC**.

**(b) [7 Marks]** An office room measuring **10 m × 6 m × 3.2 m** (L × W × H) requires an average illuminance of **400 lux** on the workplane (height 0.8 m). The proposed luminaire uses **2 × 36 W fluorescent tubes**, each producing **3350 lumens**. The room has white ceiling ($\rho_c = 0.7$), light walls ($\rho_w = 0.5$), and a darker floor ($\rho_f = 0.2$). Assume a **maintenance factor of 0.8** and use the **Lumen Method** to find:
(i) The room index
(ii) The number of luminaires required
(iii) The actual arrangement (assume square spacing on a 2-row grid)

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Daylight Factor Definition and Components:**

**Definition (2 marks):**
**Daylight Factor (DF)** is the ratio of the illuminance at a point on a given plane inside a building to the simultaneous external horizontal illuminance under standard CIE overcast sky conditions, expressed as a percentage.

$$\boxed{DF = \frac{E_{in}}{E_{out}} \times 100\%}$$

**Three Components (4 marks):**

1. **Sky Component (SC):** Light reaching the point **directly from the visible sky patch** through the window opening. It depends on window size, glass transmittance, and the angle of sky visible from the point.

2. **Externally Reflected Component (ERC):** Light from the sky that reaches the point after **reflection from external surfaces** such as the ground, adjacent buildings, or vegetation. It depends on the reflectance of these external surfaces.

3. **Internally Reflected Component (IRC):** Light that reaches the point after **one or more reflections from internal room surfaces** (ceiling, walls, floor, partitions). It depends on the average internal reflectance and the room geometry.

**Recommended values (1 mark):**
- General offices, classrooms: **DF ≥ 1.5% to 2%**
- Detailed work, drafting rooms: **DF ≥ 3% to 5%**
- Living rooms (residential): **DF ≥ 1% to 1.5%**
- (As per **IS 3646 (Part I & II)** and **ECBC 2017** for India)

**Part (b) — Lumen Method Calculation:**

**Given:**
- Room: $L = 10$ m, $W = 6$ m, ceiling height $H = 3.2$ m
- Workplane height: $h_{wp} = 0.8$ m
- Mounting height of luminaire (assumed recessed ceiling type): $H_m = 3.2 - 0.8 = 2.4$ m
- Design illuminance: $E = 400$ lx
- Luminaire: 2 lamps × 36 W, 3350 lm each → $\Phi_L = 3350$ lm, $n = 2$
- Maintenance Factor: $MF = 0.8$
- Reflectances: $\rho_c = 0.7$, $\rho_w = 0.5$, $\rho_f = 0.2$

**(i) Room Index (2 marks):**

$$K = \frac{L \times W}{H_m \times (L + W)} = \frac{10 \times 6}{2.4 \times (10 + 6)} = \frac{60}{2.4 \times 16} = \frac{60}{38.4}$$

$$K = 1.5625 \approx 1.6$$

**(ii) Number of Luminaires (3 marks):**

Area of workplane:

$$A = L \times W = 10 \times 6 = 60 \; \text{m}^{2}$$

For a fluorescent luminaire with $K = 1.6$ and the given reflectance scheme, from the manufacturer's photometric data table (typical):

$$U \approx 0.55 \; \text{(Utilization Factor)}$$

Now apply the lumen method equation:

$$N = \frac{E \cdot A}{U \cdot MF \cdot n \cdot \Phi_L}$$

$$N = \frac{400 \times 60}{0.55 \times 0.8 \times 2 \times 3350}$$

$$N = \frac{24000}{0.55 \times 0.8 \times 6700} = \frac{24000}{2948} = 8.14$$

Round up to the next whole number:

$$N = 9 \; \text{luminaires}$$

**(iii) Arrangement (2 marks):**

Total = 9 luminaires on a 2-row grid → 9 is not divisible by 2 evenly. Better to use 3 rows × 3 luminaires = 9 luminaires.

- **3 rows** along the 10 m length → row spacing along length: $10/3 = 3.33$ m
- **3 luminaires per row** along the 6 m width → spacing: $6/3 = 2.0$ m

**Spacing-to-mounting-height ratio check:** $S/H_m = 2.0/2.4 = 0.83$, which is within the recommended 1.0–1.5 range for fluorescent luminaires with diffusers, ensuring adequate uniformity. ✓

**Final layout:** 3 rows × 3 columns = 9 luminaires, with row spacing 3.33 m and column spacing 2.0 m.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost marks in KTU Module 2 (Light):**
>
> 1. **Unit confusion between lux and lumen:** Illuminance is lux (lm/m²); flux is lumen. A 14-mark problem may deduct 1–2 marks if the student writes the wrong unit.
> 2. **Forgetting the $\cos\theta$ term in the Combined Law:** Many students write only the Inverse Square Law for all inclinations. Examiners specifically allocate 1 mark for the cosine correction. **Always identify the angle of incidence** in the geometry.
> 3. **Mixing $I$ (candela) with $\Phi$ (lumen):** The conversion $I = \Phi / 4\pi$ assumes an **isotropic** point source. For directional luminaires, the examiner will specify $I$ directly. Do not assume $4\pi$ symmetry.
> 4. **Lumen Method — not rounding up correctly:** The formula gives a real number; you MUST round UP. If the answer is 8.14, you need 9 luminaires, not 8. Writing 8 loses 1 mark.
> 5. **Daylight Factor in % vs. fraction:** DF is conventionally expressed as a **percentage**. A DF of 0.02 must be written as 2%.
> 6. **Confusing $V(\lambda)$ with the wavelength of maximum visibility:** $V(\lambda) = 1.0$ at **555 nm** (not 550, not 560). Examiners test this specifically.
> 7. **Not stating assumptions** in derivations: The Combined Law derivation requires explicit listing of "point source," "isotropic," "no absorption" — losing these loses 1 mark.
> 8. **Maintenance Factor and Utilization Factor are DIFFERENT:** UF is the fraction of lamp flux reaching the workplane in a NEW, CLEAN installation. MF accounts for depreciation. Do not interchange them.
> 9. **Daylight Factor — wrong direction of subscripts:** $E_{in}/E_{out}$ is correct. $E_{out}/E_{in}$ gives the "extinction ratio" — not the same thing.
> 10. **Photopic vs. Scotopic:** All standard architectural calculations use **photopic** (cone) vision. Do not invoke scotopic values unless the problem specifies night-time conditions.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 2 — Light:**
>
> **A. Foundational Concepts**
> - Light is electromagnetic radiation in the wavelength range **380–780 nm**; the eye is most sensitive at **555 nm** (yellow-green).
> - Light has **wave-particle duality**; the photon energy is $E = h \cdot f$.
> - Photometric quantities are weighted by the **photopic luminous efficiency function $V(\lambda)$**; maximum $K_m = 683$ lm/W.
>
> **B. Core Photometric Quantities (must know symbols, units, and definitions)**
> - Luminous flux $\Phi$ — lm
> - Luminous intensity $I$ — cd (lm/sr) — flux per solid angle
> - Illuminance $E$ — lx (lm/m²) — flux per area on surface
> - Luminance $L$ — cd/m² — perceived brightness
> - Luminous efficacy $K$ — lm/W — performance metric for lamps
>
> **C. Laws of Illumination (high-yield derivations)**
> - Inverse Square Law: $E = I/d^{2}$ — for point source, normal incidence
> - Lambert's Cosine Law: $E = E_n \cos\theta$ — for tilted surface
> - Combined Law: $E = (I/d^{2}) \cos\theta$ — most-used single equation
> - Luminance of Lambertian surface: $L = \rho E / \pi$
>
> **D. Lumen Method (interior design)**
> - $N = E \cdot A / (U \cdot MF \cdot n \cdot \Phi_L)$
> - Requires Room Index $K = LW / (H_m (L+W))$
> - Requires UF from manufacturer tables (depends on $K$ and $\rho$ scheme)
> - Requires MF (typically 0.5–0.8)
> - Always round UP $N$
>
> **E. Daylight Factor (natural lighting)**
> - $DF = E_{in} / E_{out} \times 100\%$
> - $DF = SC + ERC + IRC$
> - Recommended: ≥ 1.5–2% for general offices; ≥ 3% for detailed work
> - Used in ECBC, GRIHA, LEED, BREEAM credit calculations
>
> **F. Solid Angle and Geometry**
> - Solid angle $\omega = A/r^{2}$ in steradians (sr)
> - Total solid angle around a point = $4\pi$ sr
> - Isotropic source: $I = \Phi / 4\pi$
>
> **G. Lamp Comparison (must memorize qualitative trends)**
> - LED > Metal Halide ≈ Fluorescent > Halogen > Incandescent (in lm/W)
> - CRI: Incandescent = 100 (best); HPS = 20–30 (worst among common)
> - CCT: warm (< 3000 K) → neutral (3500–4500 K) → cool (> 5000 K)
>
> **H. Recommended Illuminance (memorize key values)**
> - Offices / classrooms: **300–500 lx**
> - Detailed drafting: **750–1000 lx**
> - Corridors: **100–150 lx**
> - Operating theatres: **1000–2000 lx**
>
> **I. Key Constants to Remember**
> - $c = 3 \times 10^{8}$ m/s
> - $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s
> - $K_m = 683$ lm/W
> - $\pi = 3.14159$
> - $4\pi = 12.566$
>
> **J. Typical KTU Question Patterns**
> - 3-mark: Definitions of photometric quantities
> - 3-mark: State laws + assumptions / limitations
> - 7-mark: Derivation of Inverse Square Law OR Lambert's Cosine Law OR Combined Law OR Lumen Method equation OR Daylight Factor components
> - 7-mark: Numerical on Combined Law (point source, horizontal, vertical, inclined surfaces)
> - 7-mark: Lumen Method — calculate room index, number of luminaires, and arrangement
> - Common unit trap: lux vs. lumen, candela vs. lm/sr

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Vision and Buildings
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET415 - ARCHITECTURAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Vision and Buildings

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Vision and Buildings** is a foundational topic in architectural engineering that examines the relationship between **human visual perception** and the **built environment**. It investigates how the human eye receives, transmits, and processes visual stimuli emitted or reflected from architectural forms, surfaces, spaces, and compositions, and how this perceptual data is consciously applied by architects during the design process to shape meaningful spatial experiences.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PECET415)**, Vision and Buildings forms a critical bridge between sensory biology and design methodology. It encompasses:

- The mechanics of the human eye and visual cortex
- **Gestalt principles** of perceptual organization
- Visual properties of form (line, shape, colour, texture, pattern, light, and shadow)
- **Visual axes**, focal points, and view corridors
- Concepts of prospect, refuge, and visual hierarchy
- Psychological and physiological aspects of viewing architecture

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PECET415 - Module 2):** Vision and Buildings explores how visual perception governs the way we experience, interpret, and respond to architectural form and space. It is a *perception-driven design principle*, not merely a study of optics.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine standing at the entrance of a grand cathedral. Before you consciously *think* about the space, your **eyes and brain** are already doing immense work:

- They **capture light** bouncing off stone pillars.
- They **organize** those pillars into rhythmic vertical lines.
- They **draw your gaze** along the central aisle toward the altar — a *focal point*.
- They **judge scale** by comparing the doorway to a human figure.
- They **sense mood** through the play of light and shadow.

**Analogy:** Vision and Buildings is like the *grammar of a visual language*. Just as grammar allows us to form meaningful sentences without consciously thinking about subject-verb agreement, vision-based design principles allow architects to create powerful, emotionally resonant buildings that *feel* right — even if the viewer cannot articulate why.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition — Visual Perception:**
