## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**Define Daylight Factor. Why is the CIE Standard Overcast Sky used as a reference condition for its calculation?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Daylight Factor (DF) is defined as the ratio of the illumination at a point on a horizontal working plane indoors to the simultaneous illumination on a horizontal plane outdoors under standard overcast sky conditions, expressed as a percentage.

$$DF = \frac{E_{i}}{E_{o}} \times 100\%$$

**Why CIE Overcast Sky (1.5 marks):**
- It represents the **worst-case natural lighting scenario**, ensuring the design performs adequately even on heavily clouded days.
- The luminance distribution is mathematically defined and reproducible, giving a **standardized benchmark** for comparison across different buildings and locations.
- The **constant ratio** of indoor to outdoor illuminance means DF depends only on building geometry, not weather variability.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**List and briefly explain the three components of Daylight Factor.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The total Daylight Factor consists of three components:

1. **Sky Component (SC) — 1 mark:** Light reaching the working point directly from the visible portion of the sky through the window. It is the dominant component, contributing 60–80% of total DF in well-designed rooms.

2. **Externally Reflected Component (ERC) — 1 mark:** Light from the sky that strikes external surfaces (such as the ground or opposite buildings) and is reflected into the window. It typically contributes 5–20% of total DF.

3. **Internally Reflected Component (IRC) — 1 mark:** Light that enters the room, is reflected from internal surfaces like walls, ceiling, and floor, and ultimately reaches the working plane. IRC becomes critical in deep rooms where direct sky visibility is lost.

$$DF_{total} = SC + ERC + IRC$$

---

### Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

**Q3.A. [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 2]**

A classroom measures **8 m × 6 m × 3.2 m** (length × width × height). A clear-glass window of size **3 m × 1.8 m** is provided on the longer wall, with sill at 0.9 m and lintel at 2.7 m from the floor. The room surfaces have an average reflectance of 0.5, and the glazing transmittance is 0.85. The external ground reflectance is 0.2 and there are no major external obstructions.

**Calculate:**
**(a) (7 marks)** The Sky Component (SC) and Externally Reflected Component (ERC) at a point on the working plane located **2 m** from the window wall, on the centerline of the room.

**(b) (7 marks)** The Internally Reflected Component (IRC) and the **Total Daylight Factor** at that point. Comment on whether the room meets the **recommended DF for classrooms (≥ 5%)**.

---

#### Model Solution:

##### Part (a) — Sky Component and ERC Calculation

**Given data:**
- Room: L = 8 m, W = 6 m, H = 3.2 m
- Window: width = 3 m, height = 1.8 m
- Sill height = 0.9 m, Lintel height = 2.7 m
- Working plane height = 0.8 m (assumed)
- Point P distance from window = 2 m (on centerline)
- $\tau = 0.85$, $\bar{\rho} = 0.5$, $R_{ext} = 0.2$
- Outdoor illuminance $E_o = 10{,}000$ lux

**Step 1: Compute effective window geometry as seen from Point P**

The eye height of the observer at point P (working plane height) = 0.8 m.

**Vertical angles from point P to window edges:**

- Angle to top of window (lintel at 2.7 m):
  
$$\alpha_{top} = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{2.7 - 0.8}{2.0}\right) = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{1.9}{2.0}\right) = \tan^{-1}(0.95) = 43.5°$$

- Angle to bottom of window (sill at 0.9 m):
  
$$\alpha_{bottom} = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{0.9 - 0.8}{2.0}\right) = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{0.1}{2.0}\right) = \tan^{-1}(0.05) = 2.86°$$

- Vertical angle subtended by window:
  
$$\Delta\alpha = \alpha_{top} - \alpha_{bottom} = 43.5° - 2.86° = 40.64°$$

**Step 2: Compute horizontal angles from point P to window edges**

The point P is on the centerline of the room, 3 m from each side wall. The window is 3 m wide, centered on the wall.

**Horizontal angle subtended by the window from point P:**

The window extends 1.5 m to either side of the centerline. The horizontal distance from P to the window is 2 m.

$$\beta = 2 \times \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{1.5}{2.0}\right) = 2 \times \tan^{-1}(0.75) = 2 \times 36.87° = 73.74°$$

**Step 3: Compute solid angle subtended by the window (approximate)**

$$\omega = \Delta\alpha \times \beta \text{ (in radians)}$$

Converting to radians: $\Delta\alpha = 0.709$ rad, $\beta = 1.287$ rad.

$$\omega = 0.709 \times 1.287 = 0.912 \text{ sr}$$

**Step 4: Compute Sky Component using average luminance approximation**

For an average altitude angle $\bar{\phi}$ corresponding to the window:

$$\bar{\phi} = \frac{\alpha_{top} + \alpha_{bottom}}{2} = \frac{43.5° + 2.86°}{2} = 23.18°$$

Average sky luminance:

$$L_{\bar{\phi}} = L_z \cdot \frac{1 + 2 \sin(23.18°)}{3} = L_z \cdot \frac{1 + 0.787}{3} = 0.596 \, L_z$$

**Step 5: Calculate SC**

$$E_{i,SC} = \tau \cdot L_{\bar{\phi}} \cdot \omega \cdot \sin(\bar{\phi})$$

$$E_{i,SC} = 0.85 \times 0.596 \, L_z \times 0.912 \times \sin(23.18°)$$

$$E_{i,SC} = 0.85 \times 0.596 \times 0.912 \times 0.394 \times L_z = 0.182 \, L_z$$

Using $E_o = 7\pi L_z / 9$:

$$SC = \frac{E_{i,SC}}{E_o} \times 100 = \frac{0.182 \, L_z}{7\pi L_z/9} \times 100$$

$$SC = \frac{0.182 \times 9}{7 \times 3.1416} \times 100 = \frac{1.638}{21.99} \times 100 = 7.45\%$$

**Step 6: Compute ERC**

For an unobstructed view (or minimal external obstruction with $R_{ext} = 0.2$):

$$ERC \approx SC \times R_{ext} \times \frac{A_{ground,visible}}{A_{window}}$$

For a flat ground view, approximate ratio = 0.5 (geometric simplification):

$$ERC = 7.45 \times 0.2 \times 0.5 = 0.745\%$$

##### Part (b) — IRC and Total DF Calculation

**Step 7: Compute IRC**

Window area:
$$A_w = 3 \times 1.8 = 5.4 \text{ m}^2$$

Total internal surface area:

$$A_t = 2(LW + LH + WH) = 2(8 \times 6 + 8 \times 3.2 + 6 \times 3.2)$$

$$A_t = 2(48 + 25.6 + 19.2) = 2 \times 92.8 = 185.6 \text{ m}^2$$

Average reflectance: $\bar{\rho} = 0.5$

$$IRC = \frac{SC \times \tau \times A_w \times \bar{\rho}}{A_t \times (1 - \bar{\rho})}$$

$$IRC = \frac{7.45 \times 0.85 \times 5.4 \times 0.5}{185.6 \times (1 - 0.5)} = \frac{17.10}{92.8} = 0.184\%$$

Wait — re-deriving with proper inter-reflection formula:

$$IRC = \frac{SC \times \tau \times A_w \times \bar{\rho}}{A_t \times (1 - \bar{\rho})} = \frac{7.45 \times 0.85 \times 5.4 \times 0.5}{185.6 \times 0.5}$$

$$IRC = \frac{17.10}{92.8} = 0.184\%$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Many students apply the IRC formula in decimal form (e.g., 0.0745) instead of percentage (7.45%). Always keep units consistent — if SC is in %, IRC comes out in % correctly. Mixing decimal and percentage is the **#1 mistake** in DF numericals and costs 2-3 marks per question.

**Step 8: Total Daylight Factor**

$$DF_{total} = SC + ERC + IRC = 7.45\% + 0.745\% + 0.184\% = 8.38\%$$

**Step 9: Compliance check**

Recommended DF for classroom ≥ 5%. Since $DF_{total} = 8.38\% > 5\%$, **the room meets the recommended daylight factor** with a comfortable margin.

**Conclusion:** The classroom has adequate daylighting. The high SC indicates that 2 m from the window is in the well-lit zone; in deeper parts of the room (e.g., 5-6 m from window), the SC would drop significantly and IRC would dominate.

---

**Q3.B. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 2 — Internal Choice Alternative]**

A drawing office has plan dimensions of **10 m × 5 m** and a clear height of **3.5 m**. It has three windows, each of size **2 m × 1.5 m**, on the longer wall, with sill at 1.0 m and lintel at 2.5 m. The room has white-painted walls (reflectance 0.7) and a white ceiling (reflectance 0.8). Floor reflectance is 0.3. Glazing transmittance is 0.8. The room depth (perpendicular to window wall) is 5 m.

**Calculate:**
**(a) (7 marks)** The Total Glazing Ratio (TCR) and Average Room Reflectance ($\bar{\rho}$). State whether the TCR is within recommended limits.

**(b) (7 marks)** The approximate Total Daylight Factor at a point **2.5 m** from the window wall (centerline) using the simplified formula:

$$DF \approx \frac{SC + ERC + IRC}{E_o} \times 100$$

where $SC = 4.5\%$, $ERC = 0.8\%$, and $IRC$ is to be calculated. Comment on its suitability for **detailed drawing work** (recommended DF ≥ 6%).

---

#### Model Solution:

##### Part (a) — TCR and Average Room Reflectance

**Step 1: Total Glazing Ratio**

$$A_w = 3 \times (2 \times 1.5) = 9 \text{ m}^2$$

$$A_f = 10 \times 5 = 50 \text{ m}^2$$

$$TCR = \frac{A_w}{A_f} = \frac{9}{50} = 0.18 = 18\%$$

**Step 2: Average Room Reflectance**

Internal surface areas:
- Floor: $10 \times 5 = 50 \text{ m}^2$, $\rho_f = 0.3$
- Ceiling: $10 \times 5 = 50 \text{ m}^2$, $\rho_c = 0.8$
- Long walls (without windows): $2 \times (5 \times 3.5) = 35 \text{ m}^2$, $\rho_w = 0.7$
- Window wall (gross): $5 \times 3.5 = 17.5 \text{ m}^2$, with windows occupying $9 \text{ m}^2$, wall portion = $17.5 - 9 = 8.5 \text{ m}^2$, $\rho_w = 0.7$

Total internal surface area:
$$A_t = 50 + 50 + 35 + 8.5 = 143.5 \text{ m}^2$$

Weighted average reflectance:
$$\bar{\rho} = \frac{(50 \times 0.3) + (50 \times 0.8) + (35 \times 0.7) + (8.5 \times 0.7)}{143.5}$$

$$\bar{\rho} = \frac{15 + 40 + 24.5 + 5.95}{143.5} = \frac{85.45}{143.5} = 0.595$$

**TCR = 18%, which is within the recommended range of 15% to 25% for daylight-optimized buildings.**

##### Part (b) — Total Daylight Factor Calculation

**Step 3: Calculate IRC**

Using the inter-reflection formula:

$$IRC = \frac{SC \times \tau \times A_w \times \bar{\rho}}{A_t \times (1 - \bar{\rho})}$$

$$IRC = \frac{4.5 \times 0.8 \times 9 \times 0.595}{143.5 \times (1 - 0.595)}$$

$$IRC = \frac{4.5 \times 0.8 \times 9 \times 0.595}{143.5 \times 0.405} = \frac{19.278}{58.12} = 0.332\%$$

**Step 4: Total Daylight Factor**

$$DF_{total} = SC + ERC + IRC = 4.5\% + 0.8\% + 0.332\% = 5.63\%$$

**Step 5: Compliance check**

Recommended DF for detailed drawing office = 6.0%. Since $DF_{total} = 5.63\% < 6.0\%$, the room **falls slightly short** of the recommended value.

**Step 6: Recommendations**

- Increase window head height (raise lintel from 2.5 m to 3.0 m) to boost SC.
- Add a clerestory window on the opposite wall to increase SC and IRC in deep zones.
- Increase total window area (raise TCR to ~22-25%) by widening windows.
- Maintain ceiling whiteness to maximize IRC.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mistakes on This Type of Question:**
> 1. **Forgetting to subtract window area** from the window wall when computing internal surface area. Cost: 1-2 marks.
> 2. **Confusing the "long wall" and "short wall"** in the room dimensions. Always check which wall has the windows.
> 3. **Not converting** SC decimal vs. percentage. SC = 4.5% means use 4.5, NOT 0.045, in the IRC formula when you want IRC as a percentage.
> 4. **Missing the comment** on compliance — examiners specifically look for the **interpretation statement** comparing DF with code requirements (often worth 1-2 marks).
> 5. **Using reflectance as decimal** (0.3) in some parts and percentage (30%) in others — pick one convention and stay consistent.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU Board Exam:**

- **DF is a ratio, expressed as a percentage**, comparing indoor to outdoor horizontal illuminance under CIE overcast sky.
- **Standard outdoor illuminance** under CIE overcast sky ≈ **10,000 lux** (memorize this constant).
- **Three components:** $DF = SC + ERC + IRC$ — memorize the full expansion.
- **SC** dominates near the window; **IRC** dominates in deep zones.
- **CIE Overcast Sky luminance** formula: $L_{\phi} = L_z \cdot (1 + 2\sin\phi)/3$ — zenith:horizon ratio is **3:1**.
- **Recommended DF values:** Residential 2%, Classrooms 5%, Offices 4-6%, Drawing rooms 6-10%, Hospital wards 2%.
- **Glazing transmittance** $\tau$ typically: single clear glass 0.85–0.90, double glass 0.70–0.75, tinted 0.50–0.70.
- **TCR (Glazing-to-Floor ratio)** recommended range: **15%–25%** for daylight-optimized buildings.
- **DF depends only on geometry, glazing, and reflectance** — NOT on weather or time of day.
- **Critical room depth** = approximately **2.5 times the window head height** (from working plane). Beyond this, SC drops sharply.
- **Inter-reflection formula:** $IRC = (SC \cdot \tau \cdot A_w \cdot \bar{\rho}) / [A_t (1 - \bar{\rho})]$.
- **Average room reflectance** = area-weighted average of all internal surfaces — do NOT use simple arithmetic mean.
- **BRS Daylight Protractor** and **Waldram Diagram** are the two classical graphical methods for SC calculation.
- **Increasing ceiling reflectance** is the most cost-effective way to boost IRC in deep rooms.
- **BIS/NBC recommended minimum DF** is the benchmark used in KTU numerical problems — always state the reference standard in your final answer.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET415 - ARCHITECTURAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Recommended Daylight Factors for Interiors, Design of Side-Lit Windows Using Daylight Factor Graphs, Artificial Lighting: Illumination Requirements, Lux Meter, Lamps and Luminaires, Polar Distribution Curves, Design of Artificial Lighting – Lumen Method and Point-by-Point Method

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Daylight Factor (DF) — Foundational Concept

**Daylight Factor (DF)** is the internationally accepted metric defined as the ratio of the **illumination level at a point inside a room** to the **simultaneous illumination on a horizontal plane outdoors**, under overcast sky conditions, expressed as a percentage.

$$DF (\%) = \frac{E_{\text{indoor}}}{E_{\text{outdoor}}} \times 100$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (verbatim standard):** "Daylight factor at a point on the working plane is the ratio of illumination at that point due to all the daylight received directly or indirectly at that point to the illumination on a horizontal plane exposed to an unobstructed hemisphere of the standard CIE overcast sky, expressed as a percentage."
> 
> This definition is highly valued by KTU examiners and forms the basis of almost every Module 2 question on daylighting.

### The Three Components of Daylight Factor

The total Daylight Factor is the **linear superposition** of three independent components:

$$DF_{\text{total}} = DF_{\text{sky component}} + DF_{\text{externally reflected component}} + DF_{\text{internally reflected component}}$$

| Component | Symbol | Physical Meaning | Typical Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| Sky Component (SC) | DF_sky | Direct light from sky through window | 0.1 % – 8 % |
| Externally Reflected Component (ERC) | DF_ERC | Light reflected from ground/external surfaces into window | 0.1 % – 1.5 % |
| Internally Reflected Component (IRC) | DF_IRC | Light from internal walls, ceiling, floor | 0.5 % – 4 % |

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Umbrella" Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine standing under an open umbrella on a cloudy day. The daylight that actually reaches your face (indoor illumination) is *much less* than the bright overcast sky (outdoor illumination) because the umbrella blocks most of the sky dome. The Daylight Factor is essentially the "transparency ratio" of your umbrella-window system. A bigger window = bigger opening in the umbrella = higher DF. Hence a 2 % DF means your room gets only **2 paise worth of daylight for every 1 rupee of sky brightness** — a very intuitive way to remember it!

---

## 1.2 Recommended Daylight Factors for Interiors (KTU Standard Values)

The **Recommended Daylight Factor** for any interior is dictated by the **visual task** being performed. KTU follows the **BIS (Bureau of Indian Standards, IS 3646)** and the **Code of Practice for Daylighting (IS 7666)** recommendations.

| Type of Room / Activity | Recommended Min DF (%) | Recommended Min DF (lux eq.) |
|---|---|---|
| Drawing offices, fine workshops | 5.0 | 500 |
| Offices, classrooms, kitchens, living rooms | 2.0 | 200 |
| Stairs, corridors, passages, stores | 1.0 | 100 |
| General industrial work (coarse) | 1.0 – 2.0 | 100 – 200 |
| Warehouses, bulky goods storage | 0.5 | 50 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Mnemonic Trick:** "**5-2-1**" — *Fine work = 5 %, Ordinary work = 2 %, Circulation = 1 %*. This single sequence has appeared in multiple KTU board papers as a 3-mark direct question. Memorize it cold.

### The "Minimum Target" Rule

The recommended DF is a **minimum** value. A good designer will always try to achieve values **higher than the minimum**, with a working ideal of around **2 × minimum**, because:
- The CIE overcast sky produces minimum outdoor illumination of around **10,000 lux** (CIE standard assumption).
- A 2 % DF therefore yields 200 lux indoors, which is the borderline for office work.
- Dull days, external obstructions, and dirty glazing all reduce actual daylight, so design must compensate.

---

## 1.3 Lux Meter — The Daylight & Artificial Light Measuring Instrument

A **Lux meter** (also called a **light meter** or **illumination photometer**) is a handheld instrument used to measure the illumination level at a point in **lux (lx) = lumens/m²**.

**Functional Block Architecture of a Lux Meter:**

The lux meter converts incident light into a measurable signal using a **photocell** (either a **selenium photovoltaic cell** in older analog units or a **silicon photodiode** in modern digital units). A built-in **cosine-correction filter** ensures the cell responds correctly to light striking it from oblique angles (Lambert's cosine law).

> [!NOTE]
> **The Cosine-Lambert Connection:** A flat sensor receiving light at 60° from normal should read exactly **half** the value it reads at 0° (normal incidence), because the projected area is cos(60°) = 0.5. The cosine filter inside the lux meter enforces this physically. **This is a high-yield KTU point** — examiners love asking why a cosine correction is needed.

---

## 1.4 Lamps and Luminaires

A **Lamp** is the **light source** (the actual bulb/tube/LED chip). A **Luminaire** is the **complete lighting fixture** — lamp + housing + reflector + diffuser + control gear.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** A lamp is the "engine" of a car; a luminaire is the "entire car" with body, headlights, steering, etc. Two identical lamps in different luminaires give completely different lighting distributions.

### Classification of Lamps (KTU Syllabus Focus)

| Lamp Family | Examples | Efficacy (lm/W) | Life (hrs) | KTU Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Incandescent** | GLS, Tungsten Halogen | 10 – 25 | 1,000 | Domestic, decorative |
| **Fluorescent** | T5, T8, CFL, Induction | 50 – 100 | 8,000 – 20,000 | Offices, classrooms |
| **High-Intensity Discharge (HID)** | Mercury Vapour, Sodium Vapour, Metal Halide | 80 – 150 | 15,000 – 25,000 | Street lighting, factories |
| **LED** | SMD, COB LED | 100 – 200 | 30,000 – 50,000+ | Modern universal application |

---

## 1.5 Polar Distribution Curves (The Photometric "Fingerprint")

A **Polar Distribution Curve** is a polar plot showing how the **candlepower (intensity, in candelas)** of a luminaire varies with the **angle of emission** measured from the nadir (straight down) or from the axis of the lamp.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition (examination-ready):** "A polar curve of a luminaire is a curve, plotted in polar coordinates, showing the variation of luminous intensity in a vertical plane with the angle measured from the downward vertical."
> 
> The shape of this curve completely defines the optical "personality" of the luminaire. A "batwing" luminaire throws light sideways; a "downward" luminaire throws light straight down; a "symmetrical" luminaire throws light uniformly.

### Common Polar Curve Types

1. **Direct luminaire** — 90 %–100 % of light downward (most efficient)
2. **Semi-direct** — 60 %–90 % downward
3. **General diffusing** — 40 %–60 % each way
4. **Semi-indirect** — 10 %–40 % downward
5. **Indirect** — 0 %–10 % downward (most aesthetic, least efficient)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Daylight Factor Formula — Derivation of Components

### Sky Component (SC)

The Sky Component at a point P inside a room is defined as:

$$DF_{\text{sky}} = \frac{\theta}{180} \times \frac{A_w \times \tau}{A_p \times (1 - r_m^2)}$$

Where:
- $\theta$ = the **vertical angle subtended** by the visible sky at the reference point P (in degrees)
- $A_w$ = net glazed area of the window (m²)
- $A_p$ = total area of working plane (m²)
- $\tau$ = transmittance of glazing
- $r_m$ = average reflectance of internal room surfaces

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Meaning of Each Term:** $\theta/180$ represents what fraction of the sky hemisphere is visible through the window (since 180° = full hemisphere in vertical angular terms). $A_w/A_p$ is the geometric "window-to-floor" ratio. $\tau$ accounts for glass losses. The $(1 - r_m^2)$ term is the **room-surface multiplier** that accounts for the secondary bounce of light off walls and ceiling.

### Externally Reflected Component (ERC)

Light reflected from external ground/obstructions that enters through the window:

$$DF_{\text{ERC}} = \frac{\theta_g \times R_g \times A_w \times \tau}{180 \times A_p \times (1 - r_m^2)}$$

Where $\theta_g$ is the **vertical angle subtended by the external reflecting surface** (typically the ground) and $R_g$ is its reflectance.

### Internally Reflected Component (IRC)

The IRC is the trickiest component because it depends on the **inter-reflections of light between all internal surfaces**:

$$DF_{\text{IRC}} = \frac{R_w \times A_w \times \tau}{A_{\text{total}} \times (1 - r_m)} \times 100$$

Where $R_w$ is the average wall reflectance and $A_{\text{total}}$ is the total internal surface area (walls + ceiling + floor).

---

## 2.2 The BRS Daylight Factor Graphs (Lynes / BRS Method)

The **Building Research Station (BRS) Daylight Factor Graphs** (developed at the UK's BRS, now BRE) are the standard tool used to determine the required **window-to-floor area ratio** for a given DF target.

### Three Standard BRS Charts

1. **Chart I** — for the calculation of the **Sky Component (SC)**
2. **Chart II** — for the **Internally Reflected Component (IRC)**
3. **Chart III** — for the **Externally Reflected Component (ERC)**

### Step-by-Step Logic of the BRS Method

**Why use the BRS graphs?** Because calculating $\theta$ for a side-lit window is mathematically complex. The BRS charts pre-compute these angles for standard room geometries.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:** The BRS method assumes the working plane is at **0.85 m** above the floor. Any calculation at a different height requires correction. This is a common "trap" question in KTU board exams.

---

## 2.3 Design of Side-Lit Windows — The BRS Procedure

The standard procedure to design a side-lit window for a given target DF:

**Step 1:** Determine the target DF based on the room use (e.g., 2 % for a classroom).

**Step 2:** Estimate the IRC from Chart II for an assumed window size.

**Step 3:** Estimate the ERC from Chart III based on ground reflectance and external obstruction angle.

**Step 4:** Calculate the required Sky Component:

$$DF_{\text{sky required}} = DF_{\text{target}} - DF_{\text{IRC}} - DF_{\text{ERC}}$$

**Step 5:** Use Chart I to determine the required **window-to-floor area ratio** and **window-to-ceiling height ratio** that delivers the required SC.

**Step 6:** Iterate if the window size is unreasonable (i.e., larger than 25 % of floor area is bad practice).

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Daylighting

| # | Formula | Variables | Unit | Validity |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $DF = \dfrac{E_{\text{in}}}{E_{\text{out}}} \times 100$ | E_in, E_out | lux | Universal |
| 2 | $DF_{\text{total}} = SC + ERC + IRC$ | — | % | Linear addition |
| 3 | $SC = \dfrac{\theta \cdot A_w \cdot \tau}{180 \cdot A_p (1 - r_m^2)}$ | $\theta$, $A_w$, $A_p$, $\tau$, $r_m$ | % | BRS method |
| 4 | $E_{\text{indoor}} = DF \times 10,000 / 100$ | DF | lux | CIE overcast |
| 5 | $\text{WFR} = A_w / A_p$ | $A_w$, $A_p$ | ratio | Practical design |
| 6 | $h_{\text{header}} = 2/3 \times (H_{\text{room}} - h_w)$ | Header height | m | Good design rule |

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule of Thumb — "Two-Third Header":** The bottom of the window head should be at **2/3 of the wall height above the floor** for optimum light penetration. This is universally tested in KTU viva voce.

---

## 2.5 Artificial Lighting — The Five Foundational Quantities

| Quantity | Symbol | Unit | Definition | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Luminous Flux | $\Phi$ | **lumen (lm)** | Total light energy emitted per second weighted by human eye response | "Quantity" of light |
| Luminous Intensity | $I$ | **candela (cd)** | Luminous flux per unit solid angle in a given direction | "Directional strength" |
| Illumination / Illuminance | $E$ | **lux (lx)** = lm/m² | Luminous flux incident on a unit area | "Light on a surface" |
| Luminance | $L$ | **cd/m²** | Intensity per unit projected area of a source/surface | "Brightness" as seen by eye |
| Luminous Efficacy | $\eta$ | **lm/W** | Lumen output per Watt of input | "Efficiency" of lamp |

### The Inverse Square Law (KTU Favourite)

$$E = \frac{I}{d^2}$$

Where $E$ is the illumination on a surface **perpendicular to the light ray**, $I$ is the intensity of the source in the direction of incidence, and $d$ is the distance from the source to the surface.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Cosine Law of Illumination (Lambert's Law):** When the light ray strikes a surface at an angle $\theta$ from the normal, the illumination becomes:
> 
> $$E = \frac{I \cos^3 \theta}{h^2}$$
> 
> This is the **fundamental** KTU formula. The $\cos^3$ appears because: (1) one $\cos\theta$ from projected area, (2) one $\cos\theta$ from the inverse square law, and (3) one $\cos\theta$ from the direction of intensity measured vertically. **Examiners have asked students to derive this $\cos^3$ — learn the derivation!**

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet — Artificial Lighting Design

| # | Formula | Variables | Unit | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E = I / d^2$ | $E$, $I$, $d$ | lx, cd, m | Inverse Square Law |
| 2 | $E = \dfrac{I \cos^3 \theta}{h^2}$ | $E$, $I$, $h$, $\theta$ | lx, cd, m, ° | Point-by-Point Method |
| 3 | $N = \dfrac{E \times A}{\Phi \times \text{CU} \times \text{MF}}$ | $N$, $E$, $A$, $\Phi$ | — | Lumen Method (Number of lamps) |
| 4 | $\text{CU} = \eta \times \rho_{\text{cc}} \times \rho_{\text{w}}$ | CU, $\eta$, $\rho$ | — | Coefficient of Utilization |
| 5 | $\text{MF} = \text{LLF} = \text{AL} \times \text{RSMF} \times \text{LMF} \times \text{LSF}$ | — | — | Maintenance Factor |
| 6 | $L = I / A_{\text{proj}}$ | $L$, $I$, $A$ | cd/m², cd, m² | Luminance |
| 7 | $L = \rho \times E / \pi$ | $L$, $\rho$, $E$ | cd/m², —, lx | Lambertian surface |

### The Maintenance Factor (MF) Components

$$\text{MF} = \text{AL} \times \text{RSMF} \times \text{LMF} \times \text{LSF}$$

Where:
- **AL** = Ageing factor (lamp lumen depreciation over time)
- **RSMF** = Room Surface Maintenance Factor (dirt on walls/ceiling)
- **LMF** = Luminaire Maintenance Factor (dirt on luminaire)
- **LSF** = Lamp Survival Factor (fraction of lamps still working)

> [!NOTE]
> **Typical MF Values for KTU:** Clean office = 0.8, Average office = 0.7, Industrial dusty = 0.5. The **default 0.8** is what 80 % of KTU problems use unless otherwise stated.

---

## 2.7 The Two Design Methods — Head-to-Head Comparison

| Aspect | Lumen Method (Flux Method) | Point-by-Point Method |
|---|---|---|
| **Approach** | Calculates total lumens needed to achieve average E | Calculates E at specific points |
| **Best For** | Whole-room average, uniform lighting | Non-uniform lighting, critical tasks, spotlight design |
| **Output** | Number of luminaires + layout | Illumination at specific points |
| **Equation Used** | $N = \dfrac{E A}{\Phi \times \text{CU} \times \text{MF}}$ | $E = \dfrac{I \cos^3 \theta}{h^2}$ |
| **Time** | Quick (used by lighting engineers daily) | Slow, used for verification |
| **KTU Marks** | Usually 7 marks in Part B | Usually 7 marks in Part B |
| **Key Input** | Room index, CU from tables | Photometric data, polar curve |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall — Don't Mix the Two:** Many students use the Lumen Method formula to find N, then forget to multiply by 2 if the answer comes as a single luminaire and the question asks for the total. **Always re-read the question wording** to confirm whether N is number of lamps or number of luminaires.

---

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Sustainable building design:** Daylight design reduces electrical lighting load by 40 %–60 %, contributing to GRIHA/LEED ratings.
- **Energy codes (ECBC in India):** Mandate minimum daylight in habitable rooms.
- **Healthcare and education:** Inadequate daylight is linked to myopia in children (Taiwan, Singapore studies) and depression in office workers.
- **Industrial safety:** In factories, correct artificial lighting reduces accidents by up to 30 % (BIS statistics).
- **Smart city street lighting:** Uses the Lumen Method to design the optimal pole spacing and luminaire wattage.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Cosine Cubed Law of Illumination

> **Starting from the Inverse Square Law:** When a point source of intensity $I$ (in candelas) is at distance $d$ (in metres) from a surface **perpendicular** to the direction of light, the illumination is:
> 
> $$E_n = \frac{I}{d^2}$$
> 
> where $E_n$ is the **normal** illumination in lux.

**Step 1:** Now suppose the surface is **tilted** by an angle $\theta$ from the perpendicular to the light ray. The actual distance from the source to the surface is still $d$, but the light ray now strikes at angle $\theta$ to the normal of the surface.

**Step 2:** The illumination on the tilted surface is the **flux per unit area on that tilted surface**. For a small area $A$ on the tilted plane, the flux received is the same (since flux conservation), but the effective area of the tilted surface is $A / \cos\theta$ when projected perpendicular to the light ray.

**Step 3:** Therefore:

$$E = \frac{\Phi}{A / \cos\theta} = \frac{\Phi \cos\theta}{A} = E_n \cos\theta$$

**Step 4:** Substitute $E_n = I / d^2$:

$$E = \frac{I \cos\theta}{d^2}$$

**Step 5:** Express $d$ in terms of the **vertical mounting height** $h$. By geometry, $d = h / \cos\theta$ (the lamp is at height $h$ above the working plane, and the light ray travels the hypotenuse to the working point).

**Step 6:** Substitute $d$ into the equation:

$$E = \frac{I \cos\theta}{(h / \cos\theta)^2} = \frac{I \cos\theta \times \cos^2\theta}{h^2}$$

**Step 7:** Simplify to get the **cosine cubed law**:

$$\boxed{E = \frac{I \cos^3 \theta}{h^2}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The three cosines have three different origins:**
> 1. First $\cos\theta$ — Lambert's cosine (tilted area)
> 2. Second $\cos\theta$ — appears in inverse square law via $d^2 = h^2 / \cos^2\theta$
> 3. Third $\cos\theta$ — intensity component if the source is at an angle, but for a vertical source $I$ is independent of $\theta$ in the symmetric case.
> 
> **KTU examiners love this derivation** — memorize the steps and the **physical meaning of each cosine**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Lumen Method Formula

**Starting from the definition of average illumination:**

$$E_{\text{avg}} = \frac{\text{Total Flux reaching working plane}}{\text{Working plane area}} = \frac{\Phi_{\text{working}}}{\text{Area}}$$

**Step 1:** Total lumens emitted by all lamps = $N \times \Phi_{\text{lamp}}$ (where $N$ is the total number of lamps and $\Phi_{\text{lamp}}$ is the lumens per lamp).

**Step 2:** The **Coefficient of Utilization (CU)** is the fraction of lamp lumens that actually reach the working plane after accounting for luminaire efficiency, room geometry, and surface reflectances. So:

$$\Phi_{\text{working}} = N \times \Phi_{\text{lamp}} \times \text{CU}$$

**Step 3:** As the luminaire ages and gets dusty, only a fraction of its initial output is delivered. This is the **Maintenance Factor (MF)**:

$$\Phi_{\text{working (realistic)}} = N \times \Phi_{\text{lamp}} \times \text{CU} \times \text{MF}$$

**Step 4:** Setting this equal to the average illumination multiplied by the area:

$$E \times A = N \times \Phi \times \text{CU} \times \text{MF}$$

**Step 5:** Solve for $N$:

$$\boxed{N = \frac{E \times A}{\Phi \times \text{CU} \times \text{MF}}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why This Works for Practical Design:** Lighting catalogues (e.g., Philips, Crompton, Havells) provide ready-made $\Phi$ and CU values. This means an architect can design lighting with a calculator in 5 minutes per room.

---

## 3.3 Detailed Worked Example — Lumen Method (Full BRS)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Question Pattern Model]**
> 
> *An office room 12 m × 8 m × 3.5 m (ceiling height) is to be illuminated to 300 lux. The luminaires have a CU of 0.6, MF = 0.8, and each lamp has 4000 lumens output. Calculate the number of lamps and the layout. Assume mounting height = 2.7 m above working plane, reflectance of ceiling = 0.7, walls = 0.5, working plane = 0.2.*

**Step 1: Identify the given data.**

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Room dimensions L × W × H | 12 m × 8 m × 3.5 m |
| Required illumination E | 300 lx |
| Coefficient of Utilization CU | 0.6 |
| Maintenance Factor MF | 0.8 |
| Lumen output per lamp $\Phi$ | 4000 lm |
| Mounting height $h$ | 2.7 m |
| Reflectances | Ceiling 0.7, Walls 0.5, Floor 0.2 |

**Step 2: Calculate the working plane area.**

$$A = L \times W = 12 \times 8 = 96 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 3: Apply the Lumen Method formula.**

$$N = \frac{E \times A}{\Phi \times \text{CU} \times \text{MF}}$$

$$N = \frac{300 \times 96}{4000 \times 0.6 \times 0.8}$$

$$N = \frac{28{,}800}{1920}$$

$$N = 15 \text{ lamps}$$

**Step 4: Layout planning.**

For uniform distribution, use a **square grid layout**. Number of rows and columns approximately:

$$n_{\text{rows}} = \sqrt{N \times \frac{L}{W}} = \sqrt{15 \times \frac{12}{8}} = \sqrt{22.5} \approx 4.74$$

$$n_{\text{columns}} = N / n_{\text{rows}} = 15 / 4.74 \approx 3.16$$

Round to **4 rows × 4 columns = 16 lamps** (using a 4 × 4 layout slightly over-designs by 6.7 %, which is acceptable in practice).

**Step 5: Final spacing.**

- Row spacing = 12 / 4 = 3.0 m
- Column spacing = 8 / 4 = 2.0 m
- Maximum spacing for office work = **1.5 × mounting height = 1.5 × 2.7 = 4.05 m** → Both 3.0 m and 2.0 m are well within limit.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Note:** The "Spacing-to-Mounting-Height Ratio" (SHR) rule is a critical check. **Always verify the spacing rule** before finalizing your design. If violated, re-design with more luminaires.

---

## 3.4 Detailed Worked Example — Point-by-Point Method

> **[Model Question]**
> 
> *A 1000-candela lamp is mounted at a height of 5 m above the working plane. Find the illumination on the working plane at a point 4 m away from the foot of the lamp.*

**Step 1: Visualize the geometry.**

- Mounting height $h = 5$ m
- Horizontal distance $x = 4$ m
- Hypotenuse distance $d = \sqrt{h^2 + x^2} = \sqrt{25 + 16} = \sqrt{41}$ m
- Angle $\theta = \tan^{-1}(x/h) = \tan^{-1}(4/5) = 38.66°$

**Step 2: Apply the Cosine Cubed formula.**

$$E = \frac{I \cos^3 \theta}{h^2}$$

$$\cos(38.66°) = 0.781 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \cos^3(38.66°) = (0.781)^3 = 0.476$$

**Step 3: Compute E.**

$$E = \frac{1000 \times 0.476}{25} = \frac{476}{25} = 19.04 \text{ lux}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Quick Verification via Inverse Square + Cosine:** 
> $E = (I / d^2) \cos\theta = (1000 / 41) \times 0.781 = 24.39 \times 0.781 = 19.05$ lux. ✓ **Matches!** The two formulas are mathematically equivalent.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Decision Tree for Lighting Design

The following decision matrix helps determine which method to use based on the problem context:

| Question Asks For... | Method to Use | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Number of luminaires for whole room | Lumen Method | Quick, gives total number |
| Verification of uniformity | Point-by-Point | Checks E at multiple points |
| Average illumination level | Lumen Method | Statistical average |
| Illumination under a single source | Point-by-Point | Single source analysis |
| Critical task workplane design | Both | Lumen for sizing, P-by-P for verification |
| Stadium or street lighting | Point-by-Point | Non-uniform layout needed |
| Office / classroom layout | Lumen Method | Standard uniform task |
| Coefficient of Utilization given | Lumen Method | Direct input required |
| Polar curve given | Point-by-Point | I varies with direction |

---

## 3.6 Worked Example — Daylight Factor Side-Lit Window Design

> **[Model Question]**
> 
> *Design a side-lit window for a classroom 8 m × 6 m × 3.2 m high. The working plane is 0.85 m above the floor. The window sill is at 1.0 m and the header at 2.8 m above the floor. Target DF = 2.5 % (fine classwork). External ground reflectance = 0.15. Glazing transmittance = 0.8. Internal room reflectances: ceiling 0.7, walls 0.5, floor 0.2.*

**Step 1: Room geometry.**

- Wall length on which the window sits = 8 m (assumed).
- Window height = 2.8 - 1.0 = 1.8 m.
- Working plane height above sill = 0.85 - 1.0 = -0.15 m (working plane is **below** the sill by 0.15 m).
- Critical point: take it at the **back of the room**, 6 m from the window wall.
- Reference point vertical height above working plane = 3.2 - 0.85 = 2.35 m.

**Step 2: Compute average room reflectance.**

$$r_m = \frac{A_{\text{ceiling}} \rho_c + A_{\text{walls}} \rho_w + A_{\text{floor}} \rho_f}{A_{\text{ceiling}} + A_{\text{walls}} + A_{\text{floor}}}$$

- Ceiling = 8 × 6 = 48 m²
- Floor = 48 m²
- Walls = 2 × (8 + 6) × 3.2 = 89.6 m²
- Total = 48 + 48 + 89.6 = 185.6 m²

$$r_m = \frac{(48)(0.7) + (89.6)(0.5) + (48)(0.2)}{185.6} = \frac{33.6 + 44.8 + 9.6}{185.6} = \frac{88}{185.6} = 0.474$$

**Step 3: BRS Chart I — Determine window-to-floor area ratio for SC.**

For the given geometry, the BRS chart gives the SC for various WFRs. Iterate:

- Try WFR = 0.20 → $A_w$ = 0.20 × 48 = 9.6 m².
- From BRS Chart I (assuming a typical reading at this geometry): SC ≈ 1.5 %

**Step 4: BRS Chart II — Determine IRC.**

For WFR = 0.20 and the given reflectances, the IRC from the BRS chart ≈ 0.8 %.

**Step 5: BRS Chart III — Determine ERC.**

For external ground reflectance 0.15 and no external obstruction, ERC ≈ 0.2 %.

**Step 6: Total DF for WFR = 0.20.**

$$DF_{\text{total}} = SC + ERC + IRC = 1.5 + 0.2 + 0.8 = 2.5 \text{ %} \quad \checkmark$$

**The WFR = 0.20 (i.e., 20 % of floor area) is adequate.**

**Step 7: Window dimensions.**

$$A_w = 0.20 \times 48 = 9.6 \text{ m}^2$$

If window is 1.8 m high: width = 9.6 / 1.8 = 5.33 m. But wall is only 8 m long, so this fits comfortably.

**Recommended:** Use **5 windows of 1.0 m × 1.8 m each** placed evenly on the 8 m wall (spacing ≈ 0.67 m between units). This is more architecturally interesting than a single large pane.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Daylight Factor Components — Flow Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Total Daylight Factor DF] --> B[Sky Component SC]
    A --> C[Externally Reflected Component ERC]
    A --> D[Internally Reflected Component IRC]
    B --> B1[Direct light from sky through window]
    B --> B2[Function of window size and position]
    B --> B3[From BRS Chart I]
    C --> C1[Light from external surfaces]
    C --> C2[Ground reflectance and obstruction]
    C --> C3[From BRS Chart III]
    D --> D1[Light reflected within room]
    D --> D2[Ceiling walls floor inter-reflections]
    D --> D3[From BRS Chart II]
    E[Reference Point on Working Plane] --> A
    F[Working plane height 0.85 m] --> E
    G[CIE Overcast Sky 10000 lux] --> E
```

## 4.2 Lux Meter — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Incident Light] --> B[Cosine Correction Filter]
    B --> C[Photocell Selenide or Silicon]
    C --> D[Amplifier Circuit]
    D --> E[ADC Converter]
    E --> F[Microprocessor]
    F --> G[Digital Display in lux]
    H[Battery Power Supply] --> D
    H --> E
    H --> F
    I[V lambda Filter Human Eye Response] --> C
```

## 4.3 Lumen Method — Computational Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Given E target] --> B[Compute Room Area A L times W]
    B --> C[Look up CU from Catalogue]
    C --> D[Assume MF 0.8 default]
    D --> E[Look up Lumens per Lamp from Catalogue]
    E --> F[Apply N = E times A divided by Phi times CU times MF]
    F --> G{N is Whole Number}
    G -- No --> H[Round up to next integer]
    G -- Yes --> I[Proceed to Layout]
    H --> I
    I --> J[Compute Spacing]
    J --> K{SHR check Spacing less than 1.5h}
    K -- Pass --> L[Design Complete]
    K -- Fail --> M[Add More Luminaires and Reiterate]
```

## 4.4 Point-by-Point Method — Computational Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Reference Point P on Working Plane] --> B[Measure horizontal distance x from lamp foot]
    A --> C[Measure mounting height h]
    B --> D[Compute angle theta equals arctan x over h]
    C --> D
    D --> E[Compute d equals h over cos theta]
    E --> F[Read intensity I from polar curve at angle theta]
    F --> G[Apply E equals I cos cubed theta over h squared]
    G --> H[Get E at point P in lux]
    I[Repeat for all critical points] --> A
```

## 4.5 Polar Distribution Curves — Geometric Concept

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Luminaire Position Center] --> B[Light Ray at 0 degree downward nadir]
    A --> C[Light Ray at 30 degrees]
    A --> D[Light Ray at 60 degrees]
    A --> E[Light Ray at 90 degrees horizontal]
    B --> B1[Intensity I0 maximum]
    C --> C1[Intensity I30]
    D --> D1[Intensity I60]
    E --> E1[Intensity I90 minimum]
    F[Polar Plot] --> B
    F --> C
    F --> D
    F --> E
    G[Symmetrical luminaire: I30 I150 same] --> F
    H[Batwing luminaire: peak at 30 and 150] --> F
    I[Direct luminaire: peak at 0] --> F
```

## 4.6 BRS Daylight Factor Methodology — Master Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Target DF given] --> B[Identify room use and target DF]
    B --> C[Note room dimensions]
    C --> D[Determine window geometry]
    D --> E[Iterate over assumed WFR]
    E --> F[Use BRS Chart I for SC]
    E --> G[Use BRS Chart II for IRC]
    E --> H[Use BRS Chart III for ERC]
    F --> I[Sum components]
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J{DF total meets target}
    J -- Yes --> K[Design Finalized]
    J -- No --> L[Adjust WFR and reiterate]
    L --> E
```
