# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Daylight Factor. State the recommended DF for (i) a residential living room and (ii) a corridor. [3 Marks]

**Model Answer:**

> **Definition:** Daylight Factor at a point is the ratio of the illumination at that point on a horizontal working plane due to the light received directly and indirectly from the sky, to the simultaneous illumination on a horizontal plane exposed to an unobstructed hemisphere of the standard CIE overcast sky, expressed as a percentage.
> 
> **Recommended values (BIS IS 3646):**
> - Residential living room: **2 %**
> - Corridor: **1 %**
> 
> **Quick Mnemonic:** "5-2-1" → Fine work 5 %, Ordinary work 2 %, Circulation 1 %.

### Question 2: Explain the working of a Lux meter. What is the role of cosine correction? [3 Marks]

**Model Answer:**

> **Working:** A lux meter uses a photocell (selenium or silicon) that generates a small electric current proportional to the illumination falling on it. This current is measured and displayed in lux (1 lux = 1 lumen/m²).
> 
> **Cosine Correction:** Because the photocell is a flat surface, light striking it obliquely is "spread out" over a larger effective area. By Lambert's cosine law, the response of a flat sensor should drop with $\cos\theta$ of the angle of incidence. A **cosine-correction filter** (typically a flat diffusing element) ensures the cell responds according to this law, giving true lux values regardless of light direction.
> 
> Without cosine correction, oblique light would be overestimated.

### Question 3: What is a polar distribution curve? What is its significance in lighting design? [3 Marks]

**Model Answer:**

> A polar distribution curve is a plot in polar coordinates showing the variation of luminous intensity (in candelas) of a luminaire as a function of the vertical angle from the nadir.
> 
> **Significance:**
> 1. It defines the **optical "personality"** of a luminaire.
> 2. It is essential for **point-by-point method** calculations — the value of $I$ at a given angle is read directly from the curve.
> 3. It allows designers to choose a luminaire type (direct, indirect, etc.) suitable for the application.
> 4. Helps verify the suitability for specific tasks and ceiling heights.

### Question 4: Distinguish between a Lamp and a Luminaire. [3 Marks]

**Model Answer:**

> | Aspect | Lamp | Luminaire |
> |---|---|---|
> | Definition | The light source (bulb, tube, LED chip) | Complete fixture including lamp + reflector + diffuser + housing |
> | Function | Converts electrical energy to light | Directs, controls, and protects the light output |
> | Example | 40 W fluorescent tube | Tube-light fixture with choke and starter |
> | Photometry | Has its own polar curve | Has the overall luminaire's polar curve (after reflector) |
> | Replacement | Replaced when fused | Usually permanent |

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A — Daylighting Design (14 Marks)

**Question A (a):** Define Sky Component, Externally Reflected Component, and Internally Reflected Component of the Daylight Factor. State the formula and physical meaning of each. [7 Marks]

**Model Answer:**

> **Sky Component (SC):** The component of DF due to the light received directly from the visible portion of the sky as seen from the reference point through the window. It depends on the size of the window, its position, the position of the reference point, and glazing transmittance.
> 
> **Formula:** $SC = \dfrac{\theta \times A_w \times \tau}{180 \times A_p \times (1 - r_m^2)}$
> 
> **Physical meaning:** $\theta/180$ = fraction of the sky hemisphere visible through the window. $A_w/A_p$ = geometric window-to-floor ratio. $\tau$ = glass transmission. The denominator $(1 - r_m^2)$ accounts for the **inter-reflection of light** within the room (light that hits walls and reflects back).
> 
> **Externally Reflected Component (ERC):** The component of DF due to daylight reflected from external surfaces (ground, buildings, etc.) into the window.
> 
> **Formula:** $DF_{\text{ERC}} = \dfrac{\theta_g \times R_g \times A_w \times \tau}{180 \times A_p \times (1 - r_m^2)}$
> 
> **Physical meaning:** $\theta_g$ is the vertical angle subtended by the external reflecting surface (e.g., ground) and $R_g$ is its reflectance. A bright concrete ground contributes substantially; dark grass does not.
> 
> **Internally Reflected Component (IRC):** The component of DF due to daylight that enters the room and then undergoes multiple reflections off the internal surfaces (walls, ceiling, floor) before reaching the reference point.
> 
> **Formula:** $DF_{\text{IRC}} = \dfrac{R_w \times A_w \times \tau}{A_{\text{total}} \times (1 - r_m)} \times 100$
> 
> **Physical meaning:** The IRC depends on the **interior surface reflectances**. White walls give a much higher IRC than dark walls. The factor $(1 - r_m)$ in the denominator represents the **infinite geometric series of inter-reflections**: $1 + r_m + r_m^2 + r_m^3 + ... = 1/(1 - r_m)$.

**Question A (b):** A classroom of dimensions 10 m × 7 m × 3.3 m (height) is to be illuminated to a daylight factor of 2.5 %. The window is side-lit, sill at 1.0 m, header at 2.7 m, with 5 m × 1.7 m single window. The working plane is at 0.85 m. Assume CIE overcast sky 10,000 lux. The room reflectances are ceiling 0.7, walls 0.5, floor 0.2, and the external ground has reflectance 0.15. Glazing transmittance = 0.8. The BRS charts give: SC = 1.6 %, IRC = 0.6 %, ERC = 0.3 % for this configuration. Verify whether the design meets the requirement and suggest improvements if not. [7 Marks]

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1: Verify the achieved DF.**

$$DF_{\text{achieved}} = SC + ERC + IRC = 1.6 + 0.3 + 0.6 = 2.5 \text{ %}$$

**Step 2: Compute the indoor illumination.**

$$E_{\text{indoor}} = \frac{DF \times 10{,}000}{100} = \frac{2.5 \times 10{,}000}{100} = 250 \text{ lux}$$

**Step 3: Compare with target.**

Target = 2.5 % → 250 lux. Achieved = 2.5 % → 250 lux. **The design meets the requirement exactly.**

**Step 4: Improvements for good design practice (over-design).**

> **Recommendation:** While the design meets the minimum 2.5 % DF, real-world factors reduce the effective daylight:
> - Dirty glazing → reduce $\tau$ from 0.8 to 0.6 in practice
> - External obstructions (trees, neighbouring buildings)
> - Dull weather below CIE standard
> 
> **Suggested improvements:**
> 1. **Increase window area by 25 %** — to 6.25 m × 1.7 m to give a 25 % design margin.
> 2. **Use lighter wall colours** (reflectance 0.7) to boost IRC.
> 3. **Add a clerestory window** at the top of the opposite wall to supplement the back of the classroom.
> 4. **Use low-iron, high-transmittance glazing** with $\tau = 0.9$.
> 5. **Install light shelves** to bounce daylight deeper into the room.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often **forget that the target DF is a minimum** and stop designing once they reach 2.5 %. In real practice, design should target **3 %–4 %** to allow for dirt, weather, and external obstructions. Examiners reward awareness of this "design margin" with bonus marks.

---

### Question B — Artificial Lighting Design (14 Marks)

**Question B (a):** Explain the Lumen Method of artificial lighting design. What is the role of the Coefficient of Utilization and Maintenance Factor? [7 Marks]

**Model Answer:**

> **The Lumen Method (also called Flux Method)** is a procedure to determine the number of luminaires required to achieve a given average illumination level in a room. It treats the room as a whole and assumes uniform lighting.
> 
> **The fundamental formula is:**
> 
> $$N = \frac{E \times A}{\Phi \times \text{CU} \times \text{MF}}$$
> 
> where:
> - $N$ = number of lamps
> - $E$ = required average illumination in lux
> - $A$ = area of working plane in m²
> - $\Phi$ = lumens output per lamp
> - $\text{CU}$ = Coefficient of Utilization
> - $\text{MF}$ = Maintenance Factor
> 
> **Procedure:**
> 1. Determine room dimensions and area $A = L \times W$.
> 2. Identify the visual task and the recommended illumination $E$ (from IS 3646).
> 3. Choose the luminaire type and look up the **CU** from the manufacturer's CU table (which depends on room index $K = \dfrac{L \times W}{h \times (L + W)}$ and surface reflectances).
> 4. Assume an appropriate **MF** (typically 0.8 for clean offices, 0.5 for industrial dusty environments).
> 5. Look up the **lumens per lamp** $\Phi$ from the catalogue.
> 6. Apply the formula to get $N$.
> 7. Round up to an integer.
> 8. Design a grid layout (square or rectangular).
> 9. Verify the **Spacing-to-Mounting-Height Ratio (SHR)** — usually less than **1.5** for general lighting.
> 
> **Role of CU (Coefficient of Utilization):**
> The CU is the **fraction of lamp lumens that reach the working plane** after considering:
> - Luminaire efficiency (some light is absorbed by the fixture)
> - Room geometry (some light hits walls and is partly absorbed)
> - Surface reflectances (darker walls absorb more)
> 
> Typical CU values range from 0.4 (poor) to 0.8 (highly efficient reflector).
> 
> **Role of MF (Maintenance Factor):**
> The MF accounts for the **decline in lighting output over time** due to:
> - **Lamp ageing** (lumen depreciation, also called LLD)
> - **Luminaire dirt accumulation** (LDD)
> - **Room surface dirt** (RSDD)
> - **Lamp burnouts** (LSF)
> 
> $$\text{MF} = \text{AL} \times \text{RSMF} \times \text{LMF} \times \text{LSF}$$
> 
> Typical MF = 0.8 (clean) to 0.5 (dirty industrial).

**Question B (b):** A conference room of 16 m × 10 m × 4 m is to be illuminated to 500 lux using fluorescent tube lights. Each tube emits 5000 lumens and the CU is 0.55, MF = 0.75. (i) Calculate the number of tubes required. (ii) If each luminaire holds 2 tubes, how many luminaires are needed? (iii) Suggest a suitable layout and verify the spacing-to-mounting-height ratio (mounting height above working plane = 3 m). [7 Marks]

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1: Identify given data.**

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Room L × W × H | 16 m × 10 m × 4 m |
| Required E | 500 lx |
| Lumen per tube $\Phi$ | 5000 lm |
| CU | 0.55 |
| MF | 0.75 |
| Mounting height above WP | 3 m |

**Step 2: Area of working plane.**

$$A = 16 \times 10 = 160 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 3: Apply Lumen Method formula.**

$$N = \frac{E \times A}{\Phi \times \text{CU} \times \text{MF}}$$

$$N = \frac{500 \times 160}{5000 \times 0.55 \times 0.75}$$

$$N = \frac{80{,}000}{2062.5}$$

$$N = 38.78 \approx 39 \text{ tubes}$$

**Step 4: Calculate number of luminaires (each holding 2 tubes).**

$$N_{\text{luminaires}} = \frac{39}{2} = 19.5 \approx 20 \text{ luminaires}$$

**Step 5: Layout planning.**

For uniform lighting, use a grid with approximately equal row and column spacing:

$$n_{\text{rows}} = \sqrt{N_{\text{lum}} \times \frac{L}{W}} = \sqrt{20 \times \frac{16}{10}} = \sqrt{32} \approx 5.66$$

Round to **6 rows**. Columns = 20 / 6 = 3.33 ≈ **4 columns** (slight variation).

Use a **5 × 4 = 20 luminaire layout** (5 rows of 4 luminaires each).

**Step 6: Compute spacing.**

- Row spacing = 16 / 5 = 3.2 m
- Column spacing = 10 / 4 = 2.5 m

**Step 7: Spacing-to-Mounting-Height Ratio (SHR) check.**

$$\text{SHR}_{\text{max}} = 1.5 \times 3 = 4.5 \text{ m}$$

Both 3.2 m and 2.5 m are well within the 4.5 m limit. **The layout is acceptable.**

**Final Answer:** 39 tubes, 20 luminaires, in a 5 × 4 grid layout.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:**
> 1. **Forgetting to divide by lamps per luminaire** — A common error is to use the 39 directly as the number of luminaires, leading to twice the required number. The question explicitly says "each luminaire holds 2 tubes" — read carefully.
> 2. **Not checking the SHR** — Examiners explicitly check the Spacing-to-Mounting-Height ratio in nearly every Lumen Method problem.
> 3. **Rounding errors** — Always round UP the number of lamps (N), never down. Lighting design must never under-illuminate.

---

### Question C (Additional KTU Practice) — Point-by-Point Method [14 Marks]

**Question C (a):** Derive the Cosine Cubed Law of Illumination. State the assumptions. [7 Marks]

**Model Answer:**

> **Assumptions:**
> 1. The light source is a **point source** (size is negligible compared to distance).
> 2. The source emits uniformly in all directions (or intensity $I$ is given for the direction).
> 3. The working surface is **horizontal**.
> 4. The lamp is mounted **directly above** the working point (vertically below).
> 
> **Derivation:**
> 
> **Step 1:** From the Inverse Square Law, for a surface normal to the ray:
> 
> $$E_n = \frac{I}{d^2}$$
> 
> **Step 2:** For a tilted surface at angle $\theta$ from the normal, the illumination is reduced by $\cos\theta$ (Lambert's cosine law):
> 
> $$E = E_n \cos\theta = \frac{I \cos\theta}{d^2}$$
> 
> **Step 3:** The distance $d$ from the source to the working point is related to the vertical mounting height $h$ by:
> 
> $$d = \frac{h}{\cos\theta} \quad \Rightarrow \quad d^2 = \frac{h^2}{\cos^2\theta}$$
> 
> **Step 4:** Substitute:
> 
> $$E = \frac{I \cos\theta \times \cos^2\theta}{h^2} = \frac{I \cos^3\theta}{h^2}$$
> 
> $$\boxed{E = \frac{I \cos^3\theta}{h^2}}$$

**Question C (b):** A 200 candela lamp is suspended at a height of 4 m above the working plane. Calculate the illumination at a point 3 m away from the foot of the lamp. Also find the illumination at a point directly below the lamp. [7 Marks]

**Model Answer:**

**Part (i): Point at 3 m horizontal distance.**

**Step 1: Geometry.**

- $h = 4$ m, $x = 3$ m
- $d = \sqrt{h^2 + x^2} = \sqrt{16 + 9} = \sqrt{25} = 5$ m
- $\theta = \tan^{-1}(x/h) = \tan^{-1}(3/4) = 36.87°$
- $\cos(36.87°) = 0.8$ → $\cos^3(36.87°) = 0.512$

**Step 2: Apply Cosine Cubed Law.**

$$E_1 = \frac{I \cos^3\theta}{h^2} = \frac{200 \times 0.512}{16} = \frac{102.4}{16} = 6.4 \text{ lux}$$

**Part (ii): Point directly below the lamp.**

- $x = 0$, $\theta = 0$, $\cos(0) = 1$
- $E_2 = \dfrac{I \times 1}{h^2} = \dfrac{200}{16} = 12.5$ lux

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification:** The illumination directly below (12.5 lux) is roughly **double** that at 3 m away (6.4 lux). This is consistent with the inverse square law — going from 0 to 3 m, the distance increases from 4 m to 5 m (only 25 % farther), but the cos³ term drops from 1.0 to 0.512 (about 50 % reduction). Combined, this gives the observed ratio.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-DENSITY RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST**

### Daylight Factor Essentials
- **DF formula:** $DF = E_{\text{indoor}} / E_{\text{outdoor}} \times 100$ (always a percentage).
- **CIE Standard outdoor illumination:** 10,000 lux (memorize this value!).
- **BIS Recommended DFs:** "5-2-1" rule — Fine work 5 %, Ordinary 2 %, Circulation 1 %.
- **Three components:** SC + ERC + IRC (always add them).
- **Working plane height:** 0.85 m above the floor (KTU standard).
- **Two-Third Header Rule:** Sill at 1 m, Header at 2/3 of room height for deep light penetration.

### Side-Lit Window Design Essentials
- **BRS Method:** Three charts (I for SC, II for IRC, III for ERC).
- **Window-to-Floor Area Ratio (WFR):** 0.20 typical for offices, 0.25+ for fine work.
- **Design margin:** Always over-design by 20–30 % to account for dirt and weather.

### Artificial Lighting Essentials
- **Lumen Method formula:** $N = E \times A / (\Phi \times \text{CU} \times \text{MF})$ — commit to memory.
- **Cosine Cubed Law:** $E = I \cos^3\theta / h^2$ — derive it cold.
- **Default MF:** 0.8 (clean offices), 0.7 (average), 0.5 (industrial).
- **Spacing-to-Mounting-Height Ratio (SHR):** ≤ 1.5 for general lighting.
- **5 lux meter components:** Sensor + Cosine filter + V(λ) filter + Amplifier + Display.

### Lamps and Luminaires Essentials
- **Lamp** = source, **Luminaire** = complete fixture.
- **Most common indoor lamp:** Fluorescent T5/T8 (offices) and LED (modern).
- **Lamp life ranking:** Incandescent (1000 hr) < Fluorescent (8000-20000 hr) < LED (30000-50000 hr).
- **Efficacy ranking (lm/W):** Incandescent 10-25 < CFL 50-70 < LED 100-200.

### Polar Distribution Curves Essentials
- **Definition:** Polar plot of intensity vs. angle from nadir.
- **Use:** Read $I$ at a given angle for the Point-by-Point Method.
- **Types:** Direct, Semi-direct, General diffusing, Semi-indirect, Indirect.
- **Symmetrical luminaire:** $I_{\theta} = I_{360-\theta}$ (rotationally symmetric).

### Quick Conversion Table (Memorize This!)

| Given | To Find | Formula |
|---|---|---|
| DF % | Indoor lux | $E = DF \times 100$ (assuming 10 klx outdoor) |
| Indoor lux | DF % | $DF = E / 100$ |
| N (lumen method) | Total lumens | $\Phi_{\text{total}} = N \times \Phi_{\text{lamp}}$ |
| I (cd) and h (m) | E (lx) below lamp | $E = I / h^2$ |
| I, h, x | E (lx) at offset | $E = I \cos^3(\tan^{-1}(x/h)) / h^2$ |
| E, A, Φ, CU, MF | N | $N = EA / (\Phi \times \text{CU} \times \text{MF})$ |

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Factors Affecting Thermal Comfort
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECET415 - ARCHITECTURAL ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Factors Affecting Thermal Comfort ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Thermal Comfort** is formally defined by **ASHRAE Standard 55-2020** as *"that condition of mind which expresses satisfaction with the thermal environment."* It is a subjective state of the human body in which the net heat exchange with the surrounding environment is balanced such that the body neither feels excessively hot nor cold, and physiological functions like sweating or shivering are minimized. From an **Architectural Engineering** standpoint, thermal comfort is a critical design parameter that determines the **HVAC sizing**, **building orientation**, **envelope design**, and **glazing selection** of any habitable structure. The condition is quantified using **Fanger's Comfort Theory (1970)**, which yields a **Predicted Mean Vote (PMV)** and a **Predicted Percentage of Dissatisfied (PPD)** index, forming the basis of **ISO 7730:2005**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Definition to Memorize:** > *"Thermal comfort is the condition of thermal neutrality where a person wearing standardized clothing at a given metabolic rate expresses no dissatisfaction with the surrounding thermal environment."* — Adopted from ISO 7730 / ASHRAE 55 ### The Six Primary Factors Affecting Thermal Comfort The **Fanger Six-Factor Model** identifies the following variables that govern the thermal sensation of an occupant: | # | Factor | Symbol | Typical Range | Unit | |---|--------|--------|---------------|------| | 1 | Air Temperature | $t_a$ | 20 – 26 | °C | | 2 | Mean Radiant Temperature | $\bar{t}_r$ | 20 – 26 | °C | | 3 | Relative Humidity | $RH$ | 30 – 60 | % | | 4 | Air Velocity | $V_a$ | 0.1 – 0.5 | m/s | | 5 | Clothing Insulation | $I_{cl}$ | 0.5 – 1.0 | clo | | 6 | Metabolic Heat Production | $M$ | 1.0 – 1.4 | met | Where the standard conversions are: - **1 met** = **58.2 W/m²** (metabolic rate of a seated, resting person) - **1 clo** = **0.155 m²·K/W** (insulation of typical business suit) ### Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy: "The Bathroom Shower Test"** > Imagine stepping into a bathroom on a cold winter morning. Even if the air temperature reads 24°C on a thermostat, you may still feel chilly because the **tiled walls are cold** (low mean radiant temperature). Now imagine the same scenario on a humid monsoon day with no fan — the air reads 26°C, but you feel sticky and uncomfortable because **humidity is high** and **air is stagnant**. This is precisely why thermal comfort is **NOT just about air temperature** — it is a multi-variable equation involving radiation, convection, evaporation, and personal factors. ### Standard Comfort Boundaries (ASHRAE 7-Point Scale) The PMV index uses the Fanger 7-point thermal sensation scale: | PMV Value | Sensation | Comfort Level | |-----------|-----------|---------------| | +3 | Hot | Extreme dissatisfaction | | +2 | Warm | Significant dissatisfaction | | +1 | Slightly warm | Slight dissatisfaction | | 0 | **Neutral** | **Comfort (Ideal)** | | −1 | Slightly cool | Slight dissatisfaction | | −2 | Cool | Significant dissatisfaction | | −3 | Cold | Extreme dissatisfaction | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Exam Favourite:** The acceptable comfort band is **−0.5 ≤ PMV ≤ +0.5**, which corresponds to a **PPD ≤ 10%**. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### Fanger's Heat Balance Equation — The Foundation The human body, from a thermodynamic perspective, is a **heat engine** that must maintain a core temperature of approximately **37°C (310 K)**. Thermal comfort exists when the **internal heat production** is exactly equal to the **total heat loss** to the environment, with the body's regulatory mechanisms (sweating, vasodilation, shivering) in a state of minimum effort. The steady-state **energy balance equation** for the human body is: $$ M - W = C + R + E + C_{res} + E_{res} $$ Where: - $M$ = **Metabolic heat production** (W/m²) - $W$ = **Effective mechanical work** (W/m²) - $(M - W)$ = **Internal heat generation** available for dissipation - $C$ = **Convective heat loss** from skin (W/m²) - $R$ = **Radiative heat loss** from skin (W/m²) - $E$ = **Evaporative heat loss** from skin (W/m²) - $C_{res}$ = **Convective heat loss** through respiration (W/m²) - $E_{res}$ = **Evaporative heat loss** through respiration (W/m²) ### Component-wise Breakdown of Heat Loss #### (i) Convective + Radiative Heat Loss (Dry Heat Loss) $$ C + R = f_{cl} \cdot h_c \cdot (t_{cl} - t_a) + 3.96 \times 10^{-8} \cdot f_{cl} \cdot \left[ (t_{cl} + 273)^4 - (\bar{t}_r + 273)^4 \right] $$ Where: - $f_{cl}$ = **Clothing area factor** (dimensionless ratio of clothed to nude surface area) - $h_c$ = **Convective heat transfer coefficient** (W/m²·K) - $t_{cl}$ = **Mean clothing surface temperature** (°C) - $\bar{t}_r$ = **Mean Radiant Temperature** (°C) #### (ii) Clothing Area Factor $f_{cl}$ $$ f_{cl} = \begin{cases} 1.0 + 1.290 \cdot I_{cl} & \text{if } I_{cl} \leq 0.078 \text{ m}^2\cdot\text{K/W} \\ 1.05 + 0.645 \cdot I_{cl} & \text{if } I_{cl} > 0.078 \text{ m}^2\cdot\text{K/W} \end{cases} $$ #### (iii) Convective Heat Transfer Coefficient $h_c$ $$ h_c = \max\left( 2.38 \cdot \vert t_{cl} - t_a \vert^{0.25},\ \ 12.1 \cdot \sqrt{V_a} \right) $$ #### (iv) Evaporative Heat Loss from Skin $$ E = 3.05 \times 10^{-3} \cdot (5733 - 6.99 \cdot M - p_a) + 0.42 \cdot [(M - W) - 58.15] $$ Where $p_a$ is the **partial vapour pressure of water** in ambient air (Pa), given by: $$ p_a = RH \cdot p_{sat}(t_a) \quad \text{where} \quad p_{sat}(t_a) \approx 610.6 \cdot \exp\left(\frac{17.27 \cdot t_a}{t_a + 237.3}\right) $$ #### (v) Respiratory Heat Loss $$ C_{res} + E_{res} = 1.7 \times 10^{-5} \cdot M \cdot (5867 - p_a) + 0.0014 \cdot M \cdot (34 - t_a) $$ ### Final PMV Equation (Fanger's Master Equation) $$ PMV = \left[ 0.303 \cdot e^{-0.036 \cdot M} + 0.028 \right] \cdot L $$ Where $L$ is the **thermal load** (the imbalance in the heat budget) defined as: $$ L = (M - W) - \text{Total Heat Loss} $$ ### PPD (Predicted Percentage Dissatisfied) Equation $$ PPD = 100 - 95 \cdot \exp\left(-0.03353 \cdot PMV^4 - 0.2179 \cdot PMV^2 \right) $$ > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight:** Even at **PMV = 0** (perfect thermal neutrality), there is **always a minimum of 5% PPD** because of individual biological variation across the human population. ### KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield) | # | Quantity | Formula | Units | |---|----------|---------|-------| | 1 | Heat Balance | $M - W = C + R + E + C_{res} + E_{res}$ | W/m² | | 2 | Metabolic Unit | $1 \text{ met} = 58.2$ | W/m² | | 3 | Clothing Unit | $1 \text{ clo} = 0.155$ | m²·K/W | | 4 | Clothing Area Factor | $f_{cl} = 1.0 + 1.290 \cdot I_{cl}$ (low $I_{cl}$) | — | | 5 | Convective Coeff. | $h_c = 12.1 \cdot \sqrt{V_a}$ (forced) | W/m²·K | | 6 | Operative Temp. | $t_o = A \cdot t_a + (1 - A) \cdot \bar{t}_r$ | °C | | 7 | Saturation Pressure | $p_{sat} = 610.6 \cdot e^{17.27 t_a / (t_a + 237.3)}$ | Pa | | 8 | PMV | $PMV = [0.303 e^{-0.036M} + 0.028] L$ | Index | | 9 | PPD | $PPD = 100 - 95 e^{-0.03353 PMV^4 - 0.2179 PMV^2}$ | % | | 10 | Acceptable Limit | $-0.5 \leq PMV \leq +0.5$ → $PPD \leq 10\%$ | — | ### Real-World Utility in Architectural Engineering > [!IMPORTANT] > **Industry Application:** The PMV-PPD model is the **mandatory design tool** for: > - Green building certifications (**GRIHA, LEED, IGBC**) > - Energy simulation software (**EnergyPlus, IES VE, eQUEST, DesignBuilder**) > - HVAC load calculation per **ISHRAE Handbook (2018)** > - KTU-validated building codes for **ECBC (Energy Conservation Building Code) of India** ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### Derivation 1: PMV Equation from First-Principles Heat Balance The PMV equation is derived by **solving the steady-state energy balance** for a thermally neutral body, with the thermal sensation $L$ representing the body's deviation from thermal equilibrium. **Step 1 — State the governing heat balance equation for the human body in a conditioned space:** $$ (M - W) - (C + R + E + C_{res} + E_{res}) = 0 \quad \text{at thermal neutrality} $$ **Step 2 — Identify the six governing variables (Fanger's parametric study):** The variables are: $t_a$, $\bar{t}_r$, $V_a$, $p_a$, $I_{cl}$, $M$. Constants are absorbed into empirical coefficients. **Step 3 — Define the thermal load $L$ as the deviation from this equilibrium:** $$ L = (M - W) - (C + R + E + C_{res} + E_{res}) $$ **Step 4 — Empirically fit the relationship between $L$ and the PMV (psychophysical scale) using the ASHRAE large-scale experiments on 1300+ subjects:** $$ PMV = a \cdot L + b $$ **Step 5 — Apply non-linear regression on the experimental data (Fanger, 1970). The asymmetry of cold vs. warm sensitivity gives the final form:** $$ PMV = \left[ 0.303 \cdot e^{-0.036 \cdot M} + 0.028 \right] \cdot L $$ > The exponential term reflects the **body's non-linear response** — a higher metabolic rate (e.g., during exercise) dampens the subjective thermal sensation per unit of heat load. --- ### Derivation 2: Clothing Surface Temperature $t_{cl}$ (Iterative Solution) The clothing surface temperature $t_{cl}$ is solved **iteratively** from the following transcendental equation, which states that all heat conducted through the clothing layer must equal the sum of convective and radiative losses from the clothing surface: $$ t_{cl} = 35.7 - 0.028 \cdot (M - W) - I_{cl} \cdot \left[ 3.96 \times 10^{-8} \cdot f_{cl} \cdot \left[ (t_{cl} + 273)^4 - (\bar{t}_r + 273)^4 \right] + f_{cl} \cdot h_c \cdot (t_{cl} - t_a) \right] $$ **Step-by-Step Iterative Procedure (Killer Question for 14-mark KTU):** **Step 1 — Initialize** $t_{cl}^{(0)} = 30°C$ as a starting guess (typical skin-influenced value). **Step 2 — Calculate the clothing area factor $f_{cl}$** using the appropriate branch of the piecewise equation. **Step 3 — Calculate $h_c$** using the maximum of natural convection and forced convection expressions. **Step 4 — Substitute** $t_{cl}^{(k)}$ into the right-hand side to obtain $t_{cl}^{(k+1)}$. **Step 5 — Check convergence** using $\vert t_{cl}^{(k+1)} - t_{cl}^{(k)} \vert < 0.001°C$. If not satisfied, return to Step 4. --- ### Derivation 3: Worked Numerical Example (Full PMV Calculation) **Problem:** A sedentary office worker in light summer clothing is in a building with the following conditions: - Air temperature: $t_a = 26°C$ - Mean radiant temperature: $\bar{t}_r = 28°C$ - Relative humidity: $RH = 50\%$ - Air velocity: $V_a = 0.15$ m/s - Clothing insulation: $I_{cl} = 0.5$ clo - Metabolic rate: $M = 1.2$ met (seated office work) **Step 1 — Convert to SI units:** $$ M = 1.2 \times 58.2 = 69.84 \text{ W/m}^2 $$ $$ I_{cl} = 0.5 \times 0.155 = 0.0775 \text{ m}^2 \cdot \text{K/W} \quad (\text{use lower branch}) $$ $$ W = 0 \quad (\text{assume no external work}) $$ **Step 2 — Calculate $f_{cl}$** (since $I_{cl} \leq 0.078$): $$ f_{cl} = 1.0 + 1.290 \times 0.0775 = 1.10 $$ **Step 3 — Calculate the saturation vapour pressure at $t_a = 26°C$:** $$ p_{sat} = 610.6 \cdot \exp\left(\frac{17.27 \times 26}{26 + 237.3}\right) = 3324.6 \text{ Pa} $$ **Step 4 — Calculate the partial vapour pressure $p_a$:** $$ p_a = 0.50 \times 3324.6 = 1662.3 \text{ Pa} $$ **Step 5 — Calculate $h_c$ (forced convection dominates for $V_a \geq 0.1$ m/s):** $$ h_{c,\text{forced}} = 12.1 \cdot \sqrt{0.15} = 4.686 \text{ W/m}^2 \cdot \text{K} $$ $$ h_{c,\text{natural}} = 2.38 \cdot \vert 30 - 26 \vert^{0.25} = 3.49 \text{ W/m}^2 \cdot \text{K} $$ $$ h_c = \max(3.49,\ 4.686) = 4.686 \text{ W/m}^2 \cdot \text{K} $$ **Step 6 — Iteratively solve for $t_{cl}$** starting with $t_{cl}^{(0)} = 30°C$: - **Iteration 1:** Substitute and compute $t_{cl}^{(1)} \approx 27.18°C$ - **Iteration 2:** $t_{cl}^{(2)} \approx 27.20°C$ → **Converged** **Step 7 — Compute the radiative + convective heat loss:** $$ C + R = f_{cl} \cdot h_c \cdot (t_{cl} - t_a) + 3.96 \times 10^{-8} \cdot f_{cl} \cdot \left[ (t_{cl} + 273)^4 - (\bar{t}_r + 273)^4 \right] $$ $$ C + R = 1.10 \times 4.686 \times (27.20 - 26) + 3.96 \times 10^{-8} \times 1.10 \times \left[ 300.20^4 - 301^4 \right] $$ $$ C + R = 6.18 - 5.13 = 1.05 \text{ W/m}^2 $$ **Step 8 — Compute evaporative heat loss $E$:** $$ E = 3.05 \times 10^{-3} \cdot (5733 - 6.99 \times 69.84 - 1662.3) + 0.42 \cdot [(69.84) - 58.15] $$ $$ E = 10.97 + 4.91 = 15.88 \text{ W/m}^2 $$ **Step 9 — Compute respiratory heat loss:** $$ C_{res} + E_{res} = 1.7 \times 10^{-5} \times 69.84 \times (5867 - 1662.3) + 0.0014 \times 69.84 \times (34 - 26) $$ $$ C_{res} + E_{res} = 4.99 + 0.78 = 5.77 \text{ W/m}^2 $$ **Step 10 — Compute the thermal load $L$:** $$ L = (M - W) - (C + R + E + C_{res} + E_{res}) $$ $$ L = 69.84 - (1.05 + 15.88 + 5.77) = 69.84 - 22.70 = 47.14 \text{ W/m}^2 $$ **Step 11 — Compute the PMV index:** $$ PMV = \left[ 0.303 \cdot e^{-0.036 \times 69.84} + 0.028 \right] \times 47.14 $$ $$ PMV = \left[ 0.303 \times 0.0806 + 0.028 \right] \times 47.14 = (0.0244 + 0.028) \times 47.14 $$ $$ PMV = 0.0524 \times 47.14 = 2.47 \approx +2 \text{ (Warm)} $$ **Step 12 — Compute the PPD:** $$ PPD = 100 - 95 \cdot e^{-0.03353 \times 2.47^4 - 0.2179 \times 2.47^2} $$ $$ PPD = 100 - 95 \cdot e^{-0.03353 \times 37.21 - 0.2179 \times 6.10} = 100 - 95 \cdot e^{-2.58} $$ $$ PPD = 100 - 95 \times 0.0757 = 100 - 7.19 = 92.81\% $$ > [!WARNING] > **Interpretation:** With **92.8% PPD**, this is **highly unacceptable** (>10% threshold). The room is too hot, primarily because the **Mean Radiant Temperature exceeds the Air Temperature by 2°C** (radiant asymmetry from sun-exposed walls) and the **air velocity is too low** (only 0.15 m/s). Architectural design fix: increase $V_a$ to 0.5 m/s, apply low-e glazing, or use light-coloured external shading. ### Analytical Matrix: How Each Factor Affects PMV | Factor ↑ (Increasing) | Effect on Heat Loss | Effect on PMV | Architectural Design Response | |----|----|----|----| | Air temp $t_a$ ↑ | Increases $C$ and $E_{res}$ | **PMV ↑** (warmer) | Increase shading, reduce SHGC of glazing | | MRT $\bar{t}_r$ ↑ | Increases $R$ (loss) | **PMV ↑** | Use reflective roof, insulated walls | | RH ↑ | Decreases $E$ | **PMV ↑** | Dehumidification (energy penalty) | | Air velocity $V_a$ ↑ | Increases $C$ and $E$ | **PMV ↓** (cooler) | Use ceiling fans, natural ventilation | | Clothing $I_{cl}$ ↑ | Decreases $C$ and $R$ | **PMV ↑** (warmer) | Recommend light, breathable fabrics | | Metabolic $M$ ↑ | Increases internal load | **PMV ↑** | Task design, localized cooling | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### Diagram 1: Six Factors of Thermal Comfort — Fanger's Conceptual Model ```mermaid graph TD EnvFactors["ENVIRONMENTAL FACTORS"] PersFactors["PERSONAL FACTORS"] Ta["Air Temperature
ta in C"] Tr["Mean Radiant
Temperature tr"] RH["Relative Humidity
RH in percent"] Va["Air Velocity
Va in m per s"] Met["Metabolic Rate
M in met"] Clo["Clothing Insulation
Icl in clo"] Body["HUMAN BODY HEAT
BALANCE ENGINE"] Output1["Predicted Mean Vote
PMV index"] Output2["Predicted Percentage
Dissatisfied PPD percent"] EnvFactors --> Ta EnvFactors --> Tr EnvFactors --> RH EnvFactors --> Va PersFactors --> Met PersFactors --> Clo Ta --> Body Tr --> Body RH --> Body Va --> Body Met --> Body Clo --> Body Body --> Output1 Output1 --> Output2 ``` ### Diagram 2: Human Body Heat Exchange — Functional Block Architecture ```mermaid graph LR Input_M["INPUT
Metabolic Heat M"] Input_W["External Work W"] Block_Skin["SKIN SURFACE
Convection C
Radiation R
Evaporation E"] Block_Resp["RESPIRATORY
Convective Cres
Evaporative Eres"] Block_Balance["THERMAL LOAD
L equals M minus W
minus total heat loss"] Block_PMV["PMV CALC
PMV equals 0.303 exp minus 0.036M
plus 0.028 times L"] Block_PPD["PPD MODEL
PPD equals 100 minus 95
exp minus 0.03353 PMV4
minus 0.2179 PMV2"] Decision{"Acceptable
PPD less than 10 percent"} Comfort["COMFORT ZONE
minus 0.5 to plus 0.5 PMV"] Action["HVAC INTERVENTION
Increase Va
Reduce ta
Dehumidify"] Input_M --> Block_Skin Input_M --> Block_Resp Input_W --> Block_Skin Block_Skin --> Block_Balance Block_Resp --> Block_Balance Block_Balance --> Block_PMV Block_PMV --> Block_PPD Block_PPD --> Decision Decision --> Comfort Decision --> Action ``` ### Diagram 3: ASHRAE Adaptive Comfort Model — Decision Flow ```mermaid graph TD Start["Design Problem: Indoor Thermal Environment"] Q1{"Is Air Conditioning
Used? Yes or No"} Static["Use Fanger PMV PPD
Static Model"] Adaptive["Use ASHRAE 55 Adaptive
Model for Naturally
Conditioned Buildings"] OperTemp["Operative Temperature To
equals A ta plus 1 minus A tr"] MeanMonthly["Mean Monthly Outdoor
Temperature from IMD data"] Bounds["Comfort Band
To between 17.8 and 33.2
Function of Mean Monthly"] Outcome1["Acceptable if To in band"] Outcome2["Unacceptable: Redesign"] Start --> Q1 Q1 --> Static Q1 --> Adaptive Static --> OperTemp Adaptive --> MeanMonthly MeanMonthly --> Bounds OperTemp --> Outcome1 Bounds --> Outcome1 Outcome1 --> Outcome2 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Architectural Insight from Diagram 3:** In **passive, naturally ventilated buildings in Kerala** (e.g., traditional *Nalukettu* or modern tropical modernist homes), the **adaptive model** is far more appropriate than the static Fanger model. It allows occupants to expand their comfort tolerance when exposed to elevated outdoor temperatures over time — a key reason why ceiling fans and operable windows are more energy-efficient than central AC in tropical climates. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** Define thermal comfort. List the six primary factors affecting thermal comfort as per Fanger's comfort model. **Model Answer (3 marks):** Thermal comfort is the condition of mind in which a person expresses satisfaction with the surrounding thermal environment, as defined by ASHRAE Standard 55-2020. It is a state where the body's heat production equals heat loss to the environment, requiring no regulatory effort. The six factors per Fanger's model are: 1. **Air temperature** ($t_a$) 2. **Mean radiant temperature** ($\bar{t}_r$) 3. **Relative humidity** ($RH$) 4. **Air velocity** ($V_a$) 5. **Clothing insulation** ($I_{cl}$) 6. **Metabolic rate** ($M$) *(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for listing four factors, 1 mark for remaining two factors)* --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** State Fanger's heat balance equation. What does the term "thermal load $L$" represent in the PMV equation? **Model Answer (3 marks):** Fanger's steady-state heat balance equation for the human body is: $$ M - W = C + R + E + C_{res} + E_{res} $$ The **thermal load $L$** represents the imbalance between the body's internal heat generation $(M - W)$ and the total heat dissipation to the environment. Mathematically: $$ L = (M - W) - (C + R + E + C_{res} + E_{res}) $$ When $L = 0$, the body is in thermal neutrality (PMV = 0). Positive $L$ means heat is accumulating (feeling warm), while negative $L$ indicates excessive heat loss (feeling cool). *(1.5 marks for equation, 1.5 marks for $L$ explanation)* --- ### Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module 3 Internal Choice --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam — July 2024] **(a)** [7 marks] Explain in detail Fanger's Predicted Mean Vote (PMV) and Predicted Percentage of Dissatisfied (PPD) model. Derive the relationship between the six environmental and personal factors and thermal sensation. **(b)** [7 marks] An office space has the following thermal parameters: $t_a = 24°C$, $\bar{t}_r = 25°C$, $RH = 60\%$, $V_a = 0.1$ m/s, $I_{cl} = 0.6$ clo, $M = 1.1$ met. Determine the PMV and PPD values. Comment on whether the occupants will be comfortable. **Model Solution for (a):** The **PMV model** was developed by **Professor P. O. Fanger in 1970** based on extensive experiments on 1300+ human subjects. It expresses thermal comfort as a function of six independent variables. **Step 1 — Heat Balance Foundation:** The body maintains thermal equilibrium when: $$ M - W = C + R + E + C_{res} + E_{res} $$ **Step 2 — Express Each Component in Terms of the Six Factors:** - **Convective + Radiative loss:** Function of $(t_{cl} - t_a)$, $(\bar{t}_r - t_a)$, $V_a$, $I_{cl}$ - **Evaporative loss:** Function of $M$, $p_a$ (i.e., $RH$) - **Respiratory loss:** Function of $M$, $t_a$, $p_a$ **Step 3 — Define the Thermal Load $L$:** $$ L = (M - W) - \text{Total Heat Loss} $$ **Step 4 — Apply the empirical ASHRAE 7-point scale fit:** $$ PMV = \left[ 0.303 \cdot e^{-0.036M} + 0.028 \right] \cdot L $$ **Step 5 — Compute the percentage of dissatisfied occupants:** $$ PPD = 100 - 95 \cdot \exp\left(-0.03353 \cdot PMV^4 - 0.2179 \cdot PMV^2\right) $$ **Conclusion:** The acceptable comfort band is $-0.5 \leq PMV \leq +0.5$, corresponding to $PPD \leq 10\%$. --- **Model Solution for (b):** **Step 1 — Convert units to SI:** $$ M = 1.1 \times 58.2 = 64.02 \text{ W/m}^2 $$ $$ I_{cl} = 0.6 \times 0.155 = 0.093 \text{ m}^2\cdot\text{K/W} \quad (\text{use upper branch since } > 0.078) $$ **Step 2 — Compute $f_{cl}$:** $$ f_{cl} = 1.05 + 0.645 \times 0.093 = 1.110 $$ **Step 3 — Compute $p_a$:** $$ p_{sat}(24°C) = 610.6 \cdot e^{17.27 \times 24 / 261.3} = 2985.6 \text{ Pa} $$ $$ p_a = 0.60 \times 2985.6 = 1791.4 \text{ Pa} $$ **Step 4 — Compute $h_c$:** $$ h_{c,\text{forced}} = 12.1 \times \sqrt{0.1} = 3.826 \text{ W/m}^2\cdot\text{K} $$ Assuming $t_{cl} \approx 26°C$, $h_{c,\text{natural}} = 2.38 \times 2^{0.25} = 2.99$ W/m²·K. $$ h_c = \max(2.99,\ 3.826) = 3.826 \text{ W/m}^2\cdot\text{K} $$ **Step 5 — Iterate to find $t_{cl}$:** After convergence: $t_{cl} \approx 26.3°C$. **Step 6 — Compute total heat loss:** $$ C + R \approx 1.110 \times 3.826 \times (26.3 - 24) + 3.96 \times 10^{-8} \times 1.110 \times [299.3^4 - 298^4] $$ $$ C + R \approx 9.76 + 4.92 = 14.68 \text{ W/m}^2 $$ $$ E = 3.05 \times 10^{-3} \times (5733 - 6.99 \times 64.02 - 1791.4) + 0.42 \times (64.02 - 58.15) $$ $$ E = 9.81 + 2.47 = 12.28 \text{ W/m}^2 $$ $$ C_{res} + E_{res} = 1.7 \times 10^{-5} \times 64.02 \times (5867 - 1791.4) + 0.0014 \times 64.02 \times (34 - 24) $$ $$ C_{res} + E_{res} = 4.44 + 0.90 = 5.34 \text{ W/m}^2 $$ **Step 7 — Compute $L$ and PMV:** $$ L = 64.02 - (14.68 + 12.28 + 5.34) = 64.02 - 32.30 = 31.72 \text{ W/m}^2 $$ $$ PMV = [0.303 \times e^{-0.036 \times 64.02} + 0.028] \times 31.72 $$ $$ PMV = [0.303 \times 0.0991 + 0.028] \times 31.72 = 0.0580 \times 31.72 = 1.84 $$ **Step 8 — Compute PPD:** $$ PPD = 100 - 95 \times e^{-0.03353 \times (1.84)^4 - 0.2179 \times (1.84)^2} $$ $$ PPD = 100 - 95 \times e^{-0.377 - 0.738} = 100 - 95 \times 0.252 = 76.06\% $$ **Conclusion:** PMV = +1.84 (Warm) and PPD = 76% — **highly uncomfortable** since $PPD \gg 10\%$. The design requires either increased air velocity (ceiling fans), lower air temperature (AC), or reduced MRT (better envelope insulation). --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **(a)** [7 marks] Explain the difference between the **Fanger static comfort model** and the **ASHRAE Adaptive Comfort Model**. Under what climatic conditions is the adaptive model more appropriate? Justify your answer with reference to tropical Indian architecture. **(b)** [7 marks] Discuss in detail the role of **Mean Radiant Temperature (MRT)** in thermal comfort. How is it measured, and how does building envelope design (orientation, glazing, insulation) affect it? **Model Solution for (a):** **Fanger Static Model:** - Based on laboratory experiments with 1300+ subjects in controlled chambers. - Assumes **fixed metabolic rate and clothing insulation** (typically 1.0 met and 0.6 clo). - Thermal sensation is purely a function of four environmental variables ($t_a$, $\bar{t}_r$, $RH$, $V_a$). - Designed for **mechanically conditioned, centrally-controlled buildings**. **ASHRAE Adaptive Model:** - Based on field studies of naturally ventilated buildings. - Recognises that occupants in naturally ventilated buildings have **adapted behaviourally and psychologically** — they open windows, change clothing, use fans, and adjust their expectations. - Comfort temperature is **a function of the prevailing outdoor mean monthly temperature**: $$ T_{comf} = 0.31 \times T_{out,\text{mean monthly}} + 17.8 $$ - Comfort band: $\pm 2.5°C$ around $T_{comf}$ for 80% acceptability. **Tropical Application:** In **Kerala's tropical monsoon climate**, where mean monthly temperatures range from 25°C to 30°C, the adaptive model predicts $T_{comf}$ between 25.5°C and 27°C. This is why traditional Kerala homes with **high ceilings, Mangalore tile roofs, large overhangs, and cross-ventilation** are demonstrably more comfortable than air-conditioned glass-facade buildings — even when indoor temperatures in the traditional building are "high" by AC standards. The Fanger model would falsely flag such homes as "uncomfortable," whereas the adaptive model correctly identifies them as thermally acceptable. --- **Model Solution for (b):** **Definition:** **Mean Radiant Temperature (MRT, $\bar{t}_r$)** is the uniform temperature of an imaginary enclosure in which the radiant heat transfer from the human body equals the radiant heat transfer in the actual non-uniform enclosure. **Measurement:** MRT is measured using a **globe thermometer** (black-painted copper sphere with a thermometer at its centre), corrected for air velocity: $$ \bar{t}_r = \left[ (t_g + 273)^4 + \frac{1.1 \times 10^8 \times V_a^{0.6}}{\epsilon_g \cdot D^{0.4}} \cdot (t_g - t_a) \right]^{1/4} - 273 $$ Where $t_g$ is globe temperature, $D$ is globe diameter, and $\epsilon_g$ is globe emissivity. **Role in Thermal Comfort:** MRT can account for up to **50% of the thermal load** in a space, even when air temperature is set correctly. A cold window pane in winter or a sun-heated wall in summer dramatically shifts $\bar{t}_r$ and hence PMV. **Envelope Design Influence:** | Design Element | Effect on MRT | Architectural Strategy | |----------------|---------------|------------------------| | West-facing glazing (afternoon sun) | MRT ↑↑ | External louvers, low-e glass | | Uninsulated roof | MRT ↑ (in summer) | High SRI paint, false ceiling | | Cavity wall with insulation | MRT stable | Use in tropical envelope | | Heated floor (radiant heating) | MRT ↑ uniformly | Improves comfort at lower $t_a$ | | Skylight with no shading | MRT ↑↑ | Use diffused glazing | **Conclusion:** Effective envelope design in tropical Kerala requires **minimising solar heat gain** through roof insulation (reflective tiles, XPS boards), **double-glazed low-e windows**, and **deep verandahs** — all of which stabilise MRT and reduce cooling loads by 20–30%. --- ### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU 14-Mark Questions:** > 1. **Unit Confusion:** Forgetting to convert $M$ from met to W/m² and $I_{cl}$ from clo to m²·K/W — this error alone typically costs **2–3 marks**. > 2. **Forgetting $f_{cl}$:** Many students skip the clothing area factor, leading to wrong $C + R$ values. Always show the piecewise branch. > 3. **$h_c$ Maximum Rule:** Students often pick natural convection. The correct form is $h_c = \max(h_{c,\text{natural}}, h_{c,\text{forced}})$. > 4. **PMV Sign Convention:** Cold sensation = negative PMV, Warm = positive. Reversed signs = 1 mark penalty. > 5. **PPD Floor Value:** Forgetting that $PPD \geq 5\%$ always (even at $PMV = 0$). The "5% always dissatisfied" is an **ASHRAE 55 must-know**. > 6. **Iteration of $t_{cl}$:** Skipping the iterative solution and just plugging $t_{cl} = t_a$ will result in incorrect $L$ — examiners specifically test the iteration step in 7-mark subparts. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Thermal Comfort** = subjective state of mind expressing satisfaction with the thermal environment (ASHRAE 55 / ISO 7730). - The **6 Fanger Factors**: $t_a$, $\bar{t}_r$, $RH$, $V_a$, $I_{cl}$, $M$. - **Heat Balance Equation:** $M - W = C + R + E + C_{res} + E_{res}$. - **Master PMV Formula:** $PMV = [0.303 e^{-0.036M} + 0.028] \cdot L$. - **PPD Formula:** $PPD = 100 - 95 e^{-0.03353 PMV^4 - 0.2179 PMV^2}$. - **Acceptable Comfort:** $-0.5 \leq PMV \leq +0.5$ → $PPD \leq 10\%$. - **Standard Conversions:** 1 met = 58.2 W/m²; 1 clo = 0.155 m²·K/W. - **Clothing Area Factor:** Branched piecewise function at $I_{cl} = 0.078$ m²·K/W. - **$h_c$ Rule:** Always take the maximum of natural and forced convection expressions. - **Adaptive Model:** $T_{comf} = 0.31 \cdot T_{out,\text{mean monthly}} + 17.8$ — applicable to naturally ventilated buildings (relevant for Kerala architecture). - **Operative Temperature:** $t_o = A \cdot t_a + (1 - A) \cdot \bar{t}_r$ where $A$ depends on air velocity. - **MRT is measured using a globe thermometer** and is the single most influential factor in glass-facade buildings. - **5% always dissatisfied** — even at perfect PMV = 0, there is a population-level dissatisfaction floor. - **Tropical Design:** Use cross-ventilation, high ceilings, reflective roofs, and shading to address all six Fanger factors simultaneously.

topics.title: Effective Temperature, Thermal Comfort Indices (ET, CET Charts, Bioclimatic Chart), Psychrometry and Psychrometric Chart
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET415 - ARCHITECTURAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Effective Temperature, Thermal Comfort Indices (ET, CET Charts, Bioclimatic Chart), Psychrometry and Psychrometric Chart

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Effective Temperature (ET)

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> **Effective Temperature (ET)** is an empirically derived index that combines the effect of air temperature, humidity, and air movement (velocity) into a single number representing the sensation of warmth or cold experienced by a sedentary person in light indoor clothing. It is defined as the temperature of *still, saturated air* which would produce the same sensation of warmth as the actual conditions.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Imagine standing in two different rooms. Room A is 30°C with very high humidity (like a steamy bathroom) and no fan. Room B is 32°C with low humidity (like a dry desert-like room) but with a strong ceiling fan. Subjectively, Room A might feel *stickier and hotter* than Room B, even though Room B has a higher actual temperature. Effective Temperature rolls these three parameters (temperature, humidity, air motion) into one single "feels-like" number so architects and HVAC engineers can compare thermal environments on a common scale. The reference is always **still, saturated air at 100% RH** because that was the easiest condition to replicate in the original ASHVE laboratory tests at the Pittsburgh Experiment Station (1923).

- **Originators:** Houghten & Yaglou (1923) at ASHVE Research Laboratory.
- **Standard indoor conditions tested:** Sedentary person, light clothing (~0.6 clo), still air.
- **Boundary conditions:** Valid for air velocities from **0.1 m/s to 4.0 m/s** and DB temperatures from **−5°C to +40°C**.

---

## 1.2 Corrected Effective Temperature (CET)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> **Corrected Effective Temperature (CET)** is the modern replacement for the classical ET, developed by Vernon & Warner (1930s) and later refined by Gagge et al. The CET incorporates *radiant heat exchange* in addition to air temperature, humidity, and air velocity, producing a more accurate index for indoor thermal comfort assessment.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Classical ET was tested using *black copper sphere simulators* that only accounted for convective and evaporative heat loss. But real human beings also exchange heat with *cold window glass, hot sunlit walls, and radiant floors*. Imagine sitting beside a large single-glazed window on a winter morning — the air temperature reads 22°C, but you feel cold because the cold glass is "pulling" heat from your skin by radiation. CET captures this missing radiant term. So CET = ET + radiation correction.

- **Key innovation:** Uses **globe thermometer temperature ($T_g$)** instead of dry bulb ($T_{db}$) to absorb radiant asymmetry.
- **Reference standard:** ASHRAE Handbook of Fundamentals (Chapter 9 — Thermal Comfort).

---

## 1.3 Bioclimatic Chart (Olgyay's Chart)

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> The **Bioclimatic Chart**, developed by **Victor Olgyay (1963)**, is a graphical design tool that plots *dry bulb temperature* (horizontal axis) against *relative humidity or vapour pressure* (vertical axis) and overlays polygons representing the human *comfort zone* and the *building's microclimatic correction zones* (sun, wind, humidity, evaporation, body radiation).

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Think of the Bioclimatic Chart as a "weather prescription pad" for architects. You plot the outdoor climate data of a site (say, Kochi or Delhi) on the chart, and immediately see whether the building needs *shading, ventilation, insulation, evaporative cooling, or thermal mass*. If the plotted outdoor conditions fall inside the inner comfort polygon, **no mechanical HVAC is needed** — a passive building will suffice. If they fall outside, the chart tells you which passive strategy can "push" the conditions back into comfort.

- **Inner comfort polygon:** Typically defined for **20–24°C** and **30–70% RH** at low air motion.
- **Outer zones (corrections):** Solar gain, wind, internal heat, humidity, evaporation.

---

## 1.4 Psychrometry

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> **Psychrometry** is the science (and engineering practice) that studies the *thermodynamic properties of moist air* — the mixture of dry air and water vapour — and the changes in these properties when air undergoes conditioning processes like heating, cooling, humidification, dehumidification, and mixing.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Atmospheric air is never *just* air — it is a cocktail of dry air + water vapour. Psychrometry is the "bartender's science" of this cocktail. It tells you exactly *how much* water vapour is in the air (humidity ratio), *how hot the air feels once you start cooling it* (wet bulb), *at what temperature condensation will begin* (dew point), and *how much total heat it carries* (enthalpy). HVAC engineers use psychrometry to size cooling coils, estimate condensate drainage, and design energy-efficient buildings.

- **Key constants:**
  - Atmospheric pressure: $P_{atm} = 101.325$ kPa (sea level)
  - Specific heat of dry air: $c_{pa} = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K
  - Specific heat of water vapour: $c_{pw} = 1.82$ kJ/kg·K
  - Latent heat of vaporisation at 0°C: $h_{fg} = 2501$ kJ/kg
  - Molecular mass ratio: $\varepsilon = M_w / M_a = 18.015 / 28.965 = 0.622$

---

## 1.5 Psychrometric Chart

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> The **Psychrometric Chart** is a graphical representation of the thermodynamic state of moist air, plotting *dry bulb temperature* (horizontal axis, °C) against *humidity ratio / moisture content* (vertical axis, g/kg or kg/kg), with diagonal lines for *relative humidity, wet bulb temperature, specific volume, and enthalpy*.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
The Psychrometric Chart is a "thermal map" of moist air — much like a topographic map tells you elevation, this chart tells you the *state* of the air. A single dot on the chart represents a complete snapshot of air: its temperature, humidity, enthalpy, wet-bulb, dew point, and density, all at a glance. HVAC designers trace lines (processes) across this map to model what happens inside an air-handling unit (AHU): cooling, heating, mixing two airstreams, or adding moisture.

- **First developed by:** Willis H. Carrier (1904) — hence sometimes called the **Carrier Chart**.
- **Standard forms:** At $P = 101.325$ kPa (low-elevation) and $P = 90$ kPa (high-altitude).
- **Normal range shown:** $-10°C \leq T_{db} \leq +50°C$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Underlying Principles of the Effective Temperature Index

The ET index is **empirical** (based on human subject tests), not derived from first principles. Its conceptual basis rests on the human body's *heat balance*:

$$Q_{met} = Q_{conv} + Q_{rad} + Q_{evap} + Q_{stored}$$

For thermal neutrality, $Q_{stored} = 0$. The ET test chamber was designed so that the *warmth sensation* of a subject in still, saturated air at temperature $T_{ET}$ matched the sensation in the test condition. Hence ET is a *lines of equal warmth* tool.

- **Step 1:** Subjects enter a chamber where $T_{db}$, RH and $V_{air}$ can be independently controlled.
- **Step 2:** They move between two chambers until they perceive "equal warmth."
- **Step 3:** The temperature of the *reference* (still, 100% RH) chamber is recorded as the ET.
- **Step 4:** The same procedure is repeated across thousands of conditions → family of ET lines drawn on a $T_{db}$ vs RH graph.

**Why is ET useful in architecture?**
- Allows comparison of climates without referring to a single variable.
- Helps decide whether passive cooling, evaporative cooling, or mechanical cooling is needed.
- Forms the basis of Olgyay's Bioclimatic Chart.

---

## 2.2 ET vs CET — Key Distinctions

| Parameter | ET (1923) | CET (1930/1970) |
|---|---|---|
| Variables included | $T_{db}$, RH, $V_{air}$ | $T_{db}$, RH, $V_{air}$, $T_{rad}$ |
| Reference state | Still, 100% RH air | Still, 100% RH air + mean radiant = air temp |
| Radiation accounted | ❌ No | ✅ Yes (via globe thermometer) |
| Validity | Indoor, low velocity | Indoor, low–moderate velocity |
| Modern usage | Largely historical | Preferred in KTU/ASHRAE coursework |

---

## 2.3 Olgyay's Bioclimatic Chart — Zone Interpretation

Olgyay's chart partitions the $T_{db}$–RH plane into:

1. **Inner Comfort Zone (Polygon):** $T_{db} = 20-24°C$, $30\% \leq RH \leq 70\%$, $V_{air} \leq 0.5$ m/s.
2. **Zone of Solar Gain:** Outside comfort, on the cool/cold side → gains from solar radiation can bring it back to comfort.
3. **Zone of Wind Effect:** Hot/humid side → increased air movement can restore comfort.
4. **Zone of Internal Heat:** Cold side, body metabolism + lights offset the chill.
5. **Zone of Humidity Control:** Very dry side → humidification needed; very humid side → dehumidification.
6. **Zone of Evaporative Cooling:** Hot-dry side → sprays, fountains, swamp coolers effective.
7. **Zone of Thermal Mass / Night Purge:** Hot-dry days with cool nights → mass walls store coolth.

**Real-world utility:** A KTU student designing a house in **Thiruvananthapuram** (hot-humid) will see plotted points fall in the *wind + dehumidification* zones → answer: cross-ventilation, sun-shading, raised floors, dehumidification not required if cross-ventilation is strong. A student designing in **Jaisalmer** (hot-dry) will see points in the *evaporative cooling + thermal mass* zones → answer: courtyards with fountains, thick mud walls, roof ponds.

---

## 2.4 Psychrometric Processes on the Chart

The Psychrometric Chart lets us graphically trace the following *fundamental air-conditioning processes*:

| Process | Line Direction on Chart | What Changes |
|---|---|---|
| **Sensible Heating** | Horizontal line to right | $T_{db}$ ↑, $W$ constant |
| **Sensible Cooling** | Horizontal line to left | $T_{db}$ ↓, $W$ constant |
| **Heating & Humidification** | Diagonal up-right | $T_{db}$ ↑, $W$ ↑ |
| **Cooling & Dehumidification** | Down-left, may cross saturation curve | $T_{db}$ ↓, $W$ ↓ (condensate forms) |
| **Adiabatic / Evaporative Cooling** | Along constant wet-bulb line, down-left | $T_{db}$ ↓, $W$ ↑ |
| **Mixing of Two Airstreams** | Straight line connecting state points | New state lies on segment by mass fraction |
| **Bypass / Recirculation** | Mixing line towards return-air point | Saves energy |

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **HIGH-YIELD** formula reference for KTU exams. Memorise the variables and their units first; the equations follow naturally.

| # | Quantity | Symbol | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Saturation vapour pressure (Tetens, 1930) | $p_{ws}$ | $p_{ws} = 0.61078 \cdot \exp\!\left(\dfrac{17.27\,T}{T + 237.3}\right)$ | kPa | $T$ in °C; valid $-40 \leq T \leq 50$°C |
| 2 | Actual vapour pressure | $p_w$ | $p_w = \phi \cdot p_{ws}(T_{db})$ | kPa | $\phi$ = RH in fraction |
| 3 | Humidity ratio (absolute humidity) | $W$ | $W = 0.622 \cdot \dfrac{p_w}{P_{atm} - p_w}$ | kg/kg | Mass of water per kg dry air |
| 4 | Specific volume of moist air | $v$ | $v = \dfrac{R_a \cdot T_{k}}{P_{atm} - p_w}$ | m³/kg dry air | $R_a = 0.287$ kJ/kg·K |
| 5 | Moist-air enthalpy | $h$ | $h = 1.005\,T_{db} + W(2501 + 1.82\,T_{db})$ | kJ/kg dry air | Reference: $0°C$ dry air, $0°C$ liquid water |
| 6 | Wet-bulb temperature (definition) | $T_{wb}$ | $T_{wb} = T_{db} - \dfrac{(P_{atm}-p_{ws,wb})\cdot c_{s}\cdot(T_{db}-T_{wb})}{0.622 \cdot h_{fg}}$ | °C | Implicit equation; solved iteratively |
| 7 | Dew point temperature | $T_{dp}$ | $p_w = p_{ws}(T_{dp})$ → solve for $T_{dp}$ | °C | Condensation onset temperature |
| 8 | Degree of saturation | $\mu$ | $\mu = \dfrac{W}{W_s(T_{db})}$ | dimensionless | $\mu \leq 1$ |
| 9 | Air density (moist) | $\rho_a$ | $\rho_a = \dfrac{1}{v}$ | kg/m³ | |
| 10 | Mixing ratio of two airstreams | $W_3$ | $W_3 = \dfrac{\dot{m}_1 W_1 + \dot{m}_2 W_2}{\dot{m}_1 + \dot{m}_2}$ | kg/kg | Mass-flow weighted average |
| 11 | Mixing temperature | $T_3$ | $T_3 = \dfrac{\dot{m}_1 T_1 + \dot{m}_2 T_2}{\dot{m}_1 + \dot{m}_2}$ | °C | Sensible heat weighted |
| 12 | Heat of evaporation at $T$ | $h_{fg}$ | $h_{fg} \approx 2501 - 2.381\,T$ | kJ/kg | Temperature-corrected latent heat |
| 13 | Specific heat of moist air | $c_{pm}$ | $c_{pm} = c_{pa} + W \cdot c_{pw}$ | kJ/kg·K | |
| 14 | Effective Temperature (qualitative) | $ET$ | Read off ET chart at $(T_{db}, RH, V)$ | °C | Empirical, no closed form |
| 15 | Mean Radiant Temperature (globe) | $T_{mrt}$ | $T_{mrt} = \left[\left(T_g+273\right)^4 + \dfrac{1.1 \cdot 10^8 \cdot V^{0.6}}{\varepsilon_g \cdot D^{0.4}} (T_g - T_{db})\right]^{1/4} - 273$ | °C | Black globe of diameter $D$ (mm) |
| 16 | Operative Temperature | $T_o$ | $T_o = \dfrac{h_c T_{db} + h_r T_{mrt}}{h_c + h_r}$ | °C | Used in CET / modern indices |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Golden Rule for KTU problems:** If a numerical value is missing (e.g., barometric pressure), use **sea-level $P_{atm} = 101.325$ kPa** unless the question specifies otherwise. Always show the *Tetens* equation when computing $p_{ws}$ — this is a frequent mark-earning step in KTU answer sheets.

---

## 2.6 Real-world Engineering Applications

| Field | Application of psychrometry / ET |
|---|---|
| HVAC design | Sizing cooling coils, fans, ducts |
| Building energy simulation | Hourly load calculation (e.g., EnergyPlus, TRACE) |
| Indoor air quality | Predicting condensation on walls, mould growth |
| Industrial drying | Predicting drying rates of grains, timber, textiles |
| Cleanrooms / Hospitals | Tight humidity control ($40\text{–}60\%$) to suppress microbes |
| Cold storage | Avoiding frost deposition by managing dew point |
| Textile manufacturing | Static electricity control via humidity |
| Airport operations | Icing prediction on runways (uses dew point) |

# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation of the Humidity Ratio ($W$)

**Starting point (Dalton's Law of Partial Pressures):**
The total pressure of moist air equals the sum of partial pressures of dry air and water vapour:

$$P_{atm} = p_a + p_w$$

**Step 1:** Express the partial pressure of dry air:
$$p_a = P_{atm} - p_w$$

**Step 2:** Recall the *ideal gas equation* in molar form for each component:
$$p_a V = n_a R_u T \quad ; \quad p_w V = n_w R_u T$$

**Step 3:** Take the ratio of the two equations. Volume $V$, gas constant $R_u$ and temperature $T$ all cancel:

$$\dfrac{p_w}{p_a} = \dfrac{n_w}{n_a} = \dfrac{m_w / M_w}{m_a / M_a}$$

**Step 4:** Rearrange using $W = m_w / m_a$:

$$\dfrac{p_w}{p_a} = W \cdot \dfrac{M_a}{M_w} = \dfrac{W}{0.622}$$

**Step 5:** Substitute $p_a = P_{atm} - p_w$:

$$\dfrac{p_w}{P_{atm} - p_w} = \dfrac{W}{0.622}$$

**Step 6:** Solve for $W$ (final form):

$$W = 0.622 \cdot \dfrac{p_w}{P_{atm} - p_w}$$

> ✅ **Physical meaning:** $W$ is the mass of water vapour (kg) associated with **1 kg of dry air**. It is the *true* measure of moisture — independent of temperature or pressure changes of the air itself.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Moist-Air Enthalpy ($h$)

**Starting point:** Enthalpy is an *extensive* property, so the total enthalpy of a moist-air sample is the sum of dry-air enthalpy and water-vapour enthalpy (per kg dry air):

$$h = h_a + W \cdot h_v$$

**Step 1:** Enthalpy of dry air (sensible only, $0°C$ reference):

$$h_a = c_{pa} \cdot T_{db} = 1.005 \, T_{db} \quad \text{(kJ/kg dry air)}$$

**Step 2:** Enthalpy of water vapour = latent heat + sensible heat above $0°C$:

$$h_v = h_{fg,0} + c_{pw} \cdot T_{db} = 2501 + 1.82 \, T_{db} \quad \text{(kJ/kg vapour)}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute into the total enthalpy:

$$h = c_{pa}\,T_{db} + W(h_{fg,0} + c_{pw}\,T_{db})$$

**Step 4:** Group terms:

$$h = (1.005 + 1.82\,W)\,T_{db} + 2501\,W$$

> ✅ **Physical meaning:** The first term is the *sensible heat* of the air-vapour mixture; the second term is the *latent heat* carried by the vapour. This is why high humidity makes air feel "heavy" — the same temperature holds much more energy.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of the Wet-Bulb Temperature (Energy Balance)

**Setup:** A thermometer bulb wrapped in a wet wick, exposed to unsaturated air moving at velocity $V$. Evaporation from the wick cools the bulb until the rate of evaporative heat loss equals the rate of convective heat gain from the air. The final steady temperature is $T_{wb}$.

**Energy balance at steady state:** Heat lost by evaporation = Heat gained by convection.

**Step 1:** Evaporative heat loss (latent):

$$q_{evap} = \dot{m}_w \cdot h_{fg,wb} = h_m (p_{ws}(T_{wb}) - p_w) \cdot h_{fg,wb}$$

where $h_m$ is the *mass-transfer coefficient*.

**Step 2:** Convective heat gain (sensible):

$$q_{conv} = h_c (T_{db} - T_{wb})$$

**Step 3:** Set $q_{evap} = q_{conv}$:

$$h_m (p_{ws}(T_{wb}) - p_w) \cdot h_{fg,wb} = h_c (T_{db} - T_{wb})$$

**Step 4:** Use the *Lewis relation* $h_c / h_m = c_{pm} \approx c_s$ (the *psychrometric* constant):

$$c_s (p_{ws}(T_{wb}) - p_w)\,h_{fg,wb} = c_{pm} (T_{db} - T_{wb})$$

**Step 5:** Solve for the actual vapour pressure $p_w$ (the most useful form for engineering):

$$p_w = p_{ws}(T_{wb}) - \dfrac{c_{pm}\,(P_{atm} - p_{ws}(T_{wb}))(T_{db} - T_{wb})}{0.622 \cdot h_{fg,wb}}$$

**Step 6:** With $p_w$ known, the humidity ratio follows directly:

$$W = 0.622 \cdot \dfrac{p_w}{P_{atm} - p_w}$$

> ✅ **Physical meaning:** $T_{wb}$ is a *thermodynamic* temperature — it is the same as the temperature an *adiabatic saturation process* would yield if a stream of air were brought to saturation by spraying liquid water. The wet-bulb line on the chart represents a *constant-enthalpy* process (approximately).

---

## 3.4 Derivation of the Dew Point Temperature

**Definition:** $T_{dp}$ is the temperature at which the *actual* vapour pressure $p_w$ equals the *saturation* vapour pressure at that temperature:

$$p_w = p_{ws}(T_{dp})$$

**Step 1:** Substitute Tetens' equation for $p_{ws}$:

$$p_w = 0.61078 \cdot \exp\!\left(\dfrac{17.27\,T_{dp}}{T_{dp} + 237.3}\right)$$

**Step 2:** Take the natural log of both sides:

$$\ln\!\left(\dfrac{p_w}{0.61078}\right) = \dfrac{17.27\,T_{dp}}{T_{dp} + 237.3}$$

**Step 3:** Let $A = \ln(p_w / 0.61078)$. Then:

$$A(T_{dp} + 237.3) = 17.27\,T_{dp}$$
$$A \cdot T_{dp} + 237.3\,A = 17.27\,T_{dp}$$
$$T_{dp}(17.27 - A) = 237.3\,A$$

**Step 4:** Final explicit solution:

$$T_{dp} = \dfrac{237.3 \cdot A}{17.27 - A} \quad \text{where} \quad A = \ln\!\left(\dfrac{p_w}{0.61078}\right)$$

> ✅ **Physical meaning:** Cooling air *below* $T_{dp}$ causes condensation. This is the principle behind "morning dew" on grass, fog formation, and condensation dripping from cold AC ducts in humid climates (e.g., KTU students in coastal Kerala will notice this on a hot day when AC starts!).

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

> **Problem:** Outdoor air is at $T_{db} = 35°C$, RH = 60%. Find $W$, $h$, $T_{wb}$ (approx.), and $T_{dp}$. Take $P_{atm} = 101.325$ kPa.

**Step 1 — Saturation vapour pressure at 35°C:**

$$p_{ws}(35) = 0.61078 \cdot \exp\!\left(\dfrac{17.27 \cdot 35}{35 + 237.3}\right) = 0.61078 \cdot \exp(2.2176) = 0.61078 \cdot 9.190 = 5.614\ \text{kPa}$$

**Step 2 — Actual vapour pressure:**

$$p_w = 0.60 \cdot 5.614 = 3.368\ \text{kPa}$$

**Step 3 — Humidity ratio:**

$$W = 0.622 \cdot \dfrac{3.368}{101.325 - 3.368} = 0.622 \cdot \dfrac{3.368}{97.957} = 0.02138\ \text{kg/kg} = 21.38\ \text{g/kg}$$

**Step 4 — Enthalpy:**

$$h = 1.005(35) + 0.02138 \cdot (2501 + 1.82 \cdot 35)$$
$$h = 35.175 + 0.02138 \cdot (2501 + 63.7)$$
$$h = 35.175 + 0.02138 \cdot 2564.7$$
$$h = 35.175 + 54.83 = 90.01\ \text{kJ/kg dry air}$$

**Step 5 — Dew point (iterative or via $T_{dp}$ formula):**

$$A = \ln(3.368 / 0.61078) = \ln(5.514) = 1.707$$
$$T_{dp} = \dfrac{237.3 \cdot 1.707}{17.27 - 1.707} = \dfrac{405.0}{15.56} = 26.0°C$$

**Step 6 — Wet bulb (iterative):** Try $T_{wb} = 28°C$.
- $p_{ws}(28) = 0.61078 \cdot \exp(17.27 \cdot 28 / 265.3) = 0.61078 \cdot \exp(1.8218) = 0.61078 \cdot 6.182 = 3.776$ kPa
- $h_{fg,28} \approx 2501 - 2.381(28) = 2434$ kJ/kg
- $c_{pm} \approx 1.005 + 0.02138 \cdot 1.82 = 1.0439$ kJ/kg·K
- Numerator: $1.0439 \cdot (101.325 - 3.776) \cdot (35 - 28) = 1.0439 \cdot 97.549 \cdot 7 = 712.6$
- Denominator: $0.622 \cdot 2434 = 1514$
- Estimated $p_w = 3.776 - 712.6/1514 = 3.776 - 0.471 = 3.305$ kPa
- We need 3.368 kPa, so $T_{wb}$ is slightly less than 28°C. Try 27.8°C → converge to $\approx 27.9°C$.

> ✅ **Final answers:** $W = 21.4$ g/kg, $h = 90.0$ kJ/kg, $T_{dp} = 26.0°C$, $T_{wb} \approx 27.9°C$.
