# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Dec 2024 Style]**

### Q1. Define Effective Temperature (ET) and state its limitations.
*(3 marks — CO2, Bloom Level: Remember/Understand)*

**Model Answer:**
Effective Temperature (ET) is an empirical index developed by Houghten and Yaglou (1923) representing the temperature of *still, saturated air* that produces the same sensation of warmth as the actual combination of dry-bulb temperature, relative humidity, and air velocity.

**Limitations:**
1. Does not account for **radiant heat exchange** (this was later addressed by CET).
2. Valid only for **sedentary occupants** in light clothing (~0.6 clo).
3. Valid only for **air velocity in the range 0.1 to 4.0 m/s**; outside this, predictions are unreliable.
4. Test subjects were predominantly young adult males, limiting generalisation across age, sex, and ethnicity.
5. Strictly an *indoor* index — does not account for solar radiation on people outdoors.

---

### Q2. List the main axes and overlaid lines of a standard Psychrometric Chart.
*(3 marks — CO3, Bloom Level: Remember)*

**Model Answer:**
A standard psychrometric chart has:
- **Horizontal axis:** Dry-bulb temperature $T_{db}$ in °C (range typically $-10°C$ to $+50°C$).
- **Vertical axis:** Humidity ratio $W$ in kg water/kg dry air (or g/kg).
- **Curved lines:** Saturation (100% RH) curve along the top; family of relative humidity curves (10%, 20%, … 90%).
- **Diagonal lines (downward right):** Wet-bulb temperature $T_{wb}$ (approximately constant-enthalpy).
- **Horizontal lines:** Constant wet-bulb depression and dew-point lines.
- **Near-horizontal lines (slightly inclined):** Enthalpy $h$ in kJ/kg dry air.
- **Near-vertical curves:** Specific volume $v$ in m³/kg dry air.
- **Saturation curve:** Boundary of two-phase region — points to the left are single-phase moist air; points on the curve are saturated; points to the right are physically impossible (fog/mist).

---

## 5.2 Part B: Full-Length Questions (14 Marks each — Module-3 Internal Choice)

> **[KTU End Semester Exam Pattern — Module 3, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

---

### ✅ QUESTION A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, May 2024 Style]**

**(a)** Define Effective Temperature (ET) and Corrected Effective Temperature (CET). Explain the role of the globe thermometer in CET determination with a neat diagram. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A building interior has $T_{db} = 30°C$, RH = 55%, mean radiant temperature $T_{mrt} = 32°C$, and air velocity $V = 0.2$ m/s. Using the ET chart (assumed), the Effective Temperature is found to be $27°C$. Determine the Corrected Effective Temperature and comment on whether the indoor condition is within the ASHRAE summer comfort band. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a):

**Step 1 — Definition of ET (2 marks):**
Effective Temperature is the index that combines the effects of dry-bulb temperature, relative humidity, and air movement into a single value, defined as the temperature of *still, saturated air* producing the same thermal sensation as the actual environment. Originally determined by Houghten and Yaglou (1923) through controlled-chamber human subject tests.

**Step 2 — Definition of CET (2 marks):**
Corrected Effective Temperature is the modified index that additionally incorporates *radiant heat exchange*. It is defined as the temperature of *still, saturated air* at zero radiant asymmetry that produces the same sensation as the actual environment. Developed by Vernon and Warner (1930) and refined by Gagge (1970s).

**Step 3 — Globe Thermometer and its role (2 marks):**
A globe thermometer is a hollow copper sphere (typically 150 mm diameter) painted matte black, with a mercury-in-glass or thermistor sensor at its centre. The globe absorbs short-wave and long-wave radiation from the surroundings and is also cooled by convection and evaporation (if wetted). Its equilibrium temperature $T_g$ combines the effect of air temperature, air velocity, and mean radiant temperature. CET uses $T_g$ (or its derived $T_{mrt}$) in place of $T_{db}$ to absorb the radiation term.

**Step 4 — Sketch (1 mark — to be drawn in exam):**
A neat labelled diagram showing:
- Hollow black copper sphere
- Sensor at centre
- Surrounding environment emitting radiation
- Wind arrow showing $V$

**Conclusion:**
CET is the more accurate modern index because real occupants exchange heat with windows, walls, and the sun, and the globe thermometer integrates these radiation effects into a single measurable temperature.

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b):

**Given:** $T_{db} = 30°C$, $T_{mrt} = 32°C$, $V = 0.2$ m/s, ET (from chart) = 27°C.

**Step 1 — Approximate CET correction (3 marks):**
For low air velocity ($V \leq 0.3$ m/s) and small radiation asymmetry, the CET is often approximated as:

$$CET \approx ET + \dfrac{T_{mrt} - T_{db}}{2}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute (2 marks):**

$$CET = 27 + \dfrac{32 - 30}{2} = 27 + 1 = 28°C$$

**Step 3 — Compare with ASHRAE summer comfort band (2 marks):**
ASHRAE Standard 55 specifies the summer indoor operative temperature range as approximately **22.5°C to 25.5°C** with 50% RH. A CET of 28°C lies *above* the upper bound, indicating the indoor environment is **warmer than the summer comfort range**. The occupants would feel slightly warm; corrective measures like increased ventilation, lighter wall colours, or a chiller are advised.

**Conclusion (extra line for full marks):**
The given indoor condition is **outside the comfort band**; design recommendations: provide shading on west-facing glazing, increase cross-ventilation, and consider ceiling fan for additional air motion.

---

### ✅ QUESTION B (Choice 2 — Internal Choice) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Dec 2023 Style]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of the Bioclimatic Chart developed by Olgyay. With the help of a labelled diagram, list the various microclimatic correction zones and explain how a designer would use the chart for a *hot-dry* climate city such as Jaisalmer. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Outdoor air is at $T_{db} = 40°C$, RH = 25%. The air passes through an evaporative cooler (assuming 90% saturation efficiency) and then through a chilled-water coil. The coil brings the air to $T_{db} = 22°C$ and RH = 90%. Compute the humidity ratio, enthalpy, dew point, and density of: (i) outdoor air, (ii) air leaving the evaporative cooler, (iii) air leaving the cooling coil. Take $P_{atm} = 101.325$ kPa. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a):

**Step 1 — Bioclimatic Chart definition (2 marks):**
Olgyay's Bioclimatic Chart (1963) is a graphical tool that maps *dry-bulb temperature* (x-axis) against *relative humidity* (y-axis) and overlays the human comfort polygon along with the *building envelope correction zones*. It helps architects identify which passive design strategy can bring outdoor conditions into the comfort zone without active HVAC.

**Step 2 — Diagram description (1 mark — to be drawn):**
A neat sketch showing the comfort polygon in the centre surrounded by labelled zones:
- Zone A: Solar gain (cooler side)
- Zone B: Wind effect (hotter/humid side)
- Zone C: Internal heat (cold side)
- Zone D: Humidity control
- Zone E: Evaporative cooling
- Zone F: Thermal mass / night ventilation

**Step 3 — Six correction zones (2 marks):**
1. **Solar radiation** — for cool/cold days, solar gain can shift the effective condition upward into comfort.
2. **Wind** — for hot/humid days, increased air movement raises evaporative cooling.
3. **Internal heat** — body metabolic heat + lights + appliances can offset cold.
4. **Humidity** — for dry or very humid sides, humidification/dehumidification may be required.
5. **Evaporation** — for hot-dry conditions, water sprays and fountains cool by evaporation.
6. **Thermal mass + night purge** — for hot days and cool nights, mass walls store coolth.

**Step 4 — Application to Jaisalmer (hot-dry) (2 marks):**
Jaisalmer's annual mean conditions plot in the **hot-dry region** (high $T_{db}$, low RH). Reading the chart:
- The plotted point lies in the **evaporative cooling** zone → design with **courtyard fountains, jharokhas with water bodies, and double-roof construction** for evaporative cooling.
- It also touches the **thermal mass** zone → use **thick sandstone walls and mud-plastered roofs** to absorb daytime heat and release at night via cross-ventilation.
- Conclusion: passive design (no mechanical AC) is feasible if these strategies are combined.

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b):

**Given:** State 1 (outdoor): $T_1 = 40°C$, $\phi_1 = 25\%$. State 2 (after evaporative cooler, 90% saturation efficiency): $T_2 = T_{wb,1} \times$ efficiency effectively. For 90% saturation efficiency, $T_2 \approx T_{wb,1} + 0.1(T_{db,1} - T_{wb,1})$; first find $T_{wb,1}$.

**Stage 1 — Outdoor air (State 1):**

**Step 1 — Saturation pressure at 40°C (0.5 mark):**
$$p_{ws}(40) = 0.61078 \cdot \exp\!\left(\dfrac{17.27 \cdot 40}{40 + 237.3}\right) = 0.61078 \cdot \exp(2.4889) = 0.61078 \cdot 12.05 = 7.36\ \text{kPa}$$

**Step 2 — Vapour pressure (0.5 mark):**
$$p_{w,1} = 0.25 \cdot 7.36 = 1.840\ \text{kPa}$$

**Step 3 — Humidity ratio (0.5 mark):**
$$W_1 = 0.622 \cdot \dfrac{1.840}{101.325 - 1.840} = 0.622 \cdot \dfrac{1.840}{99.485} = 0.01150\ \text{kg/kg} = 11.50\ \text{g/kg}$$

**Step 4 — Enthalpy (0.5 mark):**
$$h_1 = 1.005(40) + 0.01150(2501 + 1.82 \cdot 40)$$
$$h_1 = 40.20 + 0.01150(2501 + 72.8) = 40.20 + 0.01150 \cdot 2573.8 = 40.20 + 29.60 = 69.80\ \text{kJ/kg}$$

**Step 5 — Dew point (0.5 mark):**
$$A_1 = \ln(1.840 / 0.61078) = \ln(3.0125) = 1.1029$$
$$T_{dp,1} = \dfrac{237.3 \cdot 1.1029}{17.27 - 1.1029} = \dfrac{261.72}{16.167} = 16.19°C$$

**Step 6 — Specific volume (0.5 mark):**
$$v_1 = \dfrac{R_a \cdot T_{k}}{P_{atm} - p_{w,1}} = \dfrac{0.287 \cdot (40 + 273.15)}{101.325 - 1.840} = \dfrac{0.287 \cdot 313.15}{99.485} = \dfrac{89.87}{99.485} = 0.9034\ \text{m}^3/\text{kg}$$
$$\rho_1 = 1 / 0.9034 = 1.107\ \text{kg/m}^3$$

**Stage 2 — Air leaving evaporative cooler (State 2):**

**Step 7 — Wet-bulb of state 1 (0.5 mark):** Iterative (try $T_{wb} = 23°C$): $p_{ws}(23) = 2.81$ kPa. Numerator = $1.025 \cdot 98.51 \cdot 17 = 1715.6$; denominator = $0.622 \cdot 2446 = 1521.4$; $p_w = 2.81 - 1.128 = 1.682$ kPa. Need 1.840 → try 24°C. Iterate to $T_{wb,1} \approx 24.5°C$.

**Step 8 — State 2 with 90% saturation efficiency (0.5 mark):**
$$T_2 = T_{wb,1} + 0.1 (T_1 - T_{wb,1}) = 24.5 + 0.1(40 - 24.5) = 24.5 + 1.55 = 26.05°C$$
$$\phi_2 = 90\%$$

**Step 9 — Saturation pressure at 26.05°C ≈ 3.36 kPa; $p_{w,2} = 0.9 \cdot 3.36 = 3.024$ kPa (0.5 mark)**

**Step 10 — $W_2 = 0.622 \cdot 3.024 / (101.325 - 3.024) = 0.622 \cdot 3.024/98.301 = 0.01914$ kg/kg = 19.14 g/kg (0.5 mark)**

**Stage 3 — Air leaving cooling coil (State 3):**

**Step 11 — Sensible cooling + dehumidification. $T_3 = 22°C$, $\phi_3 = 90\%$. $p_{ws}(22) = 0.61078 \cdot \exp(17.27 \cdot 22 / 259.3) = 0.61078 \cdot 4.197 = 2.564$ kPa (0.25 mark)**

**Step 12 — $p_{w,3} = 0.9 \cdot 2.564 = 2.308$ kPa. $W_3 = 0.622 \cdot 2.308 / (101.325 - 2.308) = 0.622 \cdot 2.308 / 99.017 = 0.01449$ kg/kg = 14.49 g/kg (0.25 mark)**

**Step 13 — Enthalpies (0.5 mark):**
$$h_2 = 1.005(26.05) + 0.01914(2501 + 1.82 \cdot 26.05) = 26.18 + 0.01914 \cdot 2548.4 = 26.18 + 48.78 = 74.96\ \text{kJ/kg}$$
$$h_3 = 1.005(22) + 0.01449(2501 + 1.82 \cdot 22) = 22.11 + 0.01449 \cdot 2541.04 = 22.11 + 36.82 = 58.93\ \text{kJ/kg}$$

**Step 14 — Dew points and densities (0.5 mark):**
- $T_{dp,2}$: $A = \ln(3.024/0.61078) = 1.598$; $T_{dp,2} = 237.3 \cdot 1.598 / (17.27 - 1.598) = 24.2°C$
- $T_{dp,3}$: $A = \ln(2.308/0.61078) = 1.328$; $T_{dp,3} = 237.3 \cdot 1.328 / (17.27 - 1.328) = 19.7°C$
- $v_2 = 0.287 \cdot 299.2 / 98.301 = 0.873\ \text{m}^3/\text{kg}$, $\rho_2 = 1.145\ \text{kg/m}^3$
- $v_3 = 0.287 \cdot 295.15 / 99.017 = 0.855\ \text{m}^3/\text{kg}$, $\rho_3 = 1.170\ \text{kg/m}^3$

**Summary Table:**

| State | $T_{db}$ (°C) | RH (%) | $W$ (g/kg) | $h$ (kJ/kg) | $T_{dp}$ (°C) | $\rho$ (kg/m³) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 — Outdoor | 40 | 25 | 11.50 | 69.80 | 16.2 | 1.107 |
| 2 — After evap cooler | 26.05 | 90 | 19.14 | 74.96 | 24.2 | 1.145 |
| 3 — After cooling coil | 22 | 90 | 14.49 | 58.93 | 19.7 | 1.170 |

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students LOSE MARKS in this module:**
> 1. **Unit errors:** Writing $W$ in kg/kg without converting to g/kg when comparing with chart values (chart is in g/kg). **Always state the unit explicitly.**
> 2. **Forgetting to convert °C to K** in Tetens' exponent when using some alternate equations.
> 3. **Mixing up $T_{wb}$ and $T_{dp}$:** $T_{wb} \leq T_{db}$ (always) and $T_{dp} \leq T_{wb}$ (always). A numerical answer violating this ordering guarantees a zero.
> 4. **Using 2501 kJ/kg as a constant** without correcting for temperature ($h_{fg} = 2501 - 2.381 T$). At 35°C, the difference is small (~2%), but at 0°C vs 50°C the error becomes ~12%.
> 5. **Skipping the Tetens step** in exam answers — KTU examiners expect to see the explicit $p_{ws}$ calculation before $W$, $h$, or $T_{dp}$.
> 6. **For Bioclimatic Chart questions:** not naming the *passive design strategy* for the given climate. A "list of zones" without a *climatic application* fetches only 3 of 7 marks.
> 7. **For ET/CET questions:** not drawing the globe thermometer (1-mark sketch component).
> 8. **Mixing-air problem:** writing $W_3 = (W_1 + W_2)/2$ instead of the *mass-flow weighted* formula. Air with higher $\dot{m}$ dominates.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (KTU Module 3)**

- 🔹 **ET** = empirical, $T_{db}$+RH+$V$, still-air saturation reference; tested on sedentary humans in 0.6 clo; limited to 0.1–4.0 m/s.
- 🔹 **CET** = ET + radiant correction; uses **globe thermometer** ($T_g$); modern preferred index.
- 🔹 **Operative Temperature** $T_o = (h_c T_{db} + h_r T_{mrt})/(h_c + h_r)$ is today's mainstream comfort metric.
- 🔹 **Bioclimatic Chart (Olgyay):** x = $T_{db}$, y = RH, inner comfort polygon + 6 microclimatic correction zones.
- 🔹 **Passive strategies by climate:**
  - *Hot-dry* → evaporative cooling + thermal mass + night flush.
  - *Hot-humid* → cross-ventilation + shading + dehumidification.
  - *Cold-dry* → solar gain + insulation + internal heat.
  - *Cold-humid* → heating + dehumidification.
- 🔹 **Psychrometric Chart carrier** invented by Willis Carrier (1904).
- 🔹 **Tetens' equation** $p_{ws} = 0.61078 \cdot \exp(17.27 T / (T + 237.3))$ is the KTU-approved $p_{ws}$ formula.
- 🔹 **Humidity ratio** $W = 0.622 \cdot p_w / (P_{atm} - p_w)$ — mass of water per kg of **dry** air (not moist air).
- 🔹 **Enthalpy** $h = 1.005 T_{db} + W(2501 + 1.82 T_{db})$ — always relative to 0°C dry air and 0°C liquid water.
- 🔹 **Dew point** satisfies $p_w = p_{ws}(T_{dp})$; below $T_{dp}$ → condensation.
- 🔹 **Wet bulb** is implicit; energy balance between convective sensible and evaporative latent using Lewis relation.
- 🔹 **Lewis relation** $h_c/h_m = c_{pm} \approx 1.005 + 1.82W$ — the *psychrometric* constant.
- 🔹 **Cooling coil** → straight (or slightly down-left) line on chart, often crosses saturation curve → condensate forms.
- 🔹 **Sensible heat factor (SHF)** = SH/(SH+LH); used to choose coil bypass factor in psychrometric problems.
- 🔹 **Bypass factor (BF)** = $(T_2 - T_{apparatus\,dew\,point})/(T_1 - T_{apparatus\,dew\,point})$; BF = 0 → perfect contact, BF = 1 → no contact.
- 🔹 **Mixing** of two airstreams → straight line on chart, dividing the line in inverse ratio of mass flows.
- 🔹 **Always quote state-point coordinates** $(T_{db}, W, h, T_{dp}, \rho)$ as a summary in numerical problems.
- 🔹 **Cited codes:** ASHRAE 55 (Thermal Comfort), ISHRAE Handbook (Indian context), NBC 2016 (India), ECBC 2017 (Energy Conservation Building Code).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Earth-Sun Relationship: Sun's Apparent Movement with Respect to the Earth, Solar Angles, Computation of Solar Radiation on Different Surfaces, Solar Path Diagram, Shadow Throw Concept and Design of Shading Devices
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET415 - ARCHITECTURAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Earth-Sun Relationship: Sun's Apparent Movement with Respect to the Earth, Solar Angles, Computation of Solar Radiation on Different Surfaces, Solar Path Diagram, Shadow Throw Concept and Design of Shading Devices

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Earth-Sun Relationship — Formal Definition

The **Earth-Sun Relationship** is the study of the geometric and thermodynamic interaction between the Earth's surface and the Sun's incoming electromagnetic radiation, as modulated by the Earth's rotation about its tilted axis (axial tilt of **23.45°**) and its revolution around the Sun on an elliptical orbit (eccentricity ≈ **0.0167**). For architectural engineering, this relationship is foundational because it determines:
- The position of the Sun in the sky at any instant of any day at any latitude.
- The intensity and direction of solar energy striking a building's envelope.
- The geometry of shadows cast by shading devices (overhangs, fins, louvers).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — PECET415 Module 3**
> The core deliverables of this module are: (a) computation of solar angles (declination, hour, altitude, azimuth, zenith, profile, wall-solar), (b) computation of total solar radiation incident on horizontal, vertical, and tilted surfaces, (c) plotting and interpretation of the solar path diagram (stereographic, orthographic, and altitude-azimuth projections), and (d) design of external shading devices using shadow-angle and profile-angle principles.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy — "The Tilted Lamp and the Spinning Top"**
> Imagine a desk lamp (the Sun) fixed at the centre of a room. Now place a tilted, spinning top (the Earth) at a fixed spot in the room. As the top spins on its own axis (rotation → day and night) and you slowly walk it around the lamp (revolution → seasons), the angle at which the lamp's light strikes any particular point on the top keeps changing. The spot on the top that is directly under the lamp's beam changes as the top tilts and spins. The room's "north wall" is sometimes fully lit, sometimes half in shadow, and sometimes completely dark — depending on where the top is in its slow dance. **Solar geometry is simply the mathematical language we use to describe "where is the lamp's beam hitting the top right now?"** Architects use this language to decide how deep an overhang to build, how to orient windows, and how to keep the afternoon sun out of a west-facing bedroom.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants (Standard Reference Values)

| Constant | Symbol | Standard Value |
|----------|--------|----------------|
| **Solar constant** | $I_{sc}$ or $G_{sc}$ | **1367 W/m²** (WMO 1981 standard) |
| Mean Earth–Sun distance | $d_m$ | **1 AU ≈ 1.496 × 10⁸ km** |
| Earth's axial tilt (obliquity) | $\varepsilon$ | **23.45°** (≈ 23°27') |
| Earth's rotational angular velocity | $\omega_e$ | **15°/hour** (= 360°/24 h) |
| Standard atmospheric pressure | $P_0$ | **101.325 kPa** |
| Albedo of typical ground | $\rho_g$ | **0.2** (dry grass/concrete average) |
| Stefan–Boltzmann constant | $\sigma$ | **5.67 × 10⁻⁸ W/m²·K⁴** |

## 1.4 The Apparent Movement of the Sun

Because the Earth rotates eastward, the **Sun appears to move westward** across the sky at **15° per hour** of solar time. The yearly path shifts north–south due to the **23.45°** axial tilt, producing the seasons. The Sun's midday altitude at any latitude $\phi$ ranges from:
- **Summer solstice (n = 172):** $\alpha_{noon,max} = 90° - \phi + 23.45°$
- **Winter solstice (n = 355):** $\alpha_{noon,min} = 90° - \phi - 23.45°$

For KTU-exam-relevant cities such as **Thiruvananthapuram ($\phi \approx 8.5°N$)** and **Kochi ($\phi \approx 10°N$)**, the Sun crosses nearly overhead twice a year (zenith passage) — a fact that critically governs shading strategy in tropical Kerala architecture.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for Kerala:** At $\phi \approx 10°N$, the Sun's altitude at solar noon on equinoxes is $90° - 10° = 80°$, and on summer solstice it is $90° - 10° + 23.45° = 103.45°$ (i.e., the Sun is slightly **north of zenith**). This means a flat horizontal overhang is largely useless against high-summer sun, and **vertical fins on east/west walls become the dominant shading strategy.**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Components of Solar Time and Position

### 2.1.1 Solar Declination Angle ($\delta$)
The angle between the Sun's rays and the equatorial plane. It varies sinusoidally between **−23.45°** (winter solstice, Dec 21–22) and **+23.45°** (summer solstice, Jun 21–22). The closed-form **Spencer (1971)** equation and the simpler **Cooper (1969)** equation used in KTU problems are:

$$\delta = 23.45° \times \sin\left[\frac{360°}{365}(284 + n)\right]$$

where $n$ is the **day-of-the-year** (1 = Jan 1, 365 = Dec 31).

### 2.1.2 Hour Angle ($\omega$)
The angular displacement of the Sun from the local meridian, positive **west of meridian** (afternoon), negative in the morning. It converts solar time to angular measure:

$$\omega = 15° \times (t_{solar} - 12) \quad \text{[degrees]}$$

At solar noon, $\omega = 0°$; at 6 a.m., $\omega = -90°$; at 6 p.m., $\omega = +90°$.

### 2.1.3 Altitude Angle ($\alpha$) — "How high is the Sun?"
The angle between the Sun's rays and the horizontal plane. Governs shadow length and overheat risk.

$$\sin \alpha = \sin \phi \cdot \sin \delta + \cos \phi \cdot \cos \delta \cdot \cos \omega$$

### 2.1.4 Zenith Angle ($\theta_z$)
The complement of the altitude; the angle from the vertical (zenith) to the Sun:

$$\theta_z = 90° - \alpha$$

### 2.1.5 Azimuth Angle ($A$) — "Which compass direction?"
Measured clockwise from true North. **KTU convention** is $0° = N, 90° = E, 180° = S, 270° = W$.

$$\cos A = \frac{\sin \alpha \cdot \sin \phi - \sin \delta}{\cos \alpha \cdot \cos \phi}$$

A **sign ambiguity** arises: a positive azimuth means the Sun is in the **eastern sky** (morning, $\omega < 0$), negative means **western sky** (afternoon, $\omega > 0$). Many KTU solutions use the explicit form:

$$A = 180° \pm \gamma_s$$

where $\gamma_s$ (wall-solar azimuth) is the surface azimuth measured from south.

### 2.1.6 Wall-Solar Azimuth ($\gamma_s$)
The horizontal angle between the wall's outward normal and the Sun's projection. For a wall with surface azimuth $\gamma_w$:

$$\gamma_s = A - \gamma_w$$

## 2.2 The Tilted Surface Angles (Building-Integrated Geometry)

When solar radiation strikes a tilted or vertical building surface, the **angle of incidence** $\theta$ (between the Sun's rays and the surface normal) is given by the **general 2-axis tilt formula**:

$$\cos \theta = \begin{aligned}[t] & \sin \delta \cdot \sin \phi \cdot \cos \beta \\ & - \sin \delta \cdot \cos \phi \cdot \sin \beta \cdot \cos \gamma_w \\ & + \cos \delta \cdot \cos \phi \cdot \cos \beta \cdot \cos \omega \\ & + \cos \delta \cdot \sin \phi \cdot \sin \beta \cdot \cos \gamma_w \cdot \cos \omega \\ & + \cos \delta \cdot \sin \beta \cdot \sin \gamma_w \cdot \sin \omega \end{aligned}$$

where:
- $\beta$ = **surface tilt angle from horizontal** (0° = horizontal roof, 90° = vertical wall).
- $\gamma_w$ = **surface azimuth** (deviation of surface normal from true south; west positive, east negative).
- For a **horizontal surface** ($\beta = 0°$), this reduces to $\cos \theta_z = \sin \alpha$, i.e., $\theta = \theta_z$ ✓
- For a **vertical south-facing surface** ($\beta = 90°, \gamma_w = 0°$), it reduces to $\cos \theta = \cos \delta \cdot \cos \omega$ ✓

## 2.3 Air Mass (m) and Atmospheric Attenuation

The **Air Mass** quantifies the path length of solar radiation through the atmosphere relative to the shortest path (Sun at zenith):

$$m = \frac{1}{\cos \theta_z} = \frac{1}{\sin \alpha}$$

For $\theta_z > 90°$ (Sun below horizon), $m$ is undefined (no direct beam). The **Kasten-Young (1989)** refinement is used in advanced problems:

$$m = \frac{1}{\cos \theta_z + 0.50572 \cdot (96.07995° - \theta_z)^{-1.6364}}$$

The **extraterrestrial normal irradiance** $I_0$ on day $n$ varies with the Earth–Sun distance $r$:

$$I_0 = I_{sc} \cdot \left(\frac{d_m}{d}\right)^2 = 1367 \cdot \left(1 + 0.033 \cdot \cos \frac{360° \cdot n}{365}\right) \text{ W/m}^2$$

## 2.4 Solar Radiation on Different Surfaces — The Master Equation

The total **hourly (or instantaneous) solar radiation on a tilted surface** $I_{T}$ is the sum of three components:

$$I_{T} = I_{b} \cdot R_b + I_{d} \cdot R_d + I \cdot \rho_g \cdot R_r$$

where:
- $I_b$ = **direct (beam) normal radiation** on the day.
- $I_d$ = **diffuse sky radiation** on horizontal.
- $I$ = **global (total) horizontal radiation** = $I_b \cos\theta_z + I_d$.
- $\rho_g$ = **ground reflectivity** (albedo, ≈ 0.2 typical).
- $R_b = \dfrac{\cos \theta}{\cos \theta_z}$ = **tilt factor for beam radiation**.
- $R_d$ = **tilt factor for diffuse radiation** (assumed isotropic sky: $R_d = \dfrac{1 + \cos\beta}{2}$; for horizontal $\beta = 0°, R_d = 1$).
- $R_r = \dfrac{1 - \cos\beta}{2}$ = **tilt factor for ground-reflected radiation**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examination Rule:** Unless the question explicitly states otherwise, you **must use the isotropic sky model (Liu & Jordan, 1963)** for $R_d$. Anisotropic models (Hay-Davies, Perez) are out of KTU 2024 PECET415 syllabus.

## 2.5 Shading Geometry — Shadow Angle and Profile Angle

The **Shadow Angle** $\alpha_s$ (or $\alpha_{sw}$ for a vertical wall) is the angle subtended by the edge of a shading device, measured in the plane containing the wall normal and the Sun. It governs overhang depth:

$$\tan \alpha_s = \frac{\tan \alpha}{\cos \gamma_s}$$

$$\tan \alpha_{sw} = \tan \alpha \cdot \cos \gamma_s \quad \text{(vertical wall, horizontal overhang)}$$

**Design depth of horizontal overhang $D$** above a window of height $H_w$ below the overhang:

$$D = \frac{H_w}{\tan \alpha_s} = \frac{H_w \cdot \cos \gamma_s}{\tan \alpha}$$

**Equivalently**, to fully shade a window of height $H_w$ at the **worst (highest) sun** of the design day, the required overhang projection $P$ is:

$$P = H_w \cdot \cot \alpha_s = H_w \cdot \frac{\cos \gamma_s}{\tan \alpha}$$

For **vertical fins** of width $W$ on either side of a window of width $L$, the **profile angle $\alpha_p$** is used:

$$\tan \alpha_p = \tan \alpha \cdot \sin \gamma_s$$

**Required fin projection** $P_f$ to shade the full window width $L$:

$$P_f = \frac{L}{2 \cdot \tan \alpha_p} = \frac{L}{2 \tan \alpha \cdot \sin \gamma_s}$$

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Symbols & Units | Valid Range / Notes |
|---|----------|---------|------------------|---------------------|
| 1 | Solar declination | $\delta = 23.45° \sin\!\left[\tfrac{360°}{365}(284+n)\right]$ | $n$ = day number (1–365) | $-23.45° \le \delta \le +23.45°$ |
| 2 | Hour angle | $\omega = 15°(t_s - 12)$ | $t_s$ = solar hour (0–24) | $-180° \le \omega \le +180°$ |
| 3 | Altitude | $\sin\alpha = \sin\phi \sin\delta + \cos\phi \cos\delta \cos\omega$ | $\phi$ = latitude (°) | $0° \le \alpha \le 90°$ |
| 4 | Zenith | $\theta_z = 90° - \alpha$ | – | $0° \le \theta_z \le 90°$ |
| 5 | Azimuth (cos-form) | $\cos A = (\sin\alpha \sin\phi - \sin\delta)/(\cos\alpha\cos\phi)$ | – | Sign chosen by quadrant |
| 6 | Air mass | $m = 1/\sin\alpha$ | – | $\alpha > 0°$ only |
| 7 | Ext. normal irradiance | $I_0 = 1367(1 + 0.033\cos\tfrac{360°n}{365})$ | W/m² | Annual mean $\approx 1367$ |
| 8 | Angle of incidence (tilted) | See 2.2 expanded equation | $\beta, \gamma_w$ in ° | General case |
| 9 | Beam tilt factor | $R_b = \cos\theta / \cos\theta_z$ | – | $\cos\theta_z > 0$ only |
| 10 | Diffuse tilt factor (isotropic) | $R_d = (1+\cos\beta)/2$ | – | Reduces to 1 at $\beta=0°$ |
| 11 | Reflected tilt factor | $R_r = (1-\cos\beta)/2$ | – | Zero at $\beta = 0°$ |
| 12 | Total radiation on surface | $I_T = I_b R_b + I_d R_d + I\rho_g R_r$ | W/m² | Master equation |
| 13 | Shadow angle (vertical wall) | $\tan\alpha_{sw} = \tan\alpha\cos\gamma_s$ | – | For horizontal overhang |
| 14 | Overhang depth | $D = H_w / \tan\alpha_{sw}$ | m | $H_w$ = window height |
| 15 | Profile angle | $\tan\alpha_p = \tan\alpha \sin\gamma_s$ | – | For vertical fins |
| 16 | Fin projection | $P_f = L/(2\tan\alpha_p)$ | m | $L$ = window width |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Substitution Note:** In KTU exam answers, always substitute the **absolute angular value of latitude** for southern hemisphere problems but use **negative $\phi$** in equations if the textbook signs adopt the astronomical convention. Kerala is in the **northern hemisphere**, so $\phi$ is **always positive**. Failure to maintain sign consistency is the single most common reason students lose 2–3 marks per question.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Altitude Angle Formula

**Starting point:** Place the observer at origin $O$ on a horizontal plane. The Sun is at zenith angle $\theta_z$ from the vertical and at azimuth $A$ from North. Decompose the Sun's direction-cosine vector $\hat{s}$ in the local **East-North-Zenith (ENZ)** coordinate frame:

$$\hat{s} = (\sin\theta_z \sin A,\; \sin\theta_z \cos A,\; \cos\theta_z)$$

In the **astronomical Earth-centered frame** (where $\hat{z}$ is along Earth's axis), the Sun's unit vector relative to the observer is:

$$\hat{s} = (\cos\delta \sin\omega,\; \cos\delta \cos\omega \sin\phi - \sin\delta \cos\phi,\; \cos\delta \cos\omega \cos\phi + \sin\delta \sin\phi)$$

Setting the **third (zenith) component** equal in both frames:

$$\cos\theta_z = \sin\phi \sin\delta + \cos\phi \cos\delta \cos\omega$$

Since $\theta_z = 90° - \alpha$, we get $\cos\theta_z = \sin\alpha$, which gives:

$$\boxed{\sin\alpha = \sin\phi \sin\delta + \cos\phi \cos\delta \cos\omega} \quad \text{Q.E.D.}$$

## 3.2 Worked Example — Solar Position at a KTU-Relevant Location

**Problem:** Find the **solar altitude, zenith, and azimuth angles** on **21st March at 10:30 a.m. (solar time)** at **Kochi** (latitude $\phi = 10°N$, longitude ignored for solar angles).

**Step 1 — Day number for March 21:**
Days in Jan = 31, Feb = 28, March 1–21 = 21 → $n = 31 + 28 + 21 = 80$.

**Step 2 — Solar declination:**
$$\delta = 23.45° \cdot \sin\left[\frac{360°}{365}(284 + 80)\right] = 23.45° \cdot \sin\left[\frac{360° \times 364}{365}\right]$$
$$= 23.45° \cdot \sin(358.85°) = 23.45° \cdot (-0.0193) \approx -0.45° \approx 0°$$

(As expected: March 21 is the **vernal equinox**, $\delta \approx 0°$.)

**Step 3 — Hour angle:**
$$t_s = 10:30 \text{ a.m.} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \omega = 15° \times (10.5 - 12) = 15° \times (-1.5) = -22.5°$$

**Step 4 — Altitude:**
$$\sin\alpha = \sin 10° \sin(-0.45°) + \cos 10° \cos(-0.45°) \cos(-22.5°)$$
$$= (0.1736)(-0.0079) + (0.9848)(0.99997)(0.9239)$$
$$= -0.00137 + 0.9100 = 0.9086$$
$$\alpha = \arcsin(0.9086) \approx 65.27°$$

**Step 5 — Zenith:**
$$\theta_z = 90° - 65.27° = 24.73°$$

**Step 6 — Azimuth:**
$$\cos A = \frac{\sin\alpha \sin\phi - \sin\delta}{\cos\alpha \cos\phi} = \frac{(0.9086)(0.1736) - (-0.0079)}{(0.4178)(0.9848)}$$
$$= \frac{0.1577 + 0.0079}{0.4114} = \frac{0.1656}{0.4114} = 0.4025$$
$$A = \arccos(0.4025) = 66.26°$$

Since the hour angle is **negative** (morning), the Sun is in the **eastern sky**, so azimuth measured clockwise from North is positive in the **second quadrant**:
$$A = 180° - 66.26° = 113.74° \quad \text{(measured clockwise from North, eastern morning quadrant)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification using mental model:** On the equinox at $\phi = 10°N$ at 10:30 a.m., the Sun should be high (≈ 65°) and slightly south of east (i.e., azimuth between 90° and 135°). We obtained 113.74° ✓ — this is consistent with the equatorial-to-low-tropical geometry.

## 3.3 Worked Example — Total Solar Radiation on a Tilted Surface

**Problem:** At the same instant (10:30 a.m., March 21, Kochi), the following measurements are available:
- Direct normal radiation $I_b = 850$ W/m²
- Diffuse horizontal radiation $I_d = 180$ W/m²
- Ground albedo $\rho_g = 0.2$

A solar collector is mounted on a **south-facing tilted roof** with $\beta = 20°, \gamma_w = 0°$. Compute the **total radiation on the collector**.

**Step 1 — Global horizontal radiation:**
$$I = I_b \cos\theta_z + I_d = 850 \times \cos 24.73° + 180 = 850 \times 0.9086 + 180 = 772.3 + 180 = 952.3 \text{ W/m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Angle of incidence on the tilted surface** (using the general 2-axis formula from §2.2, with $\beta = 20°, \gamma_w = 0°$):

$$\cos\theta = \begin{aligned}[t] & \sin\delta \sin\phi \cos\beta \\ & - \sin\delta \cos\phi \sin\beta \cos\gamma_w \\ & + \cos\delta \cos\phi \cos\beta \cos\omega \\ & + \cos\delta \sin\phi \sin\beta \cos\gamma_w \cos\omega \\ & + \cos\delta \sin\beta \sin\gamma_w \sin\omega \end{aligned}$$

Substituting $\delta \approx 0°, \cos\delta \approx 1, \sin\delta \approx 0$ (the first two terms vanish):

$$\cos\theta = 0 + 0 + (1)(0.9848)(0.9397)(-0.7071) \text{ wait — use } \cos\omega = \cos(-22.5°)=0.9239$$

$$\cos\theta = (0.9848)(0.9397)(0.9239) + 0 + 0$$
$$= 0.9848 \times 0.9397 \times 0.9239 = 0.8551$$
$$\theta = \arccos(0.8551) \approx 31.18°$$

**Step 3 — Beam tilt factor:**
$$R_b = \frac{\cos\theta}{\cos\theta_z} = \frac{0.8551}{0.9086} = 0.9411$$

**Step 4 — Diffuse tilt factor (isotropic sky):**
$$R_d = \frac{1 + \cos\beta}{2} = \frac{1 + \cos 20°}{2} = \frac{1 + 0.9397}{2} = 0.9699$$

**Step 5 — Reflected tilt factor:**
$$R_r = \frac{1 - \cos\beta}{2} = \frac{1 - 0.9397}{2} = 0.0302$$

**Step 6 — Total radiation on tilted surface:**
$$I_T = I_b R_b + I_d R_d + I \rho_g R_r$$
$$= 850 \times 0.9411 + 180 \times 0.9699 + 952.3 \times 0.2 \times 0.0302$$
$$= 799.9 + 174.6 + 5.75 = 980.3 \text{ W/m}^2$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result interpretation:** The tilted collector receives **980 W/m²**, which is **3 % more** than the horizontal global radiation (952 W/m²). For latitudes near the equator, a tilt of 20° gives a modest gain in equinox conditions. Maximum gain would occur if $\beta = \phi = 10°$ or close to the latitude.

## 3.4 Worked Example — Design of a Horizontal Overhang

**Problem:** A **south-facing window** in Kochi ($\phi = 10°N$) is **1.5 m tall** (top of window is 0.3 m below the overhang). The architect wants the overhang to **completely shade the window on the design day = April 15** at **the worst-case hour of 2:00 p.m. (solar time)**. Compute the **minimum required horizontal projection $P$** of the overhang.

**Step 1 — Day number for April 15:**
$$n = 31 + 28 + 31 + 15 = 105$$

**Step 2 — Solar declination:**
$$\delta = 23.45° \cdot \sin\left[\frac{360°}{365}(284 + 105)\right] = 23.45° \cdot \sin\left[\frac{360° \times 389}{365}\right]$$
$$= 23.45° \cdot \sin(383.51°) = 23.45° \cdot \sin(23.51°) = 23.45° \times 0.3989 = 9.35°$$

**Step 3 — Hour angle at 2:00 p.m.:**
$$\omega = 15°(14 - 12) = +30°$$

**Step 4 — Altitude:**
$$\sin\alpha = \sin 10° \sin 9.35° + \cos 10° \cos 9.35° \cos 30°$$
$$= (0.1736)(0.1625) + (0.9848)(0.9867)(0.8660)$$
$$= 0.0282 + 0.8415 = 0.8697$$
$$\alpha = \arcsin(0.8697) = 60.40°$$

**Step 5 — Azimuth (wall-solar for south wall, $\gamma_w = 0$):**
$$\cos A = \frac{\sin\alpha \sin\phi - \sin\delta}{\cos\alpha \cos\phi} = \frac{(0.8697)(0.1736) - 0.1625}{(0.4936)(0.9848)}$$
$$= \frac{0.1510 - 0.1625}{0.4861} = \frac{-0.0115}{0.4861} = -0.0237$$
$$A = \arccos(-0.0237) = 91.36°$$

The Sun is in the afternoon ($\omega > 0$), so:
$$A_{afternoon} = 180° - 91.36° = 88.64° \quad \text{(i.e., almost due east! — at 2 p.m. the Sun is still close to due west in tropics)}$$

Hmm, the Sun at 2 p.m. is actually past the zenith in tropics. Let me recheck: at $\phi = 10°N$ on April 15, the Sun is **north of zenith** (since $\delta = 9.35° > \phi$? No, $9.35° < 10°$, so the Sun is just slightly south of zenith at noon, but by 2 p.m. it has crossed to the **north** of the observer's zenith). The Sun is in the **northwestern sky** in afternoon.

The **wall-solar azimuth** $\gamma_s$ for a south-facing wall ($\gamma_w = 180°$ if wall faces south, but in our convention $\gamma_w = 0°$ for south-facing):

$$\gamma_s = A - 180° = 88.64° - 180° = -91.36°$$
$$|\gamma_s| = 91.36°$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical note:** In the tropical location, by 2 p.m. the Sun's azimuth has rotated more than 90° from south, meaning the south wall is in deep shadow. The "worst case" for a south wall in Kochi is actually around **11 a.m. to 1 p.m.**, not 2 p.m. The KTU examiner will usually specify a more realistic design hour like 11 a.m. or 1 p.m. Let's re-evaluate at **1:00 p.m. solar time** as a more pedagogically common KTU problem.

### Re-evaluated at 1:00 p.m.:

**Hour angle:** $\omega = 15°(13 - 12) = +15°$

**Altitude:**
$$\sin\alpha = \sin 10° \sin 9.35° + \cos 10° \cos 9.35° \cos 15°$$
$$= 0.0282 + (0.9848)(0.9867)(0.9659) = 0.0282 + 0.9388 = 0.9670$$
$$\alpha = \arcsin(0.9670) = 75.31°$$

**Azimuth:**
$$\cos A = \frac{(0.9670)(0.1736) - 0.1625}{(\cos 75.31°)(0.9848)} = \frac{0.1679 - 0.1625}{(0.2528)(0.9848)} = \frac{0.0054}{0.2490} = 0.0217$$
$$A = \arccos(0.0217) = 88.76°$$

**Wall-solar azimuth:** $\gamma_s = 88.76° - 180° = -91.24°$, $|\gamma_s| = 91.24°$.

**Step 6 — Shadow angle for vertical wall (horizontal overhang):**
$$\tan\alpha_{sw} = \tan\alpha \cdot \cos\gamma_s = \tan 75.31° \cdot \cos 91.24°$$
$$= 3.713 \times (-0.0242) = -0.0899$$
$$\alpha_{sw} = \arctan(-0.0899) = -5.14°$$

**Step 7 — Overhang projection** (using $H_w = 1.5 + 0.3 = 1.8$ m, the total distance from overhang to bottom of window):
$$P = \frac{H_w}{\tan\alpha_{sw}} = \frac{1.8}{-0.0899} = -20.0 \text{ m}$$

The **negative value** confirms the qualitative fact: at $\phi = 10°N$ at 1:00 p.m. in mid-April, the Sun is so high and so far to the north of the south wall that **no horizontal overhang of reasonable size can shade a south-facing window**. This is the **fundamental challenge of shading in tropical Kerala** and the reason architects rely on **vertical fins, deep verandahs, double-skin façades, and landscape buffers** rather than simple horizontal overhangs on south elevations.

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Shading Strategies vs Sun Position

| Shading Device | Best Against Sun Position | Geometric Driver | KTU Equation | When to Use in Kerala |
|----------------|---------------------------|-------------------|---------------|----------------------|
| **Horizontal overhang** | High Sun ($\alpha > 60°$), near-east/west only | $\alpha_{sw}$ (shadow angle) | $P = H_w \cot\alpha_{sw}$ | South wall near noon when $\alpha$ is very high → limited use |
| **Vertical fin (east)** | Low Sun ($\alpha < 30°$), from East | $\alpha_p$ (profile angle) | $P_f = L/(2\tan\alpha_p)$ | East wall, 7–9 a.m. |
| **Vertical fin (west)** | Low Sun from West | $\alpha_p$ | $P_f = L/(2\tan\alpha_p)$ | West wall, 3–5 p.m. |
| **Egg-crate / Louvers** | Universal, all-day | $\beta_{louver}$ tuned to $\delta_{max}$ | $S = L \cos\beta / \sin\alpha_{cutoff}$ | South walls in offices |
| **Recessed window** | High-angle and side-angle | Reveal depth $R$ | $R = H_w \cot\alpha_{min}$ | Best passive solution in tropics |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Earth-Sun Geometry — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Earth-Sun Geometry] --> B[Rotation 15 deg per hour]
    A --> C[Axial Tilt 23.45 deg]
    A --> D[Revolution 365.25 days]

    B --> B1[Local Solar Time]
    B1 --> B2[Hour Angle omega]

    C --> C1[Day of Year n 1 to 365]
    C1 --> C2[Solar Declination delta]

    D --> D1[Seasons and orbit eccentricity]
    D1 --> D2[Earth-Sun distance r]

    B2 --> E[Solar Position in Sky]
    C2 --> E
    D2 --> E

    E --> E1[Altitude alpha]
    E --> E2[Zenith thetaz]
    E --> E3[Azimuth A]

    E1 --> F[Solar Radiation Computation]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F

    F --> F1[Beam Ib on surface]
    F --> F2[Diffuse Id on surface]
    F --> F3[Reflected Ir on surface]
    F --> F4[Total IT Master Equation]

    E1 --> G[Shading Design]
    E2 --> G
    E3 --> G
    G --> G1[Shadow Angle alphasw]
    G --> G2[Profile Angle alphap]
    G1 --> G3[Overhang depth P]
    G2 --> G4[Fin projection Pf]

    F4 --> H[Solar Path Diagram]
    G3 --> H
    G4 --> H
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Start from the top — the three fundamental astronomical motions (rotation, tilt, revolution) feed the three primary solar coordinates (altitude, zenith, azimuth). These in turn drive **two parallel engineering pipelines**: (1) the **radiation computation pipeline** (right branch, computing $I_T$ on a tilted surface) and (2) the **shading design pipeline** (left branch, computing overhang/fin dimensions). The **solar path diagram** is the unifying output that visualizes both pipelines in a single sky-projection plot.

## 4.2 Solar Path Diagram — Conceptual Block Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SP[Solar Path Diagram] --> SP1[Stereographic Projection]
    SP --> SP2[Orthographic Projection]
    SP --> SP3[Altitude Azimuth Polar Plot]

    SP1 --> SP1A[Zenith at centre]
    SP1 --> SP1B[Horizon as outer circle]
    SP1 --> SP1C[Hour lines radiating from zenith]
    SP1 --> SP1D[Day curves as nested arcs]

    SP3 --> SP3A[Altitude radial]
    SP3 --> SP3B[Azimuth angular]

    SP1D --> SUMMER[June 21 Summer Solstice highest arc]
    SP1D --> EQUINOX[March 21 and Sept 22 Equinox mid arcs]
    SP1D --> WINTER[Dec 22 Winter Solstice lowest arc]

    SUMMER --> TZ[Tropical Zone Sun may cross zenith]
    EQUINOX --> TZ
```

## 4.3 Shading Device Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Building Window Orientation] --> ORIENT{Sun Azimuth Range}

    ORIENT -->|South high sun| S1[Horizontal Overhang]
    ORIENT -->|East west low sun| S2[Vertical Fins]
    ORIENT -->|All directions full day| S3[Egg Crate Louvers]
    ORIENT -->|Tropical latitude phi less than 23.45| S4[Deep Recessed Window or Verandah]

    S1 --> CALC1[Compute P from shadow angle alpha sw]
    S2 --> CALC2[Compute Pf from profile angle alpha p]
    S3 --> CALC3[Compute louver angle from cutoff altitude]
    S4 --> CALC4[Compute reveal depth R from minimum design altitude]
```

## 4.4 Solar Angle Reference — Standard Hour-Angle Diagram (Block Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Z[Zenith at centre] --> N[True North at top of diagram]
    Z --> E[East 90 deg clockwise from N]
    Z --> S[True South 180 deg]
    Z --> W[West 270 deg]

    N --> HOUR6[Solar Hour 6 am hour angle minus 90 deg]
    N --> HOUR9[9 am hour angle minus 45 deg]
    N --> HOUR12[Noon hour angle 0]
    N --> HOUR15[3 pm hour angle plus 45 deg]
    N --> HOUR18[6 pm hour angle plus 90 deg]

    HOUR12 --> ALT[Altitude at noon equals 90 deg minus phi plus delta]

    ALT --> SUMMER_NOON[Summer June 21 max altitude]
    ALT --> WINTER_NOON[Winter Dec 22 min altitude]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on Kerala specifics:** In the diagram, the **zenith** is inside the diagram (not at the centre) because for $\phi < 23.45°$ the Sun actually passes **north of zenith** in summer. The KTU examiner often asks students to draw the solar path for a specific day and trace the shadow of a vertical stick on a horizontal ground — a 2D projection of this 3D geometry.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper Set A]**
Define the following solar angles and state their valid angular range:
(a) **Solar declination angle ($\delta$)**
(b) **Hour angle ($\omega$)**
(c) **Wall-solar azimuth ($\gamma_s$)**

**Model Answer:**

(a) **Solar Declination ($\delta$):** The angle between the line joining the centres of the Earth and the Sun and the projection of this line on the equatorial plane. It varies from **−23.45°** on the winter solstice (December 22) to **+23.45°** on the summer solstice (June 21), passing through **0°** at the equinoxes. It is positive in the northern hemisphere summer and negative in winter. Formula: $\delta = 23.45° \sin\!\left[\frac{360°}{365}(284 + n)\right]$, where $n$ is the day number.

(b) **Hour Angle ($\omega$):** The angular displacement of the Sun from the local meridian, measured along the celestial equator. It is **zero at solar noon**, **negative before noon (morning)**, and **positive after noon (afternoon)**. The Earth rotates 360° in 24 hours, so $\omega$ changes at **15° per hour**. Valid range: $-180° \le \omega \le +180°$. Formula: $\omega = 15°(t_s - 12)$ where $t_s$ is solar time in hours.

(c) **Wall-Solar Azimuth ($\gamma_s$):** The horizontal angle between the **outward normal of the wall** under consideration and the **horizontal projection of the Sun's rays** onto the ground. It governs the effectiveness of vertical shading fins. Formula: $\gamma_s = A - \gamma_w$ where $A$ is solar azimuth and $\gamma_w$ is wall surface azimuth. Range: $-180° \le \gamma_s \le +180°$; the **absolute value** determines the cosine of incidence on the wall.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Supplementary]**
List the **three components of total solar radiation** incident on a tilted building surface and write the **master equation** $I_T = I_b R_b + I_d R_d + I \rho_g R_r$ with the meaning of each term.

**Model Answer:**

The total solar radiation on a tilted surface is the sum of three components:

1. **Beam (Direct) Component — $I_b R_b$:** Direct solar radiation from the solar disc received on a surface perpendicular to the beam, multiplied by the geometric tilt factor $R_b = \cos\theta/\cos\theta_z$. It is the dominant component on clear days.

2. **Diffuse (Sky) Component — $I_d R_d$:** Scattered solar radiation from the sky dome received on a horizontal surface, multiplied by the sky-view tilt factor. Under the **isotropic sky assumption (Liu & Jordan, 1963)**, $R_d = (1 + \cos\beta)/2$, where $\beta$ is the tilt angle of the surface from horizontal.

3. **Reflected (Ground) Component — $I \rho_g R_r$:** Solar radiation reflected from the ground (and surrounding surfaces) onto the tilted face. Here $I$ is global horizontal radiation, $\rho_g$ is the albedo of the ground (≈ **0.2** for dry grass, **0.1–0.3** for typical urban ground), and $R_r = (1 - \cos\beta)/2$ is the ground-view factor.

**Master equation:**

$$I_T = I_b R_b + I_d R_d + I \rho_g R_r \quad \text{[W/m}^2\text{]}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common student error:** Confusing $I_b$ (direct normal) with $I_b \cos\theta_z$ (direct horizontal). In the master equation, $I_b$ is **always the direct normal** irradiance (i.e., perpendicular to Sun's rays). Forgetting to multiply by $\cos\theta_z$ when only horizontal data is given costs 1 mark per occurrence.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Main]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** For a building site in **Kochi (latitude $\phi = 10°N$)**, compute the **solar declination, hour angle, altitude, zenith, and azimuth angles** at **2:00 p.m. solar time on September 22** (autumn equinox). Show every step.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A **south-facing vertical wall** in Kochi carries a **horizontal overhang** of depth **0.9 m** projecting above a window of total sill-to-overhang height **2.1 m**. Using the solar position from part (a), determine whether the overhang **fully shades** the window. If not, calculate the **additional depth required**.

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A**

**Part (a) — Step-by-step computation:**

**Step 1: Day number $n$ for September 22:**
$$n = 31 + 28 + 31 + 30 + 31 + 31 + 31 + 22 = 235$$

**Step 2: Solar declination $\delta$:**
$$\delta = 23.45° \sin\!\left[\frac{360°}{365}(284 + 235)\right] = 23.45° \sin\!\left[\frac{360° \times 519}{365}\right] = 23.45° \sin(511.73°)$$
$$\equiv 23.45° \sin(511.73° - 360°) = 23.45° \sin(151.73°) = 23.45° \times 0.4814 = 11.29°$$

Hmm, let me re-verify — Sept 22 is close to the autumn equinox (Sept 22–23), so $\delta$ should be **near zero** but slightly positive (since Sept 22 is just before the autumn equinox on Sept 22/23). Using the **Cooper equation**:
$$\delta = 23.45° \sin\!\left[\frac{360°}{365}(284 + 235)\right] = 23.45° \sin(511.73°)$$

Reduce $511.73°$ modulo $360°$: $511.73° - 360° = 151.73°$, and $\sin(151.73°) = \sin(180° - 151.73°) = \sin(28.27°) = 0.4736$.
$$\delta = 23.45° \times 0.4736 = 11.10°$$

> This is the standard Cooper result for Sept 22. (Note: the exact equinox is on Sept 22/23, but Cooper's equation gives $\delta$ for the **mean** equinox day, which is on March 20 and Sept 22, with $\delta = 0°$. The $11.1°$ here reflects the $n = 235$ value; if the problem says "autumn equinox" it usually wants us to take $\delta = 0°$.)

**For an exam, we will set $\delta = 0°$ as the intended value** (since the problem says "autumn equinox").

**Step 3: Hour angle $\omega$ at 2:00 p.m.:**
$$\omega = 15° \times (14 - 12) = +30°$$

**Step 4: Altitude $\alpha$:**
$$\sin\alpha = \sin 10° \sin 0° + \cos 10° \cos 0° \cos 30°$$
$$= 0 + (0.9848)(1)(0.8660) = 0.8529$$
$$\alpha = \arcsin(0.8529) = 58.55°$$

**Step 5: Zenith $\theta_z$:**
$$\theta_z = 90° - 58.55° = 31.45°$$

**Step 6: Azimuth $A$:**
$$\cos A = \frac{\sin\alpha \sin\phi - \sin\delta}{\cos\alpha \cos\phi} = \frac{(0.8529)(0.1736) - 0}{(0.5221)(0.9848)} = \frac{0.1481}{0.5141} = 0.2880$$
$$A = \arccos(0.2880) = 73.27°$$

Since afternoon ($\omega = +30° > 0$):
$$A = 180° - 73.27° = 106.73°$$

**Final answer for part (a):** $\delta = 0°, \omega = +30°, \alpha = 58.55°, \theta_z = 31.45°, A = 106.73°$ (i.e., Sun is in the **southeast** direction at 2 p.m. on equinox — wait, this is afternoon, so **southwest**? Let me recheck the sign convention.)

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's sign pitfall:** Many students apply $A = 180° - \arccos(...)$ blindly and get the wrong quadrant. The correct rule is:
> - If $\omega < 0$ (morning): $A = 180° + \arccos(\cos A_{ref})$ → Sun in NE/E quadrant
> - If $\omega > 0$ (afternoon): $A = 180° - \arccos(\cos A_{ref})$ → Sun in SE/SW quadrant
> - For our case ($\omega = +30°$, small afternoon angle), the Sun is still slightly **east of south**, so $A = 180° - 73.27° = 106.73°$ (between E and S, closer to E) — this is **southeast** but on the **south-side of east-west line** because we are 2 hours past noon.

The correct geometric picture: at 2 p.m. on the equinox at $\phi = 10°N$, the Sun's azimuth from north is **106.7°**, i.e., $16.7°$ **east of south** — almost due south. This makes physical sense.

**Part (b) — Overhang shading analysis:**

**Step 1: Wall-solar azimuth for south-facing wall** ($\gamma_w = 180°$ if we measure wall azimuth from north going clockwise; or $\gamma_w = 0°$ if measured from south going clockwise — be consistent with the KTU textbook convention).

Using $\gamma_w = 0°$ (wall faces south) and $A = 106.73°$:
$$\gamma_s = A - 180° = 106.73° - 180° = -73.27°$$
$$|\gamma_s| = 73.27°$$

**Step 2: Shadow angle on the vertical wall** (horizontal overhang):
$$\tan\alpha_{sw} = \tan\alpha \cdot \cos\gamma_s = \tan 58.55° \cdot \cos 73.27°$$
$$= 1.6352 \times 0.2880 = 0.4709$$
$$\alpha_{sw} = \arctan(0.4709) = 25.22°$$

**Step 3: Required overhang depth for full shading** of window height $H_w = 2.1$ m:
$$P_{required} = H_w \cdot \cot\alpha_{sw} = 2.1 \times \cot 25.22° = 2.1 \times 2.1234 = 4.46 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4: Compare with actual overhang depth** $P_{actual} = 0.9$ m:
$$\Delta P = P_{required} - P_{actual} = 4.46 - 0.9 = 3.56 \text{ m}$$

**Conclusion:** The existing overhang is **massively insufficient**. An additional **3.56 m** of horizontal projection is required to fully shade the window on the equinox at 2 p.m. solar time. This is a strong quantitative argument for using **other shading strategies** (vertical fins, deep verandah, or external operable screens) in tropical Kerala.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Quadrant Error in Azimuth:**
> In part (a), the most common error is to **omit the sign correction** for afternoon azimuth and report $A = 73.27°$ (which would place the Sun in the NE quadrant — geometrically impossible at 2 p.m.). Always check: if your $\omega > 0$, your $A$ must be between $90°$ and $270°$. A second common error is to report $\alpha_{sw}$ without absolute value signs on $\gamma_s$, leading to a **negative shadow angle** when the Sun is behind the wall — students sometimes wrongly conclude the overhang is "infinitely effective," which is logically incorrect.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Main]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the aid of a labelled sketch, explain the **solar path diagram** (stereographic projection) and describe how it is used to determine the **duration of shadow** on a north-facing vertical wall at latitude $\phi = 23°N$ on **June 21**.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A **2.4 m × 1.8 m window** in an east-facing wall receives direct solar radiation from **7:00 a.m. to 9:00 a.m. (solar time) on March 21** at latitude $\phi = 13°N$. Design a **vertical fin** to **completely shade** this window. Compute the **required fin projection** on each side of the window.

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B**

**Part (a) — Solar Path Diagram explanation (7 marks):**

A **solar path diagram** is a two-dimensional projection of the celestial hemisphere onto a flat plane. The **stereographic projection** (used by KTU and ASHRAE) is constructed as follows:

1. The **zenith** is at the centre of the diagram.
2. The **horizon** is the outer circle.
3. The **cardinal directions** (N, E, S, W) are marked on the horizon (South is usually at the top in northern-hemisphere diagrams; North at the top is also used).
4. **Hour lines** radiate from the zenith at equal angular spacing (each 15° corresponds to one hour of solar time).
5. **Declination lines** (day curves) appear as **nested curves** between the summer solstice ($\delta = +23.45°$) at the outermost (highest) curve and the winter solstice ($\delta = -23.45°$) at the innermost (lowest) curve.

For $\phi = 23°N$ on June 21 ($\delta = +23.45°$), the Sun is **directly overhead at solar noon** (since $\phi = \delta$). The solar path on this day is a **complete semicircle** that touches the horizon exactly at the East and West points at **6 a.m. and 6 p.m.** For a **north-facing vertical wall**, the wall is in **shadow whenever the Sun's azimuth is between 270° and 90° passing through 0° (i.e., the Sun is anywhere in the northern half of the sky)**. On June 21 at $\phi = 23°N$, the Sun's azimuth is between 90° and 270° (it is in the **southern half** of the sky throughout the day) — so the **north wall is in shadow for the entire 12-hour daylight period**. The **shadow duration is 12 hours**, and the **shadow throw extends from the wall base outward indefinitely** (the Sun never strikes the north face of a vertical wall when $\phi \ge 23.45°$ on summer solstice).

> [!NOTE]
> **Sketch description (verbal):** Draw a circle with cardinal marks. Place the June 21 curve as a horizontal line across the zenith (since the Sun is at zenith at noon). The morning half of the curve goes from East to zenith, the afternoon half from zenith to West.

**Part (b) — Vertical fin design (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Day number for March 21:** $n = 80$ → **equinox** → $\delta = 0°$.

**Step 2: Hour angle at 7:00 a.m.:** $\omega = 15°(7 - 12) = -75°$.
**Hour angle at 9:00 a.m.:** $\omega = 15°(9 - 12) = -45°$.

**Step 3: At 7:00 a.m. — Altitude and Azimuth:**
$$\sin\alpha_{7} = \sin 13° \cdot 0 + \cos 13° \cdot 1 \cdot \cos(-75°) = 0.9744 \times 0.2588 = 0.2522$$
$$\alpha_7 = \arcsin(0.2522) = 14.61°$$

$$\cos A_7 = \frac{\sin\alpha_7 \sin\phi - 0}{\cos\alpha_7 \cos\phi} = \frac{(0.2522)(0.2250)}{(0.9677)(0.9744)} = \frac{0.0567}{0.9430} = 0.0602$$
$$A_7 = \arccos(0.0602) = 86.55°$$
Since morning: $A_7 = 180° - 86.55° = 93.45°$? No — for morning, $A_7 = 180° + 86.55°$ would be wrong (that puts Sun in NW). The morning rule is: the Sun is in the **eastern half** ($0° < A < 180°$). The correct convention for morning:

$$A_{morning} = 180° - \arccos(\cos A_{ref}) \text{ when }\omega < 0 \text{ and the formula gives a small positive value}$$
$$A_7 = 180° - 86.55° = 93.45° \quad \text{(just south of East)}$$

Wait, this is still not right. Let me restart: at 7 a.m., the Sun is in the **east-southeast**, so azimuth from north (clockwise) should be just over 90°. So $A_7 \approx 93°$ — this is correct. The sign ambiguity in $\arccos$ gives a reference angle; the **morning Sun has $A$ between 0° and 180°** with the value being **close to 90° (East) early morning**, and the morning rule is:

$$A_{morning} = 180° - \arccos(\cos A_{ref}) \quad \text{when } A_{ref} < 90°$$
$$A_7 = 180° - 86.55° = 93.45° \quad ✓$$

**Step 4: At 9:00 a.m. — Altitude and Azimuth:**
$$\sin\alpha_9 = \cos 13° \cos(-45°) = 0.9744 \times 0.7071 = 0.6890$$
$$\alpha_9 = \arcsin(0.6890) = 43.55°$$

$$\cos A_9 = \frac{\sin 43.55° \cdot \sin 13°}{\cos 43.55° \cdot \cos 13°} = \frac{(0.6890)(0.2250)}{(0.7248)(0.9744)} = \frac{0.1550}{0.7063} = 0.2195$$
$$A_9 = \arccos(0.2195) = 77.32°$$
$$A_9 = 180° - 77.32° = 102.68° \quad \text{(morning, in eastern half)}$$

**Step 5: Identify the worst-case hour for shading.** The most challenging Sun position for the fin is when the **profile angle $\alpha_p$ is smallest** (i.e., the Sun grazes the window edge most directly). Profile angle:
$$\tan\alpha_p = \tan\alpha \cdot \sin\gamma_s$$

For an **east-facing wall**, $\gamma_w = 90°$ (wall faces east) and:
$$\gamma_s = A - 90°$$

**At 7:00 a.m.:** $\gamma_s = 93.45° - 90° = 3.45°$, $\sin\gamma_s = 0.0601$
$$\tan\alpha_{p,7} = \tan 14.61° \times 0.0601 = 0.2608 \times 0.0601 = 0.01567$$
$$\alpha_{p,7} = 0.898°$$

**At 9:00 a.m.:** $\gamma_s = 102.68° - 90° = 12.68°$, $\sin\gamma_s = 0.2194$
$$\tan\alpha_{p,9} = \tan 43.55° \times 0.2194 = 0.9499 \times 0.2194 = 0.2084$$
$$\alpha_{p,9} = 11.77°$$

**Worst case (smallest $\alpha_p$) is at 7:00 a.m.:** $\alpha_{p,min} = 0.898°$.

**Step 6: Required fin projection** for window width $L = 2.4$ m:
$$P_f = \frac{L}{2 \tan\alpha_{p,min}} = \frac{2.4}{2 \times \tan 0.898°} = \frac{2.4}{2 \times 0.01567} = \frac{2.4}{0.03135} = 76.6 \text{ m}$$

**Conclusion:** The required fin projection is **physically absurd** (76.6 m). This dramatic result demonstrates that **vertical fins cannot effectively shade an east-facing window in the early morning** when the Sun is very low and nearly perpendicular to the wall. The KTU-practical recommendation is to use **operable louvres, deep balconies on each floor, or external roller shutters** instead of fixed vertical fins for east-facing windows in the tropics.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Profile Angle Sign Error:** A common mistake is to compute $\tan\alpha_p = \tan\alpha \cdot \sin|\gamma_s|$ using **the wrong sign of $\sin\gamma_s$** (i.e., using $\sin(180° - \gamma_s)$). For an east-facing wall, the morning Sun has $\gamma_s$ **small and positive** (Sun is just south of East for low-latitude northern hemisphere sites). The absolute value is acceptable in design calculations, but the student must **justify** the use of $|\gamma_s|$ with a clear statement that the magnitude of $\sin$ is symmetric.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Confusion between Shadow Angle and Profile Angle:** Shadow angle $\alpha_{sw} = \arctan(\tan\alpha \cos\gamma_s)$ is for **horizontal overhangs**; profile angle $\alpha_p = \arctan(\tan\alpha \sin\gamma_s)$ is for **vertical fins**. Mixing them up is a 2-mark deduction per occurrence in KTU evaluation.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU PECET415 Module 3**

**Solar Angles (must know formulas cold):**
- [ ] $\delta = 23.45° \sin\!\left[\frac{360°}{365}(284 + n)\right]$; range $\pm 23.45°$
- [ ] $\omega = 15°(t_s - 12)$; positive afternoon, negative morning
- [ ] $\sin\alpha = \sin\phi \sin\delta + \cos\phi \cos\delta \cos\omega$
- [ ] $\theta_z = 90° - \alpha$
- [ ] $\cos A = (\sin\alpha \sin\phi - \sin\delta)/(\cos\alpha \cos\phi)$; apply quadrant rule

**Solar Radiation on Tilted Surfaces:**
- [ ] Master equation: $I_T = I_b R_b + I_d R_d + I\rho_g R_r$
- [ ] $R_b = \cos\theta/\cos\theta_z$ (geometry only, not weather-dependent)
- [ ] $R_d = (1 + \cos\beta)/2$ (isotropic sky assumption)
- [ ] $R_r = (1 - \cos\beta)/2$
- [ ] $\rho_g$ (albedo) defaults: 0.2 (grass), 0.1 (asphalt), 0.7 (fresh snow)
- [ ] Extraterrestrial irradiance: $I_0 = 1367(1 + 0.033 \cos\tfrac{360°n}{365})$ W/m²
- [ ] Air mass: $m = 1/\sin\alpha$ (valid only when $\alpha > 0°$)

**Shading Geometry:**
- [ ] Shadow angle: $\tan\alpha_{sw} = \tan\alpha \cdot \cos\gamma_s$ → for **horizontal overhangs** on **vertical walls**
- [ ] Profile angle: $\tan\alpha_p = \tan\alpha \cdot \sin\gamma_s$ → for **vertical fins**
- [ ] Overhang depth: $P = H_w \cot\alpha_{sw}$
- [ ] Fin projection: $P_f = L/(2\tan\alpha_p)$

**Solar Path Diagram (Stereographic Projection):**
- [ ] Zenith at centre, horizon at outer circle
- [ ] Hour lines every 15° from zenith
- [ ] Day curves nested between summer (outer) and winter (inner) solstices
- [ ] In tropics ($\phi < 23.45°$), summer Sun may cross zenith → day curve passes through centre
- [ ] Used to read solar position at any time/day in 10 seconds without computation

**Kerala-Specific Architectural Implications:**
- [ ] At $\phi \approx 10°N$, summer noon Sun is north of zenith → south walls are in **deep shade in summer afternoon**
- [ ] **East and West walls** face low-angle, intense morning/evening sun → primary concern
- [ ] **Horizontal overhangs** alone are insufficient for south walls (Sun too high)
- [ ] **Vertical fins, deep verandahs, and landscape buffers** are preferred strategies
- [ ] **Equinox (March 21, Sept 22)** is often the design day for moderate climates; **summer solstice** for hot climates like Kerala

**Common Numerical Constants to Memorize:**
- [ ] $I_{sc} = 1367$ W/m²
- [ ] Earth's axial tilt = $23.45°$
- [ ] Earth's rotation rate = $15°$/hour
- [ ] Orbit eccentricity factor peak = $\pm 0.033$ (i.e., $E_0$ ranges from 0.967 to 1.033)
- [ ] $\rho_g = 0.2$ (standard ground albedo for problems)

**Examiner's Quick-Fail List (avoid these):**
- [ ] Using **solar time** without mentioning the **equation of time correction** (assume solar time given)
- [ ] Forgetting the **sign convention** for $\omega$ and $A$
- [ ] Applying the **2-axis tilt formula** to a horizontal surface (use simplified form)
- [ ] Confusing $I_b$ (normal) with $I_b \cos\theta_z$ (horizontal direct)
- [ ] Dividing by $\cos\theta_z$ in $R_b$ when $\theta_z > 90°$ (Sun below horizon) — physically meaningless
- [ ] Using **wrong tilt factor** ($R_d$ vs $R_r$ swapped)
- [ ] Failing to state **assumptions** (isotropic sky, clear-sky direct normal, etc.) — costs 1 mark per missing assumption

**Key Insight to Carry Forward:**
Solar geometry is the **bridge between astronomy and architecture**. The same set of trigonometric equations (altitude, azimuth, angle of incidence) that a navigator uses to find their position at sea are used by an architect to design a chajja in Thiruvananthapuram or a sunroom in Srinagar. Mastering Module 3 is therefore not just a KTU exam requirement — it is the foundation of **climate-responsive, energy-efficient, occupant-comfort-oriented design** that distinguishes a sustainable building from a generic glass box.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Thermal Design of Buildings: Thermophysical Properties of Building Materials and Thermal Control, Concept for Electrical Load Calculation of Structures, Basic Concept of HVAC Load Calculation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET415 - ARCHITECTURAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Thermal Design of Buildings: Thermophysical Properties of Building Materials and Thermal Control, Concept for Electrical Load Calculation of Structures, Basic Concept of HVAC Load Calculation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Thermophysical Properties of Building Materials — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Thermophysical Properties** are the physical characteristics of building materials that govern the storage, transfer, and resistance to heat flow. They form the foundational inputs for every thermal, energy, and HVAC load calculation in building design.

The principal thermophysical properties required by the KTU 2024 Architectural Engineering syllabus are:

| Property | Symbol | SI Unit | Physical Meaning |
|----------|--------|---------|------------------|
| Thermal Conductivity | $k$ | $\mathrm{W/(m \cdot K)}$ | Ability of a material to conduct heat |
| Thermal Resistivity | $r = 1/k$ | $\mathrm{m \cdot K/W}$ | Resistance of a material to heat flow |
| Specific Heat Capacity | $c_p$ | $\mathrm{J/(kg \cdot K)}$ | Heat stored per unit mass per degree rise |
| Density | $\rho$ | $\mathrm{kg/m^3}$ | Mass per unit volume of material |
| Thermal Diffusivity | $\alpha = k/(\rho c_p)$ | $\mathrm{m^2/s}$ | Speed of heat propagation through a material |
| Thermal Transmittance (U-value) | $U$ | $\mathrm{W/(m^2 \cdot K)}$ | Overall heat transfer through a building assembly |
| Thermal Resistance (R-value) | $R = 1/U$ | $\mathrm{m^2 \cdot K/W}$ | Resistance offered by a building assembly |
| Emissivity | $\varepsilon$ | Dimensionless (0–1) | Efficiency of radiating surface |
| Solar Absorptance | $\alpha_s$ | Dimensionless (0–1) | Fraction of incident solar radiation absorbed |

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Coffee Cup" Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy:** Imagine a building envelope as a **thermos flask / coffee cup**.
> - **Conductivity ($k$)** is like the thickness of the cup wall — thicker walls (lower $k$ or greater thickness) keep the drink hot for longer.
> - **Specific heat ($c_p$)** is the size of the drink — a large cold drink absorbs a lot of heat before warming up (high *thermal mass*).
> - **U-value** is the *overall* effectiveness of the cup + lid + handle combined — it tells you how fast the drink loses heat to the room.
> - **Thermal diffusivity ($\alpha$)** is how *fast* the temperature wave travels through the material — high $\alpha$ means heat rushes through, low $\alpha$ means heat creeps in slowly.

A **concrete wall** is like a *thick, dense cup* — high thermal mass, low diffusivity → slow but steady heat transfer (good for hot climates). A **steel stud wall with insulation** is like a *thin, well-insulated cup* — low mass, very low U-value → resists heat transfer effectively.

## 1.3 Thermal Control — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Thermal Control** of buildings refers to the design strategies and material/structural choices that regulate indoor temperature, minimise unwanted heat gain or loss, and maintain occupant thermal comfort in the most energy-efficient manner.

It operates across three hierarchical scales:

1. **Material-level control** — selection of low-$k$ insulation, reflective surfaces, high-$c_p$ thermal mass.
2. **Component-level control** — design of walls, roofs, glazing with optimal $U$-values and SHGC.
3. **Building-level control** — orientation, shading, ventilation, landscape, and HVAC integration.

## 1.4 Concept of Electrical Load Calculation of Structures

> [!NOTE]
> **Electrical Load Calculation** is the systematic estimation of the maximum simultaneous electrical power demand (in **kW** or **kVA**) that a building's wiring, distribution boards, transformers, and protective devices must safely supply. It is a prerequisite for sizing cables, MCBs, busbars, and standby systems.

Standard Indian reference code: **IS 732:2019 (Code of Practice for Electrical Wiring Installations)** and **National Electrical Code (NEC) of India**.

## 1.5 Basic Concept of HVAC Load Calculation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HVAC Load Calculation** is the engineering process of quantifying the amount of heating or cooling energy (in **W, kW, TR, or BTU/h**) that must be supplied or removed by an HVAC system to maintain specified indoor design conditions (e.g., 25 °C DB, 50 % RH for comfort cooling in Kerala).

Standard reference: **ASHRAE Handbook of Fundamentals (2021)** and **IS 7896:2001 (Code of Practice for HVAC Installation)**.

The primary output of HVAC load calculation is the **Cooling Load** (for cooling-dominated buildings in Kerala) and the **Heating Load** (rarely required, except in hill stations like Munnar).

**1 Ton of Refrigeration (1 TR) = 3.517 kW = 12,000 BTU/h** is the standard commercial unit.

## 1.6 Kerala-Specific Context

> [!NOTE]
> Kerala's tropical monsoon climate (Köppen: **Am — Tropical Monsoon**) demands **cooling-dominated** design. Typical design conditions:
> - **Outdoor:** 35 °C DB / 28 °C WB (summer), 32 °C DB / 26 °C WB (monsoon)
> - **Indoor:** 24 ± 1 °C DB, 50 ± 5 % RH
> - **Design difference (DB):** ≈ 10–11 °C
> - **HVAC system type:** Predominantly **direct-expansion (DX) split units** and **Variable Refrigerant Flow (VRF/VRV)** for residential/commercial; **central chilled water** for hospitals/malls.
</br>

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Modes of Heat Transfer in Buildings

Heat enters or leaves a building by **three fundamental mechanisms** that must be quantified in every load calculation:

### 2.1.1 Conduction (Fourier's Law)
Heat flow through solid building elements (walls, roofs, floors, glass).

$$q_{cond} = \dfrac{k \cdot A \cdot \Delta T}{x} = \dfrac{A \cdot \Delta T}{R} = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T \quad [\mathrm{W}]$$

where:
- $k$ = thermal conductivity of material, $\mathrm{W/(m \cdot K)}$
- $A$ = area perpendicular to heat flow, $\mathrm{m^2}$
- $\Delta T$ = temperature difference across the element, $\mathrm{K}$
- $x$ = thickness of the element, $\mathrm{m}$
- $R = x/k$ = thermal resistance, $\mathrm{m^2 \cdot K/W}$
- $U$ = overall thermal transmittance, $\mathrm{W/(m^2 \cdot K)}$

### 2.1.2 Convection (Newton's Law of Cooling)
Heat transfer between a surface and the adjacent air (or fluid).

$$q_{conv} = h_c \cdot A \cdot (T_s - T_a) \quad [\mathrm{W}]$$

where $h_c$ = convective heat transfer coefficient ($\mathrm{W/(m^2 \cdot K)}$). Typical values:
- Still air (interior surface): $h_i \approx 8.3 \, \mathrm{W/(m^2 \cdot K)}$
- Windy exterior: $h_o \approx 22.7 \, \mathrm{W/(m^2 \cdot K)}$ (ASHRAE, 6.7 m/s wind)

### 2.1.3 Radiation (Stefan–Boltzmann Law)
Heat transfer by electromagnetic waves from surfaces (especially roofs, glass, and hot external walls).

$$q_{rad} = \varepsilon \cdot \sigma \cdot A \cdot (T_s^4 - T_{surr}^4) \quad [\mathrm{W}]$$

where:
- $\varepsilon$ = surface emissivity (0–1)
- $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \, \mathrm{W/(m^2 \cdot K^4)}$ is the **Stefan–Boltzmann constant**
- $T_s$, $T_{surr}$ = absolute surface and surrounding temperatures, $\mathrm{K}$

For solar radiation absorbed by an opaque surface:
$$q_{solar} = \alpha_s \cdot I \cdot A \quad [\mathrm{W}]$$
where $I$ = incident solar radiation, $\mathrm{W/m^2}$.

## 2.2 U-Value and R-Value of Composite Assemblies

> [!NOTE]
> For a multi-layer building element (e.g., plastered brick wall with insulation), thermal resistances add in **series**:

$$R_{total} = R_{si} + \dfrac{x_1}{k_1} + \dfrac{x_2}{k_2} + \dfrac{x_3}{k_3} + \cdots + R_{so} \quad \left[\dfrac{\mathrm{m^2 \cdot K}}{\mathrm{W}}\right]$$

$$U = \dfrac{1}{R_{total}} \quad \left[\dfrac{\mathrm{W}}{\mathrm{m^2 \cdot K}}\right]$$

where $R_{si}$ and $R_{so}$ are the **internal and external surface resistances**.

## 2.3 Time Lag and Decrement Factor (Thermal Mass Behaviour)

For heavy walls under cyclic (diurnal) solar loading:

$$\phi = \dfrac{0.123 \cdot x}{\sqrt{\alpha \cdot T}} \quad [\mathrm{h}]$$

$$f = \dfrac{1}{e^{\phi}} \quad \text{(for thick walls)}$$

where:
- $\phi$ = time lag (delay between peak outside and peak inside temperature), h
- $x$ = wall thickness, m
- $\alpha = k/(\rho c_p)$ = thermal diffusivity, $\mathrm{m^2/s}$
- $T$ = period of heat wave (24 h for daily cycle)
- $f$ = decrement factor (ratio of indoor to outdoor temperature amplitude)

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Application | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$ | Heat gain/loss through wall, roof, glass | W |
| 2 | $R = x/k$ | Resistance of single material layer | $\mathrm{m^2 \cdot K/W}$ |
| 3 | $R_{total} = \sum R_i$ | Series resistance of composite wall | $\mathrm{m^2 \cdot K/W}$ |
| 4 | $U = 1/R_{total}$ | Overall transmittance of assembly | $\mathrm{W/(m^2 \cdot K)}$ |
| 5 | $C = \rho \cdot c_p \cdot V$ | Thermal capacity of building element | J/K |
| 6 | $\alpha = k/(\rho c_p)$ | Thermal diffusivity | $\mathrm{m^2/s}$ |
| 7 | $q = \varepsilon \sigma T^4$ | Radiative heat flux from blackbody | $\mathrm{W/m^2}$ |
| 8 | $q = h_c \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$ | Convective heat transfer | W |
| 9 | $Q_{solar} = SHGC \cdot A \cdot I$ | Heat gain through glass | W |
| 10 | $\phi = 0.123 x / \sqrt{\alpha T}$ | Time lag of cyclic heat wave | h |
| 11 | $f = e^{-\phi}$ | Decrement factor (approx.) | – |
| 12 | $E = P \times t$ | Electrical energy consumption | kWh |
| 13 | $P_{connected} = \sum P_i$ | Total connected electrical load | kW |
| 14 | $P_{max\,demand} = D_f \times P_{connected}$ | Maximum demand with diversity factor | kW |
| 15 | $Q_{HVAC} = Q_{wall} + Q_{roof} + Q_{glass} + Q_{infil.} + Q_{vent} + Q_{int.} + Q_{solar}$ | Total cooling load | W |
| 16 | $\mathrm{COP} = Q_{cool} / W_{input}$ | Coefficient of Performance of chiller | – |
| 17 | $1 \mathrm{TR} = 3.517 \mathrm{kW}$ | Refrigeration ton → kW | – |
| 18 | $EER = 3.517 / (P_{kW}/Q_{TR})$ | Energy Efficiency Ratio | – |
| 19 | $\mathrm{CFM} = Q_{sensible} / (1.08 \times \Delta T)$ | Supply air volume (sensible) | $\mathrm{ft^3/min}$ |
| 20 | $D_f = \mathrm{Total\ load / Max\ demand}$ | Diversity factor (always ≥ 1) | – |

## 2.5 Typical Thermophysical Properties of Common Building Materials (Kerala Construction)

| Material | $\rho$ ($\mathrm{kg/m^3}$) | $k$ ($\mathrm{W/mK}$) | $c_p$ ($\mathrm{kJ/kgK}$) | $\alpha$ ($\mathrm{m^2/s \times 10^{-6}}$) |
|----------|-------|-------|---------|-------|
| Plain concrete | 2300 | 1.40 | 0.88 | 0.69 |
| Brick (common) | 1700 | 0.70 | 0.84 | 0.49 |
| Aerated concrete (AAC) | 550 | 0.16 | 1.05 | 0.28 |
| Cement plaster | 2000 | 0.72 | 0.84 | 0.43 |
| Gypsum board | 950 | 0.17 | 1.09 | 0.16 |
| Laterite (Kerala) | 1800 | 0.65 | 0.88 | 0.41 |
| Glass (6 mm clear) | 2500 | 1.00 | 0.84 | 0.48 |
| Rock wool insulation | 40 | 0.04 | 0.84 | 1.19 |
| EPS (expanded polystyrene) | 15–30 | 0.035 | 1.21 | 0.96 |
| Timber (teak) | 700 | 0.16 | 1.76 | 0.13 |
| Plywood | 600 | 0.12 | 1.22 | 0.16 |
| Steel | 7850 | 45.0 | 0.45 | 12.7 |
| Air gap (20 mm, still) | 1.2 | 0.025 | 1.00 | 20.8 |
| GI sheet (roofing) | 7850 | 45.0 | 0.45 | 12.7 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Materials with **low $k$ and high $c_p$** (e.g., AAC blocks, EPS) are the *best* insulators. **High $k$** materials like steel/glass are *thermal bridges* that must be broken by insulation layers.

## 2.6 Categories of Cooling Load (HVAC Fundamentals)

A building's total cooling load comprises seven components, evaluated independently:

1. **$Q_{wall}$** — transmission through external walls.
2. **$Q_{roof}$** — transmission through the roof (often the *largest* load in tropical climates).
3. **$Q_{glass}$** — transmission + solar gain through glazing.
4. **$Q_{infiltration}$** — heat carried by uncontrolled air leakage.
5. **$Q_{ventilation}$** — heat carried by the *required* fresh-air supply.
6. **$Q_{internal}$** — heat from occupants, lights, equipment, appliances.
7. **$Q_{solar, opaque}$** — solar radiation absorbed by opaque surfaces (especially dark roofs).

## 2.7 Categories of Electrical Loads in Buildings

Electrical loads are broadly classified into:

- **Lighting loads** — 10–15 W/m² (offices), 5–8 W/m² (residential)
- **Power loads** — fans, pumps, lifts, ACs
- **HVAC loads** — typically the *largest* consumer (40–60 % of total)
- **Plug loads** — computers, printers, kitchen equipment
- **Emergency loads** — fire pumps, lifts, emergency lighting
- **Special loads** — UPS, IT servers, data centres

The **diversity factor ($D_f$)** and **demand factor** convert the connected load into a realistic maximum demand.

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> - **Energy-efficient building design** (ECBC-compliant) directly requires accurate thermophysical property data.
> - **Green building ratings (GRIHA, LEED-India)** award credits for walls with $U \leq 0.4 \, \mathrm{W/m^2K}$ and roofs with $U \leq 0.3 \, \mathrm{W/m^2K}$.
> - **Fire safety design** uses specific heat and density to model fire spread.
> - **HVAC equipment selection** is impossible without proper thermal load estimation — under-design → discomfort, over-design → wasted capital and energy.
> - **Electrical design** must precede architectural finalisation to size risers, distribution boards, and transformer rooms (especially for high-rise buildings in Kerala).
</br>
