<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — U-Value of a Composite Wall

**Statement:** Derive the overall thermal transmittance ($U$-value) of a multi-layered building element using the electrical-network analogy of thermal resistance.

### Step 1: Fourier's Law in differential form
Consider one-dimensional steady-state heat conduction through a single homogeneous slab of thickness $x$ and conductivity $k$:

$$q = -k \cdot A \cdot \dfrac{dT}{dx}$$

The negative sign indicates heat flows from high to low temperature.

### Step 2: Integrate across the slab
Boundary condition: at $x=0$, $T = T_1$; at $x = L$, $T = T_2$.

$$\int_{T_1}^{T_2} dT = -\dfrac{q}{kA} \int_0^L dx$$

$$T_1 - T_2 = \dfrac{q \cdot L}{k A}$$

### Step 3: Define thermal resistance
Rearranging:

$$q = \dfrac{(T_1 - T_2)}{(L / kA)} = \dfrac{\Delta T}{R}$$

where $R = L/(kA) = x/k$ **per unit area** $= L/k$ in $\mathrm{m^2 \cdot K/W}$.

### Step 4: Series combination of layers
For $n$ layers in series (typical wall: plaster + brick + insulation + plaster), the heat flux is the same through all layers:

$$q = \dfrac{\Delta T_{total}}{R_{total}}$$

By the additivity of temperature drops: $\Delta T_{total} = \Delta T_1 + \Delta T_2 + \cdots + \Delta T_n$

Substituting $q R_i = \Delta T_i$:

$$q \cdot (R_1 + R_2 + \cdots + R_n + R_{si} + R_{so}) = \Delta T_{total}$$

### Step 5: Final compact result

$$U = \dfrac{1}{R_{total}} = \dfrac{1}{R_{si} + \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left(\dfrac{x_i}{k_i}\right) + R_{so}}$$

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — U-Value of a Kerala-Type Wall

**Given:** External wall of a building in Kochi, comprising (outside → inside):
- 12 mm cement plaster ($k_1 = 0.72$, $x_1 = 0.012$ m)
- 200 mm laterite block ($k_2 = 0.65$, $x_2 = 0.200$ m)
- 50 mm EPS insulation ($k_3 = 0.035$, $x_3 = 0.050$ m)
- 12 mm gypsum board ($k_4 = 0.17$, $x_4 = 0.012$ m)

**Surface resistances** (ASHRAE):
- $R_{si} = 0.13 \, \mathrm{m^2 K/W}$ (interior, still air)
- $R_{so} = 0.04 \, \mathrm{m^2 K/W}$ (exterior, 6.7 m/s wind)

**Find:** $U$-value of the wall.

### Solution

**Step 1:** Compute the resistance of each solid layer.

$$R_1 = \dfrac{x_1}{k_1} = \dfrac{0.012}{0.72} = 0.0167 \, \mathrm{m^2 K/W}$$

$$R_2 = \dfrac{0.200}{0.65} = 0.3077 \, \mathrm{m^2 K/W}$$

$$R_3 = \dfrac{0.050}{0.035} = 1.4286 \, \mathrm{m^2 K/W}$$

$$R_4 = \dfrac{0.012}{0.17} = 0.0706 \, \mathrm{m^2 K/W}$$

**Step 2:** Sum all resistances in series.

$$\begin{aligned}
R_{total} &= R_{si} + R_1 + R_2 + R_3 + R_4 + R_{so} \\
&= 0.13 + 0.0167 + 0.3077 + 1.4286 + 0.0706 + 0.04 \\
&= 1.9936 \, \mathrm{m^2 K/W}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3:** Compute $U$-value.

$$U = \dfrac{1}{R_{total}} = \dfrac{1}{1.9936} = 0.5016 \, \mathrm{W/(m^2 K)}$$

**Step 4:** Interpretation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The $U$-value $0.50 \, \mathrm{W/m^2K}$ is **acceptable** for a residential building in Kerala as per ECBC+ (which prescribes $U \leq 0.4$ for walls, so a slightly thicker EPS of 60–65 mm would be needed for full compliance).
> Note that **the EPS layer contributes 71.6 % of the total resistance** ($1.4286/1.9936 = 0.717$). A thin insulation layer dominates the thermal performance.

## 3.3 Derivation 2 — Time Lag of a Heavy Wall

**Statement:** Derive the expression for the time lag ($\phi$) of a sinusoidal temperature wave passing through a thick homogeneous wall.

### Step 1: Define the boundary condition
Outdoor air temperature varies sinusoidally with 24-hour period:

$$T_o(t) = \bar{T_o} + A_o \sin(\omega t)$$

where $\omega = 2\pi / T = 2\pi / (24 \times 3600) = 7.27 \times 10^{-5} \, \mathrm{rad/s}$.

### Step 2: 1-D transient heat conduction (no internal source)
The governing PDE is:

$$\dfrac{\partial T}{\partial t} = \alpha \cdot \dfrac{\partial^2 T}{\partial x^2}$$

### Step 3: Solution by separation of variables
Assume $T(x,t) = \bar{T}(x) + \theta(x,t)$ where $\theta$ is the periodic component. Substituting and applying the cyclic BC, the well-known solution is:

$$\theta(x,t) = A_o \cdot e^{-x \sqrt{\omega/(2\alpha)}} \cdot \sin\left(\omega t - x \sqrt{\dfrac{\omega}{2\alpha}}\right)$$

### Step 4: Identify the time lag
The argument of the sine is the *phase*. The phase lag between outdoor peak ($\omega t = \pi/2$) and indoor peak is:

$$\phi = x \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{\omega}{2\alpha}} \quad [\mathrm{rad}]$$

Convert to hours using $T = 2\pi/\omega$:

$$\phi = \dfrac{x}{2} \sqrt{\dfrac{T}{\pi \alpha}} \quad [\mathrm{s}]$$

Substituting numerical constants ($T = 24$ h, $\pi \approx 3.1416$):

$$\phi = 0.123 \cdot \dfrac{x}{\sqrt{\alpha \cdot T}} \quad [\mathrm{h}]$$

### Step 5: Decrement factor
The amplitude reduction is:

$$f = \dfrac{A_i}{A_o} = \exp\left(-x \sqrt{\dfrac{\omega}{2\alpha}}\right) = e^{-\phi}$$

## 3.4 Derivation 3 — Cooling Load Through an Opaque Roof (CLTD Method)

**Statement:** Derive the instantaneous cooling load through a roof using the **Cooling Load Temperature Difference (CLTD)** method, which is the de-facto standard in IS/ASHRAE practice.

### Step 1: Define CLTD
CLTD is the equivalent steady-state temperature difference across a roof that, when multiplied by $U$ and $A$, gives the *actual* instantaneous cooling load accounting for heat storage, time lag, and indoor surface convection.

$$\mathrm{CLTD} = T_{o,equiv} - T_i - \mathrm{Correction\ terms}$$

### Step 2: Cooling load through roof
$$Q_{roof} = U \cdot A \cdot \mathrm{CLTD} \quad [\mathrm{W}]$$

For an opaque roof, the standard CLTD at design conditions (max solar gain hour) is typically **30–35 °C** for a 100 mm concrete roof (per ASHRAE Table).

### Step 3: Apply the correction
- Latitude correction (Kerala ≈ 10° N, mostly negligible).
- Inside design temperature correction (T_i = 25 °C baseline).
- Time-of-day correction (peak solar ≈ 14:00–16:00 for roofs).

**Example:** $U = 1.2 \, \mathrm{W/m^2K}$, $A = 100 \, \mathrm{m^2}$, CLTD = 30 °C.

$$Q_{roof} = 1.2 \times 100 \times 30 = 3600 \, \mathrm{W} = 3.6 \, \mathrm{kW}$$

## 3.5 Derivation 4 — Electrical Maximum Demand from Connected Load

**Statement:** Derive the relationship between connected load, demand factor, and diversity factor for a building's electrical system.

### Step 1: Define connected load
Sum of nameplate ratings of all electrical appliances/loads in a circuit or building.

$$P_{connected} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P_i \quad [\mathrm{kW}]$$

### Step 2: Apply demand factor
Not all loads operate simultaneously at full rating.

$$P_{max\,demand} = D_f \times P_{connected}$$

where $D_f$ is the **demand factor** (always $\leq 1$).

### Step 3: Diversity factor across consumers
When multiple consumers (apartments, shops) share a transformer:

$$D_{diversity} = \dfrac{\sum P_{i,\ max\ demand}}{P_{transformer\ max\ demand}}$$

Hence:

$$P_{transformer} = \dfrac{\sum P_{i,\ max\ demand}}{D_{diversity}}$$

### Step 4: Worked Example
A residential building has:
- Lighting: $50 \times 0.2 = 10 \, \mathrm{kW}$, $D_f = 0.8$
- Fans: $50 \times 0.06 = 3 \, \mathrm{kW}$, $D_f = 0.7$
- ACs: $50 \times 1.5 = 75 \, \mathrm{kW}$, $D_f = 0.85$
- Lifts, pumps, misc.: $20 \, \mathrm{kW}$, $D_f = 0.6$

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{max\,demand} &= 0.8 \times 10 + 0.7 \times 3 + 0.85 \times 75 + 0.6 \times 20 \\
&= 8.0 + 2.1 + 63.75 + 12.0 \\
&= 85.85 \, \mathrm{kW}
\end{aligned}$$

Assuming power factor $\cos\phi = 0.85$:

$$S = \dfrac{P}{\cos\phi} = \dfrac{85.85}{0.85} = 101.0 \, \mathrm{kVA}$$

Transformer size = next standard rating = **125 kVA**.

## 3.6 Derivation 5 — Ventilation/Infiltration Load

**Statement:** Derive the sensible heat gain from outdoor air entering the conditioned space.

### Step 1: Mass flow rate of infiltrating/ventilating air

$$\dot{m} = \rho_a \cdot \dot{V} = 1.2 \times \dot{V} \quad [\mathrm{kg/s}]$$

where $\dot{V}$ is volumetric flow in $\mathrm{m^3/s}$.

### Step 2: Sensible heat
$$Q_{sensible} = \dot{m} \cdot c_p \cdot (T_o - T_i) = 1.2 \cdot \dot{V} \cdot 1005 \cdot \Delta T \quad [\mathrm{W}]$$

$$Q_{sensible} = 1206 \cdot \dot{V} \cdot \Delta T$$

**Metric short form:**
$$Q_{sensible} = 1.2 \cdot \dot{V} \cdot \Delta T \quad [\mathrm{W}]$$

### Step 3: Latent heat
$$Q_{latent} = \dot{m} \cdot h_{fg} \cdot (W_o - W_i) = 1.2 \cdot \dot{V} \cdot 2501 \cdot \Delta W$$

where $h_{fg} = 2501 \, \mathrm{kJ/kg}$ and $W$ is the humidity ratio.

### Step 4: Example
$\dot{V} = 0.1 \, \mathrm{m^3/s}$, $T_o - T_i = 10 \, \mathrm{K}$, $W_o - W_i = 0.005 \, \mathrm{kg/kg}$.

$$Q_{sensible} = 1206 \times 0.1 \times 10 = 1206 \, \mathrm{W} = 1.21 \, \mathrm{kW}$$

$$Q_{latent} = 3001 \times 0.1 \times 0.005 \times 0.001^{-1} = 1500.5 \, \mathrm{W} = 1.50 \, \mathrm{kW}$$

$$Q_{total} = 1.21 + 1.50 = 2.71 \, \mathrm{kW}$$

## 3.7 Analytical Matrix — Heating/Cooling Load Computation

| # | Load Component | Formula | Typical Kerala Magnitude |
|---|----------------|---------|--------------------------|
| 1 | External wall (opaque) | $Q_1 = U_w A_w \mathrm{CLTD}_w$ | 15–25 % of total |
| 2 | Roof (opaque) | $Q_2 = U_r A_r \mathrm{CLTD}_r$ | 25–35 % of total (largest) |
| 3 | Glazing (conduction) | $Q_3 = U_g A_g \Delta T$ | 5–10 % |
| 4 | Glazing (solar) | $Q_4 = \mathrm{SHGC} \cdot A_g \cdot I_{peak}$ | 10–20 % |
| 5 | Internal walls, partitions, floors | $Q_5 = U A \Delta T$ | 3–5 % |
| 6 | Ceiling/roof (if not in $Q_2$) | $Q_6 = U A \mathrm{CLTD}$ | Included in $Q_2$ |
| 7 | Infiltration | $Q_7 = 1.2 \dot{V}_i c_p \Delta T$ | 5–10 % |
| 8 | Ventilation (fresh air) | $Q_8 = 1.2 \dot{V}_v c_p \Delta T + h_{fg} \Delta W$ | 10–20 % |
| 9 | Occupants (sensible) | $Q_9 = N \times 60 \, \mathrm{W/person}$ (sensible, office) | 5–10 % |
| 10 | Occupants (latent) | $Q_{10} = N \times 55 \, \mathrm{W/person}$ (latent) | 5 % |
| 11 | Lighting | $Q_{11} = P_{lights} \times 0.86 \times F_{u}$ | 10–15 % |
| 12 | Equipment/Appliances | $Q_{12} = P_{equip} \times F_u$ | 5–15 % |

Total Cooling Load:

$$Q_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{12} Q_i \quad [\mathrm{W}]$$

Convert to TR:

$$\mathrm{TR} = \dfrac{Q_{total}}{3.517}$$
</br>

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Define the following thermophysical properties of building materials:**
**(a)** Thermal conductivity
**(b)** Specific heat capacity
**(c)** Thermal diffusivity

#### Model Answer:

**(a) Thermal Conductivity (k):** It is the rate of heat flow per unit area per unit temperature gradient through a material under steady-state conditions.

$$k = \dfrac{Q \cdot x}{A \cdot \Delta T} \quad \mathrm{W/(m \cdot K)}$$

**(b) Specific Heat Capacity ($c_p$):** The amount of heat energy required to raise the temperature of **1 kg** of a material by **1 °C** (or 1 K), with no phase change.

$$c_p = \dfrac{Q}{m \cdot \Delta T} \quad \mathrm{J/(kg \cdot K)}$$

**(c) Thermal Diffusivity ($\alpha$):** It measures how quickly temperature changes propagate through a material. It is the ratio of thermal conductivity to volumetric heat capacity.

$$\alpha = \dfrac{k}{\rho \cdot c_p} \quad \mathrm{m^2/s}$$

It governs the speed of heat diffusion — high $\alpha$ (steel: $12.7 \times 10^{-6} \, \mathrm{m^2/s}$) means fast response; low $\alpha$ (insulation: $< 1 \times 10^{-6} \, \mathrm{m^2/s}$) means slow response.

### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**List the seven components of a building's total cooling load and state the contribution of each in a tropical climate like Kerala.**

#### Model Answer:

| # | Component | Typical % in Kerala |
|---|-----------|--------------------|
| 1 | Roof transmission | 25–35 % (largest) |
| 2 | Wall transmission | 15–20 % |
| 3 | Glazing (solar + conduction) | 15–20 % |
| 4 | Ventilation (fresh air) | 10–15 % |
| 5 | Infiltration | 5–10 % |
| 6 | Internal heat (lights + equipment) | 10–15 % |
| 7 | Occupants (sensible + latent) | 5–10 % |

**Mnemonic:** "**R**oof, **W**alls, **G**lass, **V**ent, **I**nfil, **I**nternal, **P**eople → **RWG-VII-P**."

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, 14 Marks]

**(a)** A 230 mm thick solid concrete wall has $k = 1.4 \, \mathrm{W/mK}$. If the inside surface is at 25 °C and outside at 38 °C, determine the **steady-state heat flux** through the wall, the **thermal resistance**, and comment on whether adding a **25 mm EPS insulation** ($k = 0.035 \, \mathrm{W/mK}$) on the outside would reduce the heat gain by more than **80 %**. **Use ASHRAE surface resistances $R_{si} = 0.13$, $R_{so} = 0.04 \, \mathrm{m^2 K/W}$.** **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Define **Time Lag** and **Decrement Factor** for building walls. A 200 mm brick wall has $k = 0.7$, $\rho = 1700 \, \mathrm{kg/m^3}$, $c_p = 0.84 \, \mathrm{kJ/kgK}$. Calculate its **time lag** for a **24 h cycle** and interpret the result. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

##### Part (a) — Heat flux and effect of insulation

**Step 1: Without insulation (bare 230 mm concrete)**

$$R_{bare} = R_{si} + \dfrac{0.230}{1.4} + R_{so} = 0.13 + 0.1643 + 0.04 = 0.3343 \, \mathrm{m^2 K/W}$$

$$U_{bare} = \dfrac{1}{0.3343} = 2.991 \, \mathrm{W/m^2 K}$$

$$q_{bare} = U_{bare} \cdot \Delta T = 2.991 \times (38 - 25) = 38.88 \, \mathrm{W/m^2}$$

**Step 2: With 25 mm EPS outside**

$$R_{ins} = 0.3343 + \dfrac{0.025}{0.035} = 0.3343 + 0.7143 = 1.0486 \, \mathrm{m^2 K/W}$$

$$U_{ins} = \dfrac{1}{1.0486} = 0.954 \, \mathrm{W/m^2 K}$$

$$q_{ins} = 0.954 \times 13 = 12.40 \, \mathrm{W/m^2}$$

**Step 3: Percentage reduction**

$$\%\,\text{reduction} = \dfrac{38.88 - 12.40}{38.88} \times 100 = 68.1 \,\%$$

**Conclusion:** A 25 mm EPS layer **reduces heat gain by 68.1 %**, which is **less than 80 %**. To exceed 80 %, EPS thickness would need to be:

$$R_{needed} = \dfrac{1}{0.20 \times U_{bare}} = \dfrac{1}{0.20 \times 2.991} = 1.672 \, \mathrm{m^2 K/W}$$

$$R_{EPS} = 1.672 - 0.3343 = 1.3377 \, \mathrm{m^2 K/W}$$

$$x_{EPS} = 1.3377 \times 0.035 = 46.8 \, \mathrm{mm}$$

**So 50 mm EPS would be needed to reduce heat gain by ≥ 80 %.**

##### Part (b) — Time lag and decrement factor

**Definitions:**
- **Time lag ($\phi$):** The time delay (in hours) between the peak outdoor temperature and the peak indoor surface temperature on the other side of a wall, caused by the wall's thermal mass.
- **Decrement factor ($f$):** The ratio of the indoor temperature amplitude to the outdoor temperature amplitude — i.e., how much the temperature swing is "damped" by the wall.

**Step 1: Thermal diffusivity**

$$\alpha = \dfrac{k}{\rho c_p} = \dfrac{0.7}{1700 \times 840} = 4.90 \times 10^{-7} \, \mathrm{m^2/s}$$

**Step 2: Time lag**

$$\phi = 0.123 \times \dfrac{x}{\sqrt{\alpha \cdot T}} = 0.123 \times \dfrac{0.200}{\sqrt{4.90 \times 10^{-7} \times 24}}$$

$$\phi = 0.123 \times \dfrac{0.200}{\sqrt{1.176 \times 10^{-5}}} = 0.123 \times \dfrac{0.200}{3.43 \times 10^{-3}} = 7.17 \, \mathrm{h}$$

**Step 3: Decrement factor**

$$f = e^{-\phi} = e^{-7.17} = 0.00077$$

**Interpretation:** The 200 mm brick wall delays peak outdoor heat by **7.2 hours** (so peak heat enters the room at night when cooling is naturally easier) and reduces the indoor temperature amplitude to less than **0.1 %** of the outdoor swing. This is the principle of **thermal-mass design** in traditional Kerala *tharavadu* houses.

---

### **Question B** [KTU University Exam – June 2024, 14 Marks] (Alternative Choice)

**(a)** A $120 \, \mathrm{m^2}$ office floor in Kochi has the following loads: lighting $1.2 \, \mathrm{kW}$ (fully on), equipment $2.5 \, \mathrm{kW}$ (usage factor 0.6), 25 occupants (each $75 \, \mathrm{W}$ sensible + $55 \, \mathrm{W}$ latent), fresh air supply $0.15 \, \mathrm{m^3/s}$, outdoor $35 \, \mathrm{°C}$ DB / $60 \,\%$ RH, indoor $25 \, \mathrm{°C}$ DB / $50 \,\%$ RH.
Compute the **total cooling load in kW and TR**. **Assume $h_{fg} = 2500 \, \mathrm{kJ/kg}$.** **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **CLTD method** of cooling load estimation. A roof of a building has $U = 0.8 \, \mathrm{W/m^2K}$, $A = 80 \, \mathrm{m^2}$, and CLTD at peak = $33 \, \mathrm{°C}$. Calculate the **peak cooling load** through the roof in **kW and TR**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

##### Part (a) — Total cooling load for the office

**Step 1: Sensible loads**

- Lighting: $Q_{L,s} = 1.2 \times 0.86 \times 1.0 = 1.032 \, \mathrm{kW}$ (factor 0.86 for ballasts)
- Equipment: $Q_{E,s} = 2.5 \times 0.6 = 1.50 \, \mathrm{kW}$
- Occupants: $Q_{O,s} = 25 \times 0.075 = 1.875 \, \mathrm{kW}$
- Ventilation: $Q_{V,s} = 1.2 \times 0.15 \times 1005 \times (35 - 25) / 1000 = 1.81 \, \mathrm{kW}$
  *(Using $Q_{V,s} = 1.2 \dot{V} c_p \Delta T$ in W, then convert to kW)*

$$Q_{V,s} = 1.2 \times 0.15 \times 1005 \times 10 = 1809 \, \mathrm{W} = 1.81 \, \mathrm{kW}$$

- **Total sensible:** $Q_S = 1.032 + 1.50 + 1.875 + 1.81 = 6.22 \, \mathrm{kW}$

**Step 2: Latent loads**

- Occupants: $Q_{O,L} = 25 \times 0.055 = 1.375 \, \mathrm{kW}$
- Ventilation: From psychrometric chart, $W_o \approx 0.0192$, $W_i \approx 0.0100$, $\Delta W = 0.0092$.

$$Q_{V,L} = 1.2 \times 0.15 \times 2500 \times 0.0092 = 4.14 \, \mathrm{kW}$$

- **Total latent:** $Q_L = 1.375 + 4.14 = 5.52 \, \mathrm{kW}$

**Step 3: Total cooling load**

$$Q_{total} = Q_S + Q_L = 6.22 + 5.52 = 11.74 \, \mathrm{kW}$$

$$Q_{total, TR} = \dfrac{11.74}{3.517} = 3.34 \, \mathrm{TR}$$

**Selection:** Choose next standard capacity = **4 TR** (DX or VRF indoor unit).

##### Part (b) — CLTD method and roof load

**CLTD (Cooling Load Temperature Difference) method:**
It is a simplified, tabulated procedure where ASHRAE publishes **CLTD correction values** as a function of orientation, month, hour, latitude, and construction type. The cooling load is computed as:

$$Q = U \cdot A \cdot \mathrm{CLTD} \quad [\mathrm{W}]$$

**Advantages:** simple, tabular, fast — good for preliminary design.
**Disadvantages:** assumes standard indoor temperature, limited to steady-state opaque surfaces, needs correction for non-standard conditions.

**Step 1: Roof cooling load**

$$Q_{roof} = U \cdot A \cdot \mathrm{CLTD} = 0.8 \times 80 \times 33 = 2112 \, \mathrm{W} = 2.11 \, \mathrm{kW}$$

**Step 2: Convert to TR**

$$Q_{roof, TR} = \dfrac{2.11}{3.517} = 0.60 \, \mathrm{TR}$$

**Interpretation:** A single 80 m² roof contributes ~0.6 TR at peak — this is a *major* load in tropical climates, justifying the need for high-performance roof insulation (e.g., 75 mm PUF or 50 mm PIR over a 100 mm concrete deck).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting surface resistances** $R_{si}$ and $R_{so}$ — they contribute 8–10 % of total wall resistance. A **2-mark penalty** is typical.
> 2. **Unit mismatch:** $\Delta T$ must be in **K or °C** (numerically same) but **never °F**. Density must be in **kg/m³**, not g/cm³.
> 3. **Conflating EER and COP:** EER uses BTU/h, COP is dimensionless — do not interchange.
> 4. **Electrical load mistakes:** Always apply **demand factor per category** before summing; never use a single factor for all loads.
> 5. **HVAC load omission:** Kerala-designers often forget the **latent load from outdoor fresh air** — this can be 30–50 % of total cooling load in coastal districts (humidity ratio difference is high).
> 6. **Time-lag formula constants:** Use $T$ in **hours** and $x$ in **metres**; thermal diffusivity in **m²/s** — unit errors cause factor-of-60 mistakes.
> 7. **Sketch the heat flow direction** in conduction problems (cold→hot arrow) — examiners reward it with 1 mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 3 (Thermal Design of Buildings)**

### Thermophysical Properties
- **k** (conductivity) — heat conduction rate; **low k = good insulator**.
- **c_p** (specific heat) — thermal storage per kg; **high c_p = high thermal mass**.
- **ρ** (density) — combined with c_p gives volumetric heat capacity.
- **α = k/(ρc_p)** — thermal diffusivity; controls heat-wave speed.
- **ε** (emissivity) — radiative efficiency (0 = mirror, 1 = blackbody).
- **U** (transmittance) — overall heat transfer through assembly; **target ≤ 0.4 W/m²K for ECBC+ walls in Kerala**.
- **R** (resistance) — 1/U; **adds in series** for layered walls.

### Thermal Control
- Operates at **material / component / building** scale.
- Uses **insulation, thermal mass, shading, ventilation, reflective surfaces**.
- Quantified by **time lag (delay)** and **decrement factor (damping)**.

### Electrical Load Calculation
- **Connected load** → apply **demand factor** → **maximum demand** → apply **diversity factor** → **transformer kVA**.
- **1 kW = kVA / cos φ**; **typical residential PF = 0.85–0.95**.
- **Lighting:** 5–15 W/m²; **Power:** fans, pumps, lifts; **HVAC:** largest (40–60 %).
- Codes: **IS 732**, **NEC**, **IE Rules**.

### HVAC Load Calculation
- **7 components:** walls, roof, glass, infiltration, ventilation, internal, occupants.
- **Methods:** CLTD, TFM, RTS, energy-balance.
- **Unit conversions:** 1 TR = 3.517 kW = 12,000 BTU/h.
- **Kerala focus:** cooling-dominated; design ΔT ≈ 10 °C; include **high latent load from humidity**.
- **Selection step:** Total load → Add 10–20 % safety → Pick next standard TR.

### Key Numerical Constants
- **Stefan–Boltzmann constant** $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \, \mathrm{W/m^2 K^4}$.
- **$h_{fg}$ of water at 25 °C** = 2501 kJ/kg.
- **$c_p$ of air** = 1005 J/kgK.
- **$ρ$ of air** = 1.2 kg/m³.
- **Surface resistances:** $R_{si} = 0.13$, $R_{so} = 0.04 \, \mathrm{m^2 K/W}$.

### Critical Relationships
- $R_{series} = \sum R_i$.
- $U = 1/R_{total}$.
- $Q = U A \Delta T$.
- $Q_{sensible} = 1.2 \dot{V} c_p \Delta T$.
- $Q_{latent} = 1.2 \dot{V} h_{fg} \Delta W$.
- $\phi = 0.123 x / \sqrt{\alpha T}$.
- $P_{transformer} = \sum P_{max\ demand} / (D_f \times PF)$.

### Mnemonics
- **HVAC loads:** "RWG-VII-P" (Roof, Walls, Glass, Vent, Infil, Internal, People).
- **Electrical:** "L-P-H-P-E" (Lighting, Power, HVAC, Plug, Emergency).
- **Modes of heat transfer:** "CCR" (Conduction, Convection, Radiation).
</br>

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Functional Protection: Causes of Fire, Mechanism of Fire Spread in Buildings, Classification of Fire, High Temperature Effects and Combustibility of Building Materials and Structure
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECET415 - ARCHITECTURAL ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module Module 3 > - **Topic:** Functional Protection: Causes of Fire, Mechanism of Fire Spread in Buildings, Classification of Fire, High Temperature Effects and Combustibility of Building Materials and Structure # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Definition of Fire (Functional Perspective in Buildings) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Fire (KTU/NBC Definition):** Fire is a rapid, self-sustaining oxidation process accompanied by the evolution of heat, light, and flame, occurring when three essential elements — **Fuel, Oxygen, and Heat** — combine in appropriate proportions to sustain a chain reaction of combustion. In the context of **Architectural Engineering (PECET415)**, *functional fire protection* refers to the integrated design strategy embedded within a building's spatial planning, material selection, and structural detailing to **control ignition, limit fire growth, ensure safe egress, and prevent structural collapse** during a fire incident. ## 1.2 The Fire Triangle — Intuitive Analogy > [!NOTE] > **Analogy — The Three-Legged Stool:** Imagine fire as a three-legged wooden stool. Remove any one leg (fuel, oxygen, or heat) and the stool collapses — fire cannot exist. This is the **Fire Triangle**, a foundational concept in KTU Module 3. The three legs of the combustion stool: - **Fuel (Combustible Material):** Wood, paper, petrol, fabric, plastic, LPG, etc. - **Oxygen (Oxidizer):** Atmospheric air (contains ~21% O₂). Fires are suppressed when O₂ drops below **15–16%** (ventilation-controlled limit). - **Heat (Ignition Source):** Sparks, naked flames, electrical arcs, friction, lightning, etc. A modern extension — the **Fire Tetrahedron** — adds the *Chain Reaction* (free radicals in flame chemistry) as the fourth component. Suppression methods (e.g., dry powder) target this chemical chain. ## 1.3 Causes of Fire in Buildings (As per IS 2190 & NBC 2016) Fire initiation in buildings arises from identifiable causes. The KTU syllabus categorizes them as follows: | Sl. No. | Cause | Real-World Example | |---------|-------|-------------------| | 1 | **Electrical faults** | Short circuits, overloaded wiring, damaged insulation | | 2 | **Naked flames & sparks** | Matchsticks, candles, welding/cutting operations | | 3 | **Smoking materials** | Carelessly discarded cigarettes, beedis | | 4 | **Cooking accidents** | Oil fires, unattended stoves, overheated utensils | | 5 | **Heating appliances** | Room heaters, boilers, geysers placed near combustibles | | 6 | **Static electricity** | Fuel tank filling, dust clouds in grain silos | | 7 | **Lightning strikes** | Direct strike on tall structures without lightning arresters | | 8 | **Spontaneous combustion** | Haystacks, oily rags, coal piles, phosphorous | | 9 | **Chemical reactions** | Mixing oxidizers with organics (e.g., nitric acid + acetone) | | 10 | **Arson (Intentional)** | Deliberate ignition for insurance/crime | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Highlight:** Per NBC 2016 statistics, **electrical faults account for ~35–40% of building fires** in urban India, making electrical safety a primary design concern in PECET415. ## 1.4 The Concept of *Fire Load* (Foundation for Further Analysis) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Fire Load (Definition):** The total calorific energy of all combustible contents in a given floor area, expressed as: > $$q = \frac{\sum (m_i \times H_i)}{A}$$ > where $m_i$ is mass (kg), $H_i$ is calorific value (kJ/kg), and $A$ is floor area (m²). Units: **kJ/m²** (or MJ/m²). > > - Fire load governs **fire severity**, **duration**, and ultimately determines the **required fire resistance rating** of structural elements. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Mechanism of Fire Spread in Buildings Once ignition occurs, fire propagates through four fundamental physical mechanisms. **KTU 2024 examiners frequently test these with diagrams.** ### 2.1.1 Conduction - **Definition:** Transfer of heat through solid materials by molecular vibration, without visible movement of the medium. - **Engineering Insight:** A steel beam passing through a fire compartment will conduct heat to the unexposed side, igniting adjacent materials. - **Governing Law (Fourier's Law):** $$Q = \frac{kA(T_1 - T_2)}{L}$$ where $k$ is thermal conductivity (W/m·K), $A$ is cross-sectional area, $L$ is thickness, and $T_1, T_2$ are temperatures across the section. ### 2.1.2 Convection - **Definition:** Heat transfer by the bulk movement of fluid (hot smoke/gases rising upward). - **Engineering Insight:** Hot smoke rises through stairwells, lift shafts, and service ducts — the **chimney effect**. This is the dominant spread mode in high-rise fires. - **Stack Effect Velocity (Simplified):** $$v = \sqrt{\frac{2gH(T_i - T_o)}{T_o}}$$ where $H$ is shaft height, $T_i$ inside temp, $T_o$ outside temp (Kelvin), and $g = 9.81$ m/s². ### 2.1.3 Radiation - **Definition:** Heat transfer by electromagnetic waves, independent of any medium. - **Engineering Insight:** A blazing window radiates heat to the opposite building across a narrow street — **urban conflagration risk**. Compartment walls reduce radiation losses. - **Stefan-Boltzmann Law:** $$Q = \varepsilon \sigma A T^4$$ where $\varepsilon$ is emissivity, $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ W/m²·K⁴ is the Stefan-Boltzmann constant. ### 2.1.4 Direct Flame Contact & Re-ignition - Burning fragments (e.g., embers, melting plastics) fall or fly to adjacent areas, starting secondary fires. - Particularly severe in **multi-storey fires** where burning debris falls from upper windows. ### 2.1.5 Smoke Movement (Critical for Egress) - Smoke is the **primary cause of fire fatalities (>70%)** in building fires. - Spreads via doorways, staircases, lift shafts, HVAC ducts, pipe penetrations, and gaps around services. ## 2.2 Classification of Fire (IS 2190:1992 / NFPA 10 / NBC 2016) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Mandate:** Students must memorize the fire classification system along with the corresponding extinguishing medium. This is a **high-frequency Part A question**. | Class | Fuel Type | Common Materials | Extinguishing Medium | |-------|-----------|------------------|----------------------| | **A** | Ordinary combustibles | Wood, paper, cloth, rubber, plastics | **Water / water spray (cooling)** | | **B** | Flammable liquids | Petrol, diesel, oil, paints, solvents | **Foam / dry chemical powder (DCP)** | | **C** | Flammable gases | LPG, CNG, methane, propane, acetylene | **DCP / gas shut-off + CO₂** | | **D** | Combustible metals | Magnesium, sodium, potassium, aluminum powder | **Special dry powder (NaCl/graphite)** | | **E** (or K in NBC) | Electrical fires | Live wiring, switchgear, transformers | **CO₂ / clean agent (non-conductive)** | > [!WARNING] > **NEVER use water on Class B, C, D, or E fires.** Water on burning oil causes explosive boil-over; water on electrical fires causes electrocution; water on burning magnesium reacts violently producing hydrogen gas. ## 2.3 High-Temperature Effects on Building Materials ### 2.3.1 Structural Steel - Steel retains its structural integrity up to **~300 °C**. - At **~550 °C**, yield strength drops to **~60%** of room-temperature value. - At **~650 °C**, steel loses its design strength and begins to collapse. - **Critical temperature (θc) = 540 °C** for design per IS 800:2007 (ECCS). ### 2.3.2 Concrete - Concrete is **non-combustible** but undergoes physical-chemical changes. - Above **300 °C**: free water boils → explosive spalling. - At **~500–600 °C**: calcium hydroxide (Ca(OH)₂) dehydrates to CaO, causing shrinkage and strength loss. - At **~800 °C**: aggregate expansion + paste shrinkage → severe cracking, strength loss up to **~80%**. ### 2.3.3 Reinforcement Bars - Cold-worked bars lose strength faster than hot-rolled bars. - Bond between concrete and steel deteriorates above **~400 °C**. ### 2.3.4 Wood / Timber - Wood is combustible, but it **chars slowly** at ~0.6 mm/min. - The **char layer insulates** the inner core, so heavy timber sections retain structural integrity for 30–60 minutes. - Ignition temperature: **~250–300 °C**. ### 2.3.5 Masonry (Brick, Stone, Mortar) - Non-combustible but affected by differential expansion. - Cement mortar (Portlandite) calcines above **~500 °C** → crumbling. - Lime mortar performs better at high temperature. ### 2.3.6 Glass - Soda-lime glass softens at **~700 °C** and cracks due to thermal shock around **~250–300 °C** when water spray is applied. ### 2.3.7 Aluminum - Low melting point: **~660 °C**. Cannot be used in fire-rated assemblies. ### 2.3.8 Plaster (Gypsum) - Calcination of CaSO₄·2H₂O → CaSO₄ + H₂O at **~120–150 °C**. - Releases water vapor (acts as a fire retardant — used in **gypsum plaster fire-rated boards**). ## 2.4 Combustibility of Building Materials and Structures > [!IMPORTANT] > **Combustibility (Definition):** The property of a material to ignite, support combustion, and release heat when subjected to fire. Materials are classified per **IS 3808 / IS 875 Part 4** into: - **Non-combustible:** Concrete, brick, stone, steel (no flame, no sustained combustion). - **Limited-combustible:** Plaster, gypsum board, mineral wool, cement board. - **Combustible (Class I — Low flame spread):** Treated timber, fire-retardant plywood. - **Combustible (Class II — Moderate flame spread):** Ordinary wood, fiberboard. - **Combustible (Class III — High flame spread):** Plastics, thatch, foamed polymers. ### 2.4.1 Fire Resistance Rating (FRR) - **Definition:** The duration (in hours or minutes) for which a structural element (wall, slab, beam, column) retains stability, integrity, and insulation when subjected to standard fire exposure (per IS 3809 / ASTM E119). - **Typical requirements (NBC 2016):** Residential — 1 hr; High-rise — 2–4 hr; Hospitals — 2 hr; Storage warehouses — 4 hr. ## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Equation | Meaning / Use | Units | |---|----------|---------------|-------| | 1 | $q = \frac{\sum m_i H_i}{A}$ | Fire load density | kJ/m² | | 2 | $Q = \frac{kA\Delta T}{L}$ | Conduction heat transfer | W | | 3 | $Q = hA(T_s - T_\infty)$ | Convection heat transfer (Newton's Law) | W | | 4 | $Q = \varepsilon \sigma A T^4$ | Radiation heat transfer | W | | 5 | $v = \sqrt{2gH(T_i - T_o)/T_o}$ | Stack-effect smoke velocity | m/s | | 6 | $\text{FRR} = f(\theta_c, \text{section factor})$ | Fire resistance period | hr / min | | 7 | $\theta_c = 540\,°C$ | Critical temperature of steel | °C | | 8 | $T_{\text{fire}}(t) = 20 + 345 \log(8t+1)$ | Standard fire curve (ISO 834) | °C, t in min | > [!NOTE] > The **ISO 834 standard fire-temperature curve** gives the time-temperature profile used in furnace testing of structural elements. Memorize its form — it is a **guaranteed KTU Part B question** in some form. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Fire Load Calculation (Worked Numerical) > [!EXAMPLE] > **KTU-style numerical:** A commercial office floor has the following combustible inventory: > > - Wooden furniture: 800 kg, calorific value 17,000 kJ/kg > - Paper records: 200 kg, calorific value 16,000 kJ/kg > - Plastic (PVC) items: 100 kg, calorific value 25,000 kJ/kg > - Carpet: 50 kg, calorific value 19,000 kJ/kg > > The floor area is $A = 200\,\text{m}^2$. Calculate the **fire load density** and classify the occupancy. ### Solution (Step-by-step) **Step 1:** Calculate heat released by each combustible. $$ Q_1 = 800 \times 17{,}000 = 13{,}600{,}000\,\text{kJ} $$ $$ Q_2 = 200 \times 16{,}000 = 3{,}200{,}000\,\text{kJ} $$ $$ Q_3 = 100 \times 25{,}000 = 2{,}500{,}000\,\text{kJ} $$ $$ Q_4 = 50 \times 19{,}000 = 950{,}000\,\text{kJ} $$ **Step 2:** Sum total heat. $$ Q_{\text{total}} = 13{,}600{,}000 + 3{,}200{,}000 + 2{,}500{,}000 + 950{,}000 = 20{,}250{,}000\,\text{kJ} $$ **Step 3:** Compute fire load density. $$ q = \frac{Q_{\text{total}}}{A} = \frac{20{,}250{,}000}{200} = 101{,}250\,\text{kJ/m}^2 \approx 101.25\,\text{MJ/m}^2 $$ **Step 4:** Classify per IS 875 / NBC. - $q < 250\,\text{MJ/m}^2$ → **Low fire load** (Office/C1 category) - $250 \le q < 500$ → **Moderate** - $500 \le q < 1000$ → **High** - $q \ge 1000$ → **Very high (storage hazard)** $$ \boxed{q = 101.25\,\text{MJ/m}^2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Low Hazard Occupancy (Office)}} $$ ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — High-Temperature Effects Summary | Material | Ignition / Softening Temp | Critical Strength-Loss Temp | Behavior in Fire | Recommended Use | |----------|---------------------------|------------------------------|------------------|-----------------| | Structural Steel | Melts 1500 °C | 540 °C (yield loss 60%) | Buckles, sags rapidly | Need fire-proof cladding | | Concrete (siliceous agg.) | Non-combustible | 600–800 °C | Spalls, cracks | Good inherent FRR | | Reinforcement bar | ~600 °C (ductile failure) | 400 °C (bond loss) | Loses bond with concrete | Adequate cover required | | Wood (oak/teak) | 250–300 °C | Chars at 0.6 mm/min | Self-insulating char | Heavy sections = good FRR | | Brick (clay) | Non-combustible | 1000 °C+ (sintering) | Stable, slight expansion | Excellent FRR | | Gypsum plaster | 120 °C (calcination) | Releases H₂O | Retards fire | Fire-rated boards | | Glass (soda-lime) | Softens 700 °C | Shatters 250–300 °C (thermal shock) | Cracks easily | Use wired/ceramic glass | | Aluminum | 660 °C (melts) | 200 °C (softens) | Collapses | **Avoid in fire-rated zones** | | PVC plastic | 250 °C | 300 °C (releases HCl gas) | Burns, toxic smoke | Avoid in enclosed spaces | ## 3.3 Derivations — Standard Fire Curve (ISO 834) The standard fire-temperature curve is given by: $$ T(t) = T_0 + 345\,\log_{10}(8t + 1) $$ where $T_0 = 20\,°C$ is the ambient temperature, and $t$ is the time in minutes. **Derivation Logic:** Furnace tests require a uniform international standard to compare fire resistance across countries. ISO 834 produces a monotonically increasing temperature that: - Reaches **~556 °C at 5 min** - Reaches **~821 °C at 30 min** - Reaches **~945 °C at 60 min** - Reaches **~1049 °C at 120 min** - Reaches **~1100 °C at 240 min** **Step-by-step sample calculation at t = 30 min:** $$ T(30) = 20 + 345\,\log_{10}(8 \times 30 + 1) = 20 + 345\,\log_{10}(241) $$ $$ \log_{10}(241) = 2.382 $$ $$ T(30) = 20 + 345 \times 2.382 = 20 + 821.79 \approx 842\,°C $$ This value aligns with the **ASTM E119 / IS 3809** standard time-temperature curve used in India. ## 3.4 Time-to-Collapse of Steel Beam (Simplified) Given a simply supported steel beam in a standard fire, the time to reach the critical temperature $\theta_c = 540\,°C$ can be estimated using the lumped-mass approximation: $$ t = \frac{\rho c_p V (T_c - T_0)}{h_{\text{net}} A_s} $$ where: - $\rho$ = density of steel = 7850 kg/m³ - $c_p$ = specific heat ≈ 600 J/kg·K - $V$ = volume of steel (m³) - $h_{\text{net}}$ = net heat flux from fire (W/m²) - $A_s$ = exposed surface area (m²) This explains the rationale for **fire-resistant insulation** — by reducing $h_{\text{net}}$ to < 5 kW/m², collapse time can be extended from 15 min to 120+ min. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — The Fire Triangle & Tetrahedron ```mermaid flowchart TD A[FUEL
Combustible Material]:::fuel B[OXYGEN
21 percent in air]:::oxygen C[HEAT
Ignition Source]:::heat D[CHAIN REACTION
Free Radicals]:::chain E[FIRE
Self Sustaining Combustion]:::fire F[REMOVE FUEL
Starvation]:::action G[SMOTHER
Reduce O2 below 15 percent]:::action H[COOL
Remove heat below ignition]:::action I[INHIBIT
Break chain reaction]:::action A --> E B --> E C --> E D --> E A -. REMOVE FUEL .-> F B -. SMOTHER .-> G C -. COOL .-> H D -. INHIBIT .-> I classDef fuel fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef oxygen fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef heat fill:#ffe0b3,stroke:#cc6600,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef chain fill:#e0ccff,stroke:#4b0082,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef fire fill:#ff6600,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:3px,color:#fff classDef action fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#006633,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Mechanism of Fire Spread in a Building ```mermaid flowchart LR R1[Fire Source
Seat of Fire]:::seat R2[CONDUCTION
Through solid walls and steel members]:::mech R3[CONVECTION
Hot smoke rises in stairwell and ducts]:::mech R4[RADIATION
Heat to opposite wall across street]:::mech R5[DIRECT FLAME
Burning debris falls]:::mech R6[Adjoining Room]:::dest R7[Upper Floor]:::dest R8[Neighbouring Building]:::dest R9[Lower Floor / Vehicle]:::dest R1 --> R2 --> R6 R1 --> R3 --> R7 R1 --> R4 --> R8 R1 --> R5 --> R9 classDef seat fill:#ff4444,stroke:#660000,stroke-width:3px,color:#fff classDef mech fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#cc9900,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef dest fill:#ffd9b3,stroke:#993300,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Block-Level Functional Architecture of Fire Protection in a Building ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph PREVENTION[PREVENTION STAGE] P1[Material Selection
Low combustibility] P2[Source Control
No smoking, electrical safety] P3[Spatial Planning
Compartmentation] end subgraph DETECTION[DETECTION STAGE] D1[Smoke Detectors] D2[Heat Detectors] D3[Manual Call Points] end subgraph SUPPRESSION[SUPPRESSION STAGE] S1[Sprinkler System] S2[Fire Hydrants] S3[Extinguishers ABCDE type] end subgraph EGRESS[EGRESS STAGE] E1[Exit Signage] E2[Stairwell Pressurization] E3[Emergency Lighting] end subgraph STRUCTURAL[STRUCTURAL STAGE] ST1[Fire Rated Walls and Slabs] ST2[Protected Steel Members] ST3[Reinforced Concrete Cover] end P1 --> P3 P2 --> P3 P3 --> D1 D1 --> D2 D2 --> D3 D3 --> S1 S1 --> S2 S2 --> S3 S3 --> E1 E1 --> E2 E2 --> E3 E3 --> ST1 ST1 --> ST2 ST2 --> ST3 classDef pre fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:2px classDef det fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#cc9900,stroke-width:2px classDef sup fill:#ffd9b3,stroke:#993300,stroke-width:2px classDef egr fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#006633,stroke-width:2px classDef str fill:#e0ccff,stroke:#4b0082,stroke-width:2px ``` > [!NOTE] > **Read this diagram as a "layered defense" model:** A building's functional fire protection is the integrated output of five overlapping layers — prevention, detection, suppression, egress, and structural resistance. **Even if one layer fails, the next must contain the fire.** This concept (the "Swiss Cheese Model") is fundamental to KTU Module 3. ## 4.4 Schematic — ISO 834 Standard Fire Curve (Block Representation) ```mermaid flowchart LR T0[0 min, 20 deg C]:::pt T1[5 min, 556 deg C]:::pt T2[30 min, 842 deg C]:::pt T3[60 min, 945 deg C]:::pt T4[120 min, 1049 deg C]:::pt T5[240 min, 1100 deg C]:::pt T0 --> T1 --> T2 --> T3 --> T4 --> T5 T2 -. Asymptotic trend .-> T5 classDef pt fill:#ff9966,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]** **Define the Fire Triangle. List the four classes of fire as per IS 2190 along with one example of fuel and the appropriate extinguishing medium for each.** > **Model Answer (3 marks — 1 for definition, 2 for classification table):** > > **Fire Triangle:** Fire is a chemical chain reaction of combustion that requires three simultaneous elements — **Fuel, Oxygen, and Heat**. Removing any one element extinguishes the fire. This is called the Fire Triangle. > > **Classification of Fire (IS 2190:1992):** > > | Class | Fuel Type | Example | Extinguishing Medium | > |-------|-----------|---------|----------------------| > | **A** | Ordinary solids | Wood, paper, cloth | **Water** (cools + penetrates) | > | **B** | Flammable liquids | Petrol, diesel, oil | **Foam / Dry Chemical Powder (DCP)** | > | **C** | Flammable gases | LPG, CNG, methane | **DCP + shut off gas supply** | > | **D** | Combustible metals | Magnesium, sodium, potassium | **Special dry powder (NaCl/ graphite based)** | > | **E** | Electrical (live) | Switchgear, wiring | **CO₂ / clean agent (non-conductive)** | ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Expected Pattern]** **Explain the four mechanisms of fire spread in buildings with one real-world example each.** > **Model Answer (3 marks — 0.75 each):** > > 1. **Conduction** — Heat transfer through solid materials without visible motion. *Example:* A steel beam passing through a fire wall conducts heat to the unburnt side, igniting adjacent timber. > 2. **Convection** — Heat transfer by hot gases rising vertically. *Example:* Smoke rising through a stairwell in a high-rise fire — the *chimney effect* carries fire upward. > 3. **Radiation** — Heat transfer by electromagnetic waves through air/space. *Example:* A burning window in a building radiates heat across a narrow lane to the opposite building, igniting its facade. > 4. **Direct flame contact / Re-ignition** — Burning fragments falling or flying to other areas. *Example:* Burning debris falling from an upper-floor window to a parked car below. ## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) > **KTU 2024 Scheme Regulation:** Each Part B question has a **Choice (A or B)** and two sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks. **Both choices are answered by different students based on QP code.** --- ### **Part B — Question A (14 Marks)** #### **Q.A (a) [7 Marks] — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]** **Classify the causes of fire in buildings. Discuss in detail the fire-spread mechanism, highlighting the role of smoke movement in high-rise buildings.** > **Model Answer:** > > **Classification of Causes of Fire (3 marks):** > > **(i) Electrical causes** — Short circuits, overloading, loose connections (most common, ~35–40% per NBC). > **(ii) Thermal/Mechanical causes** — Overheating of machines, friction sparks, hot surfaces, welding operations. > **(iii) Chemical causes** — Spontaneous combustion (oily rags, phosphorous), exothermic reactions. > **(iv) Carelessness / Human factors** — Discarded cigarettes, unattended cooking, children playing with fire. > **(v) Natural causes** — Lightning, forest/brush fires approaching urban areas. > **(vi) Intentional (Arson)** — Deliberate ignition for insurance fraud, terrorism, revenge. > > **Fire Spread Mechanism (3 marks):** > > Fire spreads from the *seat of fire* to surrounding areas by four modes: > > - **Conduction** — through structural members (governed by Fourier's law: $Q = kA\Delta T / L$). > - **Convection** — via hot smoke and gases rising in shafts and ducts. > - **Radiation** — across open spaces and through glazed openings. > - **Direct flame contact** — by burning brands/debris. > > **Role of Smoke Movement in High-Rise (1 mark):** > > In high-rise buildings, smoke moves primarily by the **stack (chimney) effect** caused by temperature differential between inside and outside. The stack velocity is given by: > > $$v = \sqrt{\frac{2gH(T_i - T_o)}{T_o}}$$ > > Smoke spreads to upper floors through stairwells, lift shafts, HVAC ducts, and pipe penetrations. It is responsible for **over 70% of fire fatalities**. Mitigation includes **pressurized stairwells, smoke shafts, fire-rated doors with self-closers, and compartmentalization**. #### **Q.A (b) [7 Marks]** **A warehouse floor of area 250 m² has the following combustibles:** > - Timber pallets: 1,200 kg (H = 17,000 kJ/kg) > - Cardboard boxes: 400 kg (H = 16,000 kJ/kg) > - Polyethylene drums: 150 kg (H = 46,000 kJ/kg) > - Cotton rags: 100 kg (H = 18,000 kJ/kg) > > **Calculate the fire load density and classify the occupancy hazard. Suggest the minimum fire resistance rating for the structural elements.** > **Model Answer:** > > **Step 1: Heat from each combustible (2 marks)** > > $$Q_1 = 1{,}200 \times 17{,}000 = 20{,}400{,}000\,\text{kJ}$$ > $$Q_2 = 400 \times 16{,}000 = 6{,}400{,}000\,\text{kJ}$$ > $$Q_3 = 150 \times 46{,}000 = 6{,}900{,}000\,\text{kJ}$$ > $$Q_4 = 100 \times 18{,}000 = 1{,}800{,}000\,\text{kJ}$$ > > **Step 2: Total heat (1 mark)** > > $$Q_{\text{total}} = 20{,}400{,}000 + 6{,}400{,}000 + 6{,}900{,}000 + 1{,}800{,}000 = 35{,}500{,}000\,\text{kJ}$$ > > **Step 3: Fire load density (2 marks)** > > $$q = \frac{35{,}500{,}000}{250} = 142{,}000\,\text{kJ/m}^2 = 142\,\text{MJ/m}^2$$ > > **Step 4: Classification & Recommendation (2 marks)** > > - $q = 142\,\text{MJ/m}^2$ falls in the range **0–250 MJ/m²** → **Low Fire Load Occupancy** (general storage). > - However, the presence of polyethylene (high H = 46,000 kJ/kg) elevates the actual severity. > - **Recommended FRR for structural elements:** **2 hours** for columns, beams, and load-bearing walls; **1 hour** for non-load-bearing partitions, as per NBC 2016 for low-to-moderate hazard warehouses. > - **Additional provision:** Automatic sprinkler system is mandatory; provide fire-rated doors and 4-hour rated enclosure for the polyethylene drums. --- ### **Part B — Question B (Alternative Choice — 14 Marks)** #### **Q.B (a) [7 Marks]** **Explain the high-temperature effects on the following building materials: (i) Structural steel, (ii) Concrete, (iii) Wood, (iv) Glass, (v) Plaster.** > **Model Answer (Distribute 1.4 marks per material):** > > **(i) Structural Steel:** Retains full strength up to ~300 °C; yield strength drops to 60% at 540 °C (critical temperature per IS 800). Buckling and sagging collapse occurs at 650–700 °C. Steel is **non-combustible but loses strength rapidly** — needs fire-protective cladding (vermiculite, intumescent paint, gypsum board encasement). > > **(ii) Concrete:** Non-combustible. Free water boils above 300 °C → **explosive spalling**. Calcium hydroxide dehydrates at 500–600 °C. At 800 °C, aggregate expansion + paste shrinkage causes severe cracking, strength loss up to 80%. Reinforcement bond fails above 400 °C. > > **(iii) Wood:** Combustible; ignites at 250–300 °C. Chars at ~0.6 mm/min; the **char layer acts as insulation** preserving the inner core. Heavy timber sections can provide 30–60 min fire resistance without any treatment — a phenomenon called *self-protective charring*. > > **(iv) Glass:** Softens at 700 °C. Ordinary soda-lime glass cracks at 250–300 °C due to thermal shock (especially when cold water hits hot glass). For fire-rated assemblies, use **wired glass, tempered glass, or ceramic glass**. > > **(v) Plaster (Gypsum):** Calcines at 120–150 °C releasing water vapor (CaSO₄·2H₂O → CaSO₄ + 2H₂O). This endothermic reaction absorbs heat and delays fire spread. **Gypsum boards are the most common fire-rated partition material.** #### **Q.B (b) [7 Marks]** **Differentiate between *combustible* and *non-combustible* building materials with examples. Discuss the concept of Fire Resistance Rating (FRR) as per IS 3809 and its significance in building design.** > **Model Answer:** > > **Difference Table (3 marks):** > > | Property | Combustible Materials | Non-Combustible Materials | > |----------|----------------------|--------------------------| > | Burning behavior | Ignite, support, propagate flame | Do not ignite or support combustion | > | Heat release | Release significant calorific energy | Negligible heat release | > | Examples | Wood, plastics, thatch, rubber, bitumen, paints | Concrete, brick, stone, steel, plaster, glass | > | Smoke/toxicity | May release toxic gases (e.g., HCl from PVC) | No toxic emissions | > | Fire protection role | Need FR treatment or substitution | Form the basic fire-resistant envelope | > > **Fire Resistance Rating (FRR) (3 marks):** > > **Definition:** FRR is the duration (in hours or minutes) for which a building element retains its three fire-safety functions: > > 1. **Stability (R)** — ability to carry load without collapse. > 2. **Integrity (E)** — ability to prevent passage of flames and hot gases. > 3. **Insulation (I)** — ability to limit temperature rise on the unexposed face to < 140 °C average / 180 °C at any point. > > **Testing Standard:** IS 3809 / ASTM E119 — element exposed to **ISO 834 standard fire-temperature curve** $T(t) = 20 + 345\log(8t+1)$ °C. > > **Typical FRR values per NBC 2016:** > - Residential buildings: 1–2 hours > - High-rise (> 15 m): 2–4 hours > - Hospitals, schools: 2 hours > - Storage/industrial: 3–4 hours > > **Significance (1 mark):** FRR guides *material selection, structural detailing, and compartmentation*. A higher FRR allows more evacuation time and reduces collapse risk, directly saving lives and property. For example, a **2-hour FRR wall** is required between two adjacent offices to prevent lateral fire spread. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks (PECET415 Module 3):** > > 1. **Confusing fire classes and extinguishers** — Writing "use water on Class B fire" is a guaranteed 1-mark deduction. > 2. **Skipping units** in fire load numericals — Always write kJ/m² or MJ/m² explicitly. KTU examiners deduct 0.5 marks for missing units. > 3. **Forgetting the ISO 834 equation** — Memorize $T(t) = 20 + 345\log(8t+1)$. It appears in nearly every Part B. > 4. **Conflating combustible and non-combustible** — Steel is **non-combustible** but **loses strength in fire**. Many students wrongly classify it as combustible. > 5. **Ignoring smoke as primary cause of death** — Always mention **>70% fatalities are smoke-related**, not burns. > 6. **No diagrams in 14-mark questions** — KTU examiners award 1–2 marks for neat, labeled flowcharts/sketches (fire spread, fire load, fire classification). **Always include at least one diagram.** > 7. **Spelling "fire" as "fier" or "fire load" as "fireload"** — A minor issue, but professional KTU answer scripts use correct spacing and spelling. ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > **Module 3 — Functional Fire Protection: One-Page Recap** - ✅ **Fire Triangle** = Fuel + Oxygen + Heat. **Fire Tetrahedron** adds Chain Reaction. - ✅ **Major causes** — Electrical (~35–40%), human carelessness, heating, chemical, arson. - ✅ **Four spread modes** — Conduction, Convection, **Radiation**, Direct flame contact. - ✅ **Smoke** is the #1 killer in fires (>70% fatalities). - ✅ **Fire classes (A, B, C, D, E)** → memorize with extinguishers. - ✅ **NEVER use water on Class B, C, D, E fires.** - ✅ **Steel critical temperature** = 540 °C; loses 60% strength. - ✅ **Concrete spalls** above 300 °C; severe damage > 600 °C. - ✅ **Wood chars** at 0.6 mm/min; **char insulates** the core. - ✅ **Gypsum** releases H₂O at 120–150 °C — best fire-rated board. - ✅ **Aluminum melts** at 660 °C — avoid in fire-rated zones. - ✅ **Fire Load** $q = \sum(m_i H_i)/A$ in kJ/m². - ✅ **Standard fire curve:** $T(t) = 20 + 345\log(8t+1)$ °C. - ✅ **FRR = (R) Stability + (E) Integrity + (I) Insulation** per IS 3809. - ✅ **NBC 2016** mandates 1–4 hr FRR based on occupancy height/use. - ✅ **High-rise chimney effect** velocity: $v = \sqrt{2gH(T_i - T_o)/T_o}$. - ✅ **Layered defense** (Swiss cheese model) = Prevention + Detection + Suppression + Egress + Structure. > [!TIP] > **Last-minute mnemonic for Fire Classes — "ABCDE" of Fuels:** > - **A** = Ash (ordinary solids → water/ash residue) > - **B** = Barrels & Bottles (liquids) > - **C** = Cylinders (gases) > - **D** = *Dangerous* metals > - **E** = *Electricity* (current) > > Memorize this — KTU exam setters love the "What extinguisher is best for a petrol fire?" trap question.

topics.title: Fire Alarm System and Means of Escape, Firefighting Installations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET415 - ARCHITECTURAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Fire Alarm System and Means of Escape, Firefighting Installations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Fire Alarm System — Formal Definition

A **Fire Alarm System** is an integrated network of detection, control, notification, and power-supply components designed to identify the unwanted presence of fire at the earliest possible stage, alert the building occupants, summon the fire brigade, and initiate ancillary fire-safety actions (such as smoke control, elevator recall, and door release) in accordance with **IS 2189 : 2008** (Selection, Installation and Maintenance of Automatic Fire Detection and Alarm Systems — Code of Practice) and **NBC 2016, Part 4, Section 4 (Fire and Life Safety)**.

The four functional pillars of any KTU-compliant fire alarm system are:

1. **Detection** — sensing the by-products of combustion (smoke, heat, flame, gas).
2. **Control & Indication (CIE)** — the Fire Alarm Control Panel (FACP) that receives, processes, displays, and logs the signal.
3. **Notification** — Audio-Visual (AV) devices such as hooters, sirens, speakers, and flashing strobes.
4. **Power Supply** — a primary 230 V AC mains supply backed by a secondary battery bank (sealed lead-acid / Li-ion) capable of supporting the entire system for **at least 24 hours in quiescent mode + 15 minutes in full alarm mode**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Students are expected to clearly distinguish between **Conventional (Zone-based)** and **Addressable (Point-Identifiable)** Fire Alarm Systems, and specify the correct system for residential, commercial, high-rise, and industrial occupancies.

---

## 1.2 Means of Escape — Formal Definition

**Means of Escape** in architectural engineering refers to the continuous, unobstructed, and protected path of travel from **any point in a building to a place of ultimate safety** (open air at ground level remote from the building), as laid down in **NBC 2016, Part 4, Clause 4.4.2** and **IS 14435 : 1997**. It is engineered through the proper sizing, number, and placement of:

- **Exits** (doorways, corridors, lobbies, ramps, stairways)
- **Travel Distance** (the actual walking path length a person must cover)
- **Dead-End Corridors** (corridors with egress possible in only one direction)
- **Capacity of Exits** (computed from occupant load)
- **Pressurization** (positive-pressure stairwells in high-rise buildings)

---

## 1.3 Firefighting Installations — Formal Definition

**Firefighting Installations** comprise the active and passive suppression infrastructure installed within a building to extinguish, control, or contain a fire at its incipient stage and to support the operations of the Fire Service. These are governed by **NBC 2016, Part 4, Section 4.5** and the **TAC (Tariff Advisory Committee) guidelines** for insurance-rated buildings.

The standard hierarchy of installations includes:

| Tier | Installation | Primary Role |
|------|--------------|--------------|
| Tier-1 | Portable Fire Extinguishers | First-aid firefighting |
| Tier-2 | Hose Reels | Trained occupant-level attack |
| Tier-3 | Hydrants & Wet Riser/Down-comer | Fire Service operations |
| Tier-4 | Automatic Sprinklers | Total-flooding suppression |
| Tier-5 | Gas-based Suppression (FM-200 / Novec) | Sensitive equipment rooms |

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition — Occupant Load (OL):** The maximum number of persons reasonably expected to occupy a floor or a building at any one time, calculated from the floor area and the designated use (NBC 2016, Table 4.4.2.3).

---

## 1.4 Intuitive Analogy — The "Three Lifelines of a Building"

Imagine a building as a human body:

- **The Fire Alarm System** is the **nervous system**. The moment a hand touches a flame (a smoke detector senses particles), the nerves fire (signal travels through the loop), the brain registers pain (FACP displays the zone), and the mouth cries out (hooters ring). Without it, the body is unaware and unconscious to danger.
- **The Means of Escape** is the **circulatory system (arteries & veins)** — the corridors, staircases, and exits that carry the "lifeblood" of occupants safely from the heart of the building to the outside world.
- **The Firefighting Installations** are the **immune system** — portable extinguishers are the white-blood cells at the wound, the sprinkler system is the skin's cooling sweat, and the hydrants are the emergency antibodies waiting in reserve.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A building can have all three systems perfectly designed, yet **fail** if they are not **integrated** — a fire alarm must trigger stairwell pressurization, release magnetic fire doors, recall elevators, and start the fire pump simultaneously. **Integration = Survival**.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Fire Alarm Systems

### A. Based on Detection Technology
- **Ionization Smoke Detector (ISD):** Best for fast-flaming, invisible-smoke fires. Uses Americium-241. (Being phased out in EU but still referenced in IS 2189.)
- **Photoelectric (Optical) Smoke Detector:** Best for smouldering fires producing large visible particles. Uses a light-scattering chamber.
- **Heat Detector — Fixed Temperature (FT):** Triggers at **57 °C** (Class A1) or **90 °C** (Class B) per **IS 2175**.
- **Heat Detector — Rate-of-Rise (ROR):** Triggers when temperature rise exceeds **8.3 °C/min** (Class CR).
- **Flame Detector (IR / UV):** Used in petrochemical, transformer, and paint-storage areas.
- **Aspirating Smoke Detection (ASD / VESDA):** Continuously draws air through pipes; sensitivity 1000× greater than point detectors. Used in data centres and museums.
- **Linear Heat Detection Cable (LHDC):** Used in cable trays, tunnels, and parking decks.
- **Gas / Multi-criteria Detector:** Detects CO, CO₂, or combines smoke + heat + CO algorithms (often used in hotel rooms per **NFPA 72 / IS 2189** amendments).

### B. Based on System Architecture
- **Conventional (Zone-based) System:** Up to **20–25 detectors per zone**; the FACP shows only the *zone* in alarm, not the exact device. Used in small to mid-size buildings (≤ 4 storeys, ≤ 500 m²/zone).
- **Addressable (Analogue-Addressable) System:** Each device has a unique **loop address (1–127 or 1–159 per loop)**; FACP shows the exact device, its type, and real-time analogue value (0–100 % obscuration for smoke). Used in high-rise, hospitals, hotels, malls, and IT parks.
- **Hybrid System:** Combines conventional zones and addressable loops on a single FACP.

---

## 2.2 Means of Escape — Design Logic

The design of means of escape follows a **four-step logical chain** (NBC 2016 / IS 14435):

1. **Determine Occupant Load (OL).**
2. **Provide sufficient number, capacity, and width of Exits.**
3. **Ensure Travel Distance (actual + dead-end) is within permissible limits.**
4. **Protect the path of escape** with fire-rated enclosures, signage, pressurization, and emergency lighting.

### Occupant Load Calculation
The OL is computed from the *occupant density* (persons per m²) given in NBC 2016, Table 4.4.2.3, which depends on occupancy classification.

### Capacity of an Exit
The "unit of exit width" is taken as **500 mm** (NBC 2016, Clause 4.4.2.7.1).
- A standard **single-leaf door** of 1000 mm clear width = **2 exit units**.
- A standard **stairway landing width** for high-rise = **1500 mm minimum** clear of handrails.

### Travel Distance
The maximum permitted travel distance is a function of occupancy and whether the building is **sprinklered** (sprinklered travel distance may be increased by **30–50 %** over non-sprinklered, per NBC 2016, Table 4.4.2.6).

---

## 2.3 Firefighting Installations — Sizing Logic

- **Portable Extinguisher:** Sized by **IS 15683 / IS 2190**. Travel distance to any extinguisher shall **not exceed 15 m** for Class A and **10 m** for Class B risks.
- **Hose Reel:** One per **1000 m²** floor area or per **1000 m² of wet-riser coverage**; hose length = **30 m**; bore = **20 mm** (IS 884 : 1985).
- **Wet Riser:** Mandatory for buildings **> 15 m and ≤ 45 m tall** (NBC 2016, 4.5.4.1). Hydrant outlet at every floor landing.
- **Down-comer:** For buildings **≤ 15 m tall**, a 100 mm GI down-comer with single 63 mm outlet per floor is accepted.
- **Sprinkler:** Coverage per sprinkler head = **9–12 m²** (light hazard) and **6–9 m²** (ordinary hazard); spacing ≤ **4 m** (IS 15105).
- **Fire Pump Capacity:** Sized by hydraulic calculation, typically **2850 LPM (750 GPM) at 3.5–7.0 kg/cm² (50–100 psi)** for residential and **4500 LPM** for commercial high-rises.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Unit | Code Reference |
|---|---------------------|------------|------|----------------|
| 1 | **Occupant Load** | $OL = \dfrac{A_{\text{net}}}{F}$ where $A_{\text{net}}$ = net floor area (m²), $F$ = occupant load factor (m²/person) | persons | NBC 2016, Cl. 4.4.2.3 |
| 2 | **Unit Exit Width** | $UEW = 500 \text{ mm}$ | mm | NBC 2016, Cl. 4.4.2.7.1 |
| 3 | **Total Exit Width Required** | $W_{\text{req}} = OL \times W_p$ where $W_p$ = width per person (depends on stairs / level / ramp) | mm | NBC 2016 |
| 4 | **Number of Exits** | Min. **2** for $OL \le 500$; **3** for $501 \le OL \le 1000$; **4** for $OL > 1000$ | — | NBC 2016, Cl. 4.4.2.1 |
| 5 | **Travel Distance (Non-sprinklered)** | Office = **30 m**, Mercantile = **30 m**, Hospital = **22.5 m**, Educational = **22.5 m** (dead-end half of these) | m | NBC 2016, Table 4.4.2.6 |
| 6 | **Travel Distance (Sprinklered)** | $TD_{\text{sprink}} = 1.3 \times TD_{\text{non-sprink}}$ (typical 30 % bonus) | m | NBC 2016 |
| 7 | **Staircase Width (Residential)** | $W_{st} = OL_f \times 12.5 \text{ mm}$ (min **1250 mm**) | mm | IS 14435 |
| 8 | **Staircase Width (Non-Residential)** | $W_{st} = OL_f \times 20 \text{ mm}$ (min **1500 mm**) | mm | IS 14435 |
| 9 | **Hose-Reel Coverage** | $A_{hr} = \pi \times L^2 = \pi \times 30^2 \approx 2827 \text{ m}^2$ (ideal); 1 per **1000 m²** | m² | IS 884 |
| 10 | **Hydrant Pressure (Wet Riser)** | $P_h = 3.5 \text{ to } 7.0 \text{ kg/cm}^2$ at the most remote outlet | kg/cm² | IS 3844, TAC |
| 11 | **Sprinkler Spacing** | $S_{\text{light}} = 12 \text{ m}^2 / head$, $S_{\text{ord}} = 9 \text{ m}^2 / head$ | m² | IS 15105, NFPA 13 |
| 12 | **Battery Backup (FACP)** | $C_{bat} = (I_q \times 24) + (I_a \times 0.25)$ (Ah) | Ah | IS 2189, Cl. 6.4 |
| 13 | **Detector Area Coverage (Optical Smoke)** | $A_d = 80 \text{ m}^2$ (smooth ceiling, height $\le 6 \text{ m}$) | m² | IS 2189, Table 1 |
| 14 | **Detector Spacing** | $D_s = 1.4 \times \sqrt{A_d}$ (so $\approx 12.5 \text{ m}$ for 80 m²) | m | IS 2189 |
| 15 | **Door Swing Pressure Relief** | Stairwell pressurization $\Delta p = 12.5 \text{ Pa (0.05" w.c.)}$ to **50 Pa (0.2" w.c.)** | Pa | NBC 2016, IS 14604 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In all the formulas above, **OL (Occupant Load)** is the master variable. The moment OL is wrong, every downstream calculation — exit width, staircase width, travel time, fire pump capacity, even the number of detectors — becomes unreliable. KTU examiners frequently test this cascading impact.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Fire Alarm Systems** are now mandatory (per KMBR/Kerala Municipal Building Rules 2019, Rule 58) for all buildings **> 15 m height** and for institutional, assembly, and mercantile occupancies irrespective of height.
- **Means of Escape** design is a *pre-construction* statutory requirement — any deviation leads to rejection of the building permit by the Town Planning / Local Body.
- **Firefighting Installations** are dual-purpose: **life safety** (rescue) and **property protection** (insurance rating). A well-sprinklered building typically receives **30–60 % concession on fire-insurance premium** from the New India Assurance / Oriental Insurance.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Numerical Demonstrations

## 3.1 Derivation — Occupant Load (OL) to Total Exit Width

The relationship between occupant load, exit width, and number of exits is derived as follows:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1:} \quad OL &= \frac{A_{\text{net}}}{F} \\
\text{Step 2:} \quad W_p &= \text{width per person} = 12.5 \text{ mm (residential)} \;\vert\; 20 \text{ mm (non-residential)} \\
\text{Step 3:} \quad W_{\text{req}} &= OL \times W_p \\
\text{Step 4:} \quad N_{\text{exits}} &= \left\lceil \frac{W_{\text{req}}}{UEW \times n} \right\rceil \quad \text{where } n = \text{exit units per door} \\
\text{Step 5:} \quad W_{\text{provided}} &= N_{\text{exits}} \times UEW \times n \\
\text{Step 6:} \quad \text{Compliance check:} \quad W_{\text{provided}} &\ge W_{\text{req}}
\end{aligned}
$$

The width per person $W_p$ is itself derived from the *flow capacity* of a stair (Fruin's Level-of-Service for "E" or "F") which is approximately **33 persons/min/m of width** for stairs, yielding roughly **18 mm/person** (or 12.5 mm for residential with relaxed density).

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 1 — Occupant Load for an Office Floor

> **Problem:** An office floor in a high-rise commercial building has a **net usable area of 1200 m²**. As per **NBC 2016, Table 4.4.2.3**, the occupant load factor for Business Occupancy is **$F = 6 \text{ m}^2/\text{person}$**. Calculate the occupant load, total required exit width, and number of exits.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Net Area, } A_{\text{net}} &= 1200 \text{ m}^2 \\
\text{Occupant Load Factor, } F &= 6 \text{ m}^2/\text{person} \\
OL &= \frac{A_{\text{net}}}{F} = \frac{1200}{6} = 200 \text{ persons} \\[4pt]
W_p &= 20 \text{ mm/person} \quad (\text{non-residential, NBC 2016}) \\
W_{\text{req}} &= OL \times W_p = 200 \times 20 = 4000 \text{ mm} \\[4pt]
UEW &= 500 \text{ mm} \\
N_{\text{exits}} &= \left\lceil \frac{W_{\text{req}}}{UEW \times 2} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{4000}{1000} \right\rceil = 4 \text{ exits (each 1000 mm single-leaf)} \\[4pt]
W_{\text{provided}} &= 4 \times 1000 = 4000 \text{ mm} \\
\text{Check:} \quad W_{\text{provided}} &\ge W_{\text{req}} \;\; \Rightarrow \;\; 4000 \ge 4000 \;\; \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** The floor needs **4 staircases**, each with a **minimum 1000 mm clear landing width**, OR **2 staircases with 2 doors of 1000 mm each** at the head of the stairs.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Fire Alarm Battery Backup Sizing

> **Problem:** A Fire Alarm Control Panel (FACP) has the following connected load:
> - 60 smoke detectors @ 90 µA each (quiescent)
> - 1 addressable FACP @ 80 mA quiescent / 200 mA alarm
> - 20 hooters @ 30 mA each (alarm only)
> - 10 strobes @ 100 mA each (alarm only)
> - 8 MCPs (Manual Call Points) @ 200 µA each (quiescent)
>
> Compute the standby battery capacity in **Ah**, as per **IS 2189 : 2008, Cl. 6.4** ($I_q \times 24 + I_a \times 0.25$).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: Quiescent Current, } I_q &= (60 \times 90\mu A) + 80 \text{ mA} + (8 \times 200\mu A) \\
&= 5.4 \text{ mA} + 80 \text{ mA} + 1.6 \text{ mA} \\
&= 87 \text{ mA} = 0.087 \text{ A} \\[4pt]
\text{Step 2: Alarm Current, } I_a &= 87 \text{ mA (already loaded FACP)} \\
&\quad + (20 \times 30 \text{ mA}) + (10 \times 100 \text{ mA}) \\
&= 87 + 600 + 1000 \\
&= 1687 \text{ mA} = 1.687 \text{ A} \\[4pt]
\text{Step 3: Battery Capacity, } C_{bat} &= (I_q \times 24) + (I_a \times 0.25) \\
&= (0.087 \times 24) + (1.687 \times 0.25) \\
&= 2.088 + 0.422 \\
&= 2.51 \text{ Ah} \\[4pt]
\text{Step 4: Apply Derating Factor (1.25)} \quad C_{\text{design}} &= 2.51 \times 1.25 = 3.14 \text{ Ah} \\[4pt]
\text{Step 5: Round up to standard size} \quad C_{\text{standard}} &= 4 \text{ Ah, 12 V sealed lead-acid}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** A **12 V, 4 Ah** sealed maintenance-free lead-acid battery is sufficient. For redundancy and longer life, a **12 V, 7 Ah** is commonly preferred.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 3 — Sprinkler Spacing for Light Hazard Occupancy

> **Problem:** A library floor of **800 m²** needs light-hazard sprinkler protection. As per **NFPA 13 / IS 15105**, the maximum coverage per sprinkler is **12 m²** in light hazard, and the maximum spacing is **4.0 m on centre**. Compute the number of sprinklers required.

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{sprinklers, max coverage}} &= \left\lceil \frac{A}{A_{head}} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{800}{12} \right\rceil = 67 \text{ heads} \\[4pt]
N_{\text{sprinklers, by spacing}} &= \left\lceil \frac{\sqrt{A}}{S} \right\rceil^2 = \left\lceil \frac{\sqrt{800}}{4} \right\rceil^2 = \left\lceil \frac{28.28}{4} \right\rceil^2 = \lceil 7.07 \rceil^2 = 8^2 = 64 \text{ heads} \\[4pt]
N_{\text{design}} &= \max(67, 64) = 67 \text{ heads (governed by coverage rule)} \\[4pt]
\text{Hydraulic demand per head, } q &= K\sqrt{P}, \quad K = 80 \text{ (metric)}, \; P = 0.5 \text{ bar (min)} \\
q &= 80 \times \sqrt{0.5} = 80 \times 0.707 = 56.6 \text{ LPM/head} \\[4pt]
\text{Total design flow, } Q_{\text{design}} &= 67 \times 56.6 = 3792 \text{ LPM} \quad \text{(incl. 30\% hose-stream demand)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** A fire pump rated at **2850–4500 LPM at 7–10 bar** is to be specified. A 67-head design plus hose-stream and hydrant flow governs the pump capacity.

---

## 3.5 Worked Example 4 — Travel Distance Verification

> **Problem:** A ground-floor open-plan office has dimensions **30 m × 20 m** (600 m²). The only exit is at the far end. Verify if the travel distance is compliant as per **NBC 2016, Cl. 4.4.2.6** (non-sprinklered office = max **30 m**).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Diagonal of floor, } D &= \sqrt{30^2 + 20^2} = \sqrt{900 + 400} = \sqrt{1300} = 36.06 \text{ m} \\[4pt]
\text{Furthest occupant location, } d_{\max} &\approx 36 \text{ m (corner opposite to exit)} \\[4pt]
\text{Max permitted, non-sprinklered office, } TD_{\max} &= 30 \text{ m} \\[4pt]
\text{Decision: } d_{\max} \; (36) &> TD_{\max} \; (30) \;\; \Rightarrow \;\; \textbf{NON-COMPLIANT} \\[4pt]
\text{Remedial Option 1 (Sprinklers): } TD_{\max,sprink} &= 1.3 \times 30 = 39 \text{ m} \\
\text{Decision: } 36 \text{ m} &\le 39 \text{ m} \;\; \Rightarrow \;\; \textbf{COMPLIANT (if sprinklers installed)} \\[4pt]
\text{Remedial Option 2: Add a 2nd exit} \quad N_{\text{exits}} &= 2 \text{ on opposite faces}, \\
\text{New } d_{\max} &= 15 \text{ m} \;\; \Rightarrow \;\; \textbf{COMPLIANT}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** A single exit is non-compliant. The architect must either provide a second remote exit on the opposite face (preferred) OR provide full sprinkler coverage to avail the 30 % travel-distance bonus.

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Fire Alarm System Architecture (Addressable Loop)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Smoke Detector 1] --> L1
    A2[Smoke Detector 2] --> L1
    A3[Heat Detector 1] --> L1
    A4[MCP 1] --> L1
    A5[MCP 2] --> L1
    A6[I/O Module 1] --> L1
    A7[Strobe 1] --> L1
    L1[Addressable Loop L1 Class A] --> FACP[Fire Alarm Control Panel FACP]
    L2[Addressable Loop L2 Class A] --> FACP
    A8[Beam Detector] --> L2
    A9[Aspirating VESDA] --> L2
    A10[Linear Heat Cable] --> L2
    A11[Flame Detector IR] --> L2
    FACP --> REP[Repeater Panel Security Room]
    FACP --> BMS[BMS Integration Modbus BACnet]

    FACP --> PUMP[Fire Pump Starter Auto]

    FACP --> ELEV[Elevator Recall]

```

## 4.4 Integrated Fire-Lifecycle Sequence (Mermaid State Diagram)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Normal
    Normal --> Detection: Smoke OR Heat OR MCP
    Detection --> AlarmVerification: 10 sec pre-alarm
    AlarmVerification --> GeneralAlarm: Confirmed
    GeneralAlarm --> Notification: Hooters + Strobes
    GeneralAlarm --> AhuOff: AHU shutdown relay
    GeneralAlarm --> ElevatorRecall: Elevator to ground
    GeneralAlarm --> DoorRelease: Magnetic doors release
    GeneralAlarm --> Pressurize: Stairwell fans on
    GeneralAlarm --> PumpStart: Jockey + Main pump
    GeneralAlarm --> BrigadeNotify: Auto Dialer 101
    PumpStart --> Suppression: Sprinklers discharge
    Suppression --> Evacuation: 2.5 min total
    Evacuation --> Assembly: At refuge area
    Assembly --> [*]: All clear
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** Arrows denote signal flow (electrical, mechanical, or occupant flow). The colour-coding follows Indian KTU traffic convention — *green* for safe, *yellow* for transition, *red* for danger, *blue* for water, *purple* for pressurised air.
