# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define the following terms with appropriate units:**
(a) Thermal Transmittance (U-value)  
(b) Solar Heat Gain Coefficient (SHGC)  
(c) Energy Performance Index (EPI)

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Thermal Transmittance (U-value):** The rate of heat flow in watts through one square metre of a building element when the air temperature difference between the two sides is one Kelvin. **Unit: W/m²·K.** A lower U-value indicates better insulation.

**(b) Solar Heat Gain Coefficient (SHGC):** The fraction of incident solar radiation (shortwave) that is transmitted plus re-radiated inward through a glazing system. **Range: 0 to 1** (dimensionless). SHGC = 0.25 means only 25% of solar heat enters.

**(c) Energy Performance Index (EPI):** The ratio of annual energy consumption (in kWh) of a building to its conditioned built-up area (in m²). **Unit: kWh/m²·year.** Lower EPI = more efficient. ECBC threshold: ≤ 90 kWh/m²·year for compliance.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**List any six passive design strategies for improving energy efficiency in tropical Indian buildings.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Building orientation** with the long axis running East-West to minimize east and west wall solar exposure.
2. **Window-to-Wall Ratio (WWR) optimization** at 30–40% on south façade.
3. **External shading devices** — chajjas, fins, and louvers — to block high summer sun.
4. **Cross ventilation** with windows on opposite walls to promote wind-driven cooling.
5. **Cool roof** with high Solar Reflectance Index (SRI > 78) coating or tile finish.
6. **Daylighting** through skylights, light shelves, and clerestories to reduce artificial lighting demand.
7. **(Optional extra)** Thermal mass with exposed brick or concrete for diurnal heat absorption.
8. **(Optional extra)** Deciduous trees on south and west façades for seasonal shading.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — ESE Module Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Heat Transfer Problems:**
> Many students **omit surface resistances** ($R_{si}$ and $R_{se}$) when computing total R-value, leading to U-values that are 8–10% higher than correct. This is a guaranteed **1 to 2 mark loss** in 7-mark sub-parts. ALWAYS include both surface film resistances in your final sum. Also, **do not confuse k (W/mK) with U (W/m²K)** — these are different quantities.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Envelope Engineering Focus*

**[KTU University Exam – Sample 2024 Model Paper]**

**Q. (a)** A 300 mm thick brick wall (k = 0.81 W/mK) is proposed for a building. Calculate the steady-state heat flux through the wall per m² when the outside temperature is 38 °C and inside is 25 °C. State any assumptions. **[7 Marks]**

**Q. (b)** Explain in detail the passive design strategies that can reduce the cooling load of a building in a composite climate (like Delhi) by at least 30%. Use labelled diagrams if required. **[7 Marks]**

---

### **Model Solution — Q. (a)**

**Step 1 — Identify the knowns**
- Wall thickness: $x = 0.300$ m
- Thermal conductivity: $k = 0.81$ W/mK
- Outside temp: $T_o = 38$ °C
- Inside temp: $T_i = 25$ °C
- Area: $A = 1$ m² (per m² basis)

**Step 2 — Compute the temperature difference**

$$\Delta T = T_o - T_i = 38 - 25 = 13 \text{ K}$$

**Step 3 — Apply Fourier's Law of Heat Conduction**

$$q = \frac{k \cdot \Delta T}{x} = \frac{0.81 \times 13}{0.300}$$

$$q = \frac{10.53}{0.300} = 35.1 \text{ W/m}^2$$

**Assumptions stated:**
- Steady-state one-dimensional heat flow
- Constant thermal conductivity
- Isothermal surfaces
- No moisture migration
- Negligible thermal bridging

**Result:** Heat flux = **35.1 W/m²** flows from outside to inside.

---

### **Model Solution — Q. (b)**

Passive design strategies for a 30% cooling load reduction in **Composite Climate (Delhi)**:

| # | Strategy | Mechanism | Contribution |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Orientation E-W long axis** | Reduces east & west wall (high sun angle) gain | 8–10% |
| 2 | **Shading on south/west** | Chajja at 0.5 m depth blocks 70% summer solar | 10–12% |
| 3 | **Wall insulation (50 mm EPS)** | Drops wall U from 1.8 to 0.5 W/m²K | 12–15% |
| 4 | **Cool roof (SRI > 78)** | Reflects 75% incident solar radiation | 8–10% |
| 5 | **Low-E double glazing (SHGC 0.25)** | Blocks 60% of solar heat through glass | 8–10% |
| 6 | **Cross ventilation design** | Reduces AC dependence in transition seasons | 5–7% |
| 7 | **Daylighting (DF > 2%)** | Reduces artificial lighting heat gain | 3–5% |

> **Cumulative reduction = 30–40%**, comfortably exceeding the 30% target. These are cumulative — strategies compound, and combined with a high-COP AC upgrade, the **electrical consumption can drop by 50–60%**.

A labelled **section through a passive-designed south wall** should show:
- External chajja (horizontal projection)
- Cavity wall with EPS insulation
- Low-E double-glazed window
- Internal plaster
- Optional light shelf above window

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Energy Performance & Code Compliance Focus*

**[KTU University Exam – Sample 2024 Model Paper]**

**Q. (a)** Define the Energy Performance Index (EPI). A commercial building has an annual electricity consumption of 45,000 kWh and a conditioned built-up area of 600 m². Calculate its EPI and comment on its ECBC compliance status. **[7 Marks]**

**Q. (b)** Discuss the salient features of the Energy Conservation Building Code (ECBC) 2007. Explain how it categorizes buildings based on EPI. **[7 Marks]**

---

### **Model Solution — Q. (a)**

**Step 1 — Definition of EPI**

> **EPI** is the annual energy consumption in kWh per unit conditioned built-up area, expressed as:
> $$EPI = \frac{\text{Annual Energy Consumption (kWh)}}{\text{Conditioned Built-up Area (m}^2\text{)}}$$
> **Unit: kWh/m²·year**

**Step 2 — Calculate EPI for the given building**

$$EPI = \frac{45{,}000 \text{ kWh}}{600 \text{ m}^2} = 75 \text{ kWh/m}^2\text{·year}$$

**Step 3 — Comment on ECBC compliance**

> An EPI of **75 kWh/m²·year** qualifies the building as **ECBC+ Compliant** (threshold ≤ 90 kWh/m²·year for ECBC, and ≤ 75 for ECBC+). However, it is **not yet SuperECBC**, which requires EPI ≤ 50 kWh/m²·year.
>
> **Recommendations to reach SuperECBC:**
> - Upgrade AC to COP 4.0+ (saves ~25% on cooling electricity)
> - Replace remaining fluorescents with LEDs
> - Add 10 kW solar PV (offsets ~16,000 kWh/year)
> - New projected EPI ≈ 45 kWh/m²·year → **SuperECBC**

---

### **Model Solution — Q. (b)**

**Salient Features of ECBC 2007 (BEE, India):**

1. **Mandatory for commercial buildings** with connected load ≥ 100 kW or conditioned area ≥ 500 m².
2. **Applies to 5 climate zones** of India: Composite, Hot-Dry, Warm-Humid, Temperate, Cold.
3. **Sets minimum energy performance standards** for:
   - Building envelope (walls, roof, glazing) — U-value & SHGC limits
   - Lighting — LPD (Lighting Power Density) limits in W/m²
   - HVAC — minimum COP requirements, system sizing
   - Electrical — power factor correction, transformer efficiency
   - Renewable energy integration (optional for credit)

4. **Compliance pathway:** Prescriptive (per component) OR Whole Building Performance (EPI-based).

**EPI-based Categorization:**

| Category | EPI Range (kWh/m²·yr) | Status |
|---|---|---|
| **ECBC** | ≤ 90 | Compliant |
| **ECBC+** | ≤ 75 | Plus |
| **SuperECBC** | ≤ 50 | High Performance |
| **Net Zero (NZE)** | ≈ 0 (renewable offset) | Carbon Neutral |

5. **Energy audit mandatory** at design, commissioning, and operation stages.
6. **Compliance documentation** includes COMNET (Compliance Monitoring Tool) submission.
7. **Penalty clause:** Non-compliant buildings may face disconnection / fines under State Energy Conservation Rules.

> **ECBC+R (2018)** is the residential counterpart for buildings ≤ 250 kW load.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist — Energy Efficiency in Buildings**

- **Three laws of energy efficiency:** Reduce load → Recover waste → Renew with clean energy.
- **Master Fourier's Law:** $Q = \dfrac{kA\Delta T}{x}$ — and always remember to add $R_{si} + R_{se}$ when computing $R_{total}$.
- **U-value target (ECBC):** Walls ≤ 0.4–1.2 W/m²K, Roof ≤ 0.4–0.8 W/m²K, Glass U ≤ 3.3–5.8 W/m²K depending on climate zone.
- **SHGC sweet spot:** ≤ 0.25 for south/west glazing in tropical India.
- **EPI is the king metric:** Always quote in **kWh/m²·year**; ECBC = 90, ECBC+ = 75, SuperECBC = 50.
- **CLTD = Cooling Load Temperature Difference** — used in hourly load calculation.
- **HDD/CDD** are climatic indicators; **higher HDD = heating-dominated climate** (e.g., Srinagar); **higher CDD = cooling-dominated** (e.g., Chennai).
- **Passive priority order for tropical India:** Orientation → Shading → Insulation → Cross ventilation → Cool roof → Daylighting.
- **Thermal mass** is *good* in hot-dry climate (delay & dampen) and *bad* in warm-humid (stores heat overnight).
- **Cool roof** with SRI > 78 reduces roof heat gain by 30–40%; cheapest first step.
- **Compact building form** minimizes surface-to-volume ratio, reducing envelope heat flow.
- **WWR:** 30–40% is optimal; going beyond 40% invites excessive solar gain.
- **Daylight Factor (DF)** ≥ 2% is the design target; achieved via skylights, light shelves, and clerestories.
- **Infiltration costs energy:** Aim for ACH ≤ 1.0 with proper sealing and weather-stripping.
- **ECBC 2007** applies to commercial buildings ≥ 100 kW; **ECBC+R 2018** is for residential.
- **COP of modern VRF systems:** 3.5–4.5; older window ACs: 2.0–2.5. Higher COP = less electricity.
- **GRIHA & LEED** are voluntary rating systems; GRIHA is India-specific and aligned with ECBC.
- **Heat gain hierarchy (typical):** Roof 25%, Walls 20%, Glass 30%, Infiltration 10%, Internal 15%.
- **Reflective insulation** uses aluminum foil; **bulk insulation** uses trapped air (EPS, glass wool).
- **Thermal bridge** is a localized low-resistance path (e.g., concrete column in insulated wall) — design must minimize it.
- **Net Zero Energy Building (NZEB):** Annual on-site renewable generation ≥ annual energy consumption.
- **BEMS (Building Energy Management System)** can save 10–20% operational energy through scheduling and optimization.
- **5-Star AC** under BEE labelling consumes ~30% less electricity than 1-Star models.
- **Solar PV payback:** Typically 4–6 years in India; **solar water heating** has a 2–4 year payback.
- **Final mantra:** Design for the climate, build with the code, and operate with the meter.

> **End of PECET415 — Module 4 Notes | Energy Efficiency in Buildings | KTU 2024 Scheme**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Energy Assessment in Buildings
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET415 - ARCHITECTURAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Energy Assessment in Buildings

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Energy Assessment in Buildings** is the systematic, science-based process of quantifying the energy consumption, performance, and efficiency of a building's envelope, systems (HVAC, lighting, electrical), and operational practices against established benchmarks such as the **Energy Conservation Building Code (ECBC)** or international standards like **ASHRAE 90.1**, with the goal of identifying conservation opportunities and optimizing thermal/visual comfort.

In the context of **PECET415 – Architectural Engineering (KTU 2024 Scheme)**, Energy Assessment is treated as a **diagnostic discipline** that bridges architectural design, mechanical engineering, and sustainability metrics. It is the *evaluative counterpart* of green building design — whereas green design *creates* efficient buildings, energy assessment *verifies* whether a building actually performs as designed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Scheme – Module 4):**
> Students are expected to understand (i) the methodology of energy audits, (ii) calculation of **Energy Performance Index (EPI)**, (iii) ECBC compliance pathways (Prescriptive, Trade-off, Whole Building Performance), and (iv) passive and active strategies for reducing building energy demand.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Building Medical Check-up" 🏥

Imagine a human body going for a complete medical check-up. The doctor measures blood pressure, heart rate, cholesterol, oxygen levels — each is a *performance indicator*. Based on these readings, the doctor prescribes lifestyle changes or medication.

**Energy Assessment works the same way for a building.**

| Human Body Check-up | Building Energy Assessment |
|---|---|
| Blood test | Energy audit (kWh measurement) |
| BMI | Energy Performance Index (EPI) |
| Doctor's prescription | Retrofit recommendations |
| "Healthy" label | ECBC / GRIHA / LEED certification |

Just as a person with high cholesterol is at *risk*, a building with a **high EPI (kWh/m²/year)** is *energy-inefficient*. The auditor's job is to **measure → compare → diagnose → recommend**.

## 1.3 Key Performance Metrics (KTU Mandatory Vocabulary)

- **EPI** — Energy Performance Index (kWh/m²/year) — *the single most important number* in any energy assessment.
- **OTTV** — Overall Thermal Transfer Value (W/m²) — measures envelope heat gain.
- **LPD** — Lighting Power Density (W/m²) — installed lighting load.
- **ECBC** — Energy Conservation Building Code (India, 2007; revised 2017).
- **NZEB** — Net Zero Energy Building.
- **CDD / HDD** — Cooling / Heating Degree Days (climate-based benchmarks).
- **U-value** — Thermal transmittance (W/m²·K) of a building element.
- **SHGC** — Solar Heat Gain Coefficient of glazing.

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Hook:** Think **"E-P-I = Energy per Inch (per m²)"** — the lower the EPI, the healthier the building. BEE (Bureau of Energy Efficiency, India) classifies buildings on a **1-Star to 5-Star** scale based on EPI thresholds, which vary by *climate zone* (Composite, Hot-Dry, Warm-Humid, Temperate, Cold).

## 1.4 Why Energy Assessment Matters — The "Why" Behind the Subject

1. **Buildings consume ~33% of global final energy** (UN Environment Programme, GlobalABC).
2. In India, the **building sector accounts for ~35% of total electricity demand** (BEE estimates).
3. Air-conditioning alone can consume **40–60%** of a commercial building's total electricity.
4. Unassessed buildings waste **20–30%** of consumed energy due to poor envelope design, oversized HVAC, and inefficient lighting.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Outcome Mapping:** This topic directly satisfies **CO4** of PECET415 — *"Evaluate the energy performance of buildings using standard assessment frameworks and recommend sustainable design interventions."* (Application & Analysis levels of Revised Bloom's Taxonomy.)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three-Tier Energy Audit Framework

The KTU 2024 syllabus and the **Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE) – India** classify energy audits into three escalating levels of detail. Each subsequent level provides greater *depth of analysis* but demands *more time, instrumentation, and cost*.

### Tier 1 — Walk-through Audit (Pre-audit / Preliminary Survey)
- **What it is:** A visual inspection and interview-based survey.
- **Duration:** 1–3 days.
- **Instruments used:** Light-meter, infra-red thermometer, plug-in watt-meter.
- **Output:** Identification of *obvious* energy wastage; preliminary saving estimates.
- **Analogy:** A general physician's first glance before ordering tests.

### Tier 2 — Preliminary (Mini) Audit
- **What it is:** A quantified analysis of energy-consuming systems with limited instrumentation.
- **Duration:** 2–4 weeks.
- **Instruments used:** All of Tier 1 + portable power analysers, lux meters, ultrasonic flow meters.
- **Output:** Energy balance; cost-saving opportunities with rough pay-back.
- **Analogy:** The blood-test and ECG stage.

### Tier 3 — Detailed (Investment-grade) Audit
- **What it is:** A comprehensive, computer-simulated, instrumented analysis.
- **Duration:** 4–12 weeks.
- **Instruments used:** Data loggers (temperature, RH, kWh, kVA), thermal imaging cameras, blower-door tests.
- **Output:** Calibrated energy model, retrofit design, M&V (Measurement & Verification) baseline.
- **Analogy:** The full body MRI + specialist consultation.

## 2.2 ECBC Compliance Pathways — The Three Doors

Under **ECBC 2017**, a building can prove compliance through *any one* of three pathways. The auditor must identify which pathway is most appropriate.

| Pathway | What is Done | Best Suited For |
|---|---|---|
| **Prescriptive** | Each component (wall, roof, window, LPD, HVAC EER) must meet *individual* mandatory/prescriptive limits | Simple, repetitive buildings |
| **Trade-off** | Building envelope *trade-offs* allowed (e.g., better glass can compensate for higher wall U-value) | Mixed-use, irregular plans |
| **Whole Building Performance (WBP)** | Energy simulation (e.g., EnergyPlus, eQUEST, DesignBuilder) showing the proposed building uses *less energy* than an ECBC-compliant baseline | Complex, high-performance projects |

## 2.3 The Envelope-Load-Equipment-Systems Triad

Every building's energy profile is a function of four interacting systems. Energy assessment decomposes the total energy into these sub-systems for diagnosis:

1. **Building Envelope (Passive):** Roof, walls, windows, shading devices — control *heat gain/loss*.
2. **HVAC / Mechanical (Active):** Chillers, AHUs, pumps, fans, cooling towers — biggest energy user.
3. **Lighting & Equipment (Plug Loads):** LPD, lifts, computers, servers.
4. **Renewable / On-site Generation:** Solar PV, solar thermal, wind — reduce *net* energy.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Exam-Oriented Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MANDATORY:** All formulas below are *examinable* in KTU ESE. Memorize the bold-italic variables and their units.

| # | Formula | Description / Variables | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $EPI = \dfrac{E_{annual}}{A_{conditioned}}$ | **EPI** = Energy Performance Index; $E_{annual}$ = annual electrical energy consumed; $A_{conditioned}$ = air-conditioned (or built-up) area | kWh/m²/yr |
| 2 | $Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$ | Steady-state heat transfer through a building element | W |
| 3 | $U = \dfrac{1}{R_{si} + \sum \frac{d_i}{k_i} + R_{se}}$ | Thermal transmittance of a multi-layer assembly | W/m²·K |
| 4 | $OTTV = 15 \cdot \alpha \cdot (1 - WWR) \cdot U_w + 194 \cdot WWR \cdot SHGC + 6 \cdot U_r$ | Overall Thermal Transfer Value (ECBC formula for non-residential) | W/m² |
| 5 | $LPD = \dfrac{\text{Total Installed Lighting Load (W)}}{\text{Conditioned Area (m²)}}$ | Lighting Power Density — must not exceed ECBC table | W/m² |
| 6 | $EER = \dfrac{Q_{cooling} \text{ (Btu/hr)}}{P_{input} \text{ (W)}}$ | Energy Efficiency Ratio of an AC / chiller | Btu/Wh |
| 7 | $COP = \dfrac{Q_{useful}}{W_{input}}$ | Coefficient of Performance — more general than EER | dimensionless |
| 8 | $S = \dfrac{Q_{solar} \cdot SHGC \cdot A_{glazing}}{A_{floor}}$ | Solar heat gain per unit floor area | W/m² |
| 9 | $CDD = \sum (T_{daily} - T_{base})^{+}$ | Cooling Degree Days (climatic cooling load proxy) | °C·days |
| 10 | $\% \text{ Savings} = \dfrac{EPI_{base} - EPI_{proposed}}{EPI_{base}} \times 100$ | Energy reduction between baseline and proposed design | % |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Hint:** Notice how every formula has an **A** in the denominator or numerator — *Area is the great unifier*. Always remember to convert to **kWh/year** and **m²** when reporting EPI, or you'll lose 1–2 marks.

## 2.5 Why This Knowledge is *Engineer-Useful*

In your future career, the ability to compute **EPI** and **OTTV** will let you:
- Bid for **ECBC-compliant** projects (mandatory in India for commercial buildings > 1000 m² connected load ≥ 500 kW or contract demand ≥ 600 kVA as per ECBC 2017 notification).
- Apply for **GRIHA / IGBC / LEED** green-rating points (EA category credits).
- Diagnose *why* a building's electricity bill is high.
- Justify a **PV solar rooftop investment** by computing the net-energy balance.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📙 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Calculating the Energy Performance Index (EPI)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this derivation matters:** EPI is the *single most-asked* numerical problem in KTU ESE for any energy-related module. The complete derivation below gives you the marks for "procedure," "units," and "final answer."

### Problem Statement
A commercial office building in **Trivandrum (Warm-Humid climate)** has the following annual energy consumption record, obtained from electricity bills for FY 2023–24:

- **Lighting consumption:** 48,000 kWh/yr
- **HVAC (Air-conditioning) consumption:** 132,000 kWh/yr
- **Plug / equipment loads (lifts, computers, etc.):** 36,000 kWh/yr
- **Pumps, fans, miscellaneous:** 24,000 kWh/yr
- **Total conditioned (air-conditioned) area:** 1,200 m²

Calculate the **EPI** and classify the building on the **BEE 5-Star Rating** scale (Trivandrum lies in the **Composite / Warm-Humid** climate zone, where the 5-Star threshold is **≤ 40 kWh/m²/yr** and 1-Star threshold is **≥ 180 kWh/m²/yr** as per BEE's existing-building star-rating program).

### Step 1 — Identify the Total Annual Energy Consumption

Add up all the energy end-uses recorded in the bills. These are *site* (delivered) energy values, not *source* (primary) energy.

$$
E_{annual} = E_{lighting} + E_{HVAC} + E_{plug} + E_{misc}
$$

$$
E_{annual} = 48{,}000 + 132{,}000 + 36{,}000 + 24{,}000
$$

$$
E_{annual} = 240{,}000 \text{ kWh/yr}
$$

> **Logic row:** We are summing individual end-uses to obtain the *total site energy*. This is the numerator in the EPI definition.

### Step 2 — Identify the Conditioned Floor Area

The denominator is the *conditioned* (air-conditioned) area, NOT the total built-up area.

$$
A_{conditioned} = 1{,}200 \text{ m}^2
$$

> **Logic row:** The BEE Star Rating protocol specifies that EPI uses *conditioned* area to fairly compare buildings of different shapes and unconditioned circulation spaces.

### Step 3 — Apply the EPI Formula

$$
EPI = \frac{E_{annual}}{A_{conditioned}}
$$

$$
EPI = \frac{240{,}000 \text{ kWh/yr}}{1{,}200 \text{ m}^2}
$$

$$
EPI = 200 \text{ kWh/m}^2/\text{yr}
$$

> **Logic row:** Division yields the *per-square-metre annual* energy intensity, the universal benchmark metric.

### Step 4 — Classify the Building on the BEE Scale

For the **Composite / Warm-Humid** climate zone:

| Star Rating | EPI Range (kWh/m²/yr) | Verdict |
|---|---|---|
| 5-Star (Excellent) | $\leq 40$ | Best-in-class |
| 4-Star | $41 - 80$ | High performance |
| 3-Star | $81 - 120$ | Above average |
| 2-Star | $121 - 180$ | Average |
| 1-Star (Poor) | $\geq 180$ | Needs retrofit |

Our computed **EPI = 200 kWh/m²/yr** falls in the **1-Star category**, indicating an energy-inefficient building that requires immediate retrofitting.

> **Logic row:** Compare the calculated EPI against the *climate-zone-specific* threshold table. Always state the climate zone, otherwise marks are docked.

### Step 5 — Interpret the Result and Recommend Retrofits

Since the building is rated **1-Star**, the energy auditor should recommend:

1. **Envelope upgrade** — reduce OTTV by adding double-glazed low-E windows (SHGC < 0.25).
2. **Lighting retrofit** — replace T8/T12 fluorescent tubes with LED, reducing LPD from ~12 W/m² to ~6 W/m².
3. **HVAC efficiency** — replace old chiller (COP 2.5) with inverter-driven chiller (COP 4.0); savings ~ 30–40% of HVAC kWh.
4. **Renewable integration** — install 50 kWp rooftop solar PV to offset ~ 20% of annual consumption.
5. **Controls** — install occupancy sensors, daylight sensors, BMS scheduling.

A combined retrofit package can realistically bring this building to **EPI ≤ 90 kWh/m²/yr (3-Star)** within 3–5 years, with a typical **pay-back of 4–6 years**.

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: OTTV (Overall Thermal Transfer Value)

The **OTTV** quantifies the *envelope-driven* heat gain through the roof, walls, and windows of a non-residential (ECBC) building. Lower OTTV = cooler building = less air-conditioning load.

### Step 1 — Identify the Required Input Parameters

For a typical west-facing office wall in Trivandrum:
- Wall U-value: $U_w = 0.40$ W/m²·K
- Window-to-Wall Ratio: $WWR = 0.40$ (40% glazed)
- Wall solar absorptance: $\alpha = 0.50$
- Glazing SHGC: $SHGC = 0.25$ (high-performance double-glazed)
- Roof U-value: $U_r = 0.30$ W/m²·K

### Step 2 — Apply the ECBC OTTV Equation

$$
OTTV = 15 \cdot \alpha \cdot (1 - WWR) \cdot U_w + 194 \cdot WWR \cdot SHGC + 6 \cdot U_r
$$

### Step 3 — Substitute Numerical Values

$$
OTTV = 15 \cdot 0.50 \cdot (1 - 0.40) \cdot 0.40 + 194 \cdot 0.40 \cdot 0.25 + 6 \cdot 0.30
$$

### Step 4 — Evaluate Each Term One-by-One

**Term 1** (conduction through opaque wall):

$$
T_1 = 15 \cdot 0.50 \cdot 0.60 \cdot 0.40
$$

$$
T_1 = 15 \cdot 0.12 = 1.80 \text{ W/m}^2
$$

> **Logic row:** The constants 15 and 6 are empirical coefficients derived by ECBC for Indian conditions (solar incidence averaged across orientations, time-weighted).

**Term 2** (solar gain through glazing):

$$
T_2 = 194 \cdot 0.40 \cdot 0.25
$$

$$
T_2 = 194 \cdot 0.10 = 19.40 \text{ W/m}^2
$$

> **Logic row:** The constant 194 represents the *time-averaged incident solar radiation* on a vertical surface in India; multiplying by SHGC and WWR gives the net transmitted solar heat.

**Term 3** (conduction through roof):

$$
T_3 = 6 \cdot 0.30 = 1.80 \text{ W/m}^2
$$

> **Logic row:** The roof is exposed to higher solar flux (constant 6 reflects this), so roof heat gain is separately accounted.

### Step 5 — Sum the Three Terms

$$
OTTV = 1.80 + 19.40 + 1.80
$$

$$
OTTV = 23.00 \text{ W/m}^2
$$

### Step 6 — Compare Against ECBC Limits

ECBC 2017 specifies that OTTV for a non-residential building in the **Warm-Humid / Composite** climate zone must not exceed **40 W/m²**. Our computed value of **23 W/m²** is **well within compliance** — the envelope is *conservative* and a possible **trade-off** (e.g., slightly higher WWR) is allowed.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Mark-Hunting Tip:** When OTTV problems appear, students often *forget* the roof term or the WWR term. Always draw the wall-section first, label the glazed vs opaque area, and identify WWR = (glazing area / total wall area) clearly.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Energy End-Use Distribution (Typical Indian Office)

| End-Use | Typical Share of Total Energy | Retrofit Priority |
|---|---|---|
| HVAC (Cooling) | 40 – 60 % | **Highest** |
| Lighting | 15 – 25 % | **High** |
| Plug / Equipment | 10 – 20 % | Medium |
| Lifts & Pumping | 5 – 10 % | Medium |
| Miscellaneous (kitchen, server) | 5 – 10 % | Low (unless server-heavy) |

> **Logic row:** This distribution is the *first chart* a real auditor prepares. It shows where to focus the retrofit budget. Cooling > Lighting > Plugs is the universal mantra.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📒 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Figure 1 — Energy Audit Methodology Flow

This diagram represents the **complete Tier-3 (Detailed) Energy Audit** workflow as prescribed by BEE and ECBC, in the order in which an auditor executes it on-site.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Pre-Audit Planning: Collect Bills, Drawings, Occupancy Data] --> B[Walk-Through Survey: Visual Inspection, Interviews]
    B --> C[Tier 1 Identification of Low-Cost Measures]
    C --> D[Detailed Measurements: kWh, Lux, Temperature, RH, Flow]
    D --> E[End-Use Energy Breakdown by System]
    E --> F[Baseline Energy Model Development]
    F --> G[Identify Energy Conservation Opportunities ECOs]
    G --> H[Economic Analysis: Payback, NPV, IRR]
    H --> I[Final Report: Recommendations, M&V Plan]
    I --> J[Implementation Support and Monitoring]
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Each block is an *audit phase*. The arrows are *sequential* — you cannot compute payback (H) without first developing a baseline model (F). The dashed return from J to D represents the *M&V* (Measurement & Verification) loop that continues even after retrofits are implemented, ensuring sustained savings.

## 4.2 Figure 2 — Building Energy Assessment Block-Level Architecture

This block diagram maps the *interactions* between the four major energy systems in any building and shows how the energy assessment *intercepts* energy flows at each point.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph EXTERNAL[External Environment]
        SUN[Solar Radiation]
        AIR[Ambient Air]
        WIND[Wind]
    end
    subgraph ENVELOPE[Building Envelope Passive Layer]
        WALL[Opaque Walls, Roof]
        GLAZE[Glazing Windows]
        SHADE[Shading Devices]
    end
    subgraph ACTIVE[Active Systems Inside Building]
        HVAC[HVAC Chillers, AHUs, Fans]
        LIGHT[Lighting LPD]
        PLUG[Plug Loads Equipment]
    end
    subgraph ASSESS[Energy Assessment Layer]
        METER[Energy Meters kWh, kVA]
        AUDIT[Auditor Data Analysis]
        EPI_CALC[EPI, OTTV, LPD Calculation]
        REPORT[Retrofit Report]
    end
    SUN --> WALL
    SUN --> GLAZE
    AIR --> HVAC
    WIND --> ENVELOPE
    WALL --> HVAC
    GLAZE --> HVAC
    SHADE --> GLAZE
    HVAC --> METER
    LIGHT --> METER
    PLUG --> METER
    METER --> AUDIT
    AUDIT --> EPI_CALC
    EPI_CALC --> REPORT
    REPORT -.->|Retrofit Recommendations| HVAC
    REPORT -.->|LED Upgrade| LIGHT
    REPORT -.->|Envelope Upgrade| ENVELOPE
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Energy *flows* from the environment (top) through the envelope (middle-top), into the active systems (middle-bottom), and is finally *measured* (right). The Energy Assessment Layer (right column) *intercepts* every energy stream, computes the indicators, and *feeds back* retrofit recommendations as control signals (dashed arrows).

## 4.3 Figure 3 — ECBC Compliance Decision Tree

The auditor or designer must choose *one* of three compliance pathways; this diagram is the official ECBC decision logic.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[New Building or Major Renovation Over 1000 m²] --> Q1{Is the building typology simple and repetitive?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| P[Use PRESCRIPTIVE Pathway: Meet each component limit]
    Q1 -->|No| Q2{Are envelope trade-offs between wall, roof, glazing needed?}
    Q2 -->|Yes| T[Use TRADE-OFF Pathway: Trade between envelope components]
    Q2 -->|No, want full analysis| W[Use WHOLE BUILDING PERFORMANCE WBP: Energy simulation vs baseline]
    P --> C[ECBC Compliant]
    T --> C
    W --> C
```

> **Reading the diagram:** This is a *decision tree*, not a process flow. The auditor answers a *Yes/No* question at each diamond node and lands on one of the three pathways. All three end at "ECBC Compliant," but the *evidence* required differs.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📒 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]**

### Q1. Define Energy Performance Index (EPI). Mention its unit and significance in building energy assessment.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Energy Performance Index (EPI)** is defined as the *annual electrical energy consumed per unit conditioned floor area* of a building.

$$
EPI = \frac{E_{annual}}{A_{conditioned}}
$$

**Unit:** kWh/m²/year.

**Significance:**
- Acts as a **single-number benchmark** for comparing buildings of different sizes.
- Is the **basis of the BEE 5-Star Rating** for existing buildings in India.
- Lower EPI ⇒ more efficient building ⇒ lower operational cost and lower carbon footprint.
- Used by ECBC 2017 to set compliance thresholds for various climate zones.

> **Marking pattern (KTU):** 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for unit, 1 mark for significance.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**

### Q2. Differentiate between a Walk-through Audit and a Detailed Energy Audit.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Walk-through Audit | Detailed Energy Audit |
|---|---|---|
| **Duration** | 1 – 3 days | 4 – 12 weeks |
| **Instruments** | Visual, basic meters | Data loggers, thermal cameras, Blower-door |
| **Cost** | Low | High |
| **Output** | Identification of obvious wastage | Calibrated energy model, retrofit design |
| **Accuracy** | ± 30 % | ± 5 – 10 % |
| **Suitable for** | Pre-feasibility, small buildings | Investment-grade retrofit, large buildings |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Question]**

### Question A (14 Marks)

#### (a) [7 Marks] Explain the three ECBC compliance pathways in detail. When would you choose each?

**Model Answer:**

The **Energy Conservation Building Code (ECBC 2017)** prescribes three compliance pathways, all of which lead to an "ECBC-Compliant" building but require different types of evidence.

**1. Prescriptive Pathway (Component-by-Component):**
- Each building element (wall, roof, window, skylight) is checked *individually* against mandatory U-value, SHGC, and Visual Light Transmittance (VLT) limits given in ECBC Table 4.1 to 4.6.
- Lighting Power Density (LPD) and HVAC equipment efficiencies are also checked against mandatory tables.
- **No trade-offs allowed** — every component must pass independently.
- **Best suited for:** Simple, repetitive, rectangular-plan buildings like offices, schools, hostels.
- **Advantage:** Simple to apply; no simulation software needed.
- **Limitation:** No flexibility — sometimes forces over-design of one element to compensate for another.

**2. Trade-off Pathway:**
- Allows **envelope trade-offs** — for example, a wall with U-value *slightly above* the prescriptive limit can be accepted if a *correspondingly better* window is used, *provided* the overall envelope performance is maintained.
- Trade-offs are computed using **ECBC-compliant software** (e.g., the ECBC Trade-off Tool).
- The overall **OTTV** must remain below the ECBC limit for the climate zone.
- **Best suited for:** Buildings with mixed orientations, large glazed facades, or atypical plans.
- **Advantage:** Design flexibility without sacrificing energy performance.
- **Limitation:** Requires user to be conversant with the trade-off tool.

**3. Whole Building Performance (WBP) Pathway:**
- Uses **dynamic energy simulation software** (EnergyPlus, eQUEST, DesignBuilder, IES-VE) to compute the *annual energy use* of the proposed building.
- The result is compared against an **ECBC-baseline building** (an identical building that *just* meets all prescriptive requirements).
- If the proposed building uses **at least 25 % less energy** (or any ECBC-specified %) than the baseline, it qualifies.
- **Best suited for:** Complex, high-performance projects, hospitals, IT parks, and where the designer wants the freedom to *deviate* from prescriptive limits in exchange for proven overall performance.
- **Advantage:** Most flexible, captures real interactions between systems.
- **Limitation:** Requires skilled energy modeller; simulation is time-consuming.

**Selection Criteria (1-mark):**
- *Choose Prescriptive* when speed and simplicity matter.
- *Choose Trade-off* when envelope tuning is needed.
- *Choose WBP* when a high-performance, non-standard design is proposed.

---

#### (b) [7 Marks] A 1,500 m² office in Kochi (Warm-Humid climate) consumes 270,000 kWh/year. Calculate the EPI and state the BEE star rating. List four retrofit measures to improve it.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify the given data**
- $E_{annual} = 270{,}000$ kWh/yr
- $A_{conditioned} = 1{,}500$ m²
- Climate zone: Warm-Humid (Kochi)

**Step 2 — Calculate EPI**

$$
EPI = \frac{270{,}000}{1{,}500} = 180 \text{ kWh/m}^2/\text{yr}
$$

**Step 3 — Compare with BEE thresholds for Warm-Humid zone**

| Star Rating | EPI Range (Warm-Humid) | Verdict |
|---|---|---|
| 5-Star | $\leq 40$ | Excellent |
| 4-Star | $41 - 80$ | High performance |
| 3-Star | $81 - 120$ | Above average |
| 2-Star | $121 - 180$ | Average |
| 1-Star | $\geq 180$ | **Poor / Needs retrofit** |

Our value of **180 kWh/m²/yr** falls in the **2-Star / borderline 1-Star** category — the building is *average-to-poor* and needs retrofitting.

**Step 4 — Four Retrofit Measures (2 marks each, or 1.5+0.5 each):**

1. **LED Lighting Retrofit:** Replace conventional fluorescent lighting with LEDs, reducing LPD from ~12 W/m² to ~6 W/m² — saves ~ 36,000 kWh/yr (~ 13 % of total).

2. **High-Efficiency Chiller:** Replace old chiller (COP ~ 2.5) with inverter chiller (COP ~ 4.0) — saves ~ 40 % of HVAC energy, i.e., ~ 50,000 kWh/yr.

3. **Window Glazing Upgrade:** Replace single-glazed windows with double-glazed low-E (SHGC 0.25 instead of 0.60) — reduces solar heat gain by ~ 60 %, saving ~ 25,000 kWh/yr in cooling load.

4. **Rooftop Solar PV (50 kWp):** Generate ~ 65,000 kWh/yr from on-site solar, offsetting ~ 24 % of total consumption and improving the *net* EPI to ~ 137 kWh/m²/yr (3-Star level).

> **Mark split (typical KTU):** 1 mark for formula, 1 for substitution, 1 for answer with units, 1 for climate-zone reference, 1 for star rating, 2 marks for retrofit list.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Internal Choice Alternative*

#### (a) [7 Marks] What is OTTV? Explain the ECBC formula and the physical meaning of each term. How does OTTV guide envelope design?

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (1 mark):**
**OTTV (Overall Thermal Transfer Value)** is the *time-averaged rate of heat flow* into a conditioned space through the building envelope, expressed in **W/m²**. It accounts for conduction through walls, roof, and solar gain through windows, averaged over a typical day/season.

**ECBC Formula (3 marks):**

$$
OTTV = 15 \cdot \alpha \cdot (1 - WWR) \cdot U_w + 194 \cdot WWR \cdot SHGC + 6 \cdot U_r
$$

**Term-by-term physical meaning:**

- **Term 1 — $15 \cdot \alpha \cdot (1 - WWR) \cdot U_w$** → Conduction through the *opaque* (non-glazed) part of the wall. $\alpha$ = solar absorptance of the wall surface (typically 0.4–0.7 for paints); $(1 - WWR)$ = fraction of wall that is opaque; $U_w$ = wall thermal transmittance; the constant 15 is the time-averaged sol-air temperature differential for vertical surfaces in Indian climates.

- **Term 2 — $194 \cdot WWR \cdot SHGC$** → *Solar radiation transmitted through the glazing*. WWR is the window-to-wall ratio; SHGC is the solar heat gain coefficient of the glass (0–1). The constant 194 W/m² is the *average daily solar flux on a vertical surface* under Indian conditions.

- **Term 3 — $6 \cdot U_r$** → Conduction through the *roof* (treated separately because the roof is horizontal and receives higher solar flux). The constant 6 is the time-averaged sol-air temperature differential for horizontal (roof) surfaces.

**How OTTV guides design (3 marks):**
- Provides a **single-number envelope benchmark** — designer aims to keep OTTV **≤ 40 W/m²** (Warm-Humid zone).
- Encourages **wall insulation** (low $U_w$), **light-coloured reflective paints** (low $\alpha$), **shading devices** (reduce WWR or use overhangs), and **high-performance glazing** (low SHGC).
- Allows **trade-offs** between wall and window — e.g., a wall with $U_w = 0.5$ W/m²·K can be paired with SHGC = 0.20 glass as long as OTTV ≤ 40.

---

#### (b) [7 Marks] A west-facing wall has the following: $U_w = 0.50$ W/m²·K, $\alpha = 0.6$, WWR = 0.50, SHGC = 0.30, $U_r = 0.35$ W/m²·K. Calculate OTTV. Does it comply with ECBC (Warm-Humid limit = 40 W/m²)? Suggest one improvement.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Substitution**

$$
OTTV = 15 \cdot 0.6 \cdot (1 - 0.5) \cdot 0.50 + 194 \cdot 0.5 \cdot 0.30 + 6 \cdot 0.35
$$

**Step 2 — Term-by-term evaluation**

- $T_1 = 15 \cdot 0.6 \cdot 0.5 \cdot 0.50 = 2.25$ W/m²
- $T_2 = 194 \cdot 0.5 \cdot 0.30 = 29.10$ W/m²
- $T_3 = 6 \cdot 0.35 = 2.10$ W/m²

**Step 3 — Sum**

$$
OTTV = 2.25 + 29.10 + 2.10 = 33.45 \text{ W/m}^2
$$

**Step 4 — Compliance check**

Since **33.45 W/m² < 40 W/m²**, the building **complies with ECBC** for the Warm-Humid zone.

**Step 5 — Suggested improvement (1 mark):**

The dominant term is **$T_2$ (solar gain through glazing)**. We can reduce it by:
- Reducing **WWR** (e.g., 0.50 → 0.35) — saves 1.5 W/m² from $T_2$.
- Using **lower SHGC glass** (e.g., SHGC 0.30 → 0.20) — saves ~ 9.7 W/m² from $T_2$.
- Adding **external shading** (louvers, chajjas) — equivalent to reducing effective WWR.
- A combined approach (SHGC 0.20 + WWR 0.40) would drop $T_2$ to **15.5 W/m²**, bringing total OTTV to **~ 19.9 W/m²** — significantly better.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (read this before you write the exam):**
> 1. **Forgetting to state the climate zone** when classifying EPI — costs 1 mark. *Always* mention the climate zone explicitly.
> 2. **Mixing up conditioned area vs built-up area** in the EPI denominator — costs 1 mark. The protocol is *conditioned area* (the BEE 5-Star protocol is unambiguous on this).
> 3. **OTTV constants** (15, 194, 6) — students often *re-derive* them or use them incorrectly. **Memorize them as is** for the Indian ECBC formula.
> 4. **Forgetting to convert to kWh/m²/yr units** in the final answer — examiners deduct for missing units.
> 5. **Not writing the formula *before* the substitution** in numerical problems — you lose the "procedure" marks even if the answer is correct. Show: Formula → Substitution → Calculation → Final answer with units.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 📌

> **Rapid-revision checklist for the night before the exam:**

- ✅ **EPI formula:** $EPI = E_{annual} / A_{conditioned}$ (kWh/m²/yr). Lower is better.
- ✅ **BEE Star Rating** is *climate-zone-specific*; thresholds differ across the 5 Indian climate zones.
- ✅ **Three Energy Audit levels:** Walk-through → Preliminary → Detailed (increasing rigour).
- ✅ **ECBC 2017 applies** to commercial buildings with **connected load ≥ 500 kW** or **contract demand ≥ 600 kVA**.
- ✅ **Three ECBC pathways:** Prescriptive, Trade-off, Whole Building Performance.
- ✅ **OTTV formula** has three terms — opaque wall conduction, glazing solar gain, roof conduction. Constants: 15, 194, 6.
- ✅ **ECBC OTTV limit** for Warm-Humid = **40 W/m²**; for Cold climate = 30 W/m²; for Composite = 50 W/m².
- ✅ **LPD** = Total lighting load / conditioned area. ECBC sets maximum LPD by space type (offices ~ 9.5 W/m², corridors ~ 5 W/m²).
- ✅ **EER vs COP:** EER uses Btu/Wh, COP is dimensionless; for the *same* chiller, COP < EER (since 1 W = 3.412 Btu/hr).
- ✅ **Hierarchy of retrofit impact:** Envelope > HVAC > Lighting > Plug loads. Address envelope first for *permanent* savings.
- ✅ **NZEB** = building generates as much energy (renewable) on-site as it consumes over a year. EPI ≈ 0 to 20.
- ✅ **Soft skills for an energy auditor:** ability to read electricity bills, interpret sub-meter data, and communicate ROI to non-technical clients.
- ✅ **Common KTU 14-mark question structure:** (a) 7 marks theory + (b) 7 marks numerical (EPI / OTTV / LPD).
- ✅ **Always state units** in your final answer. KTU examiners are strict about this.

> **Final memory hook:** *"EPI tells you the symptom, OTTV tells you the cause, and the Energy Audit is the doctor."* — Revise this mental model and you will never confuse the three.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Green Building Rating Guidelines
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET415 - ARCHITECTURAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Green Building Rating Guidelines

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Green Building Rating System (GBRS)** is a structured, voluntary, third-party verified assessment tool that evaluates, scores, and certifies the environmental, social, and economic sustainability performance of a building or built environment across its entire life cycle — from site selection and design through construction, operation, maintenance, and end-of-life deconstruction.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PECET415 – Architectural Engineering)** context, green building rating guidelines refer to the standardized national and international frameworks that establish measurable benchmarks for sustainable design, energy efficiency, water conservation, material sustainability, indoor environmental quality, and innovation. The four most prominent global frameworks of academic and professional relevance are:

1. **LEED (Leadership in Energy and Environmental Design)** — United States Green Building Council (USGBC).
2. **BREEAM (Building Research Establishment Environmental Assessment Method)** — United Kingdom (BRE).
3. **GRIHA (Green Rating for Integrated Habitat Assessment)** — India (TERI & MNRE).
4. **IGBC (Indian Green Building Council)** — India (CII).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 PECET415 – Module 4):**  
> Students must be able to *define, classify, and critically compare* the major green building rating systems, identify their **credit categories**, **certification tiers**, and **evaluation criteria**, and apply them to assess a building's sustainability quotient in a typical KTU design problem.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Building's Health Report Card"**

Imagine your **school/college** has an annual health check-up. Doctors measure your **height, weight, eyesight, blood pressure, and stamina** — each metric carries a different weight. Your final "health score" places you into a category: *Excellent, Good, Average, or Needs Improvement*. A green building rating system works **exactly the same way** for a building.

- The **building** = the student.
- The **rating tool (LEED/BREEAM/GRIHA/IGBC)** = the doctor/health check-up.
- The **credits (e.g., Energy, Water, Materials)** = the different health parameters.
- The **points earned** = the score in each test.
- The **certification level (Platinum/Gold/Silver)** = the final health grade.

Just as a student cannot fake their blood test results, a building cannot fake its **energy consumption (kWh/m²/year)** or **water use (litres/person/day)** — these are **measured, audited, and verified** by independent assessors.

---

## 1.3 Why Green Building Rating Guidelines Matter in India

India's built environment consumes approximately **33%** of the nation's electricity, **40%** of its materials, and **20%** of its water. With **urbanization projected at 600 million Indians by 2030**, rating systems provide the **only credible, quantifiable, and scalable mechanism** to enforce sustainable construction.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Indian Drivers (Must-Know for KTU):**
> - **Energy Conservation Building Code (ECBC)** — mandatory benchmark for commercial buildings.
> - **Eco Niwas Samhita (ENS)** — residential equivalent.
> - **Panchamrit & Net-Zero commitments** announced at COP-26.
> - **GRIHA + IGBC + LEED-India** — voluntary third-party certification.
> - **Sustainable Development Goal (SDG) 11** — Sustainable Cities & Communities.

---

## 1.4 Key Terminology at a Glance

| Term | Meaning | KTU Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Prescriptive Criteria** | Mandatory minimums (e.g., minimum daylight factor). | Often a *prerequisite* to earn any points. |
| **Performance Criteria** | Points awarded for exceeding a baseline metric. | Forms the *bulk* of certification scoring. |
| **Prerequisite (P)** | Compulsory credit; no points, must be met. | Filter to ensure basic compliance. |
| **Credit (C)** | Optional / scored item; earns 1+ points. | Decides final certification level. |
| **Weightage Factor** | Multiplier reflecting regional priority. | Higher for energy in hot climates. |
| **Third-Party Verification** | Independent assessor audit (GRIHA, USGBC, etc.). | Ensures credibility of certification. |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Universal Framework of a Green Rating System

Every credible rating system, regardless of geographic origin, follows the same **5-stage operational logic**:

### Stage 1 — **Establishing Benchmarks (Baseline Definition)**
The system first defines a *"business-as-usual"* or **code-compliant baseline building**. All points are awarded for **performance beyond this baseline**.
- *Example:* LEED uses **ASHRAE 90.1-2007** (or later) as the energy baseline.
- *Example:* GRIHA uses **National Building Code (NBC) 2016** + **ECBC** as the baseline.

### Stage 2 — **Category Structuring (Domain Weighting)**
The system divides sustainability into **weighted categories** reflecting regional priorities.
- *Example:* In a water-scarce region (e.g., Chennai, Rajasthan), **Water credits carry higher weightage**.

### Stage 3 — **Credit Allocation (Point Scoring)**
Each category contains multiple **credits**. Each credit has a **point value** (1, 2, 3, etc.). Total points across all categories = **Aggregate Score**.

### Stage 4 — **Tier Mapping (Certification Threshold)**
The aggregate score is mapped onto a **tier** (e.g., Certified / Silver / Gold / Platinum).

### Stage 5 — **Third-Party Verification & Award**
An **independent assessor** (e.g., USGBC reviewer, GRIHA evaluator, BREEAM AP) audits documentation, conducts site visits, and confirms the score before the plaque is awarded.

---

## 2.2 Detailed Analysis of Major Rating Systems

### 2.2.1 LEED (Leadership in Energy and Environmental Design)

- **Origin:** 1998, United States Green Building Council (USGBC).
- **Versions:** LEED v4, LEED v4.1, LEED Zero (carbon/energy/water net-zero add-on).
- **Applicability:** New Construction (NC), Core & Shell (CS), Schools, Retail, Healthcare, Homes, Existing Buildings (O+M), Cities & Communities, Neighborhood Development (ND).
- **Scoring Scale:** **110 points** + 6 bonus points in Innovation (6) and Regional Priority (4).

#### LEED v4.1 — Credit Categories & Weightage

| # | Category | Max Points | KTU Memory Hook |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Location & Transportation (LT)** | 16 | *Where is the site?* |
| 2 | **Sustainable Sites (SS)** | 10 | *How is the site treated?* |
| 3 | **Water Efficiency (WE)** | 11 | *How much water is saved?* |
| 4 | **Energy & Atmosphere (EA)** | 33 | *Biggest scorer — energy.* |
| 5 | **Materials & Resources (MR)** | 13 | *Embodied carbon, recycled content.* |
| 6 | **Indoor Environmental Quality (EQ)** | 16 | *Light, air, comfort, acoustics.* |
| 7 | **Innovation (IN)** | 6 | *Excellence beyond the framework.* |
| 8 | **Regional Priority (RP)** | 4 | *Region-specific bonus.* |
| | **TOTAL** | **110 + 6 bonus** | |

#### LEED Certification Tiers

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Certified} &: \quad 40 \text{ – } 49 \text{ points} \\
\text{Silver} &: \quad 50 \text{ – } 59 \text{ points} \\
\text{Gold} &: \quad 60 \text{ – } 79 \text{ points} \\
\text{Platinum} &: \quad 80 \text{ – } 110 \text{ points}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### 2.2.2 BREEAM (Building Research Establishment Environmental Assessment Method)

- **Origin:** 1990, BRE Group, United Kingdom — *the world's first green rating system*.
- **Methodology:** **Weighted Scoring** — credits weighted by importance (e.g., Energy has 19% weight, Health & Wellbeing 15%).
- **Versions:** International New Construction, In-Use, Refurbishment & Fit-Out, Communities.
- **Stages:** Pre-Assessment → Design Stage → Post-Construction Stage → In-Use.

#### BREEAM Categories & Weightage (International New Construction)

| # | Category | Weight (%) | KTU Memory Hook |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Management | 12 | *Process & commissioning.* |
| 2 | Health & Wellbeing | 15 | *Occupant-centric.* |
| 3 | Energy | 19 | *Largest single weight.* |
| 4 | Transport | 8 | *Accessibility.* |
| 5 | Water | 6 | *Potable water use.* |
| 6 | Materials | 12.5 | *Responsible sourcing.* |
| 7 | Waste | 7.5 | *Construction & operational.* |
| 8 | Land Use & Ecology | 10 | *Greenfield vs brownfield.* |
| 9 | Pollution | 10 | *Air, water, light pollution.* |
| 10 | Innovation | 10 | *Bonus for exemplar performance.* |
| | **TOTAL** | **≈ 115%** *(Innovation is bonus)* | |

#### BREEAM Certification Tiers

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Pass} &: \quad \geq 30\% \\
\text{Good} &: \quad \geq 45\% \\
\text{Very Good} &: \quad \geq 55\% \\
\text{Excellent} &: \quad \geq 70\% \\
\text{Outstanding} &: \quad \geq 85\%
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### 2.2.3 GRIHA (Green Rating for Integrated Habitat Assessment)

- **Origin:** 2007, **The Energy and Resources Institute (TERI)** jointly with the **Ministry of New and Renewable Energy (MNRE)**, Government of India.
- **Versions:** GRIHA V.2015, GRIHA V.2019, GRIHA for Existing Buildings, GRIHA for Cities, GRIHA for Affordable Housing.
- **Scoring Scale:** **100 points** distributed across **34 criteria**.

#### GRIHA V.2019 — Criteria & Weightage

| # | Criterion | Max Points | Key Indicator |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Site Planning & Design | 7 | Heat-island reduction, topography respect. |
| 2 | Construction Management | 4 | Erosion, sedimentation control. |
| 3 | Energy (Operational + Embodied) | 31 | EUI, EPI in **kWh/m²/year**. |
| 4 | Water | 12 | Reduction over baseline **%**. |
| 5 | Materials | 8 | Local + regional materials, recycled content. |
| 6 | Waste Management | 7 | Construction & demolition waste diversion. |
| 7 | Occupant Comfort & Wellbeing | 9 | Daylight (DF), ventilation, thermal comfort. |
| 8 | Sustainable Building Management | 5 | BMS, O&M manuals. |
| 9 | Innovation & Design | 5 | Beyond-framework features. |
| 10 | Performance Audit (Verification) | 7 | Post-occupancy M&V. |
| 11 | Procurement & Green Products | 5 | Life-cycle cost analysis. |
| | **TOTAL** | **100** | |

#### GRIHA Certification Tiers

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{1 Star} &: \quad 50 \text{ – } 60 \text{ points} \\
\text{2 Stars} &: \quad 61 \text{ – } 70 \text{ points} \\
\text{3 Stars} &: \quad 71 \text{ – } 80 \text{ points} \\
\text{4 Stars} &: \quad 81 \text{ – } 90 \text{ points} \\
\text{5 Stars} &: \quad 91 \text{ – } 100 \text{ points}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** GRIHA is *India's own* rating system and is **mandatorily adopted** for all Central Government buildings of more than **2,500 sq.m. plinth area** as per the Government of India directive.

---

### 2.2.4 IGBC (Indian Green Building Council)

- **Origin:** 2001, **Confederation of Indian Industry (CII)**.
- **Versions:** IGBC Green New Buildings, IGBC Green Existing Buildings, IGBC Green Homes, IGBC Green Townships, IGBC Green Schools, IGBC Green Hospitals, IGBC Green SEZs.
- **Scoring Scale:** **100 points** across categories.

#### IGBC Green New Buildings — Categories

| # | Category | Max Points |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Sustainable Architecture & Design | 20 |
| 2 | Site Selection & Planning | 15 |
| 3 | Water Conservation | 18 |
| 4 | Energy Efficiency | 28 |
| 5 | Materials & Resources | 12 |
| 6 | Indoor Environmental Quality | 7 |
| | **TOTAL** | **100** |

#### IGBC Certification Tiers

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Certified} &: \quad 50 \text{ – } 59 \text{ points} \\
\text{Silver} &: \quad 60 \text{ – } 69 \text{ points} \\
\text{Gold} &: \quad 70 \text{ – } 79 \text{ points} \\
\text{Platinum} &: \quad 80 \text{ – } 100 \text{ points}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

### Table A: Key Energy & Water Performance Metrics (Used Across All 4 Systems)

| Metric | Formula | Units | KTU Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **EPI / EUI** | $\text{EPI} = \dfrac{\text{Annual Energy Consumption (kWh)}}{\text{Built-up Area (m}^{2}\text{)}}$ | **kWh/m²/year** | Energy credit scoring. |
| **WUI** | $\text{WUI} = \dfrac{\text{Annual Potable Water (litres)}}{\text{Built-up Area (m}^{2}\text{)}}$ | **L/m²/year** | Water credit scoring. |
| **Lighting Power Density (LPD)** | $\text{LPD} = \dfrac{\text{Total Lamp Wattage (W)}}{\text{Task Area (m}^{2}\text{)}}$ | **W/m²** | Lighting efficiency credit. |
| **Daylight Factor (DF)** | $\text{DF} = \dfrac{E_{\text{indoor}}}{E_{\text{outdoor}}} \times 100\%$ | **%** | Indoor EQ credit. |
| **COP (HVAC)** | $\text{COP} = \dfrac{Q_{\text{cooling (kW)}}}{W_{\text{input (kW)}}}$ | — | Equipment efficiency credit. |
| **Heat Island Reduction** | $\Delta T = T_{\text{surface}} - T_{\text{ambient}}$ | **°C** | Sustainable Sites credit. |
| **Embodied Energy (EE)** | $\text{EE} = \sum (M_i \times E_i)$ | **MJ/kg** | Material selection credit. |
| **U-value (Thermal Transmittance)** | $U = \dfrac{1}{R_{\text{total}}}$ | **W/m²·K** | Envelope performance. |
| **SDA (Spatial Daylight Autonomy)** | $\text{SDA} = \% \text{ of area receiving } \geq 300 \text{ lux for } \geq 50\% \text{ of hours}$ | **%** | LEED EQ v4.1. |

### Table B: Threshold Comparison Across Systems

| System | Total Points | Certified | Gold Tier | Top Tier |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| LEED v4.1 | 110 | ≥ 40 | ≥ 60 (Gold) | ≥ 80 (Platinum) |
| BREEAM Int. | ~110 weighted | ≥ 30% | ≥ 55% (VG) | ≥ 85% (Outstanding) |
| GRIHA V.2019 | 100 | ≥ 50 (1★) | ≥ 71 (3★) | ≥ 91 (5★) |
| IGBC New Buildings | 100 | ≥ 50 | ≥ 70 (Gold) | ≥ 80 (Platinum) |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Use Case |
|---|---|
| **Municipal Building Permission** | Some Indian states offer **TDR (Transferable Development Rights)** bonus for IGBC/GRIHA-certified projects. |
| **Tax & Excise Benefits** | Accelerated depreciation (40%) on solar equipment in rated buildings under IT Act 80-IA. |
| **Green Bond Financing** | LEED/GRIHA Platinum projects access cheaper **green finance** at lower interest rates. |
| **Property Valuation** | Rated buildings command a **7–12% rental premium** and **~16% higher sale value**. |
| **ECBC Compliance** | LEED EA Prerequisite 2 = compliance with **ECBC/ASHRAE 90.1**. |
| **Net-Zero Design** | LEED Zero, BREEAM Outstanding, and GRIHA 5★ align with **Panchamrit Net-Zero targets**. |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example — Energy Performance Index (EPI) Calculation

This is the **most frequently asked calculation** in KTU Part B questions on Green Rating Guidelines.

### **Problem Statement (KTU Standard):**

A commercial office building in Kochi has the following annual energy consumption data:
- Lighting: **85,000 kWh/year**
- HVAC (Chiller + AHU): **220,000 kWh/year**
- Plug Loads (computers, servers): **95,000 kWh/year**
- Lifts, Pumps & Misc.: **35,000 kWh/year**
- Total Built-up Area: **2,500 m²**

Calculate the **Energy Performance Index (EPI)** and determine the **LEED v4.1 EA Credit 1** achievement level assuming ASHRAE 90.1-2007 baseline = **120 kWh/m²/year**.

---

### **Step-by-Step Derivation**

**Step 1 — Total annual energy consumption**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{\text{total}} &= E_{\text{light}} + E_{\text{HVAC}} + E_{\text{plug}} + E_{\text{misc}} \\
&= 85{,}000 + 220{,}000 + 95{,}000 + 35{,}000 \\
&= 435{,}000 \text{ kWh/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic: Summation is linear and additive since all loads are metered at the same distribution board.*

**Step 2 — Apply the EPI formula**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{EPI} &= \dfrac{E_{\text{total}}}{\text{Built-up Area}} \\
&= \dfrac{435{,}000 \text{ kWh/yr}}{2{,}500 \text{ m}^{2}} \\
&= 174 \text{ kWh/m}^{2}\text{/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic: The unit kWh/m²/year is universally used across all 4 rating systems.*

**Step 3 — Compare with ASHRAE 90.1-2007 baseline**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Baseline (EPI}_{b}) &= 120 \text{ kWh/m}^{2}\text{/year} \\
\text{Designed (EPI}_{d}) &= 174 \text{ kWh/m}^{2}\text{/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic: 174 > 120 — the building is performing **worse** than baseline.*

**Step 4 — Compute Percentage Improvement (Negative)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\% \text{ Improvement} &= \dfrac{\text{EPI}_{d} - \text{EPI}_{b}}{\text{EPI}_{b}} \times 100 \\
&= \dfrac{174 - 120}{120} \times 100 \\
&= \dfrac{54}{120} \times 100 \\
&= +45\%
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic: Positive sign indicates the building uses **45% MORE energy** than the baseline. To earn LEED EA Credit 1, the design must use **less** than the baseline.*

**Step 5 — LEED EA Credit 1 Point Mapping (for New Construction)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Points} &= 
\begin{cases}
1 & \text{if } 6\% \leq \text{Improvement} \leq 9\% \\
2 & \text{if } 10\% \leq \text{Improvement} \leq 14\% \\
3 & \text{if } 15\% \leq \text{Improvement} \leq 19\% \\
4 & \text{if } 20\% \leq \text{Improvement} \leq 24\% \\
5 & \text{if } 25\% \leq \text{Improvement} \leq 29\% \\
6 & \text{if } 30\% \leq \text{Improvement} \leq 34\% \\
7 & \text{if } 35\% \leq \text{Improvement} \leq 39\% \\
8 & \text{if } 40\% \leq \text{Improvement} \leq 44\% \\
9 & \text{if } 45\% \leq \text{Improvement} \leq 49\% \\
10 & \text{if } 50\% \leq \text{Improvement} \leq 54\% \\
11 & \text{if } 55\% \leq \text{Improvement} \leq 59\% \\
12 & \text{if } 60\% \leq \text{Improvement} \leq 64\% \\
13 & \text{if } 65\% \leq \text{Improvement} \leq 69\% \\
14 & \text{if } 70\% \leq \text{Improvement} \leq 74\% \\
15 & \text{if } 75\% \leq \text{Improvement} \leq 79\% \\
16 & \text{if } 80\% \leq \text{Improvement} \leq 84\% \\
17 & \text{if } 85\% \leq \text{Improvement} \leq 89\% \\
18 & \text{Improvement} \geq 90\%
\end{cases}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic: This is the official LEED v4.1 EA Credit 1 lookup table — memorize the breakpoints for KTU.*

**Step 6 — Conclusion for the Problem**

> The current design uses **45% more energy** than baseline → **Fails** to meet even the basic EA Prerequisite. The architect must revise HVAC efficiency (raise COP to 4.0+), increase envelope U-value compliance, and reduce LPD below ECBC limits.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example — Water Use Reduction (LEED v4.1 WE Credit)

**Problem:** A 50-occupant office building has an annual water consumption of **4,500 m³**. Baseline water use (LEED default for office) = **25 L/person/day** (excluding irrigation).

**Step 1 — Calculate baseline annual water**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{\text{baseline}} &= N \times q_{\text{baseline}} \times 365 \\
&= 50 \times 25 \times 365 \\
&= 456{,}250 \text{ L/year} \\
&= 456.25 \text{ m}^{3}\text{/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate reduction percentage**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\% \text{ Reduction} &= \dfrac{W_{\text{baseline}} - W_{\text{design}}}{W_{\text{baseline}}} \times 100 \\
&= \dfrac{456.25 - 4500}{456.25} \times 100 \quad \text{(Hmm — design > baseline)}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Recheck: 4,500 m³ = 4,500,000 L. This vastly exceeds baseline 456 m³ → the building is **wasting** water.*

**Step 3 — Engineer corrective fixtures**

Low-flow fixtures (dual-flush WC, sensor taps, aerators) can reduce indoor water use by **40–50%**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{\text{design,revised}} &= 456.25 \times (1 - 0.45) = 250.94 \text{ m}^{3}\text{/year} \\
\% \text{ Reduction} &= 45\% \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{4 points (LEED WE Credit 1)}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Cross-System Comparison

### Table C: Side-by-Side Comparison of 4 Major Rating Systems

| Parameter | LEED v4.1 | BREEAM Int. NC | GRIHA V.2019 | IGBC GNB |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Origin Year** | 1998 (US) | 1990 (UK) | 2007 (India) | 2001 (India) |
| **Governing Body** | USGBC | BRE Group | TERI + MNRE | CII |
| **Total Points** | 110 | ~110 weighted | 100 | 100 |
| **Categories** | 7 + 1 bonus | 9 + 1 bonus | 11 | 6 |
| **Energy Weightage** | 33 pts (30%) | 19% | 31 pts (31%) | 28 pts (28%) |
| **Water Weightage** | 11 pts (10%) | 6% | 12 pts (12%) | 18 pts (18%) |
| **Innovation Credit** | 6 pts | 10% | 5 pts | (in Architecture) |
| **Cert. Levels** | 4 (C, S, G, P) | 5 (Pass to Outstanding) | 5 (★ to ★★★★★) | 4 (C, S, G, P) |
| **Top Tier Threshold** | 80/110 ≈ 73% | ≥ 85% | ≥ 91/100 | ≥ 80/100 |
| **Mandatory for India** | No | No | Central Govt > 2500 m² | No |
| **Code Baseline** | ASHRAE 90.1 | UK BR Part L | NBC 2016 + ECBC | ECBC |
| **Verification** | Online + GBCI audit | Site + online audit | Site visit + 12-mo M&V | Site + audit |

---

### Table D: KTU-Mandated Compliance Mapping

| Indian Regulation | Connected GBRS Credit | KTU Exam Tip |
|---|---|---|
| ECBC 2017 | LEED EA Prerequisite 2 | *State as a prerequisite, not a credit.* |
| National Building Code 2016 | GRIHA Criterion 1 | *NBC is the universal Indian baseline.* |
| IS 3362 (Thermal Insulation) | BREEAM Energy | *Cite Indian Standards (BIS) where possible.* |
| IGBC Green Homes | IGBC Healthcare cross-credits | *IGBC has 18+ rating variants.* |
| GRIHA for Existing Buildings | Renovation projects | *Apply GRIHA-EB for retrofit work.* |

---

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Operational Flow of a Green Rating Certification

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Project Inception and Site Selection] --> B[Feasibility Study and Code Baseline Check]
    B --> C[Choosing Appropriate GBRS: LEED / BREEAM / GRIHA / IGBC]
    C --> D[Design Stage: Integrating Credits and Prerequisites]
    D --> E[Pre-Assessment Submission and Registration]
    E --> F[Detailed Design with Energy Modeling: eQUEST / EnergyPlus]
    F --> G[Construction Phase: Documentation and Material Tracking]
    G --> H[Third Party Audit: Site Visit and Document Review]
    H --> I{All Prerequisites Met?}
    I -- No --> J[Rectification and Resubmission]
    J --> H
    I -- Yes --> K[Point Aggregation and Tier Mapping]
    K --> L[Certification Award: Plaque and Registry Listing]
    L --> M[Post Occupancy Performance Verification: M and V]
    M --> N[Annual Reporting and Recertification if Needed]
    N --> O[End of Life: Deconstruction and Material Reuse]
```

*This flowchart maps the **lifecycle workflow** of any green-rated project. Note the **iterative loop** between design, audit, and rectification — a single failed prerequisite blocks the entire certification.*

---

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Root[GRIHA V.2019: 100 Points]

    Root --> C1[Site Planning and Design: 7 pts]
    Root --> C2[Construction Management: 4 pts]

    Root --> C4[Water: 12 pts]
    Root --> C5[Materials: 8 pts]
    Root --> C6[Waste Management: 7 pts]
    Root --> C7[Occupant Comfort: 9 pts]
    Root --> C8[Sustainable Bldg Mgmt: 5 pts]
    Root --> C9[Innovation: 5 pts]
    Root --> C10[Performance Audit: 7 pts]
    Root --> C11[Procurement: 5 pts]

    C3 --> C3a[Energy Performance: 18 pts]
    C3 --> C3b[Renewable Energy: 6 pts]
    C3 --> C3c[Embodied Energy: 7 pts]

    C4 --> C4a[Water Use Reduction: 6 pts]
    C4 --> C4b[Rainwater Harvesting: 4 pts]
    C4 --> C4c[Recycled Water: 2 pts]
```

*This diagram shows the **hierarchical tree** of GRIHA's 100-point distribution — useful for KTU questions that ask students to "break down the credit structure of a chosen GBRS."*

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Comparison Matrix: Four Rating Systems Side-by-Side

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    LEED[LEED v4.1]
    BREEAM[BREEAM International]
    GRIHA[GRIHA V.2019]
    IGBC[IGBC GNB]

    LEED -- Energy 33pts --> E1[EA Category]
    BREEAM -- Energy 19 percent --> E2[Energy Category]
    GRIHA -- Energy 31pts --> E3[Energy Criterion]
    IGBC -- Energy 28pts --> E4[Energy Efficiency]

    LEED -- Top Tier --> T1[80 to 110 Platinum]
    BREEAM -- Top Tier --> T2[85 percent Outstanding]
    GRIHA -- Top Tier --> T3[91 to 100 Five Star]
    IGBC -- Top Tier --> T4[80 to 100 Platinum]
```

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Decision Tree: Selecting the Right Rating System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Start: Green Rating Selection] --> Q1{Is the project in India?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Is it a Central Govt. building > 2500 m²?}
    Q1 -- No --> Q3{Is the project in the UK or EU?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> Path1[Use GRIHA V.2019 Mandatory]
    Q2 -- No --> Q4{Is it a residence or commercial?}
    Q4 -- Residence --> Path2[Use IGBC Green Homes]
    Q4 -- Commercial --> Path3[Use IGBC Green New Buildings]
    Q3 -- Yes --> Path4[Use BREEAM International NC]
    Q3 -- No --> Q5{North American project?}
    Q5 -- Yes --> Path5[Use LEED v4.1]
    Q5 -- No --> Path6[Use BREEAM International as default]
```

*This is a **practical decision-support flow** that KTU students can replicate in design studio projects.*
