# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### **Q1. Define a Green Building Rating System. List the four major rating systems used globally.** [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A Green Building Rating System (GBRS) is a structured, third-party verified, voluntary framework that evaluates, scores, and certifies the environmental, energy, water, material, and occupant-wellbeing performance of a building across its life cycle.

The four major global rating systems are:

| # | System | Origin |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **LEED** (Leadership in Energy and Environmental Design) | United States, USGBC, 1998 |
| 2 | **BREEAM** (Building Research Establishment Environmental Assessment Method) | United Kingdom, BRE, 1990 |
| 3 | **GRIHA** (Green Rating for Integrated Habitat Assessment) | India, TERI + MNRE, 2007 |
| 4 | **IGBC** (Indian Green Building Council) | India, CII, 2001 |

*(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for naming, 1 mark for correct attribution.)*

---

### **Q2. Differentiate between a "Prerequisite" and a "Credit" in LEED v4.1 with one example each.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Prerequisite (P) | Credit (C) |
|---|---|---|
| **Mandatory?** | Yes — must be complied with. | No — optional. |
| **Points earned?** | Zero points. | 1 to 18 points each. |
| **Purpose** | Establishes a **minimum baseline** of sustainable practice. | Rewards **performance beyond** the baseline. |
| **Example (LEED EA)** | **EA Prerequisite 2: Minimum Energy Performance** (must meet ASHRAE 90.1 baseline). | **EA Credit 1: Optimize Energy Performance** (earn up to 18 points for additional savings). |
| **Penalty** | If unmet → **whole certification rejected**. | If unmet → no points lost beyond that credit. |

*(2 marks for tabular difference, 1 mark for correct example.)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (Internal Choice, 1 × 14 = 14 Marks)

### **Question A: LEED v4.1 + Energy Calculation (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Model 1]

**Q. (a)** List the seven credit categories of **LEED v4.1** with their maximum point allocation. Explain the **Energy & Atmosphere (EA)** category in detail, listing any three credits with their point values. **[7 Marks]**

**Q. (b)** A proposed 3,000 m² hospital building in Thrissur has the following annual energy data:
- HVAC: 350,000 kWh
- Lighting: 95,000 kWh
- Medical Equipment: 180,000 kWh
- Hot Water + Sterilization: 45,000 kWh
- Lifts, Pumps, Misc.: 30,000 kWh

**ASHRAE 90.1-2007 baseline EPI = 150 kWh/m²/year.**

Calculate the **EPI** and determine the **LEED EA Credit 1 points** the design will earn. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### **Model Solution**

### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**LEED v4.1 — Seven Credit Categories with Maximum Points**

| # | Category | Abbreviation | Max Points |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Location & Transportation | LT | 16 |
| 2 | Sustainable Sites | SS | 10 |
| 3 | Water Efficiency | WE | 11 |
| 4 | **Energy & Atmosphere** | **EA** | **33** |
| 5 | Materials & Resources | MR | 13 |
| 6 | Indoor Environmental Quality | EQ | 16 |
| 7 | Innovation | IN | 6 |
| 8 | Regional Priority | RP | 4 (bonus) |
| | **Total** | | **110 + 6 bonus** |

*(2 marks — list with correct totals.)*

**Energy & Atmosphere (EA) Category — 3 Selected Credits**

| Credit | Description | Max Points |
|---|---|---|
| **EA Prerequisite 1: Fundamental Commissioning & Verification** | Engage CxA from design stage; verify HVAC systems perform to design intent. | Mandatory (0) |
| **EA Prerequisite 2: Minimum Energy Performance** | Building must meet ASHRAE 90.1-2007 baseline. | Mandatory (0) |
| **EA Credit 1: Optimize Energy Performance** | Earn 1–18 points based on % improvement over baseline. | 18 |
| **EA Credit 2: Demand Response** | Participate in a demand-response program. | 2 |
| **EA Credit 4: Renewable Energy** | On-site or off-site renewable energy generation. | 5 |
| **EA Credit 6: Enhanced Commissioning** | Independent CxA for monitoring-based commissioning. | 2 |

*(3 marks — three credits with description and points.)*

**Conclusion:** EA is the **highest-weighted** category in LEED because operational energy is the single largest contributor to a building's lifetime carbon footprint.

*(1 mark — conclusion / critical observation.)*

---

### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Total annual energy**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{\text{total}} &= 350{,}000 + 95{,}000 + 180{,}000 + 45{,}000 + 30{,}000 \\
&= 700{,}000 \text{ kWh/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

*(1 mark for summation.)*

**Step 2 — EPI**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{EPI}_{d} &= \dfrac{700{,}000}{3{,}000} \\
&= 233.33 \text{ kWh/m}^{2}\text{/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

*(1 mark for EPI formula and substitution.)*

**Step 3 — Percentage Improvement (Note: Medical equipment inflates EPI)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta\% &= \dfrac{233.33 - 150}{150} \times 100 \\
&= \dfrac{83.33}{150} \times 100 \\
&= +55.55\% \quad \text{(i.e., uses 55.55% more than baseline)}
\end{aligned}
$$

*(1 mark for percentage formula.)*

**Step 4 — Engineering Justification**

> *Note for KTU:* Hospitals are **energy-intensive** by nature (sterilization, OT HVAC class, 24×7 lighting). The 150 kWh/m²/year ASHRAE baseline is for commercial buildings, not healthcare. Under **LEED v4.1 Healthcare**, the baseline shifts to ~190–220 kWh/m²/year.

**Adjusted baseline for hospital = 220 kWh/m²/year:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta\%_{\text{adj}} &= \dfrac{233.33 - 220}{220} \times 100 \\
&= +6.06\%
\end{aligned}
$$

*Even with adjusted hospital baseline, the building still uses ~6% more energy — only 1 LEED point is earned (since 6% ≤ 9% threshold).*

*(2 marks for adjustment and credit mapping.)*

**Step 5 — Recommendations**

1. Replace fluorescent OT lighting with **LED** (saves 30%).
2. Install **VFDs on AHU motors** (saves 15–20%).
3. Adopt **solar PV rooftop 100 kWp** (offsets 1.5 lakh kWh/year → reduces EPI to ~183 kWh/m²/year → 16.8% saving → **3 LEED points**).

*(1 mark for any one practical recommendation.)*

---

### **Question B: GRIHA + GRIHA-India Context (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Model 2 — Alternative Choice]

**Q. (a)** Explain the **GRIHA rating system** in detail. List all 11 criteria with their point distribution. State the certification tiers and the minimum number of stars required for a Central Government building in India. **[7 Marks]**

**Q. (b)** A 5,000 m² school in Thiruvananthapuram adopts GRIHA. The total annual energy consumption is **600,000 kWh**. The GRIHA baseline for a school in tropical Kerala is **140 kWh/m²/year**. Calculate:
- (i) The Energy Performance Index (EPI).
- (ii) The percentage improvement over baseline.
- (iii) The GRIHA Energy Criterion (max 31 points) points awarded, given:
  - ≥ 50% saving = 31 pts
  - 40–49% saving = 26 pts
  - 30–39% saving = 22 pts
  - 20–29% saving = 18 pts
  - 10–19% saving = 12 pts
  - < 10% saving = 6 pts. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### **Model Solution**

### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**GRIHA — Full Form, Origin, Scope (3 Marks):**

- **Full Form:** **G**reen **R**ating for **I**ntegrated **H**abitat **A**ssessment.
- **Developed by:** **TERI (The Energy and Resources Institute)** in collaboration with the **Ministry of New and Renewable Energy (MNRE), Government of India**, in 2007.
- **Latest version:** **GRIHA V.2019** (100 points, 11 criteria).
- **Mandatory status:** All **Central Government buildings > 2,500 m² plinth area** must be GRIHA-rated (Office Memorandum dated 2007, reinforced 2014).
- **Variants:** GRIHA for Existing Buildings, Affordable Housing, Villages, Cities, MSME, Industrial.

**11 Criteria with Points (3 Marks):**

| # | Criterion | Points |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Site Planning & Design | 7 |
| 2 | Construction Management | 4 |
| 3 | Energy (Operational + Embodied) | 31 |
| 4 | Water | 12 |
| 5 | Materials | 8 |
| 6 | Waste Management | 7 |
| 7 | Occupant Comfort & Wellbeing | 9 |
| 8 | Sustainable Building Management | 5 |
| 9 | Innovation & Design | 5 |
| 10 | Performance Audit & Verification | 7 |
| 11 | Procurement & Green Products | 5 |
| | **TOTAL** | **100** |

**Certification Tiers + Government Mandate (1 Mark):**

| Tier | Points | Mandatory for Central Govt? |
|---|---|---|
| ★ (1-Star) | 50–60 | — |
| ★★ (2-Star) | 61–70 | — |
| ★★★ (3-Star) | 71–80 | **Minimum mandatory** (per current MNRE directive) |
| ★★★★ (4-Star) | 81–90 | — |
| ★★★★★ (5-Star) | 91–100 | — |

*Note: Some recent directives have raised the mandate to 4-Star for new central projects.*

---

### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — EPI Calculation (2 Marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{EPI} &= \dfrac{600{,}000 \text{ kWh/yr}}{5{,}000 \text{ m}^{2}} \\
&= 120 \text{ kWh/m}^{2}\text{/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — % Improvement (2 Marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\% \text{ Saving} &= \dfrac{140 - 120}{140} \times 100 \\
&= \dfrac{20}{140} \times 100 \\
&= 14.29\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — GRIHA Energy Criterion Points (2 Marks)**

> 14.29% falls in the **10–19% band → 12 points** out of 31.

**Step 4 — Engineering Interpretation (1 Mark)**

The 14.29% saving is a **moderate achievement**. To escalate to 22 points (30–39% band), the school should:
1. Replace CFL T5 tube lights with **LED** (additional 5–8% saving).
2. Install **rooftop solar 75 kWp** (additional 6–7% saving).
3. Adopt **natural ventilation design + daylighting** to reduce HVAC daytime loads (5–6% saving).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusing EPI with EUI** — both have units kWh/m²/year, but EUI includes all fuels while EPI may exclude renewables.
> 2. **Forgetting prerequisites** — students list credits but forget to mention that ALL prerequisites must be cleared **before** any point scoring begins.
> 3. **Mixing GRIHA and IGBC** — students erroneously say "GRIHA was developed by CII" (it was TERI + MNRE; CII developed IGBC).
> 4. **Not stating units** in energy/water calculations — "174" without kWh/m²/year loses 0.5 marks.
> 5. **Wrong threshold mapping** — students say 50% saving earns 30 points in GRIHA, but GRIHA caps Energy at 31 and uses 50% for the *full* 31.
> 6. **Treating LEED, BREEAM, GRIHA as identical** — BREEAM uses *weighted percentage*, not absolute points.
> 7. **Forgetting the 12-month Performance Audit** in GRIHA — this is a unique mandatory post-occupancy verification step (Criterion 10).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Green Building Rating Guidelines**

- **LEED** = USGBC, 1998, 110 pts + 6 bonus, Platinum at ≥ 80.
- **BREEAM** = BRE, 1990, weighted % scores, Outstanding at ≥ 85%.
- **GRIHA** = TERI + MNRE, 2007, 100 pts, 5-star at ≥ 91, **mandatory for Central Govt > 2500 m²**.
- **IGBC** = CII, 2001, 100 pts, Platinum at ≥ 80.
- **Prerequisite (P)** = mandatory, no points; **Credit (C)** = optional, earns points.
- **EPI (kWh/m²/year)** = Total Energy / Built-up Area.
- **WUI (L/m²/day)** = Total Water / (Occupants × Days).
- **LPD (W/m²)** = Lamp Wattage / Task Area.
- **Daylight Factor (DF)** = (Indoor Lux / Outdoor Lux) × 100%.
- **COP of HVAC** ≥ 3.5 for good rating; ≥ 4.5 for exemplary.
- **SDA ≥ 55%** in regularly occupied space → LEED EQ Credit 7.
- **BREEAM Innovation credits** = up to 10% (exemplary performance).
- **LEED v4.1 Regional Priority** = 4 bonus points for regional credits (e.g., heat island in India).
- **GRIHA Criterion 10** = 7 points for **12-month post-occupancy M&V** — unique to India.
- **Mandatory minimum for new GRIHA-rated projects** = 3-Star (or 4-Star in recent directives).
- **All four systems** use **third-party verification** (USGBC/GBCI, BRE, TERI, CII-GBC).
- **Code baselines:** ASHRAE 90.1 (LEED), UK Part L (BREEAM), ECBC + NBC 2016 (GRIHA), ECBC (IGBC).
- **Net-Zero add-ons:** LEED Zero, BREEAM Net Zero, GRIHA Net-Zero, IGBC Net Zero.
- **Common Indian Standards (BIS):** IS 3362 (Thermal Insulation), IS 3792 (HVAC), IS 2064 (Daylighting), IS 1172 (Water Supply).
- **Quick formula memory hook for EPI:** "Energy per square meter, per year — kWh / m²·year."

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET415 - ARCHITECTURAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Case Studies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 📘 MODULE 4 — CASE STUDIES IN ARCHITECTURAL ENGINEERING

## 1.1 What is a "Case Study" in Architectural Engineering?

> [!NOTE]
> **Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
> A *Case Study* in Architectural Engineering is an empirical, in-depth, and systematic investigation of a real-world built project wherein the architectural intent, structural logic, material behaviour, construction methodology, services integration, and contextual response are critically analysed to extract transferable design and engineering principles.

In simpler terms, it is the architectural engineer's equivalent of a "post-mortem" — but instead of finding faults, we extract **transferable wisdom** from buildings that have already been conceived, designed, and constructed. It is the bridge between theoretical knowledge (covered in Modules 1–3) and the messy, beautiful reality of the built environment.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Medical Case File" Approach

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuition:**
> Imagine a medical student studying a *case file* of a patient. The student does not just memorise the disease — they study the patient's history, symptoms, diagnosis, treatment, and recovery. Each case sharpens clinical judgment. Similarly, an architectural engineering case study is a *patient file* for a building. We study its "genetics" (site, brief, client), its "anatomy" (structure, envelope, services), its "physiology" (how it performs over time), and its "pathology" (what failed, what succeeded). A handful of well-studied case files builds a doctor's intuition — and the same is true for an architectural engineer.

### 1.3 Why Case Studies Matter in KTU's NEP-2020 Curriculum

| Pedagogical Reason | What it Trains |
|---|---|
| **Bridging Theory ↔ Practice** | Translates abstract concepts (loads, materials, forms) into real spatial outcomes |
| **Develops Critical Thinking** | Students must *judge* design decisions, not memorise them |
| **Builds Design Vocabulary** | Exposes students to typologies, scales, and construction logics |
| **Outcome-Based Learning (OBE)** | Direct mapping to **CO4 & CO5** — analyse and evaluate real architectural-engineering projects |
| **Industry Readiness** | Mirrors the way consultants actually *learn* on the job |

### 1.4 KTU Classification of Architectural Engineering Case Studies

Under the 2024 Scheme, case studies are broadly classified into **five typological clusters**:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Five Standard Case-Study Clusters:**
> 1. **Typological Case Studies** — buildings sharing a function (e.g., museums, airports, stadiums).
> 2. **Structural/Constructional Case Studies** — buildings celebrated for *how* they stand up (e.g., long-span roofs, shells, high-rises).
> 3. **Material-Innovation Case Studies** — buildings pushing the limits of a single material (e.g., reinforced concrete cathedrals, timber towers, ETFE cushions).
> 4. **Contextual/Responsive Case Studies** — buildings deeply tied to climate, culture, or site (e.g., desert vernacular, tropical modernism, seismic zones).
> 5. **Sustainability/Performance Case Studies** — buildings engineered for net-zero energy, water, or carbon targets.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 📘 SECTION 2 — THEORETICAL ANALYSIS FRAMEWORK & FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Architectural Engineering Case Study Methodology (AECSM)

A *robust* case study does not ramble. At KTU level, every case study is expected to be analysed through a **structured 7-lens framework**. Each lens corresponds to a specific *Module* of the syllabus, ensuring cross-module integration.

> [!NOTE]
> **The 7-Lens Analytical Matrix (AECSM):**
> 1. **Lens-1: Brief & Context** — Who, what, where, why? (Project genesis, client intent, site, climate.)
> 2. **Lens-2: Form & Geometry** — Why this shape? (Plan, section, massing, proportion.)
> 3. **Lens-3: Structure & Load Path** — How does it stand? (Frames, shells, trusses, load path continuity.)
> 4. **Lens-4: Materials & Construction** — How was it built? (Material logic, construction sequence, jointing.)
> 5. **Lens-5: Services & Performance** — How does it perform? (HVAC, lighting, acoustics, energy.)
> 6. **Lens-6: Contextual & Cultural Response** — How does it *resonate*? (Climate, culture, urbanism, symbolism.)
> 7. **Lens-7: Critical Evaluation** — What worked, what didn't, and what can *we* learn?

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Lens — KTU Logic

- **Lens-1** is the *DNA* of any project. Without the brief, the building is meaningless.
- **Lens-2** proves that *form is never arbitrary*; it follows structure, light, or function.
- **Lens-3** is the *engineer's heart* — every architectural gesture must be validated structurally.
- **Lens-4** is where *physics meets craft* — material behaviour dictates the construction logic.
- **Lens-5** is the *invisible architecture* — services decide whether the building actually *works* for users.
- **Lens-6** is the *soul* — a building that ignores its context fails as architecture.
- **Lens-7** is the *exam-room skill* — KTU specifically tests critical evaluation, not just description.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet for Case Study Analysis

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following formulas are *not* about a single building. They are the **analytical tools** you carry into *any* case study to extract numbers from a building — exactly what KTU expects in 14-mark questions.

### Table 2.1 — Core Analytical Equations for Architectural Case Studies

| # | Parameter | Formula | Variables & Units | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Floor Area Ratio (FAR / FSI)** | $\text{FAR} = \dfrac{\text{Total Built-up Area}}{\text{Plot Area}}$ | Ratio (dimensionless) | Measures *building density* on land; context for high-rise case studies |
| 2 | **Ground Coverage Ratio (GCR)** | $\text{GCR} = \dfrac{\text{Ground Floor Covered Area}}{\text{Plot Area}} \times 100\%$ | Percentage | Reveals how much of the site is "touched" by the building |
| 3 | **Aspect Ratio of a Slab** | $\text{AR} = \dfrac{L_y}{L_x} \leq 2$ | $L_x$ = short span, $L_y$ = long span | Decides if the slab is one-way or two-way — vital for floor-system case studies |
| 4 | **Effective Slab Thickness (deflection control)** | $h_{\min} \geq \dfrac{L}{20} \text{ (simply supported)}$ | $L$ = span in mm, $h$ in mm | IS 456 thumb-rule to *verify* a case study's slab depth |
| 5 | **Column Load Estimate (tributary area)** | $P \approx q \times A_{\text{trib}} \times n$ | $q$ = load intensity (kN/m²), $A_{\text{trib}}$ (m²), $n$ = floors | Quick *sanity check* for column sizes in case studies |
| 6 | **Building Height vs Footprint** | $H \leq 1.5\sqrt{A_f}$ | $H$ = height (m), $A_f$ = footprint (m²) | Stiffness rule-of-thumb for *slenderness* — used in tall building case studies |
| 7 | **Window-to-Wall Ratio (WWR)** | $\text{WWR} = \dfrac{A_{\text{window}}}{A_{\text{wall}}}$ | Dimensionless | Drives daylighting and cooling load analysis |
| 8 | **Daylight Factor (DF)** | $\text{DF} = \dfrac{E_{\text{indoor}}}{E_{\text{outdoor}}} \times 100\%$ | Percentage (CIE standard: 1.5–5 %) | Benchmarks how well-lit a case study's interior is |
| 9 | **U-value of a Composite Wall** | $\dfrac{1}{U} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{t_i}{k_i} + R_{si} + R_{se}$ | $t_i$ = thickness, $k_i$ = thermal conductivity (W/m·K), $R$ = surface resistance | Validates a case study's *thermal envelope* |
| 10 | **Annual Energy Use Intensity (EUI)** | $\text{EUI} = \dfrac{E_{\text{annual}}}{A_{\text{conditioned}}}$ | kWh/m²/year | Compares buildings against benchmarks (e.g., ECBC, LEED) |
| 11 | **Cost per m²** | $C_m = \dfrac{\text{Total Project Cost}}{\text{Total Carpet Area}}$ | ₹/m² or $/m² | Cross-checks a case study's *economy of construction* |
| 12 | **Structural Efficiency Index (SEI)** | $\text{SEI} = \dfrac{A_{\text{usable}}}{V_{\text{enclosed}}}$ | m$^{-1}$ (higher = more efficient) | Used for long-span case studies (stadiums, airports) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Markdown Pipe-Safety Rule:** All vertical bars used as mathematical operators (e.g., absolute value $\vert x \vert$) have been replaced with `\vert` to prevent breaking the table syntax — this is the KTU board examiner's sanity check.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility of the AECSM Framework

In industry, this framework is the **bedrock of design post-occupancy evaluations (POE)** used by firms like *HOK, Arup, Foster + Partners, and CBRE*. A consultant who can walk through a completed building and *quantify* its success on each of the 7 lenses is a *trusted advisor* — not just a designer. KTU trains you to be that consultant.

# 📘 SECTION 3 — STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 KTU-Mandated Case Study: The Sydney Opera House (Jørn Utzon, 1973)

This is the **canonical case study** in every Architectural Engineering syllabus because it touches *every* lens of the AECSM.

### 3.1.1 Lens-1: Brief & Context

- **Client:** New South Wales Government (1956 competition brief).
- **Site:** Bennelong Point, Sydney — a peninsula jutting into the harbour.
- **Climate:** Temperate oceanic, strong solar gain from the north-east, salt-laden marine air.
- **Original Brief:** A multi-venue performing arts centre to *elevate* Sydney's cultural stature.

### 3.1.2 Lens-2: Form & Geometry — The "Spherical Solution"

Utzon's original competition entry proposed **free-form shells** — impossible to engineer. The breakthrough came from **Ove Arup's** team: the "shells" are not shells at all but **segments cut from a single sphere of radius $R = 75 \text{ m}$**.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Spherical Solution" Derivation:**
> Every roof "shell" of the Opera House is a portion of a sphere. A sphere of radius $R$ when sliced yields a *circular* boundary in plan. By using the *same* sphere for all roofs, every segment shares a *common geometric origin* — this is what allowed a single set of formwork (made from thousands of pre-cast ribs) to be reused.

If the radius of the sphere is $R$ and the chord (span) across the shell base is $L$, then the *rise* (sagitta) of each shell is:

$$h = R - \sqrt{R^{2} - \left(\dfrac{L}{2}\right)^{2}}$$

For the **largest shell** of the Opera House, $L = 75 \text{ m}$ and $R = 75 \text{ m}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
h &= 75 - \sqrt{75^{2} - \left(\dfrac{75}{2}\right)^{2}} \\[4pt]
&= 75 - \sqrt{5625 - 1406.25} \\[4pt]
&= 75 - \sqrt{4218.75} \\[4pt]
&= 75 - 64.95 \\[4pt]
&\approx 10.05 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

This **10.05 m rise** matches the actual constructed value — confirming the spherical solution.

### 3.1.3 Lens-3: Structure & Load Path

Each "shell" is actually a **rigid rib system**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Shell self-weight} &= \gamma_c \cdot V_{\text{shell}} \\
V_{\text{shell}} &= \text{Surface area of spherical segment} \times \text{average thickness} \\
A_{\text{segment}} &= 2\pi R h = 2\pi \times 75 \times 10.05 \\
A_{\text{segment}} &= 4736.5 \text{ m}^{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

With an average concrete thickness of **1.2 m** (varying from 1.07 m at the leading edge to 2.0 m at the valley):

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{\text{shell}} &= 4736.5 \times 1.2 = 5683.8 \text{ m}^{3} \\
\text{Self-weight} &= 25 \text{ kN/m}^{3} \times 5683.8 = 142{,}095 \text{ kN} \approx 142 \text{ MN}
\end{aligned}
$$

This **~142 MN** load is funnelled through **pre-cast concrete ribs** down to **in-situ concrete columns** that rise out of the podium. Arup's genius was the **pre-cast rib "ball-joint"** — a cast-steel node that locks 2 to 4 ribs together, transferring forces in pure compression and tension.

### 3.1.4 Lens-4: Materials & Construction

- **Material:** Pre-cast reinforced concrete (in 1959, a daring choice for marine exposure).
- **Construction innovation:** The **6000+ ribs** were cast on-site using *electrically heated oil-filled moulds* — a world-first.
- **Lid:** The ribs are clad with **pre-cast concrete panels** in a chevron tile pattern — these are not decorative; they are *load-sharing*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Lesson:**
> A common KTU pitfall is to call the Opera House a "concrete shell." It is *not*. It is a **pre-cast rib system** that *looks* like a shell. The distinction matters because it determines formwork, construction time, and cost.

### 3.1.5 Lens-7: Critical Evaluation

- ✅ **What worked:** The spherical solution enabled *modular repetition* — 7 years of construction (vs. an estimated 20+ with original free-form shells).
- ❌ **What didn't:** The original interior finishes (designed by Utzon) were never built after he resigned in 1966 — a *massive* architectural loss.
- 🎓 **What we learn:** *Geometry unifies architecture and engineering*. When Arup forced the geometry into a single sphere, he gave Utzon what he wanted and gave the builder what he needed.

---

## 3.2 KTU-Mandated Case Study: The Lotus Temple (Fariborz Sahba, 1986)

### 3.2.1 Lens-3: Structure & Load Path — The "27 Free-Standing Petals"

The Lotus Temple in New Delhi has **27 free-standing marble-clad "petals"** arranged in three concentric rings (9 inner, 9 middle, 9 outer). Each petal is a **rigid frame** of reinforced concrete.

For any single **outer petal**:
- Height $H_p = 34.27 \text{ m}$
- Width at base $W_p = 14.5 \text{ m}$
- Thickness $t = 0.7 \text{ m}$

The **critical load case** is wind. NCR's basic wind speed is $V_b = 47 \text{ m/s}$ (per IS 875 Part 3). With a topography factor $k_1 = 1.0$, terrain factor $k_2 = 1.05$, and importance factor $k_3 = 1.0$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_z &= V_b \cdot k_1 \cdot k_2 \cdot k_3 \\
V_z &= 47 \times 1.0 \times 1.05 \times 1.0 = 49.35 \text{ m/s} \\
p_z &= 0.6 \cdot V_z^{2} = 0.6 \times (49.35)^{2} = 1461 \text{ N/m}^{2} \approx 1.46 \text{ kPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

This wind pressure acts on the **projected area** of one petal. The projected area of an outer petal (treating it as a triangle of base 14.5 m and height 34.27 m):

$$
A_p = \frac{1}{2} \times 14.5 \times 34.27 = 248.5 \text{ m}^{2}
$$

Total wind force on a single outer petal:

$$
F_w = p_z \times C_d \times A_p = 1.46 \times 1.2 \times 248.5 = 435.4 \text{ kN}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters:** This ~**435 kN** horizontal force is resisted by the petal acting as a **cantilever** fixed at the podium. The 27-petal arrangement, however, creates **mutual self-shielding** — wind hitting one petal is partially blocked by the next, reducing the design wind load by nearly 30 %.

### 3.2.2 Lens-4: Materials & Construction

- **Primary structure:** Reinforced concrete.
- **Cladding:** Pentelikon marble (Greece) and Udaipur white marble (India) — chosen for *reflectivity* (low heat gain).
- **Innovation:** Each petal was cast using **custom CNC-milled timber formwork** (a first in India, 1980s).

---

## 3.3 KTU-Mandated Case Study: The Centre Pompidou (Piano & Rogers, 1977)

### 3.3.1 Lens-3 & Lens-4: The Inverted Structural Logic

The Centre Pompidou is the **antithesis** of the Opera House's sculptural shells. Its engineering philosophy is the **inside-out structural expression**: all services (HVAC ducts, water pipes, electrical conduits, escalators) are *outside* the building. The result is a **column-free interior**.

The **internal columns** are **14 steel box columns** spaced at 12.8 m in one direction and 48 m in the other. Each column is a **built-up box** of:

$$
A_{col} = 2 \times (b \times t) + 2 \times ((h - 2t) \times t)
$$

For the actual column ($b = 800 \text{ mm}$, $h = 800 \text{ mm}$, $t = 25 \text{ mm}$):

$$
A_{col} = 2(800 \times 25) + 2((800 - 50) \times 25) = 40{,}000 + 37{,}500 = 77{,}500 \text{ mm}^{2}
$$

Tributary load per column (approx. 6 floors, $q = 12 \text{ kN/m}^{2}$):

$$
P_{col} = 12.8 \times 48 \times 12 \times 6 = 44{,}236.8 \text{ kN} \approx 44.2 \text{ MN}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters:** This 44 MN load per column is *extremely high*. The reason it works is **high-strength steel (S355)** and **gusseted box-section connections** at every floor. The Pompidou is a textbook in *how to hide nothing*.

---

> [!NOTE]
> **The CAM is your exam-room cheat sheet** — for any "compare two case studies" question, fill this table.

### Table 3.1 — KTU High-Yield Comparative Analysis of Three Case Studies

| Lens / Parameter | Sydney Opera House | Lotus Temple | Centre Pompidou |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Year & Location** | 1973, Australia | 1986, India | 1977, France |
| **Architect** | Jørn Utzon | Fariborz Sahba | Renzo Piano & Richard Rogers |
| **Engineer** | Ove Arup & Partners | Flint & Neill (UK) | Peter Rice (Ove Arup) |
| **Primary Material** | Pre-cast concrete | In-situ concrete + marble | Structural steel |
| **Structural Form** | Spherical-segment ribs | Free-standing petals | Internal mega-columns + girders |
| **Span (largest)** | 75 m (shell) | 14.5 m (petal base) | 48 m (column grid) |
| **Floor Area** | ~ 62,000 m² | ~ 27,000 m² (incl. gardens) | ~ 100,000 m² |
| **FAR** | ~ 1.6 | ~ 0.45 | ~ 5.0 |
| **Critical Lens** | Geometry-driven design | Climate + Symbolism | Service-driven expression |
| **Innovation** | Spherical solution | Self-shielding petal cluster | Inside-out services |
| **Construction Time** | 14 years (1959–73) | 8 years (1978–86) | 6 years (1971–77) |
| **SEI (Usable / Enclosed)** | ~ 0.6 | ~ 0.7 | ~ 1.2 |
| **Cultural Symbolism** | "Sails of Sydney" | "Lotus — multi-faith unity" | "High-tech transparency" |
| **Key Lesson** | Form follows geometry, then structure | Form follows symbolism, then climate | Form follows services |

## 4.1 AECSM 7-Lens Analytical Flow

The following Mermaid block visualises the **7-Lens Architectural Engineering Case Study Methodology (AECSM)** as a sequential decision tree, so that the student can apply it to *any* new case study in the exam.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Choose a Case Study Building] --> B[Lens 1: Brief and Context]
    B --> B1[Identify client, site, climate, programme]
    B1 --> C[Lens 2: Form and Geometry]
    C --> C1[Decode plan, section, massing logic]
    C1 --> D[Lens 3: Structure and Load Path]
    D --> D1[Identify primary structural system]
    D1 --> E[Lens 4: Materials and Construction]
    E --> E1[Map material choice to construction sequence]
    E1 --> F[Lens 5: Services and Performance]

```

# 📘 SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define the term "Case Study" as applied in Architectural Engineering. List the five standard typological clusters of case studies recognised under the KTU 2024 Scheme.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> A *Case Study* in Architectural Engineering is an empirical, in-depth investigation of a real-world built project wherein the architectural intent, structural logic, material behaviour, construction methodology, services integration, and contextual response are critically analysed to extract transferable design and engineering principles. **(1 mark)**
> The five standard typological clusters recognised under the KTU 2024 Scheme are: **(½ mark each)**
> 1. Typological Case Studies
> 2. Structural/Constructional Case Studies
> 3. Material-Innovation Case Studies
> 4. Contextual/Responsive Case Studies
> 5. Sustainability/Performance Case Studies

### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**What is the AECSM 7-Lens framework? Name any four lenses.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> The **AECSM (Architectural Engineering Case Study Methodology)** is a structured analytical framework that uses seven "lenses" to examine any built project comprehensively. Each lens corresponds to a specific dimension of architectural engineering and ensures that no aspect of the building is left unanalysed. **(1 mark)**
> Any four of the seven lenses: **(½ mark each)**
> 1. Lens-1: Brief & Context
> 2. Lens-2: Form & Geometry
> 3. Lens-3: Structure & Load Path
> 4. Lens-4: Materials & Construction
> 5. Lens-5: Services & Performance
> 6. Lens-6: Contextual & Cultural Response
> 7. Lens-7: Critical Evaluation

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks with a sub-division typically as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. You are expected to write a structured, illustrated answer in approximately **6–8 pages** of the answer booklet. **Internal choice** is mandatory.

---

### ❓ QUESTION A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]`

**"Architecture and engineering are not two disciplines but two halves of the same act of building." With reference to the Sydney Opera House, justify this statement by analysing the project through the AECSM framework. Also calculate the rise (sagitta) of the largest roof shell given $R = 75 \text{ m}$ and chord $L = 75 \text{ m}$.**

#### Part (a) — AECSM Analysis of Sydney Opera House (7 Marks)

**Lens-1 — Brief & Context (1 mark):**
The project originated from a 1956 international competition by the Government of New South Wales to create a national performing arts centre at Bennelong Point, Sydney. The site is a man-made peninsula reclaimed from a fort, exposed to marine climate, strong NE solar gain, and high salt content in the air.

**Lens-2 — Form & Geometry (1 mark):**
The roof comprises multiple "sail" or "shell" forms. Crucially, these are *not* free-form shells but segments cut from a single sphere of radius $R = 75 \text{ m}$. This "spherical solution" gave geometric unity to the entire roofscape.

**Lens-3 — Structure & Load Path (1 mark):**
Each shell is a **pre-cast rib system**, not a true concrete shell. 6000+ pre-cast ribs are joined at cast-steel "ball" nodes. Load is transferred as compression/tension in the ribs, down to in-situ concrete columns, and into the podium foundation.

**Lens-4 — Materials & Construction (1 mark):**
Reinforced concrete was the primary material, with the ribs cast in heated oil-filled moulds (a world-first). The pre-cast panels forming the roof skin carry part of the load and double as the final finish.

**Lens-5 — Services & Performance (1 mark):**
The podium (designed by the Hall, Todd, and Littlemore team after Utzon's exit) houses the major halls, with services routed through interstitial ceilings. The thick concrete shell provides excellent thermal mass — a passive cooling asset.

**Lens-6 — Contextual & Cultural Response (1 mark):**
The shell forms evoke the sails of the harbour — a contextual response to Sydney's maritime identity. The building has become a cultural icon and a UNESCO World Heritage Site (2007).

**Lens-7 — Critical Evaluation (1 mark):**
The Arup-Utzon collaboration turned an "unbuildable" free-form vision into a constructible masterpiece. However, the *interior* of the original design was never realised after Utzon's resignation, a tragic loss to architecture.

#### Part (b) — Sagitta Calculation (7 Marks)

**Given:** Sphere radius $R = 75 \text{ m}$, chord (span) $L = 75 \text{ m}$.

**Formula:**

$$h = R - \sqrt{R^{2} - \left(\dfrac{L}{2}\right)^{2}}$$

**Step-by-step solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
h &= 75 - \sqrt{(75)^{2} - \left(\dfrac{75}{2}\right)^{2}} \\[4pt]
&= 75 - \sqrt{5625 - 1406.25} \\[4pt]
&= 75 - \sqrt{4218.75} \\[4pt]
&= 75 - 64.95 \\[4pt]
&\approx 10.05 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** The rise of the largest shell is approximately **10.05 m**, which matches the actual constructed value. **(1 mark for formula, 5 marks for stepwise substitution, 1 mark for result + remark)**

---

### ❓ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]`

**"The Lotus Temple is a masterclass in structural symbolism and climate response." Discuss the structural and material innovation of the Lotus Temple, New Delhi, and estimate the wind force on a single outer petal given $V_b = 47 \text{ m/s}$, $k_1 = 1.0$, $k_2 = 1.05$, $k_3 = 1.0$, $C_d = 1.2$, and projected area $= 248.5 \text{ m}^{2}$.**

#### Part (a) — Structural & Material Innovation (7 Marks)

**Brief & Form (2 marks):**
The Lotus Temple (Fariborz Sahba, 1986) is the **Bahá'í House of Worship** in New Delhi. It comprises 27 free-standing "petals" arranged in three concentric rings of 9 each, surrounding a central worship hall. The form directly evokes the lotus flower, India's national flower — a contextual-symbolic choice.

**Structural System (3 marks):**
Each petal is a **rigid reinforced concrete frame** that cantilevers from the podium. The 27 petals are not connected to each other — they are *structurally independent*. The outer ring petals act as **wind shields** for the middle and inner rings, achieving a 30 % reduction in net wind load. The petals are thickened at the base (0.7 m) and tapered to a knife-edge (0.1 m) at the tip — an efficient distribution of bending moment.

**Material Innovation (2 marks):**
The petals are clad with **Pentelikon marble (Greece)** for the inner surface and **Udaipur white marble (India)** for the outer surface, both chosen for *high albedo* (low solar absorption). Concrete was cast using CNC-milled timber formwork — a first in India at that time (1980s).

#### Part (b) — Wind Force Calculation (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Design Wind Speed $V_z$:**

$$V_z = V_b \cdot k_1 \cdot k_2 \cdot k_3 = 47 \times 1.0 \times 1.05 \times 1.0 = 49.35 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Design Wind Pressure $p_z$:**

$$p_z = 0.6 \cdot V_z^{2} = 0.6 \times (49.35)^{2} = 0.6 \times 2435.4 = 1461.24 \text{ N/m}^{2} \approx 1.46 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step 3 — Wind Force on One Petal:**

$$
F_w = p_z \cdot C_d \cdot A_p = 1.46 \times 1.2 \times 248.5
$$

$$
F_w = 435.4 \text{ kN}
$$

**Result:** The wind force on a single outer petal is **~435.4 kN**. This is resisted as a cantilever bending moment at the base of the petal:

$$M_{base} = F_w \times \dfrac{H_p}{2} = 435.4 \times \dfrac{34.27}{2} = 7462 \text{ kN·m}$$

This bending moment governs the petal's reinforcement design at the base. **(Marks: 2 for $V_z$, 2 for $p_z$, 2 for $F_w$, 1 for moment remark)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Description vs. Analysis trap:** Students *describe* a building (e.g., "It has a beautiful roof") but do not *analyse* it through a lens. KTU rewards *engineering reasoning* — always quantify, always justify.
> 2. **Formula without substitution:** Writing $h = R - \sqrt{R^{2} - (L/2)^{2}}$ without substituting values gets 0 marks. *Show the arithmetic.*
> 3. **Ignoring the Brief:** A case study without Lens-1 (brief) is incomplete. KTU deducts 1 mark minimum.
> 4. **Mixing up case studies:** Calling the Opera House a "concrete shell" or the Pompidou "all glass" loses *easy* marks. Memorise 2–3 case studies thoroughly rather than 10 superficially.
> 5. **No diagrams:** Every KTU case study answer *must* include at least one labelled sketch/diagram. Carry a pencil.

## 5.3 📋 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (last 60 seconds before the exam):**

- ✅ A **Case Study** is an empirical analysis of a real built project, not a marketing brochure.
- ✅ The **AECSM 7-Lens** framework is the gold standard: **Brief → Form → Structure → Material → Services → Context → Critique**.
- ✅ The **5 Typological Clusters**: Typological, Structural, Material, Contextual, Sustainability.
- ✅ **Sydney Opera House:** Spherical solution, $R = 75 \text{ m}$, **not** a shell — a pre-cast rib system.
- ✅ **Lotus Temple:** 27 free-standing petals, **self-shielding wind reduction ~30 %**, marble cladding for albedo.
- ✅ **Centre Pompidou:** **Inside-out services** — colour-coded ducts on the exterior, column-free interior.
- ✅ **Must-know formulas:** FAR, GCR, WWR, DF, U-value, EUI, sagitta $h = R - \sqrt{R^{2} - (L/2)^{2}}$.
- ✅ **Critical evaluation (Lens-7) carries 1 mark in every Part A/B question** — never skip it.
- ✅ **Always quantify** your answer: numbers, formulas, units. KTU values *measurable* analysis.
- ✅ **Always include a labelled sketch or Mermaid-style flow diagram** in your answer booklet.
- ✅ **Memorise 2–3 case studies deeply** (with all 7 lenses + 2 numerical examples) rather than 10 shallowly.
- ✅ The **Comparative Analytical Matrix (CAM)** is your exam-room cheat sheet for "compare two case studies" questions.
